data.author: Rauscher (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would:

  • Change the receipt deadline for UOCAVA ballots for special elections from the 15th day after Election Day to the 10th day after Election Day. The bill would leave in place the existing requirement that ballots be mailed by Election Day. This change would align the deadline with primary elections rather than with general elections, as is the case under existing law.
  • Change the requirements for the appointment of members of an election board. Specifically, the bill would require the election supervisor to appoint one nominee of the political party of which the governor is a member and one nominee of the political party that received the second largest number of votes statewide in the preceding gubernatorial election. Under existing law, an election supervisor must appoint one nominee of the political party with the largest number of registered voters at the time of the preceding gubernatorial election and one nominee of the political party with the second largest number of voters. 
  • Change the requirement for the appointment of members of the Alaska Public Offices Commission. Specifically, the bill would require the governor to appoint two members of each of the two political parties whose candidate for governor received the highest number of votes in the most recent gubernatorial election. Under existing law, the governor is required to appoint two members of each of the two political parties or political groups with the largest number of registered voters at the time of the most recent gubernatorial election.
  • Specify that a candidate for office that does not affiliate with a political party would still be entitled to appoint a watcher at a counting center. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated September 26, 2025

data.author: Story (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would:

  • Move the minimum age for pre-registration earlier, from 90 days before an applicant turns 18 to 16 years old.
  • Require the Division of Elections to register pre-registered individuals 90 days before their 18th birthday and mail them a registration card. The individual would be required to return the card no later than 45 days after the date of the notice in order to become registered.
  • Establish that the addresses and phone numbers of pre-registered voters are confidential and not open to public inspection.

Last Updated April 2, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on FINANCE

data.author: Schrage (NP)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 14, 2025, would eliminate the existing witness requirement for mail ballots; create a new statewide notice and cure process for mail ballots; create same-day registration and permit registration within 30 days of an election; extend the early voting period; and make additional changes to voting procedures and election administration. 


Concerning the completion, return, and verification of mail ballots, the bill would:

  • Eliminate the requirement that a voter complete their mail ballot in the presence of a notary or witness, and instead require the voter only to sign and date the ballot. Under the bill, the date listed on the certificate must be on or before Election Day for the ballot to be counted. Existing law requires voters to have their mail ballots witnessed by an individual qualified to administer oaths, such as a notary public, unless one is not reasonably accessible. In that case, the voter may use anyone over 18 years old. The witness must sign the ballot attesting that the voter cast the ballot on the date shown. In addition, the voter must include an identifier, which can be their birthdate. 
  • Create a cure process for ballots that are rejected due to a missing signature or insufficient voter identification. Existing law does not provide for any cure process. The new cure process would require the following: 
    • The director of elections would be required to immediately make a reasonable effort to contact the voter, explain the ballot deficiency, explain how the deficiency may be cured, and inform the voter of the deadline to cure the ballot. 
    • Notice to the voter would be required within 24 hours by email, text, and/or phone if possible, and within 48 hours but no later than five days after Election Day by first class, non-forwardable mail. 
    • For a defective ballot to be cured and counted, the voter would be required to return the completed form sent with the notice of deficiency electronically or by mail within 14 days of the election, confirm that they returned a ballot, provide a signature, and include a copy of their photo ID, and the ballot must otherwise be valid. 
    • If the voter returns the cure form indicating that they did not return a ballot, the director would be required to send the signature on the mail ballot and in the voter's file to the attorney general for investigation. 
  • Modify requirements concerning the timeliness of postmarked mail ballots. Under existing law, if a ballot is postmarked, it may only be counted if the postmark is dated on or before Election Day, and the ballot is received by the tenth day after the election. The bill would eliminate this postmark requirement, and instead provide that mail ballots without a postmark, or with a postmark after Election Day, may not be counted unless the ballot is marked with (1) a postal service barcode indicating it was mailed on or before ElectionDay, or (2) a Division of Elections barcode indicating the same.  
  • Require officials to provide a pre-addressed, postage prepaid return envelope for mail ballots. Existing law is silent as to return postage for mail ballots, and in practice, voters must pay for return by mail. The bill would also expressly prohibit identification of a voter's party affiliation on the ballot envelope. Existing law has no comparable provision. 
  • Move the start of mail ballot verification and counting earlier, to no less than 10 days before Election Day. Under existing law, ballot verification must begin no later than seven days before Election Day, and counting must begin at 8 p.m. on Election Day. The bill would also require the count of mail ballots to be made by 8 p.m. on Election Day. Existing law has no deadline for reporting a first count. 
  • Make conforming changes throughout the code consistent with the new provisions. 

Concerning standard and same-day registration, the bill would: 

  • Create same-day registration (SDR), and permit registration by any method during the 30 days before Election Day for all elections. Under existing law, same-day registration is available only for the purposes of voting for president and vice president of the United States. For all other elections, registration ends 30 days before Election Day and registrants after this time are not permitted to vote in that election. Under the bill, individuals registering within 30 days of an election or on Election Day would be required to:
    • Provide a declaration stating that they established residency at least 30 days before the date of the election in the state and the house district in which they seek to vote. 
    • Cast only an in-person mail ballot, special needs ballot, or questioned ballot. Election officials would be expressly prohibited from rejecting a ballot from such voters on the grounds that the voter is not on the official registration list for that election. The bill would also allow SDR voters to declare a political party affiliation when voting their ballot. 
  • Modify the rules for determining residence for voter registration by:
    • Defining residence as the place of "physical habitation," for which when the person is absent they have an "articulable and reasonable plan" for return. Under existing law, the definition is for a place of habitation, for which the person has an "intention" to return when absent.
    • Eliminating the existing provision that defines a change of residence as requiring an act of removal joined with the intent to remain in another place. 
    • Expressly requiring loss of residence status when residence is established in another state. Existing law specifies loss of residence for voting in another state, and provides that there can be only one residence, but does not otherwise directly address establishing residence elsewhere.  
  • Require officials to provide registration applicants with the option to request election materials in certain available non-English languages. 
  • Expressly allow registration applicants or their agent to provide an electronic signature when applying by mail, fax, scanning, or other electronic means. Existing law has no comparable provision.
  • Require officials to develop a process to allow a voter to cancel their registration electronically or in person, and to prominently display instructions on how to cancel registration at each polling place. Existing law has no comparable provision. 
  • Require the director of elections to develop a cybersecurity program to "defend" voter registration records from cyber attacks and security breaches. 

Concerning the request of mail ballots and alternative ballots, the bill would:

  • Expand the period for special needs ballot requests, by moving the start date to the 30 days before an election. Under existing law, these requests are accepted beginning 15 days before. The bill would also expressly provide that a special needs ballot may not be rejected due to errors by the voter's representative or election officials, if the ballot is otherwise valid. Existing law has no comparable provision.
  • Apply existing requirements for voter certificates for in-person mail ballots and questioned ballots to special needs ballots, and require the form to include instructions for designating party affiliation.  
  • Require officials to provide mail ballot materials in other languages, as a conforming change to a new requirement to do so for all election materials. 

Concerning in-person early voting, the bill would:

  • Extend the early voting period by moving the first day of voting earlier, from the 15th day before an election to the 20th day before. As under existing law, the voting period would end the day before Election Day. Existing law provides for both "early voting" and "in-person absentee" voting during the early voting period, depending on the voting location's access to the state's voter database. Under the bill, the new earlier start date would apply for in-person requests of a mail ballot from an elections official or at an early voting location (in-person absentee voting), and for the early voting process.
  • For the "early voting" method, permit voters who cannot provide required voter ID or verification of residence to vote a mail ballot by the "in-person absentee" method, which would later be verified prior to counting. . NB: Existing provisions for in-person absentee voting, which the bill does not change, also require presentation of an accepted form of voter ID.  
  • Move the deadline earlier for designation of early voting locations, from 15 days before Election Day to 30 days before.

Concerning voter list maintenance, the bill would:

  • Expand the categories of voters who receive an address confirmation notice during annual list maintenance of the voter roll, and shorten the timeframe for inactivating a registration. The bill would:
    • Modify and expand the categories of voters that receive a confirmation notice. Under existing law, non-forwardable notice is sent to voters who (1) have had mail returned to election officials as undeliverable in the last two years, or (2) have not contacted the Election Division in the last two years, or (3) have not voted or appeared in vote in the last two general elections. Under the bill, notice would be sent to voters who: 
      • Had mail returned to the election division within the last two years, or
      • Have not contacted the division and have not voted or appeared to vote within the last two years, or
      • (1) Registered to vote in another state, (2) received a driver's license in another state, (3) registered a vehicle in another state, (4) received public assistance from another state, (5) served on a jury in another state, (6) obtained a resident hunting or fishing license in another state, (7) paid the state resident tuition rate for a public university in another state, (8) received a homestead or residential property tax exemption in another state, or (9) received a benefit available only to residents of another state
    • Require that confirmation mailings be sent by forwardable mail, rather than non-forwardable mail as required under existing law, and by email.  
    • Require voters to respond to the initial confirmation notice within 45 days to maintain their active registration status. Existing law requires officials to notify voters that their registration will be made inactive only after (1) an initial confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, and (2) the voter has not contacted the division, voted, or appeared to vote in the past four calendar years. If the voter does not respond within 45 days, their registration is inactivated, and subject to cancellation. 

Concerning polling place procedures, the bill would:

  • Permit voting by in-person mail ballot, special needs ballot, or questioned ballot for voters who:
    • Re-register, or transfer their registration within a house district, within 30 days of an election. Under existing law, these voters were subject to the general registration deadline of 30 days before an election. 
    • Claim to be a registered voter but for whom no evidence of registration can be found to vote. Under existing law, these individuals must vote a questioned ballot and the board of directors must determine whether the voter is registered in the house district before counting the ballot.
    • Have lived in a municipality for at least 30 days but not registered within the jurisdiction, and wish to vote in a municipal election except for election for specific districts. The bill would also repeal the requirement that individuals be registered at an address in a municipality to qualify to vote in municipal elections.
  • Require the division to provide a voter with election materials in a language in which the division is required to provide materials under federal law. The division would be required to notify a voter when materials in their designated language are not available and allow them to designate another language.
  • Expressly require language assistance at polling places, consistent with federal law, and require officials to post notice of available assistance at each location. 
  • Repeal a requirement that specifies provision of voting booths that are at least six feet in height. 
  • Require an election official to advise an individual seeking to vote in person that if their name appears on the registration list as having received or voted a mail ballot, they may surrender their mail ballot for destruction or cast a questioned ballot. If the voter does not surrender their mail ballot, they would be allowed to vote a questioned ballot only. 
  • Allow, during a challenge to a voter's qualifications, the challenger to rebut the presumptive evidence of the voter's residence by providing evidence that the voter has established residence in another state or place, or is not a resident. Under existing law, the address of a voter on the voting list is presumptive evidence of their residence, negated only by the voter's written notification of a change, and voters challenged as ineligible may provide a sworn affirmation of their qualification to vote. 
  • Expressly specify that compensation for election board members may only be reduced from the set rate by regulation. Existing law has no comparable provision. 

Concerning election observers and facilitation of voting access for rural communities, the bill would:

  • Narrow the role of political party representatives in the ballot counting process by permitting them to observe counting, rather than to assist as provided by existing law. 
  • Require the elections director to employ five rural community liaisons responsible for collaborating with tribes, municipalities, corporations, regional nonprofit corporations, and other community organizations to facilitate access to early and mail voting in rural areas of the state and ensure that rural areas of the state are fully staffed. 

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.



A previous version of this bill contained provisions that were amended out. Those provisions would have:

  • Eliminated automatic voter registration, by repealing the provisions currently providing for the process through applications to the Permanent Fund Dividend, and make conforming changes to reflect the repeal.
  • Required an individual registering in the 30 days before Election Day or on Election Day to provide a specified form of ID with the applicant's name and address. Under existing law, applicants must provide an Alaska driver's license, ID card, or the last four digits of their Social Security number.

Last Updated September 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: Additionally referred to HOUSE Committee on FINANCE

data.author: House Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would eliminate automatic voter registration, shorten the early voting period, move the deadline earlier for receipt of mail ballots, change voter list maintenance practices, make changes to mail ballot processing procedures, redefine a voter's residence for purposes of voter registration, and expand the circumstances in which elections can conduct an election by mail. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Eliminate automatic voter registration, by repealing the provisions currently providing for the process through applications to the Permanent Fund Dividend, and make conforming changes to reflect the repeal.
  • Shorten the early voting period from a total of 15 days to 10 days, by moving the end date to five days before Election Day. Under existing law, early voting is available "for 15 days before an election." Under the bill, the period would begin on the 15th day before an election and end five days before.
  • Move the receipt deadline earlier for return of a mail ballot, to the close of business on Election Day, and make conforming changes. Under existing law, a completed mail ballot is timely if postmarked no later than Election Day and received by the close of business on the tenth day after the election. 
  • Expand the categories of voters who receive an address confirmation notice during routine list maintenance of the voter roll, and shorten the timeframe for inactivating a registration. Under existing law, notice is sent to voters who (1) have had mail returned to election officials as undeliverable in the last two years, or (2) have not contacted the Election Division in the last two years, or (3) have not voted or appeared in vote in the last two general elections. The bill would:
    • Expand the categories of voters that receive a confirmation notice to include to voters who, in the last two years, became ineligible to receive a Permanent Fund dividend or obtained a driver's license in another state.
    • Require that confirmation notices be sent by forwardable mail. Under existing law, initial confirmation notice must be sent by non-forwardable mail only.
    • Require officials to inactivate a voter's registration if the voter fails to respond to the confirmation notice within 45 days. Under existing law, officials may only inactivate a voter's registration if (1) an initial confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, and the voter has not contacted the election division or voted or appeared to vote in the last four years; and (2) the voter fails to respond to a second confirmation notice, sent by forwardable mail, within 45 days.
  • Eliminate the requirement that an individual have the intention to return home for the purposes of determining a voter's residence, and make conforming changes.
  • Require officials to provide a pre-addressed, postage prepaid return envelope to voters requesting a mail ballot. Existing law is silent as to return postage for mail ballots, and in practice, voters must pay for return by mail.
  • Allow absentee voting officials to send ballots to election supervisors via electronic transmission as a means of "mail service." Under existing law, those ballots must be sent by the most expeditious mail service, which does not include any electronic methods.
  • Increase the amount of time election officials have to process mail ballots before an election. The bill would require the election supervisor to review all voter certificates of absentee ballots received by that date no less than 10 days before an election. Under existing law, the election supervisor must review them no less than seven days before an election.
  • Expand the elections that may be conducted by mail ballot, to include an election for a community with a population of 750 or less. Under existing law, elections by mail are permitted only for those elections held at a time other than when the general, primary party, or municipal election is held.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated February 12, 2025

data.author: House Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 7, 2026, would layer signature verification on top of existing absentee voting verification requirements, create a cure program for some rejected absentee ballots; make changes to accepted forms of voter ID; require new routine list maintenance procedures and modified the existing annual procedure; create new criminal and civil penalties related to elections; and make various other changes. 


Concerning mail ballot voting, the bill would:

  • Add voter signature verification to existing requirements for absentee ballot verification. Existing law requires absentee voters to have a witness or notary sign their absentee ballot envelope. The bill would maintain that requirement and also require election officials to compare the voter's signature on the absentee ballot envelope to the voter's registration record.
    • The bill would require the use of signature verification software and the signature comparison training for local election officials.
  • Create a cure process for mail ballots that are rejected due to a missing or mismatched voter signature. Under existing law, voters are notified when their mail ballot is rejected, but there is no statewide process for curing defects. Under the new process: 
    • If a ballot is rejected, officials must contact the voter explaining the ballot deficiency, how the deficiency may be cured, and the deadline to cure the ballot. Notice must be sent within 48 hours (and no later than two days after Election Day) by mail, and may be provided by email, text, or telephone if the voter provided that information. The notice must include a printed form for the voter to provide a signature for verification and copy of voter ID for ballot verification.
    • The cure deadline would be 14 days after Election Day, and a rejected ballot would be counted if the voter confirmed that they returned a ballot, and provided a signature and copy of voter ID, and the ballot was otherwise valid. 
    • If the voter indicates on the cure form that they did not return a ballot, the bill would authorize the director of elections to send the signature on the return ballot to the attorney general for investigation.
  • Require officials to provide mail ballot voters with a postage-paid return envelope. Under existing law, voters must pay for postage to return a mail ballot.
  • Move the receipt deadline earlier for all mail ballots returned from outside the U.S. or by UOCAVA voters, to the 10th day after an election. Under existing law, the receipt deadline is the 10th day after a primary or special primary, and the 15th day after a general election or special election. 
  • Move the start of mail ballot processing earlier, from seven days before Election Day to 12 days before. 
  • Require the director of elections to create a procedure for challenging mail ballots, special needs ballots, and questioned ballots during the counting process, that provides a "reasonable amount of time" to submit a challenge. Existing law is silent as to a challenge process. 
  • Require officials to provide an online ballot tracking system that allows voters to know the date a ballot was sent out by officials, delivered, and received back by officials; whether the ballot was counted; and steps required to cure a defective ballot. The bill would specify that any vendor for the system must be a business incorporated in the U.S., and that the system must use multi-factor authentication. Online ballot tracking is not required under existing law, but the state currently provides information online as to delivery and return of ballots, and provides notice to a voter when a ballot is rejected.  
  • Specify that for the existing requirement that completed mail ballots be delivered by local officials to election supervisors for processing "by the most expeditious mail service," such service includes delivery by optical scanning and electronic transmission.

Concerning voter ID, the bill would:

  • Change the list of accepted ID for voter registration and casting a ballot.
    • For registration, the bill would eliminate use of a hunting or fishing license, and add tribal ID to accepted ID. The bill would leave in place the remaining forms of accepted ID: driver's license; state ID card; current and valid photo ID; birth certificate; passport; or waiver of the ID requirement by a registration official who knows the identity of the voter.
    • For voting in person (and certain first-time mail ballot voters), the bill would eliminate use of a hunting or fishing license, and add tribal ID to accepted ID. The bill would leave in place some remaining forms of accepted ID: driver's license; state ID card; current and valid photo ID; birth certificate; passport. The bill would also repeal existing language allowing the use of a utility bill, bank statement, government check, or other government document showing the voter's name and current address, the document must be dated within the previous 60 days. Existing law requires only that these documents be "current."

Concerning voter list maintenance, the bill would:

  • Permit the Division of Elections to use information provided by the Department of Revenue to conduct voter roll maintenance. Existing law has no comparable provision. The bill would also require the Division to submit an annual report to the governor, state secretary, and chief clerk of the House of Representatives annually detailing the number of permanent fund dividend applicant records shared with the division; the effect that shared permanent fund dividend applications had on voter roll maintenance and on election integrity; and security measures taken to protect voter information.
  • Expand the categories of voters who receive an address confirmation notice during annual list maintenance of the voter roll, and shorten the timeframe for inactivating a registration. Under existing law, non-forwardable notice is sent to voters who (1) have had mail returned to election officials as undeliverable in the last two years, or (2) have not contacted the Election Division in the last two years, or (3) have not voted or appeared in vote in the last two general elections. The bill would:
    • Modify the existing categories of voters receiving notice by requiring confirmation from voters who have not contacted the election division in the last two years and also have not voted or appeared in vote in the last 28 months, rather than each category of voters separately, as required under existing law. The bill also expressly provides that registering to vote does not constitute "contact" with the division. Existing law specifies various actions that constitute contact, but is silent as to registration.
    • Expand the categories of voters that receive a confirmation notice to additionally include voters who: 
      • During the last 28 months, (1) registered to vote in another state, (2) received a driver's license in another state, (3) registered a vehicle in another state, (4) received public assistance from another state, (5) served on a jury in another state, (6) received a homestead or residential property tax exemption in another state, or (7) received a benefit available only to residents of another state.
    • Require that confirmation notices be sent by forwardable mail and to the voter's email address, if available. Under existing law, initial confirmation notice must be sent by non-forwardable mail only. 
    • Require officials to inactivate a voter's registration if the voter fails to respond to the confirmation notice or provides an incomplete or ambiguous response within 45 days, and require notice to state the response deadline. Under existing law, officials may only inactivate a voter's registration if (1) an initial confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, and the voter has not contacted the election division or voted or appeared to vote in the last four years; and (2) the voter fails to respond to a second confirmation notice, sent by forwardable mail, within 45 days.
  • Require new routine list maintenance procedures, in addition to the existing annual address confirmation process. The bill would require the adoption of regulations to review voter registrations and update the master register, and direct the Division of Elections to:
    • Review the list for deceased voters, voters convicted of a felony involving moral turpitude, voters not qualified to vote, voters registered to vote in another state, whether the number of registered voters on the master list exceeds the number of eligible voters in the state, and voter registration information data breaches. 
    • Gather and examine available data from other states and information from the USPS forwarding databases, motor vehicle records, Department of Corrections records, property and sales tax records, U.S. Social Security Administration databases, jury duty records, national change of address records, and similar records. 
    • Annually audit the master register in consultation with an external, nationally recognized subject-matter expert. The division would be required to publish a report describing the audit by April 1 of each year.
    • Specify requirements for notice to voters under the removal provisions, and authorize the division to contact a voter and request information from the voter to determine their residence, in addition to issuing a required notice. 

Concerning voter registration, the bill would:

  • Expand grounds for challenges to a voter based on their residence. Under existing law, the address of a voter on their registration record is presumptive evidence of their voting residence, and may only be negated by the voter changing their residence in writing. Under the bill, the presumption may also be rebutted by providing evidence to the division that the voter has established residence in another country, state, or place in Alaska.
  • Require officials to post data about any breach of confidential voter information, and specify the scope and timing for such reports. 
  • Repeal a provision allowing an elections official to substitute a mailing address provided by a voter for the Permanent Fund Dividend program as the mailing address for the voter's registration record. 


Concerning criminal and civil penalties related to elections, the bill would:

  • Create a new class C felony for intentionally opening or tampering with a submitted mail ballot or package of ballots without express authorization from the director or intentionally breaching, hacking, altering, or tampering with election machinery. 
  • Create a new class C felony for knowingly disclosing, sharing, or reporting official election results, returns, or any confidential election data to an individual who is not an election official before the close of polls on Election Day.
  • Prohibit the use of "synthetic media" in electioneering activity with the intent to influence an election, and create a private right of action for individuals harmed by its use. The bill would set out definitions for prohibited and exempt conduct, defenses, and forms of relief. 

Concerning election outreach, data security, and observation of counting processes, the bill would:

  • Require the elections director to employ a rural community liaison responsible for collaborating with tribes, municipalities, corporations, regional nonprofit corporations, and other community organizations to facilitate access to early and mail voting in rural areas of the state and ensuring that rural areas of the state are fully staffed.
  • Require the Division of Elections to provide a report to the legislature by the first day of the 35th Legislature recommending options for expanding early voting in rural communities and low-income neighborhoods.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


The new signature comparison and cure provisions for absentee ballots would be effective on June 1, 2027.

Last Updated May 11, 2026

The resolution would also commit the legislature to monitoring the implementation of any federal voter registration requirements to ensure that no eligible Alaska citizen is denied the right to vote by citizenship documentation barriers.

status_actions.summary: INTRODUCED

data.author: Hughes (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 24, 2025, would: 

  • Require that all ballots be tabulated, counted, and the election be certified by 11:59 p.m. on Election Day. Under existing law, ballots may not be counted before 8 p.m. on Election Day and the election must be certified no later than the 15th day following the election. The bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes because of this change.
  • Allow the director of elections to delay the certificate of results of the election in order to receive and count ballots. 
  • Would require that all ballots be "delivered" to the director after counting rather than mailed.
  • Shorten the timeframe for early voting. The bill would require that early voting begin 15 days before an election and end the Saturday immediately preceding Election Day. Under existing law, early voting is available for 15 days before an election and ending on Election Day.
  • Require mail ballots to be postmarked and received on or before Election. Under existing law, mail ballots must be mailed no later than Election Day and received by the close of business on the 10th day after the election.
  • Move up the return deadline for mail ballots from overseas. The bill would require mail ballots returned from qualified overseas voters to be received no later than the close of business on Election Day. Under existing law, those bellows must be received no later than the close of business on the 10th day following a primary election or special primary election and the 15th day following a general election or special election.
  • Require the director to send a mail ballot and other voting materials to a UOCAVA voter at least 60 days before the election if the voter submits an application to the division at least 60 days in advance. If the voter submitted an application at least 45 days before an election, the director would be required to send them a mail ballot at least 45 days in advance, as is required under existing law.


Last Updated March 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: Vetoed by GOVERNOR

data.author: Senate Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill was vetoed by Governor Mike Dunleavy on April 30, 2026. The bill, as amended on March 23, 2026, would have created a cure program for rejected ballots; made changes to accepted forms of voter ID; required new routine list maintenance procedures and modified the existing annual procedure; created new criminal and civil penalties related to elections; and made various other changes. 


Concerning mail ballot voting, the bill would:

  • Create a cure process for mail ballots that are rejected due to a missing voter or witness signature or insufficient voter identification. Under existing law, voters are notified when their mail ballot is rejected, but there is no statewide process for curing defects. Under the new process: 
    • If a ballot is rejected, officials must "immediately make a reasonable effort" to contact the voter explaining the ballot deficiency, how the deficiency may be cured, and the deadline to cure the ballot. Notice must be sent within 24 hours by email and/or telephone call and text, if contacts were provided, and within 48 hours (and no later than five days after Election Day) by mail. The notice must include a printed form for the voter to provide required information for ballot verification, and officials must also make a cure form available for electronic submission.
    • The cure deadline would be 10 days after Election Day, and a rejected ballot would be counted if the voter confirmed that they returned a ballot, and provided a signature and copy of voter ID, and the ballot was otherwise valid. 
    • If the voter indicates on the cure form that they did not return a ballot, the bill would authorize the director of elections to send the signature on the return ballot to the attorney general for investigation.
  • Require officials to provide mail ballot voters with a postage-paid return envelope. Under existing law, voters must pay for postage to return a mail ballot.
  • Move the receipt deadline earlier for all mail ballots returned from outside the U.S. or by UOCAVA voters, to the 10th day after an election. Under existing law, the receipt deadline is the 10th day after a primary or special primary, and the 15th day after a general election or special election. 
  • Move the start of mail ballot processing earlier, from seven days before Election Day to 12 days before. 
  • Require the director of elections to create a procedure for challenging mail ballots, special needs ballots, and questioned ballots during the counting process, that provides a "reasonable amount of time" to submit a challenge. Existing law is silent as to a challenge process. 
  • Require officials to provide an online ballot tracking system that allows voters to know the date a ballot was sent out by officials, delivered, and received back by officials; whether the ballot was counted; and steps required to cure a defective ballot. The bill would specify that any vendor for the system must be a business incorporated in the U.S., and that the system must use multi-factor authentication. Online ballot tracking is not required under existing law, but the state currently provides information online as to delivery and return of ballots, and provides notice to a voter when a ballot is rejected.  
  • Specify that for the existing requirement that completed mail ballots be delivered by local officials to election supervisors for processing "by the most expeditious mail service," such service includes delivery by optical scanning and electronic transmission.

Concerning voter ID, the bill would:

  • Change the list of accepted ID for voter registration and casting a ballot.
    • For registration, the bill would eliminate use of a hunting or fishing license, and add tribal ID to accepted ID. The bill would leave in place the remaining forms of accepted ID: driver's license; state ID card; current and valid photo ID; birth certificate; passport; or waiver of the ID requirement by a registration official who knows the identity of the voter.
    • For voting in person (and certain first-time mail ballot voters), the bill would eliminate use of a hunting or fishing license, and add tribal ID to accepted ID. The bill would leave in place some remaining forms of accepted ID: driver's license; state ID card; current and valid photo ID; birth certificate; passport. The bill would also repeal existing language allowing the use of a utility bill, bank statement, government check, or other government document showing the voter's name and current address, the document must be dated within the previous 60 days. Existing law requires only that these documents be "current."

Concerning voter list maintenance, the bill would:

  • Permit the Division of Elections to use information provided by the Department of Revenue to conduct voter roll maintenance. Existing law has no comparable provision. The bill would also require the Division to submit an annual report to the governor, state secretary, and chief clerk of the House of Representatives annually detailing the number of permanent fund dividend applicant records shared with the division; the effect that shared permanent fund dividend applications had on voter roll maintenance and on election integrity; and security measures taken to protect voter information.
  • Expand the categories of voters who receive an address confirmation notice during annual list maintenance of the voter roll, and shorten the timeframe for inactivating a registration. Under existing law, non-forwardable notice is sent to voters who (1) have had mail returned to election officials as undeliverable in the last two years, or (2) have not contacted the Election Division in the last two years, or (3) have not voted or appeared in vote in the last two general elections. The bill would:
    • Modify the existing categories of voters receiving notice by requiring confirmation from voters who have not contacted the election division in the last two years and also have not voted or appeared in vote in the last 28 months, rather than each category of voters separately, as required under existing law. The bill also expressly provides that registering to vote does not constitute "contact" with the division. Existing law specifies various actions that constitute contact, but is silent as to registration.
    • Expand the categories of voters that receive a confirmation notice to additionally include voters who: 
      • During the last 28 months, (1) registered to vote in another state, (2) received a driver's license in another state, (3) registered a vehicle in another state, (4) received public assistance from another state, (5) served on a jury in another state, (6) received a homestead or residential property tax exemption in another state, or (7) received a benefit available only to residents of another state.
    • Require that confirmation notices be sent by forwardable mail and to the voter's email address, if available. Under existing law, initial confirmation notice must be sent by non-forwardable mail only. 
    • Require officials to inactivate a voter's registration if the voter fails to respond to the confirmation notice or provides an incomplete or ambiguous response within 45 days, and require notice to state the response deadline. Under existing law, officials may only inactivate a voter's registration if (1) an initial confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, and the voter has not contacted the election division or voted or appeared to vote in the last four years; and (2) the voter fails to respond to a second confirmation notice, sent by forwardable mail, within 45 days.
  • Require new routine list maintenance procedures, in addition to the existing annual address confirmation process. The bill would require the adoption of regulations to review voter registrations and update the master register, and direct the Division of Elections to:
    • Review the list for deceased voters, voters convicted of a felony involving moral turpitude, voters not qualified to vote, voters registered to vote in another state, whether the number of registered voters on the master list exceeds the number of eligible voters in the state, and voter registration information data breaches. 
    • Gather and examine available data from other states and information from the USPS forwarding databases, motor vehicle records, Department of Corrections records, property and sales tax records, U.S. Social Security Administration databases, jury duty records, national change of address records, and similar records. 
    • Annually audit the master register in consultation with an external, nationally recognized subject-matter expert. The division would be required to publish a report describing the audit by April 1 of each year.
    • Specify requirements for notice to voters under the removal provisions, and authorize the division to contact a voter and request information from the voter to determine their residence, in addition to issuing a required notice. 

Concerning voter registration, the bill would:

  • Expand grounds for challenges to a voter based on their residence. Under existing law, the address of a voter on their registration record is presumptive evidence of their voting residence, and may only be negated by the voter changing their residence in writing. Under the bill, the presumption may also be rebutted by providing evidence to the division that the voter has established residence in another country, state, or place in Alaska.
  • Require officials to post data about any breach of confidential voter information, and specify the scope and timing for such reports. 
  • Repeal a provision allowing an elections official to substitute a mailing address provided by a voter for the Permanent Fund Dividend program as the mailing address for the voter's registration record. 


Concerning criminal and civil penalties related to elections, the bill would:

  • Create a new class C felony for intentionally opening or tampering with a submitted mail ballot or package of ballots without express authorization from the director or intentionally breaching, hacking, altering, or tampering with election machinery. 
  • Create a new class C felony for knowingly disclosing, sharing, or reporting official election results, returns, or any confidential election data to an individual who is not an election official before the close of polls on Election Day.
  • Prohibit the use of "synthetic media" in electioneering activity with the intent to influence an election, and create a private right of action for individuals harmed by its use. The bill would set out definitions for prohibited and exempt conduct, defenses, and forms of relief. 

Concerning election outreach, data security, and observation of counting processes, the bill would:

  • Require the elections director to employ a rural community liaison responsible for collaborating with tribes, municipalities, corporations, regional nonprofit corporations, and other community organizations to facilitate access to early and mail voting in rural areas of the state and ensuring that rural areas of the state are fully staffed.
  • Require the Division of Elections to provide a report to the legislature by the first day of the 35th Legislature recommending options for expanding early voting in rural communities and low-income neighborhoods.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Previous versions of the bill contained additional provisions that were amended out of this version. Those provisions would have:

  • Eliminated the existing witness requirement for voting a mail ballot, and instead require that voters provide a signature and date on the ballot for verification. Under existing law, mail ballot voters must have their ballot witnessed by an individual qualified to administer oaths, such as a notary public or judge, or by an individual aged 18 or over.
  • Created a permanent mail voting list. Under existing law, a voter can request a mail ballot for a specific election or for all elections in that calendar year, and certain voters may request delivery of an automatic mail ballot application each year.
  • Required the provision of secure ballot drop boxes and require the director of elections to adopt regulations governing their use and location. While existing law is silent as to use of drop boxes, they have been available for some past elections.
  • Created same-day voter registration for all elections and extended the general registration period to include the 30 days before Election Day. Voters registered within 30 days or on Election Day would have been required to vote an absentee, special needs, or questioned ballot. Under existing law, same-day registration is available only for the purposes of voting for President and Vice President of the United States. For all other elections, registration ends 30 days before Election Day. 
  • Allowed preregistration for individuals who were at least 16 years of age. Existing law permits eligible residents to register within 90 days of their 18th birthday, but does not otherwise provide for preregistration. 
  • Change the requirements for what constitutes a voter's residence for registration purposes.
  • Specified that a mail ballot without a postmark or postmark after Election Day is timely only if marked with a USPS or Division of Elections tracking barcode verifying that the ballot was mailed on or before Election Day. Existing law provides that if a ballot is postmarked, it must be marked on or before Election Day, with no requirement related to barcode data.
  • Required the Division of Elections to permit a voter registration applicant to choose their desired language from a list of possible language options for election-related materials, and specified related requirements for language accessibility. 
  • Set out specific requirements for the location and number of ballot drop boxes.
  • Required an election official to advise an individual seeking to vote in-person that if their name appeared on the registration list as having received or voted a mail ballot, they may surrender their mail ballot for destruction or cast a questioned ballot. If the voter did not surrender their mail ballot, they would have been allowed to vote a questioned ballot.
  • Permitted a voter who has changed the place in which their physical habitation is fixed to continue to vote in the senate district or judicial district that includes the individual's former place of physical habitation if that voter has an articulable and reasonable plan to remain in or return to that senate or judicial district.
  • Repealed existing provisions allowing voters who have recently left their house district to vote by mail ballot for certain races in that district, and instead provide for voting following a move from a senate or judicial district. Under the provision, voters moved from a senate or judicial district would have been permitted to vote in their prior jurisdiction if they have "an articulable and reasonable plan" to move to a different place in the same district.
  • Permitted use of an electronic signature, by the voter or individual with power of attorney for the voter, for voter registration applications. Existing law permits only a signature or mark.

Last Updated May 4, 2026

status_actions.summary: INTRODUCED

data.author: Senate Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 24, 2025, would:

  • Shorten early voting to begin on the 15th day before an election and end five days before Election Day. Under existing law, early voting is available for the 15 days before Election Day and on Election Day.
  • Move up the deadline for the return of mail ballots. The bill would require absentee ballots returned by mail, including ballots sent by overseas voters, to be received by the close of business on Election Day. Under existing law, mail ballots must be postmarked by Election Day and received by the 10th day after the election. 
  • Eliminate automatic voter registration through the Permanent Fund Dividend application. The bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect that change.
  • Expand the categories of voters who receive an address confirmation notice during annual list maintenance of the voter roll, and shorten the timeframe for inactivating a registration. Under existing law, notice is sent to voters who (1) have had mail returned to election officials as undeliverable in the last two years, or (2) have not contacted the Election Division in the last two years, or (3) have not voted or appeared in vote in the last two general elections. The bill would:
    • Expand the categories of voters that receive a confirmation notice to include to voters who, in the last two years, became ineligible to receive a permanent fund dividend or obtained a driver's license in another state.
    • Require that confirmation notices be sent by forwardable mail. Under existing law, initial confirmation notice must be sent by non-forwardable mail only.
    • Require officials to inactivate a voter's registration if the voter fails to respond to the confirmation notice within 45 days. Under existing law, officials may only inactivate a voter's registration if (1) an initial confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, and the voter has not contacted the election division or voted or appeared to vote in the last four years; and (2) the voter fails to respond to a second confirmation notice, sent by forwardable mail, within 45 days.
  • Increase the amount of time election officials have to process absentee ballots before an election. The bill would require the election supervisor to review all voter certificates of absentee ballots received by that date no less than 10 days before an election. Under existing law, the election supervisor must review them no less than seven days before an election.
  • Eliminate the requirement that the lieutenant governor mail an absentee ballot application and pamphlet containing information about the upcoming election, including a notice of any change in the location of a polling place, to every household identified on the official registration list prior to every state general election. Instead, the bill would require the lieutenant governor to "publish" this information.
  • Require that voters be given pre-addressed and postage prepaid envelopes with absentee ballots. Under existing law, postage is not prepaid.
  • Eliminate the requirement that an individual have the intention to return home for the purposes of determining a voter's residence. The bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect that change.
  • Prohibit election officials from rejecting a voter's special needs ballot based on an error by an election official or voter's representative.
  • Allow absentee voting officials to send ballots to election supervisors via electronic transmission as a means of "mail service." Under existing law, those ballots must be sent by the most expeditious mail service, which does not include any electronic methods.
  • Allow election officials to conduct an election by mail for a community with a population of 750 or less. This bill would leave in place the existing law that officials may conduct an election by mail if it is held at a time other than when the general, primary party, or municipal election is held.
  • Repeal a requirement that polling places have a certain number of voting booths that are at least six feet in height.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated October 28, 2025

status_actions.summary: Additionally referred to SENATE Committee on FINANCE

    • The bill would require the use of signature verification software and the signature comparison training for local election officials.

data.author: Kiel (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 16, 2026, would expand access to voter lists, specifically permitting access of voter lists and confidential information to the federal and local government entities. The bill would prohibit the Secretary of State from charging more than 1,000$.

Last Updated April 20, 2026

data.author: Lovvorn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 13, 2026, requires county judges of probate to oversee a publicly available post-election audit for every county wide and state wide general election. The post-election audit must start no earlier than 31 days following the statewide general election or the expiration of the time period for filing an election contest, whichever is less, and must be completed within 30 days of commencement. The secretary of state must report all findings of the post-election audit to the governor and the legislature within 30 days of receipt of the audits. Prior state law did not require routine post-election audits.

Last Updated April 23, 2026

data.bill_name: Digital Driver Licenses and Nondriver ID Cards
data.author: Tillman (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 26, prohibits the use of mobile digital ID as an acceptable ID for voting purposes. The bill makes other changes not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

Last Updated April 13, 2026

data.bill_location: House Constitution, Campaigns and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on CONSTITUTION, CAMPAIGNS AND ELECTIONS

data.author: Clarke (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill would establish at least five (5) days of early voting with voting that would occur at early voting centers. The bill would require each county to have one early voting center for every 100,000 residents. Currently, Alabama does not permit in-person early voting.

Last Updated January 21, 2026

status_actions.summary: From HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY:  Reported favorably

data.author: Clarke (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 19, would establish automatic voting rights restoration for individuals disenfranchised due to a felony conviction. The bill would provide for automatic voting rights restoration for individuals who have been:

  • Released upon completion of their sentence;
  • Pardoned;
  • Completed all condition of probation or parole, except for the payment of court costs, fees, fines, or restitution.

This bill would not restore voting rights for an individual who has been impeached, or convicted of treason. The bill would be applicable to individuals who were convicted in a state, federal, or foreign court. Currently, Alabama does not have automatic voting rights restoration and requires individuals convicted of certain felonies to apply under a state process to obtain the ability to register to vote.


This bill would require the board of registers to register these individuals up to and including on Election Day.


This bill would establish no excuse absentee mailing and clarifies who may deliver a marked ballot for a voter. This bill would change administrative absentee voting procedures and relevant forms. Currently, Alabama requires an excuse for absentee voting.


This bill would establish early voting to be conducted starting the fourth Monday before the election. This bill would make multiple administrative changes in compliance with conducting early voting. Under existing law, Alabama does not conduct early voting.


This bill would establish the Alabama Voting Rights Act which would establish multiple standards and requirements for elections, including but not limited to:

  • Multiple languages for voting materials;
  • Establishing an Alabama Voting Rights Commission to collect, maintain, and distribute relevant information and statistics on voting in the state and oversee certain policies;
  • Establishing cause(s) of action for aggrieved parties related to voting and election violations;
  • Preclearance requirements regarding certain voting policies and legislation;

Last Updated March 2, 2026

status_actions.summary: From SENATE Committee on GOVERNMENTAL AFFAIRS:  Reported favorably

data.author: Yarbrough (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced March 19, would require a voter to register with a political party in order to vote in the party's primary or primary runoff election. The bill would establish a blackout period 60 days before the primary election, prohibiting any individual that is registered with a political party from changing parties. The bill would permit any unaffiliated person to register with a political party at any time, including the 14 days before the election. A person registered with a party would be able to become unaffiliated at any time. This bill eliminates political parties' ability to govern qualifications of party members. This bill would require conforming changes to the voter registration application and polling place signage. Under existing law, Alabama is an open primary state.

Last Updated March 20, 2026

data.bill_location: House Constitution, Campaigns and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on CONSTITUTION, CAMPAIGNS AND ELECTIONS

data.author: Moore M (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 17, would prohibit the clerk from removing or making inactive in the statewide voter registration database any individual during the 90 days before an election. Under existing law, a county board of registrars is required to change an elector's status to inactive if the elector has not voted in four years. If a county board of registrars determines that an elector has moved to a different address, sends a notice to the elector, and the elector fails to respond to the notice, the board is required to change the elector's status to inactive.

Last Updated April 13, 2026

data.author: Coleman-Madison (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 15, would require the Board of Pardons and Paroles to publicly post on their website information on how a disenfranchised individual may get their voting rights back.


The Board of Pardons and Paroles would be required to post publicly on their website:

  • The names of those who have had their voting rights restored; and
  • The county the individual was last registered or county of last known residence, or the county where the individual resides if provided on the application.

Last Updated April 16, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Stewart (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 10, would establish automatic voting rights restoration for certain disenfranchised individuals. The bill would provide for automatic restoration, regardless of fees or fines, for all disenfranchised individuals except those convicted of treason or impeachment. Under existing law, individuals convicted of certain crimes lose their voting rights and must apply to have their rights restored upon meeting certain criteria once their sentence has ended.

This bill would establish no-excuse absentee. This bill would expand the list of third-parties who may deliver a completed absentee ballot application. This bill would clarify the administrative forms and process for voting by absentee ballot.

This bill would establish a process to cure certain deficiencies on absentee ballots. Under existing law, Alabama does not have a statewide cure process.

Last Updated February 12, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Coleman-Madison (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2026, would establish 5 days of early voting in the state.


The bill would require:

  • Early voting availability on weekdays and weekends,
  • Each county to have an early voting center for every 100,000 residents with the location determined by the county commission,
  • The early voting process to mirror Election Day voting for voters,
  • The county commission to develop voter education to inform eligible electors of the early voting opportunity, and
  • The Secretary of State to adopt rules to implement the process and changes.

The bill would permit the county commission to decrease voting sites if less sites would be sufficient for voters.


This bill would be effective July 1, 2026.


Existing law only permits absentee mail voting with an excuse.

Last Updated May 28, 2026

This bill, as enacted on February 6, 2026:

  • Moves up the primary election date from the first Tuesday in August to the second-to-last Tuesday in July.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to candidate nomination processes that are not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

It would have shortened the cure period for voters to correct minor errors on their returned ballots from five business days to five calendar days. Under existing law, this shortened cure period is already in effect for 2026, though it will return to five business days in 2027.

data.author: Kolodin (R)
data.public_commentary:
This constitutional amendment, as amended on March 18, 2026, would:

  • Require all voters to provide government-issued identification before voting in person or by mail. Under existing law, Arizona requires in-person voters to provide ID before receiving a ballot, and mail voters ("early voters") prove identity through signature verification. This bill would also limit the forms of identification currently allowed in statute by prohibiting the use of non-government-issued identification, such as a bank statement, to verify a voter's identity.
  • Require all voting locations to provide on-site tabulation – including during early in-person voting. Under current law, counties may provide on-site tabulation, but are not required to do so.
  • Provide that only citizens of the United States are eligible to vote for any office or ballot measure in any election in Arizona. This is the same as existing law.
  • Require elections to be decided solely by citizens. Under current law, only citizens are eligible to vote.
  • Stipulate that any laws enacted by the people or the legislature must be reasonably connected to legitimate state interests, including timely and accurate election results, efficient election administration, election security, and public confidence in elections. Current law is silent on this issue.

In order to go into effect, this bill would need to be passed by both chambers and approved by a majority of voters at the next general election.


The bill also contains additional provisions related to campaign contributions that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


A previous version of this bill contained provisions that have been amended out of this version. Those provisions would have:

  • Ended early voting by 7 p.m. on the Friday before Election Day, except for ballots returned through the U.S. Postal Service, which may be received until the close of polls on Election Day. Under existing law, voters may return their mail ("early") ballots in person and by mail through the close of polls on Election Day; voters may also cast early ballots in person through 7 p.m. on the Friday before Election Day, but counties may offer emergency in-person voting on the Saturday, Sunday, and Monday before Election Day.
  • Prohibited votes from being cast or accepted after the polls close on Election Day, except for qualified electors who are standing in line when the polls close. This is the same as existing law.
  • Ended the permanent absentee voting list by allowing mail voting only upon request for voters who have provided documentary proof of citizenship and periodic confirmation of their address before each biennial general election. Under existing law, any voter can request a mail ballot or to be placed on the state's permanent absentee voting list.
  • Provided that all qualified voters have the right to vote in person on Election Day at "conveniently located polling places." Under existing law, counties may use countywide vote centers in lieu of or in addition to precinct-based polling locations.
  • Required all voters to provide government-issued identification "concurrent" with casting a ballot. Under existing law, Arizona requires in-person voters to provide ID before receiving a ballot, but mail voters ("early voters") are not required to provide ID and are instead subject to signature verification.

Last Updated March 20, 2026

data.author: Finchem (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 18, 2026 and engrossed on April 21, 2026, would set several new security requirements for election equipment. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Prohibit internet connections for all electronic voting and tabulation equipment.
  • Require unique credentials for each user be changed at least once per election cycle.
  • Create new chain of custody documentation requirements for election-related equipment.
  • Require that "all activities" at a vote counting center must be included in a "nonstop video" posted to the county website.
  • Create a new class 1 misdemeanor offense for a violation of the new equipment requirements. 

A previous version of this bill would have required all electronic voting and tabulation equipment to meet Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency standards.

data.author: Sundareshan (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 2, 2026, would extend the early voting period, create same-day registration, and create back-end automatic voter registration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Extend the voter registration period to include the 28 days preceding an election and create same-day voter registration. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Allow a qualified individual who has been a resident of the county and precinct for at least 29 days prior to the election to register by appearing at a polling place during early voting or on Election Day, completing a registration form, and providing proof of residence. Under existing law, voter registration applications must be received prior to midnight 29 days before Election Day.
    • Allow an individual who registers to vote during the 28 days preceding Election Day or on Election Day to vote a provisional ballot.
    • Prohibit an individual who registers to vote during this timeframe from voting in a partisan primary election until at least 29 days after that Election Day.
  • Create back-end automatic voter registration for voters applying for a driver's license or renewal, an ID card or renewal, or updating their ID information. The voter would be automatically registered to vote upon completing an application, renewal, or submitting changes at the Motor Vehicle Division or via mail. Under existing law, Arizona does not have automatic voter registration.
  • Require the county recorder, upon receipt of the registration information from the Department of Transportation, to send notice to individuals automatically registered informing them of their registration. The individual would be registered to vote unless they declined within 21 calendar days of issuance of the notice.
  • Provide that an individual who is not qualified to register to vote and who unknowingly registers under this bill is not guilty of false registration or false swearing. 

Last Updated February 9, 2026

data.author: Bolick (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on January 21, 2026, proposes statutory changes that prohibit use of foreign funds for election administration, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:


  • Prohibit the use of foreign money, goods, or services for election administration, create record keeping requirements, and create a civil enforcement action for violations. Existing law prohibits the use of private monies, but is silent as to foreign sources. The bill would:
    • Prohibit any government entity in the state from using money or in-kind goods or services donated by a foreign government or non-governmental source for election administration.
    • Require that all entities that provide services to the government for election administration certify to the secretary of state on an annual basis that they have not accepted such money or services, and require all government entities to provide a quarterly report listing all parties that provide election services.
    • Create a class 1 misdemeanor for knowingly failing to provide accurate or updated certification to the secretary of state.
    • Create a civil right of action for any voter to enjoin or enforce the new provisions, including specified damages for violations. 

This bill also includes provisions relating to foreign contributions to ballot measure campaigns that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Final approval of changes proposed by this resolution requires passage by the legislature and approval by a majority of voters at the November 2026 general election.

Last Updated March 2, 2026

data.bill_location: House Federalism, Military Affairs & Elections Committee

data.author: Bolick (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on January 21, 2026, proposes statutory changes that would extend the general early voting period; move the deadline earlier for certain methods of mail ballot return; and prohibit use of foreign funds for election administration, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:


  • Extend the early in-person voting period by moving the end date later, to the Monday before Election Day. Under existing law, early in-person voting ends on the Friday before an election, with limited emergency voting available through the following Monday. As a conforming change, the resolution would also eliminate this emergency voting period. 
  • Move the deadline earlier for return of a mail ballot to a drop box or a voting location, and require voter ID for return during certain times.
    • For return of a mail ballot at a drop box or voting location, the bill would move the deadline from 7pm on Election Day to 7pm on the Friday before the election. 
    • After 7pm on the Friday before an election, in-person ballot return to a voting location would require voters to present an accepted form of voter ID. Existing law permits these voters to present ID in lieu of signature verification but does not require voters to do so. 
  • Expressly permit on-site vote tabulation of mail ballots at early voting locations. Existing law provides for on-site tabulation at Election Day voting locations only. 
  • Require election officials to ensure that chain of custody procedures for early ballots are the same as those for in-person Election Day voting.
  • Eliminate the existing requirement that county recorders count the number of early ballots that are returned at voting locations on Election Day and post those totals with the last unofficial results released on election night.
  • Repeal the requirement that the county recorder "take necessary steps" to contact a voter if their pre-election notice letter is returned as undeliverable, prior to placing the voter in inactive status and removing them from the permanent mail voting list.
  • Prohibit the use of foreign money, goods, or services for election administration, create record keeping requirements, and create a civil enforcement action for violations. Existing law prohibits the use of private monies, but is silent as to foreign sources. The bill would:
    • Prohibit any government entity in the state from using money or in-kind goods or services donated by a foreign government or non-governmental source for election administration.
    • Require that all entities that provide services to the government for election administration certify to the secretary of state on an annual basis that they have not accepted such money or services, and require all government entities to provide a quarterly report listing all parties that provide election services.
    • Create a class 1 misdemeanor for knowingly failing to provide accurate or updated certification to the secretary of state.
    • Create a civil right of action for any voter to enjoin or enforce the new provisions, including specified damages for violations. 
  • Eliminate the use of emergency voting centers. 
  • Repeal a provision granting a public school principal authority to deny a request for their school to serve as a voting location based on space not being available or the safety or welfare of the children being at risk.

This bill also includes provisions relating to foreign contributions to ballot measure campaigns that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Final approval of changes proposed by this resolution requires passage by the legislature and approval by a majority of voters at the November 2026 general election.

This bill, as amended on July 16, 2025, would change language in the Voter Bill of Rights by specifying that voters have the right to receive a replacement ballot if prior to submitting their ballot and before polls close, they make a mistake, do not have their ballot, or their ballot has been damaged. Existing law allows a voter to request a new ballot if they believe they made a mistake or they have lost, destroyed, not received, or cannot surrender their mail ballot. The bill would repeal related language regarding spoiled ballots and the procedure for getting a replacement.

data.author: Patterson J (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 22, 2025, does not contain any provisions tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

A previous version of the bill contained provisions that were amended out of this version. Those provisions would have:

  • Required election officials to count at least 25 percent of ballots by the seventh calendar day following the election, at least 67 percent of ballots by the 14th calendar day following the election, and all ballots by the 21st calendar day following the election. The official canvass would be required to be completed by the 21st calendar day following the election. Under existing law, there is no deadline by which the ballots must be counted as long as election officials can prepare a statement of the results within 30 days of the election.
  • Moved up the deadline by which the elections official must send a complete copy of election results to the secretary of state from within 31 days of the election to within 22 days. The deadline for officials to send the results for the presidential primary for delegates to national conventions would have been moved up from 28 days after the election to 22 days after the election.
