data.author: Avila Farias (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 11, 2026, no longer includes provisions tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to restrictions on law enforcement presence at childcare facilities.


A prior version of the bill included a provisions that were amended out of the current text. The provisions would have:

  • Expanded the existing criminal prohibition against possession of a firearm in the immediate vicinity of a polling place without authorization. Existing law makes this conduct a misdemeanor or felony offense for individuals, peace officers, private guards, security personnels, or any person wearing the uniform of a peace officer, guards, or security personnels. The bill would have applied that criminal prohibition to officers or federal law enforcement agents, or anyone acting on behalf of a federal law enforcement agency.
  • Defined “immediate vicinity” to include a polling place building and areas within 100 feet of entrances, exits, and parking facilities.
  • Established exceptions for unarmed security officers and peace offices who are conducting official business in the course of their public employment or those casting a vote.
  • Increased penalties and expanded the scope of prohibited conduct near polling places by making it a felony, punishable by a fine of up to $10,000 and imprisonment for up to five years, for a person to be stationed at or near a polling place while possessing a firearm, wearing a law enforcement or security uniform, or displaying clothing or insignia suggesting affiliation with law enforcement without authorization. Existing law makes such conduct a misdemeanor or felony but does not apply as broadly to individuals displaying law enforcement affiliation.
  • Prohibited local election officials from authorizing immigration or federal law enforcement to be stationed within the immediate vicinity of a polling place and clarify that any such authorization is invalid. Existing law allows election officials to authorize certain security presence at polling locations.

Last Updated June 12, 2026

data.author: Sharp-Collins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 19, 2026, would:

  • Make address confidentiality optional rather than automatic. Under the bill, a candidate or elected official would submit a written request to have their residence, address, telephone number, and email address kept confidential.
  • Extend confidentiality protections to immediate family members (spouse, domestic partner, parent, or child living in the same residence) of elected officials.
  • Establish detailed procedures for requesting, maintaining and removing confidential status.
  • Specify timelines for the duration of the confidentiality for elected officials and candidates. Specifically, the bill wold:
    • Require that an elected official's address confidentiality remain until two years after they leave office.
    • Require that a candidate's address confidentiality remain in effect through the canvass of votes. For candidates who are elected, their status will remain until they opt out or until two years after leaving office.
  • Require the secretary of state and county elections officials to implement the provisions of this bill immediately once the statewide voter registration database and county election management system s have made conforming changes.

This resolution, as introduced on April 7, 2026, would declare the legislature's opposition to federal actions restricting vote by mail. Specifically, the resolution would:

Existing law requires every registered voter to receive a mail ballot for each election.
  • Affirm the safety and accessibility of voting by mail.
  • Call on Congress to reject federal legislation that would restrict or eliminate vote-by-mail options available in California and other states and to protect states’ constitutional authority to administer their own elections.

This bill, as enacted on May 27, 2026:

The bill specifies that this prohibition does not apply to any agreement authorizing agents to provide logistical, transportation, or security to support during the voting period, on Election Day, or during the canvass of the vote.

  • Prohibits a mail voter observer from challenging a signature on statements on the basis that they believe the signature does not compare with the signature appearing in the voter's registration record.

data.author: Reyes (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on July 17, 2025, known as the "High School Voter Registration Act" would set forth requirements for civic education in high schools. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Specify that the administrator of a public or private high school appoint students to be outreach coordinators. Under existing law, only administrators of "a high school" can do this.
  • Require, beginning with the 2026-2027 school year, the governing boards of school districts, county boards of education, state special schools, and the governing bodies of charter school that serve pupils in grade 11 to:
    • Ensure that all students receive information on how to properly register to vote at least once before the student completes grade 11. That information must include, but is not limited to, voting eligibility and guidance published by the secretary of state, services provided and materials published by the county elections office, the opportunity to register to vote, the student poll worker program, how to vote, and other voter education.
    • Upon request from from a student or their parent or guardian, ensure that information regarding where to acquire a voter registration card is provided to the student
    • Ensure that any information shared with parents, guardians, and students is handled according to state and federal privacy laws and regulations
  • Allow the governing board of a school district, a county board of education, a state special school, and the governing body of a charter school to contract with a third-party nonprofit organization with demonstrated experience providing nonpartisan youth civic engagement to implement requirements of this section.
  • Encourage the governing board of a school district, a county board of education, a state special school, and the governing body of a charter school to consider satisfying the requirements of this bill during the high school voter education weeks established under state law.

Last Updated July 23, 2025

data.author: Umberg (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 14, 2026, would:

  • Increase ballot drop-off location requirements. Counties conducting universal mail ballot elections would be required to provide at least two drop-off locations or one for every 11,250 registered voters, whichever is greater, while other counties would be required to provide at least two drop-off locations or one for every 15,000 registered voters. Existing law requires one drop-off location for every 15,000 voters in all-mail counties and one for every 30,000 voters in other counties. 
  • Require ballot drop-off locations to open at least 30 days before Election Day. Existing law requires drop-off locations to open at least 28 days before the election. 
  • Consider universal mail ballots timely if received by election officials up to 10 days after Election Day, provided the ballot was postmarked or otherwise indicated as mailed on or before Election Day.
  • Prohibit federal, state, or local law enforcement officers from making arrests within 200 feet of a polling place on election day, except for a crime related to disrupting the operation of the polling place, a crime against a person, or a crime against property.
  • Expand the misdemeanor prohibition on electioneering by giving county boards of supervisors authority to expand the prohibited area of electioneering to up to 200 feet. Under the bill, if the board expands the prohibited area, the approved distance must be uniform at all designated locations in the county.
  • Specify that provisions of the bill are severable and if any part of the chapter or application is invalid, that would not affect provisions or applications that can be given effect without the invalid portion.

A previous version of the bill included provision that were amended out the current text. The provisions would have:

  • Required counties conducting universal mail ballot ("all-mail ballot") elections to provide at least one vote center for every 50,000 registered voters beginning 20 days before the election and continuing daily through the fourth day before the election. Existing law requires vote centers beginning 10 days before the election. 

Note: The bill’s provisions would apply to regular or special elections beginning with the November 3, 2026 through 2029. If enacted, the bill would take effect immediately.

Last Updated May 26, 2026

data.bill_name: County Mail Ballot Signature Verification Requirements
data.bill_location: House State, Civic, Military and Veterans Affairs Committee

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE.  To second reading

data.author: Richardson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 2, 2026, would:

  • Require a team of bipartisan election judges to verify the signature on a mail ballot return envelope by comparing it to the voter’s signature on file in the statewide voter registration system. Existing law requires a single election judge to perform signature verification unless a signature verification device is used.
  • Permit one election judge of any political party to review a ballot if the bipartisan team determines the signatures do not match or if a signature verification device cannot verify the match. Under existing law, two judges of different political affiliations review mismatched signatures.
  • Clarify that when election judges disagree on whether a signature matches, the signature is deemed to match and the ballot is counted. Existing law includes a similar provision for disagreements among individual judges.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the Code accounting for the option allowing bipartisan teams of judges.

Last Updated February 5, 2026

status_actions.summary: Session Law Chaptered.  Chapter No. 267

data.author: Sirota (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on June 1, 2026:

  • Allows voters to return mail ballots to drop boxes beginning 22 days, instead of 15 days, before Election Day.
  • Lowers the enrolled-student threshold from 2,000 to 1,000 students for purposes of requiring a drop box on campus.
  • Expands acceptable forms of voter identification by adding a Division of Youth Corrections ID card and written confirmation from a county sheriff indicating that an voter is confined in a jail or detention facility.
  • Specifies that "identification" includes a "passport card" in addition to a valid U.S. passport.
  • Prohibits individuals from serving as election watchers if they have been convicted of an election offense or certain serious crimes, including offenses related to insurrection, treason, or attempts to overthrow the government by force. Under prior law, “watchers” are defined as eligible electors selected by candidates, political parties, or ballot issue proponents, but there were no specified disqualifications based on prior criminal convictions.
  • Requires institutions of higher education to provide students with information about voter eligibility, registration, voting locations and ballot drop off locations by email on the 15th day before an election and again on the final day before the election. Prior law did not include specific requirements for these institutions.
  • Lowers the minimum age threshold for a student election judge from 16 to 15 years old.
  • Requires each county clerk and recorder to establish and publicly post operational hours for the clerk’s office. Prior law did not expressly require clerks to set or publish standardized office hours.
  • Modifies procedures for correcting voter registration affiliation errors by requiring the county clerk, rather than an election judge, to provide an affidavit allowing a voter to correct an incorrect party affiliation recorded in the statewide voter registration system. Prior law authorized an election judge to provide such an affidavit.
  • Requires counties to measure and record wait times at each voter service and polling center on Election Day in accordance with rules adopted by the secretary of state and report that data to the secretary within 30 days after the election. Prior law did not require counties to measure or report polling place wait times.
  • Establishes procedures for counting paper ballots when a hand count is required, including requiring ballots to be counted by a team of four election judges, with one judge reading each vote aloud, another observing and assisting in determining voter intent, and two judges independently recording the results on separate tally sheets that must be periodically compared to resolve discrepancies. Prior law permits hand counts but does not prescribe this detailed counting process.
  • Expands the existing misdemeanor offense of "interference with voters while voting" by prohibiting voter interference within 100 feet of any polling location, drop-off location, or drop box voting area. Prior law prohibited interference with voters who are inside the immediate voting area, marking their ballot, or using a voting device.
  • Prohibits election officials or any person with custody of election records from transferring, relinquishing, or providing access to those records to any third party except as authorized by law, including through formal public records requests or court order.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates, filling vacancies, and political party procedures.

A previous version of the bill contained a provision that was amended out of the current text. The provision would have:

  • Clarified that the terms “term of imprisonment” and “full term of imprisonment” do not include the period during which an individual is on parole or under the jurisdiction of the Department of Corrections in a transitional status classification, including individuals in residential or nonresidential transitional placements. Under existing law, residents lose their voting rights only when convicted of a felony and confined to a correctional institute, facility, or community residence.

Last Updated June 8, 2026

data.bill_name: Accessible Language Statutory Ballot Title Requirements

status_actions.summary: Session Law Chaptered.  Chapter No. 235

data.author: Nguyen (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 29, 2026:

  • Requires the title board to use accessible language when drafting ballot titles. The bill would define "accessible language" as plain language that is understood by the widest possible audience. Existing law requiresCO ballot titles to be brief and in question form, but does not expressly require the use of accessible or plain language.
  • Establishes a criteria for the title board to use when determining whether a ballot title is written using accessible language, including:
    • Avoiding legal, technical, or specialized terminology when possible
    • Clearly identifying the principal change in law or policy proposed by the law or constitutional amendment
    • Organizing clauses so that the effect of a "yes/for"or"no/against" vote is readily understood
    • Presenting necessary information within the ballot title in a logical and readable order

Last Updated June 8, 2026

status_actions.summary: From SENATE Committee on STATE, VETERANS, & MILITARY AFFAIRS: Postponed indefinitely

data.author: Zamora Wilson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2026, would:

  • Require county clerks and recorders to mark an active voter’s registration as inactive if the voter fails to vote in at least one election during a time period that includes two consecutive general elections held in even-numbered years and to send a confirmation card allowing the voter to verify or restore active status. Under existing law, if an active voter's mail ballot is returned to the county clerk and recorder, the county clerk and recorder must mark their registration record “Inactive” and send to the elector a confirmation card by which the voter may verify or correct the address information.
  • Replace current practice of automatically registering or preregistering individuals as unaffiliated voters through automatic voter registration (AVR) with a "pending" registration or preregistration status when a person applies for a driver’s license or ID card and does not affirmatively select a party affiliation or complete a self-affirmation. Under the bill, only individuals who affirmatively choose to be unaffiliated would be registered or preregistered as such. Existing law defaults AVR registrants to unaffiliated status without requiring affirmative confirmation.
  • Establish procedures for finalizing pending voter registration or preregistration records, including requiring county clerks and recorders to send notice by nonforwardable mail and allowing individuals to decline registration, select a party affiliation or identify as unaffiliated, and complete a required self-affirmation. Pending records would be canceled or marked inactive if notices are returned as undeliverable or if the individual fails to respond or provides incomplete information. Existing law provides for AVR processing but does not create a pending status with these notice and inactivity procedures.
  • Specify that a county clerk and recorder may, in their discretion, provide notice of an "inactive" pending registration or preregistration record and further attempt to finalize the pending registration or preregistration.
  • Specify that a pending voter registration or preregistration marked inactive may become active if the individual subsequently votes in an election, and authorize county clerks and recorders to provide additional notice and outreach to attempt to finalize inactive pending registrations. Existing law does not include a pending registration category that can be activated through subsequent voting.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the code regarding pending voter registrations.

Last Updated March 23, 2026

data.author: Zamora Wilson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 11, 2026, would:

  • Create a process for voters to opt out of receiving mail ballot packets. Under the bill, the secretary of state would be required to establish an opt out process beginning with elections occurring on or after November 2, 2027. Voters who opt out would be required to vote in person or request a mail ballot. Existing law requires officials to automatically send a mail ballot to all active registered voters.
  • Specify that the opt out process must be available online, in person, or by a paper form that can e sent by mail or electronically.
  • Specify that voters who have opted not to receive a mail ballot but later wish to get a mail ballot must make the request to the county clerk and reporter for a mail ballot no later than the eighth day before an election.
  • Require county clerks to report the number of voters who opted out and the number of ballots not sent, and require the secretary of state to calculate cost savings and publish this information publicly.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE, VETERANS, & MILITARY AFFAIRS

data.author: Mullica (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 20, 2026, would authorize the use of electronic ballot return in certain local elections. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow municipalities and special districts to conduct elections using electronic ballot return. Existing law does not authorize electronic ballot return for most voters in local elections and generally requires ballots to be returned by mail or in person.
  • Establish requirements for electronic ballot return systems. To qualify for the use of electronic ballot return in an election, a transmission system must meet specified standards, including:
    • Providing secure identification and authentication
    • Transmitting encrypted information over a secure network
    • Protecting the privacy, anonymity, and integrity of each ballot
    • Protecting against abuse
    • Meeting any additional security, auditability, or accessibility requirements established by the secretary of state
  • Require election officials to ensure that all electronically returned ballots are anonymously transcribed to paper ballots and then counted, secured, and stored in the same manner as other ballots. Existing law does not address procedures for handling electronically returned ballots.
  • Establish confidentiality protections, ensuring that electronic data generated by a voter using the transmission system is confidential and can only be used for voter participation statistics.
  • Require election notices to include instructions for accessing the electronic ballot return system when it is used and require the secretary of state to publish guidance and adopt rules governing system standards and implementation.

The bill would take effect following the standard period following adjournment of the general assembly, unless referred to voters by a referendum petition. In that case, the bill or provisions would not take effect until approved by the voters at the November 2026 general election.

Last Updated March 23, 2026

status_actions.summary: Public Act No. 26-42

data.author: Joint Committee on Government Administration and Elections
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 23, 2026, would establish no-excuse mail ballot voting; create a permanent mail ballot application list; establish a cure process for missing signatures on mail ballot envelopes; establish risk-limiting audits; and create new election interference offenses, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Expand access to mail ("absentee") voting and modify mail voting procedures:
    • Establish no-excuse mail ballot voting. Existing law requires voters to provide a qualifying excuse, such as physical disability, sickness, or out-of-town absence, to vote by mail.
    • Establish a permanent mail voting application list available to all voters. Under the bill, any voter could request permanent mail status to automatically be issued a mail ballot application prior to all future elections. Under existing law, permanent mail voting status is restricted to voters with a permanent physical disability or long-term illness certified by a medical provider. The bill would require the removal of a voter from the permanent application list if their ballot application is returned as undeliverable, they submit a National Change of Address form indicating a move out of state, registrars receive official data they moved or registered in another state, or they are placed on the inactive registry list. A previous version of the bill would have created a true permanent mail voter list, where opted-in voters would have been mailed a ballot for all future elections without needing to submit an application for each election.
    • Establish a cure process for missing signatures on mail ballot return envelopes. The bill would require the municipal clerk to use "best efforts" (including phone and email) to contact the voter within 24 hours. The voter would be permitted to cure the ballot by appearing in person at the clerk's office until the close of polls on Election Day. Under existing law, unsigned ballots are simply marked "rejected" with no explicit cure process.
    • Eliminate the "inner envelope" for mail ballots and simplify instructions. The bill would require voters to place their marked ballot directly into a single return envelope accompanied by a privacy sleeve. Municipal clerks would also be required to include a new simplified instruction sheet. Prior law required a separate inner envelope and lacked the simplified instruction mandate.
    • Establish procedures for municipal clerks to mail applications directly to voters with their name and barcode pre-printed, centralizing the process. Existing law has no comparable provisions.
    • Require the secretary of the state to develop and install integrated statewide ballot-tracking software. The software would allow voters to track the status of their application and ballot. Existing law has no comparable statutory requirement.
    • Require registration for mail ballot application distributors. Individuals who distribute five or more mail ballot applications would be required to register with the municipal clerk, subject to certain exemptions. Prior law did not require distributors to register.
    • Establish specific application forms for incarcerated voters. The bill would mandate the creation of a specifically designated, consecutively numbered mail ballot application form exclusively for voters incarcerated in a Department of Correction facility, containing a dedicated space for the applicant's facility mailing address. Prior law had no comparable requirement.
    • Require disclosures on unsolicited mail ballot applications. Candidates, parties, or committees mailing unsolicited applications would be required to include a disclaimer stating who paid for and approved the mailing. Prior law required only a warning about eligibility requirements and penalties.
    • Mandate video surveillance of drop boxes. Municipalities would be required to provide a video recording device for each secure drop box by July 1, 2025, capturing the location, date, and time. Existing law does not expressly mandate video surveillance for drop boxes.
    • Require the State Elections Enforcement Commission to prepare a summary of mail voting laws for public dissemination. Existing law has no comparable requirement.
  • Modify audits and other post-election procedures:
    • Replace the standard manual audit for state elections with risk-limiting audits (RLAs). Beginning in 2027, RLAs featuring a maximum 5% risk limit would be used. The bill would require the secretary of the state to order a full manual recount of all ballots for an office if an RLA produces an "INCONCLUSIVE" outcome. Existing law relies on manual or electronic audits of a random 5% of voting districts.
    • Create an RLA pilot program for the 2026 state election. The secretary of the state would be required to randomly select three municipalities of varying population sizes to participate. Existing law has no comparable provision.
    • Require the results of municipal manual or electronic audits to be sent to The University of Connecticut for analysis. The university would submit a written report identifying any discrepancies to the secretary of the state and State Elections Enforcement Commission. Existing law has no comparable requirement.
    • Authorize the use of high-speed tabulators for recanvasses. A recanvass would be permitted to be conducted using a different voting tabulator or a high-speed tabulator borrowed from the state. Existing law requires the recanvass to be performed using the original tabulator from the election.
  • Establish protections against election interference:
    • Authorize the attorney general to seek equitable relief for federal election interference. The attorney general would be authorized to seek preliminary or permanent injunctive, declaratory, or equitable relief from the state Supreme Court to prevent or redress interference. Existing law has no comparable provision granting this specific equitable relief channel to the attorney general.
    • Require the reporting of election subpoenas. Municipal officials or election workers who receive a subpoena or warrant for election records or equipment would be required to provide a copy to the attorney general and secretary of the state within 36 hours. Existing law has no comparable requirement.
    • Prohibit election offenders from serving in election administration. Individuals convicted of violating Connecticut election laws would be explicitly barred from serving in positions of trust within the election administration process, such as serving as election officials. The existing law has no comparable prohibition.
    • Modify the 250-foot polling place buffer zone. The bill would explicitly exempt off-duty officers (allowing them to vote and engage in protected political expression), individuals residing within 250 feet of the polling place, and individuals merely passing through the zone to reach a different destination. Prior law did not specify these exemptions.
  • Permit eligible 17-year-olds to vote early and by mail in primaries. Citizens who are 17 years old, but will be 18 by the day of the general election, would be explicitly authorized to cast a ballot in a primary election during the early voting period or by mail ballot. Under existing law, these 17-year-olds may vote in the primary, but the statute is silent regarding their use of early and mail voting.

Some provisions from previous versions of the bill were amended out of the current text. These provisions would have:

  • Authorized the secretary of the state to establish a secure electronic ballot transmission system for the return of mail ballots by military/overseas voters, voters with a disability, federal-only voters, and all voters with permanent mail voting status. This provision effectively would have authorized electronic ballot return for any voter in the state.
  • Required election officials to conduct nationwide criminal background checks on petition circulators and individuals distributing mail ballot applications.
  • Required the secretary of the state to conduct a statewide public awareness mailing campaign regarding the new voting laws.
  • Broadened the authority of the State Elections Enforcement Commission by allowing it to investigate and hold hearings regarding alleged violations of regulations related to elections, primaries, and referendums (existing law limits this authority to violations of statutes).
  • Required the secretary of the state to review current procedures regarding in-person, off-site voter registration applications to assess accessibility for persons with disabilities, and submit a report with legislative recommendations.
  • Modified the reasons and process for removing municipal clerks from office, allowing the secretary of the state or the State Elections Enforcement Commission to refer misconduct or incompetence directly to the attorney general.

Last Updated April 27, 2026

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 8, 2026, would require presidential electors to cast their electoral college ballots for the candidates to whom they are pledged. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require presidential electors to execute a pledge to vote for their party's nominees, and direct the secretary of the state to reject any electoral college ballots marked inconsistently with that pledge. Under existing law, there is no mechanism for the secretary to reject a non-compliant ballot.
  • Establish that any elector who fails to vote according to their pledge automatically forfeits their office and is permanently ineligible to serve as a presidential elector in the future. The remaining electors would then choose an eligible replacement to fill the vacancy.
  • Clarify that the state's presidential electors are appointed based on the state's popular vote unless the National Popular Vote Interstate Compact (Agreement Among the States to Elect the President by National Popular Vote) takes effect.

status_actions.summary: Reported out of Legislative Commissioner's Office

data.author: Joint Committee on Government Administration and Elections
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 9, 2026, would prohibit firearms and unauthorized law enforcement near election sites; establish new felony offenses for tampering with drop boxes or sharing voting equipment with unauthorized parties; and increase penalties for harassing election workers, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit firearms and deadly weapons near elections sites, and create a new felony offense for possessing them within 250 feet of such locations. Elections sites include polling places, early voting and same-day registration locations, absentee drop boxes, and central counting locations. The bill provides limited exceptions for peace officers on duty, firearms secured in locked containers in vehicles, and individuals briefly passing through the area. Existing law has no comparable prohibition.
  • Prohibit unauthorized law enforcement presence near elections sites, and create a new felony offense for violations. The bill would prohibit law enforcement officers or federal agents from coming within 250 feet of an elections site, including for the purpose of attempting to examine voter qualifications or loitering. It provides narrow exceptions for exigent threats, repelling armed enemies, acting under a judicial warrant with 24 hours’ notice to the secretary of the state and attorney general, responding to a request from an election moderator to suppress disorder, or briefly passing through the 250-foot radius while en route to another location. Existing law is silent as to the presence of law enforcement at elections sites.
  • Prohibit face-obscuring masks and unauthorized identification checks within 250 feet of an elections site. The bill would prohibit anyone from wearing a mask that obscures their identity, unless medically or religiously necessary, or reasonable given weather conditions provided the individual complies with a moderator's request to remove it. It would also prohibit anyone from requiring another person to present identification within the 250-foot radius. The bill expressly exempts election officials performing their lawful duties from the identification restriction. Existing law has no comparable provisions.
  • Create a new felony offense for tampering with absentee ballot drop boxes. The bill would make it a class D felony to unlawfully tamper with, alter, destroy, or carry away a drop box, or to fraudulently deposit ballots into a drop box. Existing law penalizes fraudulent voting and ballot tampering generally, but does not have a specific provision addressing drop boxes.
  • Create a new felony offense for unauthorized sharing of voting equipment. The bill would make it a class D felony for any election official to provide a voting tabulator, or its parts, to any third party not authorized by the secretary of the state. While existing law penalizes election officials who tamper with tabulators to defraud or deceive, it does not expressly prohibit providing them to unauthorized third parties.
  • Increase the penalty for harassing election workers. The bill would elevate the penalty for publicly disclosing an election worker's personal identifying information with the intent to harass, terrorize, or improperly influence them. Under the bill, a subsequent offense would become a class C felony. Under existing law, the offense is a class A misdemeanor. The bill would also expand the definition of "election worker" to explicitly include assistant municipal clerks.
  • Expand the attorney general's authority to protect elections and voting rights. The bill would:
    • Establish a mandatory reporting process for election-related subpoenas. The bill would require municipal officials and election workers to provide a copy of any subpoena, warrant, or request for election records, tabulators, or ballot boxes to the attorney general and secretary of the state within 36 hours of receipt. Existing law has no comparable requirement.
    • Authorize the attorney general to seek expedited injunctive or declaratory relief directly from the state Supreme Court to prevent or redress interference in federal elections. Existing law has no comparable provision.
    • Expand the attorney general's existing authority to bring civil actions against individuals who interfere with constitutional rights, to include interference by "physical obstruction" and the establishment of a "policy" that causes the deprivation of rights.

Last Updated May 20, 2026

status_actions.summary: *****To GOVERNOR

data.author: Looney (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 3, 2026, modifies early voting and same-day registration procedures, limits the public disclosure of voter data, and expands municipal recanvass requirements, among other changes. Specifically, the bill:

  • Makes changes to early voting:
    • Changes early voting tabulation procedures. The bill eliminates the requirement that voters place their marked early voting ballot into an early voting envelope. Instead, voters must complete a printed affirmation in a log book and deposit their ballot directly into a voting tabulator. Prior law required voters to place their early voting ballot in an envelope, sign an affirmation on the outside, and deposit it in a secure receptacle.
    • Establishes an annual certification process for early voting locations. The bill requires registrars of voters to certify these locations to the Secretary of the State annually by February 15. Prior law required certification 60 days before an election.
    • Provides a backup process for early voting location designation. The bill authorizes the municipal legislative body or board of selectmen to designate the early voting location if the local registrars of voters cannot agree. Prior law granted designation authority exclusively to the registrars, but was silent on resolving disagreements.
    • Increases reporting requirements for moderators. The bill requires registrars to submit the names and mobile phone numbers of early voting moderators to the Secretary of the State 14 days before early voting begins. Prior law had no comparable requirement.
    • Prohibits partisan activity by early voting officials. The bill prohibits early voting officials from performing services for any political party or candidate on the days they serve. Prior law had no comparable provision.
  • Changes voter registration and same-day registration:
    • Moves the deadline for an unaffiliated voter to enroll with a party prior to a primary by mail or other non-in-person methods earlier, from five days before a primary to 18 days before. The deadline for an unaffiliated voter to enroll with a party in person remains noon on the last business day before the primary election.
    • Shifts same-day registration location certification to an annual February 15 deadline, from 45 days before each election.
    • Applies the new reporting requirements for moderator contact information and the prohibition on partisan activity to same-day registration officials. Prior law lacked these requirements.
  • Limits disclosure and use of voters' personal information and ballots:
    • Exempts all cast ballots, including write-in ballots, from public disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act. Prior law had no comparable exemption for cast ballots.
    • Limits the public disclosure of voter registration information to only the year of birth, unless requested for a state governmental purpose. Prior law permitted the disclosure of both the month and the year of birth.
    • Prohibits commercial and personal use of voter data. The bill expressly prohibits the use of voter registration information for personal, private, or commercial purposes, including harassment or marketing, subject to civil penalties. Prior law had no comparable provision explicitly banning these uses for the general voter list.
  • Expands post-election recanvass requirements. The bill requires that when a discrepancy triggers a recanvass, officials must recanvass the returns of every voting tabulator and all absentee and write-in ballots used in the entire municipality. Prior law required a recanvass only for the specific voting district where the discrepancy was found
  • Requires absentee ballot counters to maintain a log of each rejected absentee ballot and the specific reason for its rejection, which must be transmitted to the secretary of the state. Prior law required counters to endorse the reason for rejection on the ballot envelope, but did not require a separate log.
  • Prohibits registrars of voters from appointing as an election moderator any person convicted of a felony involving fraud, forgery, larceny, embezzlement, or bribery, or any election-related criminal offense. Prior law had no comparable restriction.
  • Authorizes the secretary of the state to commence a declaratory judgment action in Superior Court during the 90 days preceding an election if an elector alleges their voting rights are aggrieved. Prior law did not grant the Secretary this specific declaratory judgment authority.

The bill also contains additional non-substantive changes and provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track, such as public education, intermediate care facilities, and municipal pensions.

Last Updated April 21, 2026

data.bill_name: Security of Election Administration Processes Elections

status_actions.summary: Reported out of Legislative Commissioner's Office

data.author: Joint Committee on Government Administration and Elections
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 9, 2026, would establish new security requirements for election sites, restrict access to voting equipment, and expand the authority of the attorney general to intervene in election interference. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish new criminal offenses and elevate penalties for election-related misconduct. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Create a Class D felony for a first offense and a Class B felony for subsequent offenses for the possession of a firearm or deadly weapon within 250 feet of any elections site, including polling places, early voting locations, same-day registration locations, absentee drop boxes, and counting or recanvass locations. Exceptions would be made for unloaded and locked weapons in vehicles, peace officers on duty, and individuals briefly passing through the radius. Existing law has no comparable blanket prohibition for all election sites.
    • Create a Class C felony, punishable by disenfranchisement, for officers or agents authorized to use force or make arrests by the state or federal government who knowingly enter, loiter, or attempt to examine voter qualifications within 250 feet of an elections site. This prohibition would not apply to officers acting under a judicial warrant with 24 hours' prior notice to the state, operating under exigent circumstances to protect life or property, or responding to a request by the secretary of the state or moderator to suppress disorder. Existing law has no comparable provision.
    • Create a Class C felony, punishable by disenfranchisement, for wearing a mask or face covering that obscures a person's identity within 250 feet of an elections site, unless the covering is medically necessary, of religious significance, or is reasonable given the weather conditions (provided the individual complies with a moderator's request to remove it).
    • Create a Class C felony, punishable by disenfranchisement, for requiring any person to present any form of identification within 250 feet of an elections site, except to the extent required by election officials performing their lawful duties. Existing law has no comparable provision.
    • Create a new Class D felony for knowingly tampering with, altering, destroying, or carrying away a ballot drop box, or fraudulently depositing or introducing ballots into or from a drop box. Existing law criminalizes destroying ballots, but does not have a specific felony targeting drop box tampering.
    • Create a new Class D felony for any election official who provides a voting tabulator, or any part or appliance thereof, to an unauthorized third party. Existing law criminalizes tabulator tampering, but does not explicitly criminalize handing over equipment to unauthorized parties.
    • Expand an existing Class A misdemeanor prohibiting the public disclosure of an election worker’s personal identifying information with the intent to harass or improperly influence them, making any subsequent offense a Class C felony. The bill would also expand the definition of “election worker” to include assistant municipal clerks.
  • Authorize the attorney general to seek injunctive, declaratory, or equitable relief to prevent or redress interference in federal elections. The bill would establish an expedited judicial process wherein a judge may issue ex parte orders to preserve the status quo, including ordering election workers to retain custody of voting tabulators and records. Existing law has no comparable provision.
  • Expand the attorney general’s existing authority to bring civil actions against those who interfere with civil rights by threats, intimidation, or coercion to also include interference by "physical obstruction" and to apply to the establishment of a "policy" that deprives individuals of their rights.
  • Require municipal officials or election workers who receive a subpoena, warrant, or request to inspect election records or equipment to provide a copy to the attorney general and the secretary of the state within 36 hours. Existing law has no comparable provision.

Last Updated May 20, 2026

status_actions.summary: Reissued by Legislative Commissioner's Office with File No. 573

data.author: Joint Committee on Government Administration and Elections
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 9, 2026, would expand and clarify public observation rights during election recanvasses. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require that all recanvassing procedures afford members of the public the right to observe in person, without the need to observe through electronic means. Under existing law, recanvass procedures must be open to public observation, but the law does not explicitly guarantee in-person observation without the use of electronic alternatives.
  • Prohibit election moderators from impeding the ability of the public to observe recanvassing procedures, and establish a fine of not less than $100 for any moderator who violates this prohibition or the right to in-person observation. Existing law has no comparable penalty.
  • Clarify that the expanded right to observe does not allow members of the public to interfere with the conduct of the recanvass. This maintains the prohibition on public interference present under existing law.

Last Updated May 20, 2026

data.author: Bush (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 4, 2026, proposes a constitutional amendment that would permit the establishment of same-day voter registration by repealing various provisions in the state constitution that impose restrictions on the time period in which voters can register to vote before an election. This amendment is a response to the Delaware Supreme Court's decision in Albence v. Higgin, which found that same-day registration is not permissible under the Delaware constitution. The amendment would have to be approved by two-thirds of the members of each chamber of the legislature in consecutive sessions. If that were to occur, statutes permitting same-day registration would be able to take effect.

Last Updated June 11, 2026

data.author: Shupe (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 8, 2025, would require voters to present photo ID when appearing to vote. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require voters to present photo ID when appearing to vote. Under existing law, voters are only required to present "proof of identity" and a voter who does not present such proof may still vote a regular ballot if they execute an affidavit attesting to their identity. Under the bill, a voter would be required to present a driver's license. state identification card, U.S. passport, or official military identification card, and the option to vote a regular ballot after executing an affidavit would be removed.
  • Reduce the cost of a state identification card. Under existing law, such cards cost $40 to be issued. Under the bill, the first such card issued to a person would be free of charge, and subsequent cards would be $20 apiece.

Last Updated April 9, 2025

status_actions.summary: From HOUSE Committee on HOUSE ADMINISTRATION: Reported without recommendation

data.author: Harris (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 8, 2025, would propose an amendment to the state constitution adjusting the qualifications to vote. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change state law regarding voter eligibility for persons convicted of a felony. Under existing law, a person's right to vote is lost upon conviction of a felony but may be regained after a completed sentence (including probation or parole) for all cases except for a conviction of murder, manslaughter (except vehicular homicide), an offense against public administration involving bribery or improper influence or abuse of office, election offenses, or any felony constituting a sexual offense. Under the bill:
    • A person convicted of a felony would only lose their right to vote while incarcerated for that offense, and permanent revocation of the right to vote would no longer be imposed.
    • A provision in the constitution that requires that person convicted of certain election offenses lose their right to vote for a period of 10 years beyond the person's sentence would be repealed.
    • Restoration of voting rights may not be made contingent on financial restitution.
    • The legislature's existing right to impose removal of suffrage as punishment for an offense would be revoked.
  • Change some requirements for voter eligibility that exist in the constitution but are not currently enforced, mostly due to incompatibility with federal law, as follows:
    • Adjust the voting age from 21 to 18 years of age. 18 is already the voting age in Delaware under federal law, but this amendment would bring the state constitution into agreement with existing practice.
    • Remove a requirement that a person be a resident of the state, county, and district for a minimum amount of time in order to register. Under existing practice, an otherwise eligible person may register to vote as long as they reside in Delaware, with no minimum period of residency required.
    • Remove a literacy requirement to be able to vote.

To become law, a constitutional amendment in Delaware must pass through the legislature with a two-thirds majority vote in consecutive sessions.

Last Updated May 9, 2025

data.author: Brown (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as adopted on March 26, 2026, proposes a constitutional amendment requiring ten days of early voting, including the Saturday and Sunday before Election Day, for each general, primary, or special election for a vacancy in the general assembly. This early voting period is currently required under statutory law for all elections, but is not mandated by the state constitution. 


Final adoption of the amendment requires passage by both legislative chambers in consecutive legislative sessions. 

Last Updated May 27, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter Number 245

data.author: Brown (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 26, 2026, proposes a constitutional amendment that would:

  • Establish no-excuse mail voting for all voters, and repeal existing provisions that limit mail voting to individuals with a qualifying excuse. Under existing constitutional law, mail voting is limited to voters (and spouse/dependents, as specified) who are unable to appear at the polling place due to: state or federal public service; the nature of one's business or occupation; sickness or disability; absence from the jurisdiction for vacation; religious tenets or teachings. Statutory law also specifies eligibility due to: absence from the state for injury during military service; service in the military, merchant marine, Red Cross, or USO; certain caregiving duties for family members; and authorization for mail voting under federal law.
  • Expressly require voters to request a mail ballot, and provide that once an application is made it remains in effect for receiving a ballot through the election cycle (the period ending with the next general election). Existing constitutional law has no express requirement that a mail ballot must be actively requested by a voter and is silent as to the duration of a mail ballot application; existing statutory law provides that a voter "may" request a ballot, and applicants may indicate whether they want to receive a ballot for an upcoming primary, general, and/or special election. The amendment would also require the ballot to include an affirmation that the vote was made "free from improper influence." 

A prior version of the bill contained a provision that was amended out of the final version. This provision would have:

  • Created a permanent mail voter list ("permanent absentee status") for qualified voters, and expanded availability of the list as currently provided by existing statutory law. Existing constitutional law is silent as to permanent mail voting status. Under existing statutory law, permanent mail ballot voting is available for voters (and spouse/dependents, as specified) due to: military service; absence from the state for injury during military service; service in the military, merchant marine, Red Cross, or USO; sickness or physical disability; UOCAVA status; or other authorization for mail voting under federal law. The proposed constitutional amendment would have expand the criteria to also include voters with certain caregiving duties, and would specify that "permanently" physically disabled voters qualify for the list. 

Final adoption of the amendment requires passage by both legislative chambers again in the subsequent legislative session.

Last Updated April 24, 2026

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would require the Florida Commission on Offender Review to develop and maintain a database to help individuals with felony convictions determine if their voting rights have been restored. The database would compile court-ordered supervision, fines, restitution, and other conditions necessary for rights restoration and be updated monthly with data from state agencies. The commission would be required to make the database publicly available online by July 1, 2029. The bill would also protect individuals from criminal prosecution if they register to vote or cast a ballot after a reasonable reliance on database information indicating their voting rights have been restored.

data.author: Jacques (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would require election officials to verify the U.S. citizenship of all voter registration applicants. Applicants whose citizenship could not be verified by election officials would need to provide proof of citizenship.


For voters using a Florida driver license or ID card number to register, election officials would verify that the number is classified as belonging to a U.S. citizen in Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles (DHSMV) records. For voters using the last four digits of their Social Security number to register, election officials would verify the applicant's citizenship through the Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) Program.


If an applicant's citizenship cannot be verified, the applicant would need to provide proof of citizenship. The bill does not define the documents that would be acceptable proof of citizenship.


The bill would also:

  • Eliminate student IDs, retirement center IDs, and neighborhood center IDs from the existing list of acceptable voter ID;
  • Require election officials, on receipt of a vote by mail request, to verify the voter's current residential and mailing addresses and the voter's U.S. citizenship. If the voter's registered address is not up to date, the voter must update it or vote in person. If the voter's U.S. citizenship cannot be verified, the voter must provide proof of citizenship before voting in person or by mail.
  • Require election officials to treat information from DHSMV indicating that a registered voter has obtained a driver's license in another state as a written request by the Florida registered voter to cancel their Florida voter registration
  • Require voters who presented a Florida ID card with their voter registration application to present a Florida ID card as voter ID at a polling location
  • Require Florida driver licenses and ID cards issued to non-U.S. citizens to be marked "NC." If a person presents an ID indicating non-citizenship when voting, they will vote a provisional ballot.
  • Require election officials to report suspected false affirmations on voter registration applications to the Office of Election Crimes and Security.

Last Updated January 14, 2026

status_actions.summary: Filed as Chapter No. 2026-26

data.author: Persons (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 1, 2026, creates a new proof of citizenship requirement applicable to new registrants and currently registered voters in Florida. The bill also makes changes to voter ID, voting systems, and the candidate qualifying process.


The bill:

  • Defines the following documents as acceptable evidence to prove U.S. citizenship:
    • Current and valid U.S. passport;
    • U.S. birth certificate;
    • Consular Report of Birth Abroad provided by the U.S. State Department;
    • Current and valid Florida driver license or Florida ID card issued by DHSMV if the license or ID indicates U.S. citizenship;
    • Naturalization certificate issued by the U.S. Department of Homeland Security;
    • Current and valid photo identification issued by the federal government or Florida state government which indicates U.S. citizenship; or 
    • An order from a federal court granting U.S. citizenship.
  • Conditions approval of a voter registration application on verification that the applicant has provided a document acceptable as evidence of U.S. citizenship to the Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles (DHSMV).
  • Requires voters whose legal name differs from the name on a document submitted as proof of citizenship to also submit proof of legal name change.
  • Adds verification of an applicant’s U.S. citizenship to the existing process of comparing an applicant’s Florida driver’s license or Florida identification number with information maintained by DHSMV when a person applies through the online voter registration system. The applicant’s U.S. citizenship status must be recorded in the statewide voter registration system.
  • Provides that if an applicant's U.S. citizenship cannot be confirmed by DHSMV records, the online voter registration system will transmit the application to the supervisor of elections, who must follow up with the voter to obtain proof of citizenship.
  • Requires the Department of State to identify voters who are potentially ineligible due to lack of U.S. citizenship. The Department will review relevant state and federal databases to identify potentially ineligible voters. If the Department cannot verify a currently registered voter's U.S. citizenship, it must notify the supervisor who must notify the registered voter.
  • Requires voters who fail to verify their citizenship after receiving notice from a supervisor of elections to be subject to the same cancellation process as voters deemed ineligible for other reasons.
  • Requires DHSMV to transmit weekly to the Department of State information identifying persons who presented evidence of U.S. citizenship; changed their address; or received a new, renewed, or replacement Florida driver license or ID card number during transactions with DHSMV in the preceding week.
  • Changes the types of ID acceptable as voter ID. The bill would add U.S. passport cards as acceptable ID, and remove student IDs, retirement center IDs, public assistance IDs, neighborhood association IDs, and debit and credit cards as acceptable ID. These changes also apply to acceptable ID for provisional ballot verification, vote by mail ballot cure, and IDs required of certain first time voters who register by mail.
  • Requires, by July 1, 2027, a Florida driver license or ID card issued to an applicant who is a U.S. citizen to indicate that the holder is a U.S. citizen.
  • Requires all voters to mark all selections on an official ballot using a pen or marker recommended by the voting system vendor unless they request to use a voter interface device that produces a voter-verifiable paper output. The bill also amends the statutory definition of "ballot" to mean a printed piece of paper that a voter must mark with the pen or marker recommended by the voting system vendor and remove existing language related to "electronic or electromechanical devices" and "marksense ballots."
  • Requires candidates seeking a party's nomination to be affiliated with that party for the 365 consecutive days prior to the beginning of the qualification period. Similarly, the bill requires a candidate seeking election with no party affiliation to be unaffiliated with any political party for the 365 consecutive days prior to the beginning of the qualification period. Candidates will also need to file an affirmation if they hold dual citizenship with another nation. Candidates for federal office will also need to file an affirmation concerning whether they will trade stocks while in office.
  • Allows supervisor of elections' offices to close for holidays only when they are not otherwise required to be open for election-related purposes.
  • Increases the statute of limitations on prosecutions for felonies under the Election Code from three years to five years.
  • Expands prohibitions against foreign nationals making election contributions and candidates and political organizations accepting contributions from foreign nationals.
  • Clarifies the definition of "racketeering activity" in the Criminal Code to include specific offense related to initiative petitions. Prior law only included generalized irregularities or fraud in relation to initiative petitions in the definition of "racketeering activity."

The proof of citizenship and voter ID provisions of this bill go into effect on January 1, 2027.

Last Updated April 7, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE AFFAIRS

data.author: Cross (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would authorize county supervisors of election to take certain actions during an emergency declared by the Governor.


If the the Governor declares an emergency in a county within 60 days of an election, the bill would allow the supervisor to seek approval from the Secretary of State to:

  • Allow early voting on the day before Election Day;
  • Allow Election Day voting at early voting sites. Essentially, this would allow a county to operate a vote center model for Election Day voting;
  • Designate additional secure ballot intake stations; and
  • Appoint poll workers who are registered voters in the state, but not the applicable county.

If the Governor declares an emergency in a county within 60 days of an election, the bill would allow the supervisor to take these actions with notice to the Secretary of State:

  • Change the location of early voting sites;
  • Designate early voting sites at other locations where eligible early voting locations are unavailable;
  • Appoint poll workers who have not met all statutory training requirements for the current election if they have received the training in previous years; and
  • Send a vote-by-mail ballot to a voter who has requested one by forwardable mail or to an address other than the address in the voter's registration records.

The bill also contains provisions about emergency contingency planning, emergency training, and a statewide strategic election equipment reserve.

Last Updated April 17, 2026

data.author: Grall (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 4, 2026, would create a new proof of citizenship requirement applicable to new registrants and currently registered voters in Florida. The bill would also make changes to voting systems and require citizenship markers on Florida driver's licenses and ID cards beginning January 1, 2027.


The bill would:

  • Add verification of an applicant’s U.S. citizenship to the existing process of comparing an applicant’s Florida driver’s license or Florida identification number with information maintained by the Department of Highway Safety and Motor Vehicles (DHSMV) when a person applies through the online voter registration system. The applicant’s U.S. citizenship status must be recorded in the statewide voter registration system.
  • Provide that if an applicant's U.S. citizenship cannot be confirmed by DHSMV records, the online voter registration system will notify the appropriate supervisor of elections that the applicant's citizenship was not verified and transmit the application and the applicant's digital signature to the supervisor.
  • Require the Department of State to identify registered voters who are potentially ineligible to vote due to noncitizenship. If the Department determines that a registered voter may be ineligible due to noncitizenship, it must notify the supervisor who must notify the registered voter. The Department will review information received from DHSMV, clerks of state and federal courts, and the U.S. Department of Homeland Security.
  • Require voters whose citizenship cannot be verified through state and federal data sources to provide a copy of one of the following documents to prove U.S. citizenship:
    • Valid and unexpired U.S. passport;
    • Original or certified copy of a U.S. birth certificate;
    • Consular Report of Birth Abroad provided by the U.S. State Department;
    • Current and valid Florida driver license or Florida ID card issued by DHSMV if the license or ID indicates U.S. citizenship;
    • Naturalization certificate issued by the U.S. Department of Homeland Security;
    • Current and valid photo identification issued by the federal government or Florida state government which indicates U.S. citizenship; or 
    • An order from a federal court granting U.S. citizenship.
  • Require voters whose legal name differs from the name on a document submitted as proof of citizenship to also submit proof of legal name change.
  • Require voters who fail to verify their citizenship after receiving notice from a supervisor of elections to be subject to the same cancellation process as voters deemed ineligible for other reasons. These voters will receive a provisional ballot if they present themselves at a polling location. The provisional ballot will only be counted if the voter provides documentation verifying their citizenship within two days after the election.
  • Require DHSMV to transmit weekly to the Department of State information identifying persons who presented evidence of U.S. citizenship; changed their address; or received a new, renewed, or replacement Florida driver license or ID card number during transactions with DHSMV in the preceding week.
  • Require, by July 1, 2027, a Florida driver license or ID card issued to an applicant who is a U.S. citizen to indicate that the holder is a U.S. citizen.
  • Require all voters, other than those with a disability, to mark all selections on an official ballot using a pen or marker recommended by the voting system vendor. The bill would also amend the statutory definition to mean a printed piece of paper that a voter must mark with the pen or marker recommended by the voting system vendor and remove existing language related to "electronic or electromechanical devices" and "marksense ballots."
  • Increase the statute of limitations on prosecutions for felonies under the Election Code from three years to five years.

This bill would take effect on July 1, 2026. The previous version of the bill would have taken effect on January 1, 2027.


A previous version of the bill would have required an independent vote validation (tabulation audit) of the voting systems used in an election prior to county certification. Existing law requires a tabulation audit to occur immediately following county certification. This provision was amended out on February 4, 2026.

Last Updated February 5, 2026

This bill, as amended on February 27, 2025, would:

If joined, the Department of Driver Services would be required to provide driver's license or identification card information related to voter eligibility, and the secretary of state must retain the ability to withdraw from the compact at any time.

data.author: Fleming T (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 28, 2025, would require the state to withdraw from the Electronic Registration Information Center (ERIC); prohibit hand delivery of mail ballots after early ("advanced") voting ends; and delay implementation of State Election Board rules, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Terminate the state's membership in ERIC within 90 days of enactment, by requiring withdrawal from "any multistate voter list maintenance organization that: (A) Requires or encourages the contacting of individuals who are not currently registered to vote with the intent to register them to vote; or (B) Shares voter data outside of the explicit purpose of removing deceased, duplicate, or otherwise ineligible voters from the list of electors." The bill would also repeal existing provisions that authorize participation in such organizations, and expressly prohibit state and local officials from doing so. 
  • Authorize the secretary of state to enter into joint compacts with other states to share voter registration information for list maintenance. If joined, the Department of Driver Services would be required to provide driver's license or identification card information related to voter eligibility, and the secretary of state must retain the ability to withdraw from the compact at any time.
  • Require the State Election Board to submit a report to the legislature by December 31, 2025, evaluating ways to improve the voter registration system without joining any nongovernmental entity for sharing voter data. The report would include recommended changes to state law and would be submitted to the chairs of the Senate Ethics Committee and House Governmental Affairs Committee.
  • Move the general deadline earlier for hand delivery of a completed mail ballot, from the close of polls on Election Day to the end of the early voting period (Friday before Election Day). The bill would make an exception for ballots delivered on Election Day voters at their precinct, through the U.S. Postal Service, or under federal law for overseas and military voters.
  • Require video surveillance of drop boxes beginning at the end of each early voting day, and mandate that if video surveillance is not possible, the drop box must be removed from the publicly accessible area, locked, and secured so that no ballots may be inserted. Under existing law, drop boxes must be constantly observed by election officials or security personnel when available to the public, and closed when early voting ends each day, but there is no requirement for video surveillance when they are not in use. 
  • Specify that poll watchers must have access to areas where tabulation processes take place, "including, but not limited to, adjudication of provisional ballots, closing of advance voting equipment, the verification and processing of absentee ballots, memory card transfers, or election reconciliation processes." Existing law authorizes poll watchers at locations deemed "necessary for the assurance of fair and honest procedures in the tabulating center," including check-in area, computer room, and duplication area, but does not otherwise specify locations.
  • Delay the implementation of new State Election Board rules adopted within 60 days of a primary or general election, except for emergency rule-making. Any rule adopted close to a presidential preference primary, general primary, general election, or runoff would not take effect until 30 days after the election, while rules affecting special elections or runoffs would follow existing legal timelines but could not apply to the election for which they were adopted. Currently, the State Election Board can adopt and implement new rules at any time.
  • Authorize a municipal governing body to opt out of conducting Saturday voting on one or more Saturdays during the period of early voting for any municipal election. Existing law requires that early voting be available on the second and third Saturday of the voting period for all elections.
  • Expressly permit county officials to provide public access to the voter list for free, in their office or on their website, and repeal the existing authority of the secretary of state to determine the cost for accessing the voter list. 

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates, reporting of election results, and dates for certain special elections.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

data.author: Byrd (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would specify that a violation of various existing prohibitions related to voting without qualifications are felony offenses and repeal any conflicting law. Under existing law, registering to vote without qualifications required by law or knowingly giving false information in registering is a felony. Under the bill, the offenses that would be deemed felonies include: 

  • Voting without being registered as a voter in accordance with the law
  • Voting without being a U.S. citizen and a resident of the state and county or municipality where voting
  • Voting under the age of 18 on or before the election date
  • Voting after being convicted of a felony involving moral turpitude without completing the sentence
  • Voting after being judicially determined mentally incompetent (unless the disability has been removed)

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 25, 2025, would require local election superintendents to post a notice on the county or municipal website if the office closes due to a declared state of emergency. The bill would also require officials to post signage on the door of the office detailing when the office will be opened and how to contact staff during the closure. 

Last Updated April 1, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE: Read 2nd time

data.author: Carter (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 4, 2025, would authorize county election superintendents to allow voters to appear at any precinct in the county on Election Day, effectively creating a vote center model without new, dedicated vote centers. Under existing law, voters must appear at their assigned precinct in order to vote on Election Day.

Last Updated April 4, 2025

status_actions.summary: In SENATE.  Read third time.  Passed SENATE.  *****To HOUSE for concurrence

data.author: Clark J (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 25, 2026, would make significant changes to Georgia's voting infrastructure. This bill would:

  • Impose civil fines on clerk's for not removing voter's names and registration from the official voter list that were successfully challenged and proven ineligible;
  • Require all elections after July 1, 2026 to be tabulated with an optical scanning voting system with pre-printed handmarked paper ballots;
  • Require the Secretary of State to post an “enumerated list of electors” who have cast ballots during a primary, general, and special election;
  • Require the SOS to post certain election related data; including, authentication of ballot scans, cast vote record, security logs, system log files of central tabulators; and
  • Require manual recounts for certain election races.
  • Make conforming changes related to ballot design, early voting, and access for disabled voters.

Last Updated April 2, 2026

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE: Read 2nd time

data.author: Byrd (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 29, 2026, would remove the Secretary of State's oversight of certain election responsibilities and duties, such as oversight of election equipment, method of voting, and voter education, and prescribe these responsibilities to the Georgia State Election Board.

Last Updated February 10, 2026

data.author: Gullett (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 6, would change the voter list maintenance process for individuals who move in and out of Georgia.


The bill would require the Secretary of State to provide information regarding individuals who have moved to Georgia and registered to vote to the state they were previously registered to vote in. The bill would require the Secretary of State to publicly post the total numbers of notices regarding people who have moved out state, the state these notices were sent, and the total number of records received from other states.


If enacted, this bill would become effective January 1, 2027.

Last Updated March 9, 2026

This bill, as introduced on February 25, would eliminate Georgia's automatic voter registration system at the DMV. This bill would change the process from opt-out to opt-in and would require the voter to affirmatively consent to registration rather than affirmatively decline registration.

This bill, as introduced on March 4, would put forward a constitutional amendment to eliminate felony conviction involving moral turpitude as a disenfranchising crime. This bill would eliminate disenfranchisement for all felony convictions as moral turpitude is not defined in Georgia.


This bill would require the voters of Georgia to decide by majority vote for ratification.
