data.author: Burns (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 18, would make multiple changes to Georgia's voting infrastructure. This bill would:

  • Require hand-marked paper ballots and compatible machines for tabulation purposes after January 1, 2028. The state would be required to fund compatible machines for each county.
  • Establish a committee of partisan appointees to assist the SOS in certifying voting equipment.
  • Expand the State Elections Board (SEB) authority to oversee the audit of paper ballots images.
  • Expand the election materials required to be publicly posted by the SOS to include cast vote records, system log files of central tabulators.
  • Amend multiple election administration processes relevant to election night reporting and ballot reconciliation.
  • Make conforming changes relevant to disabled access voting and ballot design and content.

Last Updated March 20, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Watson S (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would:

  • Delay the effective date of new State Election Board rules by requiring that any rule adopted within 60 days of a primary, general election, or runoff would take effect 30 days after the election, except for emergency rule-making under specified conditions. Currently, the State Election Board can adopt and implement new rules at any time.
  • Prohibit the hand delivery of mail ("absentee") ballots after early ("advanced") voting ends, except for ballots delivered on Election Day voters at their precinct, through the U.S. Postal Service, or under federal law for overseas and military voters. Existing law does not include a similar prohibition.
  • Establish "supervised absentee ballot assistance" events at assisted living, nursing homes, and long-term care facilities, allowing residents to receive assistance in completing their absentee ballots under the supervision of election officials. Under the bill:
    • Events would need to be requested at least 30 days before an election by either the facility administrator or a resident.
    • Election officials would be required to coordinate with the facility to schedule the event and provide notice to all candidates, political parties, and nonpartisan candidates appearing on the ballot.
    • A supervised assistance team consisting of at least two election officials would deliver and oversee ballot completion.
    • Residents could choose to submit their ballot immediately to election officials, retain it for later submission, or vote in person at an advance voting site or on Election Day.
    • If a voter refuses or is unable to complete their ballot, election officials would be required to document the refusal and return the ballot to the elections office.
  • Establish a process for candidates, political parties, and nonpartisan candidates to designate poll watchers at supervised assistance events.
  • Require daily public reporting of mail and provisional ballot data during the early voting period and for three days after an election, including the number of ballots issued, returned, and rejected, as well as real-time precinct-level election results posted by 11:59 PM on Election Day.

Last Updated March 11, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Parkes (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would: 

  • Establish a permanent mail ("absentee") voting list. Under the bill, all voters would be able to opt into receiving mail ballots for all future elections via the general absentee ballot application. Under existing law, Georgia only allows voters who are disabled or elderly to apply once to receive absentee ballots for all primary and general elections and runoffs in a given election cycle.
  • Voters would be removed from the permanent mail voting list if they are moved to inactive status, although their permanent status will be restored if they respond to a confirmation notice updating or verifying their address.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the Code. 

Last Updated March 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Mangham (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would: 

  • Establish same-day voter registration. Eligible individuals could register to vote at their precinct on Election Day or at an early ("advanced") voting location.
  • Require same-day registrants to present proof of residency (Georgia driver's license, government issued photo identification card bearing the person's name and current address, current public or private college photo identification card with proof of residence on the campus of such institution, or current utility bill, bank statement, government check, payroll check, or other government document showing the person's name and current address) in addition to the standard photo ID required of all voters.
  • Require same-day registrants to cast provisional ballots.
  • Require officials to verify same-day registrations. If the individual is found ineligible, their application to register to vote would be rejected and they would be notified of the rejection and reasons why within three business days.
  • Specify that individuals in line to register at the time polls close would be permitted to register and vote.


Last Updated March 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Harrell (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 25, 2025, would:

  • Require that challenges be made at least 45 days before the start of early ("advance") voting.
  • Require that the challenger specifically allege that the challenged voter is one of the following:
    • Not 18 years old and will not be by the next primary, election, or runoff
    • Not a citizen of the United States
    • Convicted of a felony offense without having had their rights restored
    • Declared ineligible due to mental incompetency
    • Not a resident at the residential address in the registration record
  • Require that a challenger alleging that a voter does not live at their address of registration submit evidence that they exercised due diligence in verifying the information alleged by providing the challenged voter's actual address or by performing at at least two of the following acts:
    • Sending a letter with return service requested to voter's residential and mailing address
    • Searching county property records to see if the challenged voter owns property in the county.
    • Searching the statewide voter registration database to see whether the voter has registered at another address in the state.
  • Require challengers to provide a signed affidavit swearing that based on personal knowledge and belief the challenged voter is neither qualified to vote nor lives at the address on their registration card.
  • Provide that a challenge based on voter address will only be entertained if it is alleged that the actual residence of the challenged voter is outside the precinct of their registration address.
  • Direct the secretary of state to prepare a challenge form and stipulate that all documents relating to challenges are public records.


Last Updated April 1, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: McLaurin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 27, 2025, would repeal disenfranchisement for conviction of a felony offense involving moral turpitude, effective upon ratification of an amendment to the state constitution's disenfranchisement provision in the November 2026 election. Under existing law, disenfranchisement for such felony offenses is required under both constitutional and statutory law, and following convictions, individuals are not eligible to register or vote until the completion of all terms of their sentences (including payment of financial obligations).


Last Updated March 31, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Dolezal (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 31, 2025, would require voters to designate an affiliation with a political party 30 days before the start of early ("advanced") voting in order to participate in a party's primary. Under existing law, any voter can vote in either party's primary election. The bill would also make conforming changes throughout the code, including requiring officials to check the partisan affiliation of voters seeking to submit mail ("absentee") ballots. 

Last Updated April 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Burns (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 4, 2025, would authorize curbside voting for voters who, due to age or disability, are unable to enter the polling place or wait in line. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow eligible voters to cast a physical ballot from their vehicle in a designated area outside the polling place, as long as they appear on the voter list and present proper identification.
  • Require at least two poll workers to bring the voter’s certificate and a physical ballot to the voter’s vehicle, and for the completed, folded ballot to be immediately carried inside and deposited in a sealed ballot box.
  • Prohibit anyone other than the voter from being in the vehicle during ballot marking, unless the voter qualifies for assistance under existing law.
  • Require ballots cast curbside to use the same type of physical ballots as absentee voters and be tabulated in the same manner as other precinct ballots.
  • Require local election officials to provide sufficient ballots, equipment, signage, and secure ballot boxes to support curbside voting.
  • Add curbside voting to the election day ballot reporting requirements.
  • Direct the secretary of state to conduct a "pilot program to test and evaluate the use" of curbside voting during the general election or any special election held in November 2025 and to submit a report to the General Assembly on the results of the pilot program not later than 90 days following the election.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 17, would eliminate drop boxes for absentee voting and all relevant processes.

data.bill_name: Henry Mcneal Turner Voting Rights Act

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Jones (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 19, 2026, would establish the Henry McNeal Turner Voting Rights Act. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit any policy or practice that would result in the abridgment of the right to vote for any individual of a protected class. The bill would establish guidelines for determining if a practice or policy is a violation.
  • Establish the Georgia Voting Rights Commission, made of five commissioners, this entity would oversee and approve certain election-related changes around the state.
  • Require certain localities to provide language assistance programs when their population reaches a set threshold. The bill would require election and voting related materials to be translated into the specific language.
  • Establish legal standing for aggrieved parties. The bill would waive sovereign immunity held by the state.
  • Establish a state database to draft, publish, and conduct reports, seminars, and classes providing information on election administration. The state database would also be able to disseminate election data.

Last Updated March 25, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

data.author: Mangham (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 24, would permit unhoused individuals with no permanent address to use certain addresses for residency and mailing purposes. The address that could be used are: (1) the mailing address of the registrar in the locality the individuals resides, (2) the address of of the postsecondary educational institution in which the individual is enrolled; or (3) a place of worship the individual is affiliated. Under existing law, unhoused individuals are required to use the address of the registrar in the locality in which they reside for residency and mailing purposes.

Last Updated March 2, 2026

status_actions.summary: In SENATE.  Read third time.  Failed to pass SENATE

data.author: Dolezal (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 3, would make several changes to Georgia's election and voting system, including:

  • Establish civil fines of up to 10,000$ for a registrar's failure to remove a voter's name after a successful challenge;
  • Changing the type of voting machines used for all voting (Election Day and Advance Voting) from electronic ballot marking machines to optical scanning machines and pre-printed hand marked ballots;
  • Change advance voting infrastructure from county-wide to precinct-wide voting. The bill would require clerks to assign voters to specific precincts for advance voting if more than precinct was needed in the county;
  • Change election night reporting;
  • Expand the election related materials the Secretary of State must publicly post to include authentication files of ballots scans, cast vote record, security logs, and system log files for central tabulators;
  • Change the percentage difference required for a recount from .05% to 1% difference. Any recount conducted would be required to be counted by hand;
  • Change the audit process to require precertification risk limiting audit for all statewide and federal offices;
  • Increase the State Elections Board's oversight to include audits

Last Updated March 5, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ETHICS

This bill, as introduced on February 25, would limit when a new regulation or rule adopted by the State Board of Election may become effective. The bill would prohibit any rules or regulations adopted within 180 days of an election to be effective the day after the election.


The bill would prohibit any practice related to elections or voter registration adopted or revised by a local election superintendent within 180 days of any election to be effective the day after the election.

data.author: Alcos (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 16, 2025, expands access to voter service centers. Under existing law, Hawaii conducts its elections by mail, but offers voter service centers for voters who prefer to vote in person. Under the bill, the number of voter service centers would increase to a minimum of at least one per county council district open for in-person voting on election day. The clerk would be instructed to prioritize the use of public schools as voter service centers.

Last Updated January 23, 2025

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 16, 2025, would require at least one voter service center in each county council district to be open for in-person voting on Election Day. While Hawaii primarily conducts its elections by mail, existing law provides for the establishment of these voter service centers for in-person voting. The bill would change the law by requiring more such centers be available.

Last Updated January 23, 2025

data.author: Kapela (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 10, 2026, would expand automatic voter registration to additional agencies and change automatic registration from front-end to back-end while limiting automatic registration to individuals who produce documentary proof of citizenship during their agency transaction. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit an automatic voter registration agency from submitting a registrant for voter registration if the applicant has not provided proof of citizenship. Under existing law, the DMV equivalent (which is the only automatic voter registration agency) is only prohibited from submitting a registrant's application for voter registration if the registrant has submitted evidence of a lack of citizenship.
  • Permit any state agency to be designated by the office of elections as an automatic voter registration agency if the agency collects information sufficient to register a person as a voter in conducting its regular business.
  • Change automatic voter registration from front-end to back-end. Under existing law, applicants are offered the chance to opt out during their transaction with an AVR agency. Under the bill, the applicant would be mailed a notice after the transaction and would be required to respond to the notice to decline registration.
  • Permit an automatic voter registration agency to also automatically pre-register any minor eligible for pre-registration.
  • Clarify that an ineligible person who is automatically registered in error does not commit an offense.

Last Updated March 13, 2026

status_actions.summary: Subsequent referral set for: HOUSE Committee on FINANCE

data.author: Lee (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 17, 2025, would facilitate the voter registration of high school students. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require officials at each public high school, charter high school, or private high school to provide voter affidavits to each student 16 years of age or older in January of each year. The officials would be permitted to collect the completed affidavits for submission.
  • Permit the board of education, each charter high school, and each private high school to adopt policies that would facilitate the automatic preregistration and registration of eligible students.

Last Updated January 21, 2025

status_actions.summary: Subsequent referral set for: HOUSE Committee on FINANCE

data.author: Garcia (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 17, 2025, would expand access to voter service centers in Hawaii. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Increase the availability of voter service centers. Under existing law in Hawaii, elections are conducted entirely by mail, but voters have the option in the ten business days before an election to appear in person at a "voter service center" to deliver their ballots, register to vote, or vote in person if preferred. Existing law only requires a single voter service center in each county. This bill would require the number of voter service centers within a county to be equal to the number of members serving on the county council, with one such center in each district (or equitably placed if the county uses at-large districts).
  • Eliminate variation in the hours that a voting service center may be open. Existing law permits a county to open additional centers with varying days and hours of operation. Under the bill, each voter service center would be required to be open the same days and hours.

Last Updated January 29, 2025

Under existing law, a voter whose mail ballot envelope is deficient in a curable way has until the fifth business day after the election to fix the problem and have their ballot counted. Under the bill, this would be extended to the thirteenth calendar day after the election.

data.author: Belatti (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 12, 2026, would expand automatic voter registration to additional agencies and change automatic registration from front-end to back-end. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit any state agency to be designated by the office of elections as an automatic voter registration agency if the agency collects information sufficient to register a person as a voter in conducting its regular business. Individuals could only be registered if they either presented proof of U.S. citizenship or attest to their U.S. citizenship.
  • Change automatic voter registration from front-end to back-end. Under existing law, applicants are offered the chance to opt out during their transaction with an AVR agency. Under the bill, the applicant would be mailed a notice after the transaction and would be required to respond to the notice to decline registration.
  • Permit an automatic voter registration agency to also automatically pre-register any minor eligible for pre-registration.
  • Clarify that an ineligible person who is automatically registered in error does not commit an offense.

A previous version of this bill would have required automatic voter registration applicants to present documentary proof of U.S. citizenship. Those provisions were amended out of the bill on February 12, 2026.

data.author: Chang (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 15, 2025, would dramatically expand the list of agencies offering automatic voter registration. Under existing law, automatic voter registration is only available at Hawaii's equivalent of the DMV. Under the bill, automatic voter registration would be available at any state agency that interacts with the public and collects reliable information concerning citizenship. Each such agency would be instructed to treat an application for its services as a dual-purpose application which an individual can use to apply for voter registration, change an existing registration, or decline to be registered. An applicant who indicated that they wished to be registered or update their registration would have their application sent to the county clerk for processing.

This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would expand access to voter service centers in Hawaii. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Increase the availability of voter service centers. Under existing law in Hawaii, elections are conducted entirely by mail, but voters have the option in the ten business days before an election to appear in person at a "voter service center" to deliver their ballots, register to vote, or vote in person if preferred. Existing law only requires a single voter service center in each county. This bill would require the number of voter service centers within a county to be equal to the number of members serving on the county council, with one such center in each district (or equitably placed if the county uses at-large districts).
  • Eliminate variation in the hours that a voting service center may be open. Existing law permits a county to open additional centers with varying days and hours of operation. Under the bill, each voter service center would be required to be open the same days and hours.


Last Updated February 12, 2025

data.bill_name: On Call Circuit Judges for Elections Disputes

status_actions.summary: In Committee: Measure Deferred

data.author: Kouchi (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 18, 2025, would change the process for resolving voter challenges. Under existing law, voter challengers are heard by the board of registration, and is subject to appeal to an appellate court. Under the bill, a challenge could be heard by a relevant circuit court if the board of registration lacks full membership, and an on-call circuit judge would be required to be designated for each circuit with the goal of expediting challenges.

Last Updated February 25, 2025

data.author: Rhoads (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 7, 2026, establishes an order of hierarchy for who serves as the state's chief election officer in the event of a vacancy. To the extent each official with the state's elections office is available to serve, the order is the supervisor of election support services, the general counsel, the supervisor of counting center operations, the supervisor of ballot operations, and the supervisor of voting services.


A previous version of the bill would have made the attorney general the state's interim chief election officer in the event of a vacancy. That provision was amended out of the bill on March 5, 2026.

Last Updated May 28, 2026

data.author: Chang (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 29, 2026:

  • Makes changes to the automatic voter registration process at the DMV. Under existing law, persons applying for a driver's license or state ID card must complete a portion of the application indicating whether they wish to register to vote or to update their registration before they can complete the license or state ID application. Under the bill, the applicant would instead automatically be registered to vote (or have their registration updated) unless they affirmatively indicate that they do not wish for this to happen.
  • Requires that the person attest that they meet the requirements for voter registration, and require that they be advised that completing the application will result in them being registered.
  • Requires the DMV to obtain or confirm proof of citizenship from existing DMV records before transmitting an individual's application for registration and prohibits transmitting information for an individual who provides documentation indicating they are not a citizen. If the DMV cannot confirm an applicant's citizenship status, they must provide information to the applicant about eligibility criteria.
  • Takes effect January 1, 2027.

A previous version of this bill would have expanded automatic voter registration to additional agencies, but those provisions were amended out of the bill on March 6, 2026.

Last Updated June 11, 2026

data.author: Gustoff (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025 requires voter identification cards, driver’s licenses, and state ID cards applied for, issued, or renewed on or after the effective date of the bill to include an indication of whether the person identified on the card is a U.S. citizen. The bill also requires a voter, when appearing to vote, to present proof of citizenship if their voter ID does not indicate whether the voter is a U.S. citizen.

Last Updated January 27, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Golding (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2025, would require that a person who registers to vote at a polling place during early voting or on election day vote a provisional ballot. The ballot would only be counted if election officials determine that the voter was eligible to vote. Under existing law, same-day registrants can cast a regular ballot if their eligibility to register can be confirmed with an electronic pollbook.

Last Updated January 31, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Croken (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2025, would mandate the sending of mail ballots to every voter in Iowa. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require county commissioners of elections to send a mail ballot to each registered voter 45 days before an election. Existing law permits all voters to request a mail ballot, but requires a voter to submit an application. Existing law also provides that a mail ballot be sent to a voter no earlier than 20 days before the election.
  • Create an electronic tracking system by which voters can verify that their voted ballot has been received. The bill would require voter registration cards and mail ballots to include information on how to use the tracking system.
  • Require a special precinct election board to meet on November 1 to begin counting mail ballots. Under existing law, an election board is only permitted to begin doing this on the day before Election Day.

Last Updated January 31, 2025

data.author: Wulf (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 12, 2025, would require a voter (other than a first-time voter who is not affiliated with another party) to have been affiliated with a political party for thirty days in order to vote in the party's primary election. Under existing law, a voter may register with a party on the day of the primary.

Last Updated February 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Andrews (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 20, 2025, would end no-excuse mail voting in Iowa, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Limit the voters who may vote by mail ballot. Under existing law, any voter may vote by mail ballot if they expect to be absent from their home precinct on election day. Under the bill, voting by mail would be limited to the following groups:
    • Voters who will be absent from their home precinct due to a responsibility related to their employment
    • Voters who will be prevented from going to the polls due to age, disability, or illness
    • Voters who expect to be unable to go to the polls because they are a member of the military
  • Make Election Day a state holiday.
  • The bill also makes changes to areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab concerning campaign finance.

Last Updated February 21, 2025

Under existing law, Iowa does not have automatic restoration in statute, though Governor Reynolds issued an executive order under which the rights of those who have fully discharged their sentences are restored. This bill would put restoration into statute.

The bill would permit the program to either be operated statewide or in enough counties to determine whether statewide implementation would be possible in the future. The state commissioner would be required to file an annual report on the pilot program with the legislature until its expiration in 2028.

data.author: House State Government Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 5, 2026, narrows exceptions to voter ID requirements, creates new voter list maintenance practices, and clarifies the conduct constituting an election offense. Specifically, the bill:

  • Eliminates the attestation of another registered voter as an option for persons lacking required voter identification.
  • Requires the cancellation of voter registration for anyone whose voter registration has been designated as incomplete, pending, or unconfirmed for ninety days if the commissioner has been unable to contact them or the voter has failed to supply or correct any missing or inaccurate information.
  • Specifies that conduct constituting the offense of election misconduct is an offense if committed on any day in which voting takes place in person. Under existing law, election misconduct is defined as certain acts taking place on election day.

The bill contains additional provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including provisions concerning certain local election filings.

Provisions from a previous version of the bill were amended out of the current text. They would have:

  • Specified that the online application voters may use to track the status of their mail ballot request form or mail ballot must allow a voter to choose to receive notification of changes in the status of the voter’s absentee ballot request form or absentee ballot by electronic mail, text message, or other electronic means.
  • Required the implementation of a postal service barcode for mail ballots.

Last Updated May 7, 2026

data.author: Senate State Government Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 6, 2025, would require elections to be conducted with paper ballots and optical scan voting systems, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Strike a provision of existing law authorizing county commissioners to choose whether to use automatic tabulating equipment. The bill would require elections to be conducted with paper ballots and optical scan voting systems.
  • Assign responsibility for investigating allegations of election misconduct reported to the attorney general to the county sheriff or other law enforcement agency with jurisdiction. The law enforcement would be responsible for submitting their report to the attorney general and the state commissioner. Under existing law, the attorney general is responsible for these investigations.
  • Permit the use of an electronic poll book or election register if the product has been certified for use by the state commissioner.

The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including affidavits of candidacy.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Senate State Government Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 6, 2025, would make several minor adjustments to election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require mail ballot materials to include the voter’s four-digit personal identification number instead of the voter’s voter identification number.
  • Remove the first and second Monday following an election as a possible day for conducting a first or second canvass of an election.
  • Require the special precinct election board to convene no later than 9:00 a.m. on election day for the counting of mail ballots. Existing law only requires that this time be set in such a manner as to permit the completion of counting by 10:00 p.m.

The bill would also make minor changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

Last Updated March 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Senate State Government Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 6, 2025, would create a new voter registration designation called "unconfirmed" registration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a new type of voter registration designation called "unconfirmed" for voters about whom the state registrar or county commissioner has received information from a reliable source indicating that the registered voter is not qualified. Such a voter would not be permitted to vote until they prove their qualifications.
  • Explicitly prohibit any voter registration agency from providing voter registration services to a person who has submitted documentation indicating that they are not a citizen. The department of transportation would be required to share information on any such person with the state registrar.
  • Specify that state and federal government agencies and private entities are among the entities with which a state registrar may contract for purposes of maintaining accurate voter registration rolls. The bill also requires the registrar to examine noncitizen identification files when performing list maintenance duties.
  • Clarify that mail ballots with defects that are not cured by the deadline must be rejected.

Last Updated March 31, 2025

data.bill_name: Verification of United States Citizenship of Voters

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Senate State Government Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 25, 2026, would create a requirement that the state registrar of voters attempt to verify the citizenship of all registered voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the registrar of voters to attempt to verify the citizenship of all registered voters using the federal systematic alien verification for entitlements system, and provide notice to county commissioners of the results. This check would include a monthly check of all newly registered voters.
  • Require that, when a county commissioner receives notice that the registrar was unable to verify a voter as a citizen, the commissioner designate the voter as "unconfirmed" and send notice that the voter must provide proof of citizenship to remain registered. Notice to an affected voter would include information on how to verify the voter's citizenship, how to contact the proper federal agencies to correct their information, and how to cancel their registration.
  • Require a voter's registration to be canceled if the voter receives notice as provided under the bill and does not provide proof of citizenship within 90 days of receipt.
  • Instruct the state registrar to adopt rules as needed to implement the legislation, which would presumably include establishing what constitutes proof of citizenship.

Last Updated February 26, 2026

data.author: Senate Education Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on June 2, 2026, creates a requirement that the state registrar of voters attempt to verify the citizenship of all registered voters. Specifically, the bill:

  • Requires the registrar of voters to attempt to verify the citizenship of all registered voters using the federal systematic alien verification for entitlements system, and provide notice to county commissioners of the results. This check includes a monthly check of all newly registered voters.
  • Requires that, when a county commissioner receives notice that the registrar was unable to verify a voter as a citizen, the commissioner designate the voter as "unconfirmed" and send notice that the voter must provide proof of citizenship to remain registered. Notice to an affected voter includes information on how to verify the voter's citizenship, how to contact the proper federal agencies to correct their information, and how to cancel their registration.
  • Requires a voter's registration to be canceled if the voter receives notice as provided under the bill and does not provide proof of citizenship within 90 days of receipt.

data.author: Bisignano (D)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on January 29, 2025, proposes a constitutional amendment. Under the existing Iowa constitution, persons convicted of an "infamous crime" - a term that the Iowa legislature has defined in other statute as meaning "felonies" - are disqualified from voting. This amendment would remove that disqualification. If the amendment were to be passed (requiring ratification by successive Iowa legislatures and then voted on by the public), further statutory changes would likely be needed to restore the right to vote to persons convicted of felonies.

data.author: Ford (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would repeal a provision of existing law under which a person who is serving a sentence of imprisonment loses their right to vote until released. Under the bill, such a person would not lose their right to vote due to imprisonment. The state constitution does require a "person convicted of a felony, or otherwise under sentence in a correctional institution or jail, [to] lose the right to vote," meaning that this bill may require a constitutional amendment to fully take effect.


Last Updated January 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on RULES

data.author: Ford (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would restore voting rights after conviction for a criminal offense within 14 days and require facilitation of registration and mail voting during incarceration, and would streamline the mail ballot request process during registration for all voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Restore voting rights to an individual convicted of a felony, or otherwise under sentence in a correctional institution or jail, 14 days following their conviction. The bill would expressly apply the restoration provision to all individuals under any form of state supervision or custody, including those owing court fines or fees, and expressly prohibit the denial of voting rights due to prior criminal conviction. Existing constitutional law mandates disenfranchisement upon conviction of a felony, and under existing statutory law, voting rights are restored upon completion of a sentence of incarceration.
  • Require local election authorities to coordinate with correctional agencies to facilitate voting by mail from each correctional institution in the state. Each correctional institution would be required to provide voter registration applications, applications to vote by mail, ballots received from election authorities, and all other election materials, including voter education materials. The bill would also require distribution of voting related materials upon release from custody.
  • Establish a confined voter's residence for purposes of voting as their last place of residence before confinement.
  • Provide generally that an individual completing a voter registration application be informed of the option to vote by mail, and allow that individual to request that their voter registration application be treated as a simultaneous application for a ballot to be voted by mail. The bill would also require provision of registration applications and mail ballots in non-English languages, in locations covered by federal or local language access requirements.

data.author: Meier (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would make several procedural changes to voter registration and voter list maintenance practices. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require that local elections officials register any new resident in their jurisdiction who is moving within Illinois as a voter within 90 days after receipt of that information from the National Change of Address database. Under existing law, no timeframe or data source for this purpose exists.
  • Require county clerks to conduct voter list verification after the consolidated election in each odd-numbered year, to be completed before the first date for circulation of candidate petitions for the next even-year primary. Under existing law, such maintenance must be conducted every other year, but the time frames for the maintenance are not specified.
  • Mandate county clerks to review returned juror summons to determine if any person is not a qualified voter based upon the information in the returned summons. The county clerk shall send a notice to any such person to appear at a hearing within 5 days of the notice to show cause as to why that person's voter registration should not be canceled. Failure to appear would cause automatic cancellation of the voter's registration.
  • Direct jury administrators and jury commissioners to, every three months, send the local election authority a list of change of address information gleaned from jury summons returns.

Last Updated January 10, 2025

data.author: Grant (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would change processes for the maintenance of voter registration lists, related to the death or address update of voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require, rather than permit, county clerks to issue certifications of death records from an electronic death reporting system on a monthly basis, and use those records to cancel the registrations of voters who have died in the past month. The bill would also newly require parties who issue death certificates to submit records to the county clerk within seven days of a death, and require clerks and coroners to certify their compliance with reporting requirements on a quarterly basis to county boards.
  • Permit any individual to request that clerks or parties who issue death certificates to provide evidence of records and reports related to list maintenance for the death or address update of voters, and create a new civil cause of action for enforcement of requests by injunction or declaratory relief.
  • Create a new civil penalty for a public body's knowing and willful failure to comply with the new maintenance provisions, or actions in bad faith, imposed on the public body. Fines would be $2500-$5000 for each occurrence, and could include attorney's fees and additional penalties for delay in compliance.

Last Updated January 23, 2025

data.author: Miller (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would establish the Office of Election Integrity within the office of the Attorney General, and would change processes for the maintenance of voter registration lists, related to the death of voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish the Office of Election Integrity within the office of the Attorney General. The responsibilities of the office would include:
    • Receiving and reviewing notices and reports concerning alleged election law violations or election irregularities
    • Conducting preliminary investigations into alleged violations and irregularities
    • Establishing and overseeing an election fraud hotline
    • Reporting its activities and findings annually to the governor and legislature.
  • Require, rather than permit, county clerks to issue certifications of death records from an electronic death reporting system on a monthly basis, and use those records to cancel the registrations of voters who have died in the past month. The bill would also newly require parties who issue death certificates to submit records to the county clerk within seven days of a death, and require clerks and coroners to certify their compliance with reporting requirements on a quarterly basis to county boards.
  • Permit any individual to request that clerks or parties who issue death certificates to provide evidence of records and reports related to list maintenance for the death of voters, and create a new civil cause of action for enforcement of requests by injunction or declaratory relief.
  • Create a new civil penalty for a public body's knowing and willful failure to comply with the new maintenance provisions, or actions in bad faith, imposed on the public body.. Fines would be $2500-$5000 for each occurrence, and could include attorney's fees and additional penalties for delay in compliance.

The bill also makes a change to an area of law Voting Rights Lab does not track concerning the use of campaign funds.

Last Updated January 23, 2025

data.author: Tipsword (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 24, 2025, would:

  • Require all voters to present a valid and current form of photo ID prior to voting in person early or on Election Day. Acceptable forms of ID would include: an Illinois driver's license or state ID, a disabled person's ID, a senior citizen ID, a firearm owner's ID, a U.S. passport with the voter's current address, or any other government-issued ID with the voter's name, address, and photo. Voters who are indigent and unable to obtain a voter ID without paying a fee and voters with a religious objection to being photographed may vote a provisional ballot and sign an affidavit stating their exemption.
  • Require voters who do not present acceptable photo ID to vote a provisional ballot. The voter would need to provide an acceptable form of ID to election officials within 10 calendar days of casting the provisional ballot.
  • Require the secretary of state to issue a voter identification card to registered voters who do not have one of the acceptable forms of ID. The voter identification card would contain the voter's name, signature, and photo. The voter would need to provide one of a list of documents to establish their eligibility for the card.
  • Require voter signature matching for vote by mail ballots to be conducted by three elections judges, only two of which may be from the same political party. Signatures would be presumed to match unless all three judges determine they are non-matching.
  • Require election officials to notify voters within two days of rejection of their vote by mail ballot, unless the ballot is rejected after Election Day, in which case notification must occur within one day. Notification would be made by mail, email, or both. Voters whose ballots are rejected for missing or mismatched voter signatures may complete and return a statement that the voter cast the ballot prior to the end of the time for counting provisional ballots. Voters whose ballots are rejected due to the ballot envelope being returned opened may cast another ballot by mail or in person prior to the close of polls on Election Day, if possible.


Last Updated February 3, 2025

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 29, 2025, would extend weekday voting hours during early voting. Existing law requires permanent early voting locations to be open from either 8:30 a.m. to 4:30 p.m. or 9:00 a.m. to 5:00 p.m. on weekdays until the last week when hours extend. Under the bill, the extended hours would be applicable throughout the entire early voting period, meaning that these locations would be open from either 8:30 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. or 9:00 a.m. to 7:00 p.m. during each weekday of the early voting period.

data.author: Windhorst (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 31, 2025, would tighten deadlines to return a mail ballot and eliminate the use of mail ballot drop boxes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require mail ballots to be received no later than the close of the polls on election day to be counted. Under existing law, a mail ballot can be counted if postmarked by election day and received in the office of the election authority as late as the 14th day after the election. The bill would also require ballots sent to overseas members of the armed forces and their families to be received no later than the 7th day after the election, a week earlier than required under existing law.
  • Remove a provision of law that authorizes the establishment of secure ballot drop-off locations for mail ballots. The bill would also remove a provision of law that permits the counting of mail ballots that are returned with insufficient or no postage.

The bill also makes changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including internet postings about the ballot counting process.

Last Updated February 3, 2025

data.author: West (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would permit the establishment of additional vote centers - locations in a jurisdiction where anyone residing in the jurisdiction may vote, regardless of precinct - during the early voting period and on election day. An authority that established additional vote centers in compliance with the bill would need to do so according to the following rule set:

  • For counties with at 250,000 registered voters:
    • from the 15th day before election day to the 5th day before election day, at least one vote center for each 75,000 registered voters;
    • from the 4th day before election day to the day before election day, at least one vote center for each 20,000 registered voters; and
    • on election day, at least one vote center for each 12,500 registered voters
  • For counties with at least 37,500 registered voters but fewer than 250,000 registered voters:
    • from the 15th day before election day to the 5th day before election day, at least one vote center for each 75,000 registered voters, except that there must be at least one vote center in each county;
    • from the 4th day before election day to the day before election day, at least one vote center for each 20,000 registered voters; and
    • on election day, at least one vote center for each 12,500 registered voters
  • For counties with at least 12,500 registered voters but fewer than 37,500 registered voters at least one vote center during early voting and at least 3 on election day
  • For counties with fewer than 12,500 registered voters, at least one vote center during early voting and on election day.

data.author: Hoffman (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would change the automatic voter registration process at state agencies. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change the format of automatic voter registration from "front-end AVR" to "back-end AVR." Instead of being notified of the opportunity to opt out of registration at the time of an agency transaction, the bill would instead require a notice to be sent to the voter within five business days. The voter would then have 23 days to return the notice and decline to be registered.
  • Allow documents that demonstrate a person's citizenship and voting age to be used to support a dual-purpose application (one that serves as either an application to register to vote or an application to change the voter's registered residential address or name). Under existing law, only identification documents meeting REAL ID standards may serve as the basis for a dual-purpose application. This bill would also allow documents that are silent as to citizenship to serve as the basis for a dual-purpose application. The bill makes clear that an identity document that affirmatively shows a person's noncitizen status cannot be the basis for a voter registration application.
  • Modify the dual-purpose application to permit an applicant to indicate their preferred language. The bill would also ensure that persons completing a dual-purpose application have access to materials in the five most common non-English languages spoken in the state.
  • Remove a requirement that the state board of elections make an official determination that a state or federal agency has access to reliable personal information before entering into an agreement to make the agency an automatic voter registration agency.
  • Specify that an applicant for a drivers license or state ID card who provides a substitute mailing address under the states confidentiality program for certain crime victims, or who provides a work address due to the persons status as a peace officer or judicial officer, shall not be offered automatic voter registration services. The state board of elections would be permitted to provide alternative voter registration services to such persons.


Last Updated February 12, 2025

data.author: Buckner (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 6, 2025, would guarantee certain protections for voting rights. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all provisions of election law in the state to be interpreted in a manner that favors the right to vote.
  • Prohibit any political subdivision, state agency, or government official from implementing any policy or practice that would create a material disparity in access to the vote for members of a protected class or impair their ability to participate in the political process. An exception might be made for policies that advance an important government interest, but the bill specifies that violations are likely if polling place closures, consolidation, or similar activities make it harder for members of a protected class to vote.
  • Prevent political subdivisions from implementing at-large methods of election or any other method with the effect of diluting the vote of protected class members.
  • Require a local election authority to provide language access assistance if the political subdivision reaches certain thresholds for non-English language speakers.
  • Permit a civil action to be brought by the attorney general or anyone aggrieved by a violation of one of these provisions.

Last Updated March 3, 2025

data.author: Ammons (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 7, 2025, would authorize a local election authority to issue universal mail ballots to voters in its jurisdiction. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Authorize a local election authority to universally mail ballots to qualified voters within its jurisdiction instead of sending notices and applications for permanent mail ballots. Universal ballot mailing is not available under existing law, but the local election authority is required to mail every registered voter an invitation to join the permanent voter list.
  • Require a local election authority that sends out universal mail ballots to issue them in the same timeframe currently required for permanent mail ballots. The bill would also require the local election authority to post in a public space at the entrance of an election office a list of the names of eligible voters and their street address, ward and precinct number. The list would be required to be posted not more than 90 days and not less than 5 days prior to Election Day. The local election authority must also send the list to the State Board of Elections for the board to post on its website. Existing law contains a similar provision mandating public posting of eligible mail ballot applicants.
  • Prohibit a person who has never voted from being sent a universal mail ballot, unless they provide sufficient proof of identification and that identification is verified by the local election authority.
  • Permit a person to request, in writing, not to receive a universal mail ballot. The bill would require the local election authority to keep a record of those who opt out and send them a notice and application for a permanent mail ballot in all subsequent elections.

Last Updated February 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: Rule 3-9(a) / Re-referred to ASSIGNMENTS Committee

data.author: Rose (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 17, 2025, would:

  • Require all voters to present a valid and current form of photo ID prior to voting in person early or on Election Day. Acceptable forms of ID would include: an Illinois driver's license or state ID, a disabled person's ID, a senior citizen ID, a firearm owner's ID, a U.S. passport with the voter's current address, or any other government-issued ID with the voter's name, address, and photo. Voters who are indigent and unable to obtain a voter ID without paying a fee and voters with a religious objection to being photographed may vote a provisional ballot and sign an affidavit stating their exemption.
  • Require voters who do not present acceptable photo ID to vote a provisional ballot. The voter would need to provide an acceptable form of ID to election officials within 10 calendar days of casting the provisional ballot.
  • Require the secretary of state to issue a voter identification card to registered voters who do not have one of the acceptable forms of ID. The voter identification card would contain the voter's name, signature, and photo. The voter would need to provide one of a list of documents to establish their eligibility for the card.
  • Require mail ballot applications to be submitted no later than 30 days before an election. Under existing law, these applications may be submitted as late as 5 days before the election. The bill would also require mail ballot applicants to attest that they will not be physically present in the county on the day of the election, which is not required under current law.
  • Eliminate the permanent mail voting list.
  • Prohibit the use of drop boxes for mail ballots.
  • Generally require mail ballots to be returned by the 7th day after the election to be counted, instead of the 14th day as under existing law. UOCAVA voters would be exempted from this change.
  • Create a felony criminal offense for any person who gathers more than 3 mail ballots on behalf of another person and submits them to an election authority during a single election, other than a person acting in their professional scope as a courier. A person convicted of this offense would be ineligible for public employment for a 5-year period after the conviction.
  • End the voter registration "grace period," under which same-day registration is available, on the 7th day before the election. Under existing law, the grace period extends through the day of the election.
  • Prohibit a polling place from being located in a residential building.
  • Require at least one vote center to be located in a municipality with a population of 5,000 or less. Under existing law, election authorities only need to establish a single vote center, in their greatest population center.
  • Ensure that only persons acting on behalf of an election authority may print or distribute ballots.
  • Require provisional ballots to be counted no later than 7 days after the election. Existing law provides for this to be completed within 14 days of the election.
  • Specify that the distribution of food or drinks to people waiting in line to vote constitutes prohibited electioneering activity.

Last Updated March 10, 2025

If that were to occur, the election authority and school district would cooperate to find a satisfactory alternative location for the polling place. If an agreement cannot be reached, the authority and district would submit a report to the board of county commissioners, which would then be responsible for determining a location for the polling place that will meet the needs of the voters.

data.author: Villivalam (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would change the automatic voter registration process at state agencies. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change the format of automatic voter registration from "front-end AVR" to "back-end AVR." Instead of being notified of the opportunity to opt out of registration at the time of an agency transaction, the bill would instead require a notice to be sent to the voter within five business days. The voter would then have 23 days to return the notice and decline to be registered.
  • Allow documents that demonstrate a person's citizenship and voting age to be used to support a dual-purpose application (one that serves as either an application to register to vote or an application to change the voter's registered residential address or name). Under existing law, only identification documents meeting REAL ID standards may serve as the basis for a dual-purpose application. This bill would also allow documents that are silent as to citizenship to serve as the basis for a dual-purpose application. The bill makes clear that an identity document that affirmatively shows a person's noncitizen status cannot be the basis for a voter registration application.
  • Modify the dual-purpose application to permit an applicant to indicate their preferred language. The bill would also ensure that persons completing a dual-purpose application have access to materials in the five most common non-English languages spoken in the state.
  • Remove a requirement that the state board of elections make an official determination that a state or federal agency has access to reliable personal information before entering into an agreement to make the agency an automatic voter registration agency.
  • Specify that an applicant for a drivers license or state ID card who provides a substitute mailing address under the states confidentiality program for certain crime victims, or who provides a work address due to the persons status as a peace officer or judicial officer, shall not be offered automatic voter registration services. The state board of elections would be permitted to provide alternative voter registration services to such persons.


Last Updated February 12, 2025

data.author: Morrison (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would permit the establishment of additional vote centers - locations in a jurisdiction where anyone residing in the jurisdiction may vote, regardless of precinct - during the early voting period and on election day. An authority that established additional vote centers in compliance with the bill would need to do so according to the following rule set:

  • For counties with at 250,000 registered voters:
    • from the 15th day before election day to the 5th day before election day, at least one vote center for each 75,000 registered voters;
    • from the 4th day before election day to the day before election day, at least one vote center for each 20,000 registered voters; and
    • on election day, at least one vote center for each 12,500 registered voters
  • For counties with at least 37,500 registered voters but fewer than 250,000 registered voters:
    • from the 15th day before election day to the 5th day before election day, at least one vote center for each 75,000 registered voters, except that there must be at least one vote center in each county;
    • from the 4th day before election day to the day before election day, at least one vote center for each 20,000 registered voters; and
    • on election day, at least one vote center for each 12,500 registered voters
  • For counties with at least 12,500 registered voters but fewer than 37,500 registered voters at least one vote center during early voting and at least 3 on election day
  • For counties with fewer than 12,500 registered voters, at least one vote center during early voting and on election day.


Last Updated February 7, 2025

data.author: Collins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would restore voting rights after conviction for a criminal offense within 14 days and require facilitation of registration and mail voting during incarceration, and would streamline the mail ballot request process during registration for all voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Restore voting rights to an individual convicted of a felony, or otherwise under sentence in a correctional institution or jail, 14 days following their conviction. The bill would expressly apply the restoration provision to all individuals under any form of state supervision or custody, including those owing court fines or fees, and expressly prohibit the denial of voting rights due to prior criminal conviction. The bill would also apply retroactively to those convicted of a felony and incarcerated at the time the bill takes effect, restoring their voting rights 14 days after January 1, 2026. Existing constitutional law mandates disenfranchisement upon conviction of a felony, and under existing statutory law, voting rights are restored upon completion of a sentence of incarceration.
  • Require local election authorities to coordinate with correctional agencies to facilitate voting by mail from each correctional institution in the state. Each correctional institution would be required to provide voter registration applications, applications to vote by mail, ballots received from election authorities, and all other election materials, including voter education materials. The bill would also require distribution of voting related materials upon release from custody.
  • Establish a confined voter's residence for purposes of voting as their last place of residence before confinement.
  • Permit the attorney general or an individual or organization aggrieved by a violation of an incarcerated person's voting rights, to bring a civil action to enforce them, including recovery of attorney's fees.
  • Provide generally that an individual completing a voter registration application be informed of the option to vote by mail, and allow that individual to request that their voter registration application be treated as a simultaneous application for a ballot to be voted by mail. The bill would also require provision of registration applications and mail ballots in non-English languages, in locations covered by federal or local language access requirements.


Last Updated February 14, 2025

This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would transfer administrative duties of mail voting from each election authority to the state board of elections. Specifically the bill would:

  • Transfer administrative duties of mail voting from each election authority to the state board of elections. Before early voting in each election, the state board would be responsible for sending each election authority a list of the voters to whom the board provided a mail ballot.

This bill, as introduced on February 7, 2025, would require the state board of elections to conduct oversight on any private, nongovernmental funds used for the purpose of conducting elections. Under the bill, an election authority would no longer be permitted to apply for such funding. Instead, the bill would place the state board of elections in charge of regulation and oversight of such funds; only the board would be permitted to apply for such funds. When acquired, the funds would go into a dedicated account, over which the board would have authority to disburse funds to election authorities for the purposes of conducting elections. Election authorities may apply to the board for a proportional share of funds not disbursed.

data.author: Turner S (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 7, 2025, would make a number of minor adjustments to Illinois election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change the timing of required updates to the registrations of voters who move within the state. Under existing law, election authorities are required to update voter registrations when voters move within the state, but the statute does not provide a time frame by which this task must be completed. Under the bill, the authority would be required to make the change within 90 days of receiving an update about a voter's address from the national change of address database.
  • Change the timing of the voter verification. Under existing law, the county clerk or board of election commissioners is required, at least once every two years, to conduct a verification of voter registrations and cancel registration of people who have ceased to be qualified voters. Under the bill, this process would be required to be conducted "after a consolidated election in an odd-numbered year but before the first day of candidate circulation for candidate filing for the following primary election in an even-numbered year." The clerk or board would also be required to certify to the state board of elections that the verification has been completed; existing law requires only "submit[ting]" this information.
  • Require judges to sign an affidavit explaining the circumstances if a tally sheet is delivered to the county clerk's office more than 5 hours after the polls close.
  • Require an audit to be conducted of mail ballots received after election day.
  • Require the state board of elections to develop training materials for election judges. Training would be required to include, at a minimum, training on the duties concerning voter verification, campaign-free zones, electioneering, mail ballot processing, provisional voting, and ballot handling and processing.
  • Require information of vote-by-mail procedures to be accessible via a public website.

Last Updated February 12, 2025

  • Prohibit any election judge, watcher, or other person present at a polling place from engaging in any activity that is intended to intimidate a voter.

data.author: Sweet (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 5, 2026, would impose numerous substantial changes on Indiana elections that would limit voter access and threaten voter privacy. The bill would:

  • Prohibit in-person early voting. Existing law allows up to 28 days of early voting (in-person absentee voting).
  • Limit the availability of absentee voting to members of the military, voters with disabilities, voters working the polls on Election Day, UOCAVA voters, and voters age 65 or older. Existing law provides several other reasons for absentee voting, including absence from the precinct on Election Day, confinement due to illness or injury, work schedule, religious observance, and unavailability of transportation to the polls.
  • Require absentee voters to include a copy of their photo ID and the last four digits of their Social Security number with their returned ballot for verification purposes. Existing law requires election officials to compare the ballot signature with the voter's signature on file for verification.
  • Move the absentee ballot return deadline to noon on Election Day. Existing law allows voters to return ballots until 6 P.M. on Election Day.
  • Require all elections to be conducted on paper ballots designed and printed in Indiana. The ballots must include a watermark and a unique serial number. Polling locations must have one accessible voting system to comply with the requirements of HAVA.
  • Require election officials to count the paper ballots by hand beginning at 7 A.M. on Election Day.
  • Require election officials to open and count mail-in ballots at precinct locations beginning at noon on Election Day. Under existing law, mail-in ballots may be verified by county officials on receipt.
  • Require ballot counting at precinct locations to be recorded and live streamed. Voters at polling locations must place completed ballots in a transparent ballot box.
  • Require each precinct worker to affirm the final vote totals, be present for the calling in of results to the county board, and receive a copy of the final vote totals. The county board must announce election results on the night of the election.
  • Require the use of paper pollbooks
  • Deem voted ballots as not confidential records. Election officials must maintain election records at secure county-owned facilities. Only the county clerk and a member of the opposing political party may have access to the stored records.
  • Allow all Indiana citizens to inspect and copy voter lists, pollbook signatures, voter histories, absentee voting applications and signatures, chain of custody records, and ballots (except those belonging to individuals in the address confidentiality program).
  • Require the Secretary of State to select randomly 10 counties for a post-election hand recount following each election.
  • Require the Secretary of State to maintain a statewide public voter database. The Secretary must reconcile the list monthly with various named databases and any other database the Secretary "deems useful."
  • Prohibit vote centers and internet voting.
  • Limit absentee ballot return to the voter or an immediate family member. Existing law also allows a voter to name an attorney-in-fact for ballot return.
  • Require voter registration applications to include an undefined "proof of citizenship" and the voter's signature.
  • Prohibit third party organizations from conduction voter registration activities.
  • Prohibit staffed and unstaffed drop boxes. Existing law allows staffed drop boxes.
  • Prohibit the use of digital ID for voter ID purposes.
  • Prohibit anyone from sending a voter an absentee ballot application unless the voter requests one.
  • Create a Class A misdemeanor for any violation of the provisions of the bill. Additionally, any person convicted of a violation would be ineligible to register or vote for 10 years following conviction.
  • Require the Attorney General to create an "Elections Crime Unit" to investigate allegations of election law violations. The Unit would have jurisdiction throughout Indiana, but would not limit the jurisdiction of local officials to investigate and prosecute election law violations. The Unit would administer an election fraud hotline and would submit an annual report to the legislature.

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 141

data.author: House Elections Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 9, 2026, makes various changes to voter list maintenance and removal practices and restricts online voter registration by third parties. The bill:

  • Adds new data sources and processes for removing noncitizens:
    • Requires the secretary of state to compare the voter registration system against the systematic alien verification for entitlements (SAVE) database twice a year. One check must occur at least 180 days before a regularly scheduled federal election. 
      • The secretary of state must report potential noncitizen registrations identified to county election officials for immediate review.
      • County election officials must notify the voter, place them in a challenged or pending verification status until they confirm their citizenship, and remove any voters confirmed ineligible.
    • Requires the secretary of state to submit an annual report to the legislature of the number of records checked against the SAVE database, the number of voters flagged as potential non-citizens, the number of voters flagged as potentially deceased, and the number of voters ultimately removed from the registration database that were first identified as potentially ineligible in the SAVE system.
    • Requires state agencies that provide public assistance benefits and are designated as voter registration agencies under the National Voter Registration Act to prepare a quarterly report for the secretary of state of the names and personal information of noncitizens receiving public assistance.
  • Adds new data sources and processes for removing voters who have changed their address to another jurisdiction:
    • Requires the division of motor vehicles to provide the secretary of state with a quarterly list of residents who have received a licenses from another jurisdiction.
      • The secretary must compare these lists to the statewide voter registration system and, on finding a matching registration, direct the county election official to remove them within five business days.
      • The county election official must notify removed persons they may have their registration reinstated by providing proof of residency in Kansas.
    • Adds the division of motor vehicles as a source of change of address data triggering a confirmation notice from a county election official.
    • Authorizes the secretary of state to obtain change of address information from federal agencies and provide it to local election officials.
  • Adds new data sources and processes for removing deceased voters:
    • Requires the secretary of state to request a list of deceased residents from the Social Security Administration at least once a year.
    • Adds family notarized attestations of the death as a valid reason for removal.
    • Specifies that voters appearing in a list of deceased persons provided by the Social Security Administration must have their death verified by other reliable means before removal.
  • Change processes for removing convicted felons:
    • Extends the secretary of state's deadline for notifying county election officials of a felony conviction in a US District Court from five days after notice to five business days.
    • Creates a deadline of five business days after notice for local election officials to remove convicted felons from registration records. Under prior law, removal is required but no deadline is specified.
  • Requires the secretary of state to provide appropriate county election officials with the results of any duplicate voter registration checks twice a year.
  • Allows the secretary of state to obtain comparisons of voter registration records from other jurisdiction and provide them to county election officials.
  • Prohibits individuals or non-goverment third-party groups from operating websites that accept and transmit voter registration applications unless approved by the secretary of state. To qualify for approval, websites must meet specified technological, security, and privacy requirements. A violation of this prohibition is a misdemeanor offense.

Last Updated April 13, 2026
