data.author: House Elections Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 17, 2026, would change multiple aspects of the voting process, including mail voting, early voting, and voter registration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Modify the mail voting (“advance voting”) process. The bill would:
    • Move the deadline for submitting an application for a mail ballot earlier, from seven days before Election Day to 14 days before.
    • Move the start date for officials to issue mail ballots earlier, from 20 days before Election Day to 22 days before.
    • Move the deadline for mail ballots to be received, from 7 p.m. on Election Day to the close of polls on Election Day. Under existing law, polls must close by 7 p.m. but may remain open until 8 p.m. under certain conditions. The bill would also delete the provision allowing the secretary of state to adopt rules and regulations to implement this section.
  • Restrict the in-person early voting period. The bill would:
    • Move the last day of early voting earlier, from noon on the day before Election Day to the Friday before. The bill would provide an exception for counties that opt in to holding weekend early voting.
    • Expand optional weekend early voting hours to no later than noon on the Sunday before the election. Under existing law, counties can hold optional early voting only on the Saturday preceding the election. The bill would also require county election offices holding weekend early voting to publish notice of voting times on the county website beginning at least three weeks prior to the election.
    • Move the deadline for new voter registrations earlier, from the 21st day before Election Day to the 25th day.

A provision from a previous version of the bill was amended out of the current text. It would have moved the deadline for new voter registrations earlier, from the 21st day before Election Day to the 23rd day.

Last Updated March 10, 2026

status_actions.summary: Vetoed by GOVERNOR

data.author: House Elections Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as vetoed on April 8, 2026, modifies mail ballot signature verification requirements, establishes a trigger law to restrict mail and early voting access if those requirements are enjoined by a court, and creates a new venue requirement for election litigation. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit a third party to sign a mail ballot envelope if the voter cannot provide a signature consistent with their signature on record in the voter registration database. Under existing law, this is only permitted for voters who cannot physically sign their envelope.
  • Trigger restrictions on mail and early voting eligibility if the state's mail ballot signature verification requirement is enjoined by a court order or judgment.
    • Require the secretary of state to monitor relevant litigation and publish a notice in the state register in the event of such an order or judgment to implement new restrictions. The notice must be published in the next scheduled publication of the register or, if the judgement is issued on or after 45 days before an election, the first register following the certification of results.
  • Limit eligibility for mail and early voting ("advance voting") to voters who cannot vote in person on election day due to:
      • Absence from their county of residence
      • Illness, disability, or religious belief or practice
      • Service as an election judge or clerk
    • Absence as a uniformed or overseas voter

Under existing law, no excuse is required to apply for a mail ballot or vote in person during early voting.

  • Require any legal challenges to state election statutes, regulations, or rules to be brought in the Shawnee County District Court, and require that any action brought in another court be transferred there. Existing law has no comparable provision.

Last Updated April 14, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTIONS, CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENTS AND INTERGOVERNMENTAL AFFAIRS

data.author: Brown (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would propose amending the state constitution to:

  • Establish automatic restoration of voting rights for individuals disenfranchised due to felony convictions after completion of any term of imprisonment, probation, or parole. Under existing constitutional law, the only path to rights restoration is gubernatorial action. An existing executive order has established a practice of automatically restoring the voting rights of individuals convicted of most non-violent crimes following completion of the terms of their sentences.
  • Establish election fraud, alongside treason and bribery, as a permanently disenfranchising conviction.
  • End the ability of the legislature to designate any "high misdemeanors" as disenfranchising convictions.
  • Modernize language regarding the disenfranchisement of individuals adjudicated by a court to be mentally incompetent.
  • Automatically restore civil rights five years after completion of any term of imprisonment, probation, or parole.

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires passage by the current legislature and approval by voters.

Last Updated January 8, 2026

status_actions.summary: Act No. 175

data.author: Decker (R)
data.public_commentary:
The legislature overrode Governor Andy Beshear's veto of this bill. This bill, as enacted on April 15, 2026:

  • Authorizes the State Board of Elections to enter into agreements with federal agencies to identify registered voters who are deceased or not U.S. citizens. Under the bill, these agreements would have been governed by a written memorandum and allowed the state to provide federal agencies a voter’s name, date of birth, and last four digits of their Social Security number only. Prior law did not expressly authorize this type of federal data-sharing for citizenship verification.
  • Requires the state board to mark any voter identified as a noncitizen in the statewide voter registration system and notify the individual by mail, including steps required for an individual to cast a ballot again. Prior law did not require this type of designation or notification based on federal identification.
  • Prohibits individuals flagged as noncitizens from voting unless they verify their citizenship by presenting to the county clerk specified documents, such as a birth certificate, U.S. passport, naturalization certificate, or certificate of citizenship. Under the bill, if an individual provides acceptable documentation, the county clerk must update the voter registration system and notify the state board to remove the noncitizen designation.
  • Requires individuals identified as noncitizens to vote only by provisional ballot and only if they attest under penalty of perjury that they are U.S. citizens.
  • Requires removal from voter rolls if not verified. The bill requires that individuals flagged as noncitizens be removed from the voter rolls if they did not verify their citizenship by the day after the second federal general election following their identification.
  • Requires the state board to refer all individuals identified as noncitizens to the attorney general’s election investigations unit, and to notify that unit if citizenship is later verified.
  • Prohibits officials from purchasing a voting system or voting equipment that used nonhuman readable codes, including but not limited to barcodes, QR codes, or other encrypted markings, to represent or tabulate a voter's choices. The bill specifies that officials may continue to use equipment that was approved for use by the State Board of Elections as of the effective date of this section of this Act until a replacement voting system was acquired by the county.
  • Eliminates alternative forms of ID that voter's with qualified impediments to attaining a photo ID could present, including:
    • Social Security card
    • Food stamp identification card, electronic benefit transfer card, or supplemental nutrition assistance card, that is issued by this state and has the name of the voter stated

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates, school district boundaries, and write-in votes.

Last Updated April 17, 2026

data.author: Johnson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 5, 2026, would:

  • Require the Administrative Office of the Courts to send to the State Board of Elections by July 24, 2026, an all-time list of individuals convicted of a felony who are currently ineligible to vote so that the state board can remove from their names from the voter list by August 4, 2026.
  • Authorize the state board to enter agreements with agencies of the federal government to identify individuals who are are registered to vote but are not U.S. citizens. Under the bill, any agreement must be memorialized in a written memorandum of understanding and may only provide the federal agency with a limited voter list containing the voter’s name, date of birth, and last four digits of the Social Security number, if available.
  • Prohibit a voter flagged as a noncitizen from casting a ballot unless their U.S. citizenship is verfied. A voter may verify citizenship by presenting specified documentation, including:
    • U.S. birth certificate meeting specified requirement
    • Valid U.S. passport
    • Consular Report of Birth Abroad or Certification of Birth
    • Certificate of Naturalization
    • Certificate of Citizenship
  • Specify that if an individual identified as a noncitizen presents proof of citizenship the county clerk must make a copy of the documentation and immediately advise the State Board of Elections of the documentation. The state board must then remove the mark from the voter's registration that indicated they were a noncitizen.
  • Permit a voter flagged as a noncitizen who appears at a polling place to cast a provisional ballot only after attesting under penalty of perjury that they are a U.S. citizen.
  • Require the State Board of Elections to refer the registrations of individuals identified as noncitizens to the attorney general’s Election Investigations and Security Unit and require removal of the voter from the registration list if the individual does not verify citizenship before the day after the second federal general election following the identification.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to special candidates, political committees, campaign finance, and contesting elections.

Last Updated March 11, 2026

data.author: Bauman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 3, 2026, would propose amending the state constitution to:

  • Establish automatic restoration of voting rights for individuals disenfranchised due to certain felony convictions after completion of any term of imprisonment, probation, or parole, and any court-ordered monetary conditions. Under existing constitutional law, the only path to rights restoration is gubernatorial action. An existing executive order has established a practice of automatically restoring the voting rights of individuals convicted of most non-violent crimes following completion of the terms of their sentences.
  • Establish election fraud, a violent felony offense, a felony sexual offense, or a criminal offense against a child, alongside treason and bribery, as a permanently disenfranchising conviction.
  • End the ability of the legislature to designate any "high misdemeanors" as disenfranchising convictions.
  • Specify that individuals who have had their civil rights restored and are subsequently convicted of another felony would not have their rights automatically restored again until completion of the sentence for the subsequent offense, unless restored earlier by executive pardon.
  • Modernize language regarding the disenfranchisement of individuals adjudicated by a court to be mentally incompetent.

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires passage by the current legislature and approval by voters.

  • Establish automatic restoration of voting rights for individuals disenfranchised due to certain felony convictions after completion of any term of imprisonment, probation, or parole (exclusive of any monetary conditions). Under existing constitutional law, the only path to rights restoration is gubernatorial action. An existing executive order has established a practice of automatically restoring the voting rights of individuals convicted of most non-violent crimes following completion of the terms of their sentences.
  • Establish election fraud, a violent felony offense, a felony sexual offense, or a criminal offense against a child, alongside treason and bribery, as a permanently disenfranchising conviction.
  • End the ability of the legislature to designate any "high misdemeanors" as disenfranchising convictions.
  • Modernize language regarding the disenfranchisement of individuals adjudicated by a court to be mentally incompetent.

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires passage by the current legislature and approval by voters.

Last Updated February 20, 2026

This bill, as amended on March 26, 2026, would require the secretary of state to submit voter registration data to the federal government. Specifically, the bill would:

If SAVE identifies a registrant as a potential noncitizen, the secretary would then be required to submit that registrant to the division of election integrity for investigation.

status_actions.summary: Act No. 7

data.author: Beaullieu (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as signed by Governor Jeff Landry on May 14, 2026, makes several changes to voter registration, mail voting, and election administration more generally. Specifically, this bill makes the following changes:

  • Regarding voter registration, the bill:
    • Allows a registered voter who is no longer capable of signing their name or making a mark due to a physical disability, to authorize another person to sign documents on that voter's behalf.
    • Removes the requirement for a voter who was previously registered in a different parish to surrender that notice of registration (if available) when registering in a new parish. Requires the previous parish registrar to cancel the voter's previous registration.
    • Requires that a change of registration based on a change of residence within a parish that is received after the closing of registration for a closed primary election goes into effect the day after the second party primary. Current law is silent on this specific situation.
    • Requires that a change of registration based on a change of residence within a parish -- and when the change of residence does not change any issues or offices upon which the voter can vote -- can go into effect for a special primary election or general election only if the change was made before the close of registration for the general election. Under existing law, the registration change does not need to be made before the closing of the registration period.
    • Allows a law enforcement officer whose address is confidential to submit written notice to the registrar of voters that their name no longer needs to be kept confidential. Current law is silent on this process.
    • Provides that the Department of State does not need to conduct a canvass of registered voters in a calendar year when there is less than sixty days between an election and the opening of qualifying for a regularly scheduled election. Under existing law, the Department of State is required to canvass the registered voters twice a year.
    • Removes the requirement that address information used to prevent a person from being placed on the inactive voter list must come from the United States Postal Service.
    • Changes the address confirmation notice to an address confirmation form and make conforming changes throughout.
    • Provides that if a voter on the inactive list applies to vote by mail, that the registrar must transfer the voter's name to the official list and update their registration record. Existing law makes the same provision for voters on the inactive list who vote by mail or during early voting.
    • Changes the limitation on canceling voter registrations between any primary and the subsequent general election to between any open primary and subsequent general election. The bill would also add voter ineligibility (except for change of residence) to the list of exceptions to this prohibition.
    • Allows the voter information of a person who is 17 years old to appear on a precinct register when that person will be 18 years old within seven days before an election. Under existing law, voter information of a person who is 16 or 17 years old is prohibited from disclosure, including on a precinct registrar.
  • Regarding election administration, the bill:
    • Removes the requirement that the parish board of election supervisors must meet at 10 a.m. on the fifth day after an election to count absentee and early ballots. The bill would allow the board to meet anytime on those days.
    • Changes voter assistance procedures so that only the person assisting the voter -- and not the voter -- must complete the voter assistance form.
    • Changes the number of compiled statements of election returns that the parish board of supervisors must prepare from two to one and require that an electronic copy be transmitted to the secretary of state.
    • Requires the parish clerk and registrar to confirm the feasibility of precinct boundary changes in writing and for the parish governing authority to submit that confirmation to the secretary of state.
    • Requires an early voting commissioner to be a qualified voter and to meet the same qualifications as an Election Day commissioner. Under existing law, early voting commissioners are not explicitly required to be qualified voters.
    • Changes the deadline by which a list of early voting watchers must be filed with the registrar from the fourteenth business day before the first day of early voting to the tenth business day before the first day of early voting.
    • Allows the secretary of state to contract for the delivery of voting machines and equipment without reference to a specific method of procurement. Existing law stipulates a specific method of procurement.
    • Changes the end of the time interval in the Election Code that ends on a Sunday or other legal holiday from noon of the next legal day to 4:30 p.m. of the next legal day.
  • Regarding mail and early voting, the bill:
    • Expands eligibility for mail (absentee) voting to employees of the Department of State and employees of a registrar of voters.
    • Requires mail voters to completely fill in the oval of each selection on their ballot and provide that selecting more than the number of candidates for an office or making selections for and against the same candidate will render the selections for that office void. Existing law is silent on this issue.
    • Provides that if the time selected by the parish board of election supervisors to count mail and early ballots is after 4 p.m. on Election Day, that the start time must be submitted to the commissioner of elections for approval by the fourth day before Election Day. Existing law does not require the board to get approval.
    • Changes ballot change procedures. Under existing law, challenges to mail or early ballots may be filed no later than the fourth day before Election Day. The bill limits that to only mail ballots and instead provides that early ballots may be challenged prior to the voter casting a ballot, in which case the voter must vote on a paper ballot rather than a voting machine.
    • Requires the secretary of state to promulgate rules regarding the rejection of mail or early ballots with distinguishing marks. Existing law provides for which ballots with distinguishing marks should be rejected, but does not explicitly direct the secretary of state to adopt rules for implementation.
    • Alters the ballot curing process to stipulate that the failure of a witness to provide their printed name or address on the ballot certificate is not a deficiency requiring cure.
    • Extends the nursing home early voting program by moving back the end date from seven days before the election to four days before the election.
  • Regarding election contests, the bill:
    • Authorizes a qualified elector to challenge the placement of a constitutional amendment on the ballot. Existing law allows challenges to candidacy, election results, recall petitions, and the calling of special elections to fill vacancies. Clarifies that the secretary of state is a proper party in an objection to the placement of a constitutional amendment on the ballot.
    • Provides the first day that actions contesting an election, candidacy, recall petition and more can be filed. Existing law provides deadlines by which actions must be filed, but is silent as to when actions may begin to be filed. Specifically, actions contesting an election can begin no earlier than the day after the election; actions contesting the calling of a special election can begin no earlier than the date the election is called, and actions objecting to the placement of a constitutional amendment on the ballot can begin no earlier than the date of the final passage of the join resolution calling the election and no later than 4:30 p.m. of the 30th day after the final passage.

This bill also contains provisions related to local election officials, candidates, and party primaries that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated May 21, 2026

status_actions.summary: Act No. 5

data.author: Pressly (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on June 2, 2026, would change the state's voter ID requirements. Specifically, this bill:

  • Repeals the option for voters to sign an affidavit if they do not have photo ID.
  • Creates a new process for voters who do not have photo ID to provide two different documents -- either two primary documents or one primary and one secondary document -- in order to vote. Under this bill, if a voter cannot provide sufficient primary and/or secondary documentation, then they can go to the office of the registrar of voters in their parish and cast a conditional (provisional) ballot. Voters who cast conditional ballots must provide proper identification to the registrar of voters by 4:30 p.m. on the second day after the election for their ballot to be counted.
  • Changes the list of acceptable photo IDs to remove "other generally recognized picture identification card that contains the name and signature of the applicant" and add U.S. passport book, U.S. certificate of citizenship, U.S. certificate of naturalization, and a tribal ID card, provided that they contain the voter's name, picture, and signature.
  • Creates a list of primary documents for voter identification, which includes a domestic birth certificate, birth registration card, certificate of birth abroad, certificate of naturalization, certificate of citizenship, social security card, U.S. passport card, tribal ID card without a photo or signature, and U.S. veteran health ID card.
  • Creates a list of secondary documents for voter identification, which includes a voter registration notice, current utility bill, bank statement, government check or document, or paycheck or paystub.
  • Directs the secretary of state to promulgate rules and guidance to implement the bill.
  • Makes conforming changes.

Last Updated June 11, 2026

data.author: Uyterhoeven (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 3, 2025, proposes an amendment to the state constitution to end all disenfranchisement due to criminal convictions. Under existing law, citizens lose their right to vote while incarcerated for a felony conviction and have their voting rights restored automatically upon release.

In order to be adopted, this measure would have to pass the legislature in both this and the subsequent legislative session and then be approved by the voters at the next general election.

Last Updated March 26, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Arriaga (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Repeal the requirement that a voter be removed from the voter list if they fail to respond to the annual census for two consecutive years and would instead provide that an individual who fails to respond even once may receive a fine.
  • Repeal the requirement that a voter be moved from the active voter list to the inactive voter list if they are not entered in the annual register for two consecutive years and fail to vote in any election during that time. Instead, the bill would require the registrar to move a voter to the inactive voter list if the registrar receives information that the voter no longer lives in the city or town from permanent change-of-address information supplied by the U.S. Postal Service or the Electronic Registration Information Center.
  • Require the registrar to update a voter's address in the register upon receiving information that the voter has moved and would require the registrar to notify the voter in writing. Existing law does not require the registrar to update a voter's registration unless notified by the voter that they have moved.
  • Eliminate the requirement that the registrar send notice to voters moved to the inactive list before the first Monday of June in each year. While the bill would still require the registrar to send these notices, there is no timeframe specified.
  • Permit voters on the inactive list to vote a regular ballot upon their written affirmation of their continued residence in the city or town.
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar make all inquiries and investigations necessary to identify every individual listed on the annual register.
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar prepare a list of the names of voters not entered on the annual register that is available for public inspection and may be posted in the newspaper. 
  • Require that the registrar only remove a voter from the register if: 
    • The voter requests so in writing
    • The registrars receive notice from the city or town clerk or a death record from the department of public health indicating that the voter has died
  • The registrars receive official written notice that the voter is: 
    • incarcerated for a felony conviction
    • disqualified by law because of corrupt practices in respect to elections
    • under guardianship that prohibits voting
  • The registrars receive official written notice that the voter is registered to vote in another jurisdiction
  • The registrars determine that the voter is illegally or incorrectly registered after complaint, notice, and hearing as required under existing law
  • The registrars determine that the voter no longer resides in their city or town.
  • Under existing law, the registrar must remove a voter from the register if:
    • The registrar "objects to" that individual
    • The individual resides at a licensed inn, lodging house, or public lodging house 
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar send an annual notice on or before the first Monday of June to each voter of the preceding year whose name has not been entered in the annual register of the current year that notifying them that they may be removed from the voting list if they fail to respond to the notice and do not vote in the next two biennial state elections following the mailing of the notice.
  • Repeal the requirement that all voters make an affidavit of registration when registering to vote.

Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Donaghue (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025 would:

  • Require an agent of the secretary of state to inspect each polling place and voting site at least once every four years to ensure compliance with federal and state laws concerning accessibility for voters with disabilities.
  • Require the inspecting agent to promptly and specifically report in writing to the secretary of state and city or town clerk every failure to comply with state or federal law and what action must be taken to ensure access. The city or town clerk would be required to submit a written plan to comply with the secretary of state within five days. 
  • Allow the secretary of state to order a city or town to comply with the law if the city or town failed to take action to to comply with the law. 
  • Require the secretary of state to report in writing to the clerks of the senate and house of representatives on all activities under this bill no later than December 31 of each year.
  • Allow the attorney general to bring a civil action to enforce compliance with accessibility laws, a written plan, or with an order from the secretary of state.

data.bill_name: Regulation of Elections in Certain Emergency Situations

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5253

data.author: Donato (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Provide the secretary of the commonwealth with the authority to regulate elections in emergency situations, including when the governor declares a state of emergency or a federal agency declares a state of emergency. That authorization would include, but would not be limited to:
    • Postponing the date of any election for no more than 45 days. 
    • Providing for procedures for the orderly conduct of elections.
    • Suspending or postponing municipal elections for no more than 45 days after consultation with local election officials. 
  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to adopt by rule or regulation an elections contingency plan, which must contain goals and policies that give specific direction to state and local elections officials when an election has been suspended or delayed due to an emergency or alternate voting procedures are necessary. The plan must include, but is not limited to, the following concerns:
    • Providing a procedure for state and local elections officials to follow to ensure notice of the suspension or delay to the proper authorities, voters, the communications media, poll workers, and custodians of voting places.
    • Providing a procedure for the release and certification of election returns and documents relating to the election.

Last Updated April 21, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Garry (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would require a voter to provide an acceptable form of ID prior to voting in person. The voter would need to provide their voter registration card, Social Security card, valid Massachusetts driver's license, an ID card issued by the U.S. or Massachusetts or any of its subdivisions, or a valid photo employee ID card.

Last Updated April 16, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Gentile (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would create same-day registration on Election Day and during the early voting period. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow a voter to register at a polling place by presenting proof of residence and completing and signing an affidavit attesting to their eligibility. Under existing law, the deadline for voter registration is 10 days before an election, and no registration is permitted at a voting location. 
  • Specify accepted forms of proof of residence:
    • Valid photo ID including, but not limited to, a Massachusetts driver's license or state ID card
    • Copy of a current utility bill, bank statement, government check, residential lease agreement, telephone statement, paycheck, other government document, or a document from a post-secondary educational institution verifying a student's current address
  • Permit same-day registrants who do not present proof of residence to vote a provisional ballot, which would be counted if the individual presents proof of residence to the city or town clerk within two business days after a primary or within six days after a general election.
  • Expressly prohibit changing party enrollment at a primary through the same-day registration process. 
  • Expressly require that upon credible information or allegation of illegal voter registration or multiple voting, registrars carry out existing procedures for investigation. The bill would also require the attorney general or appropriate district attorney to investigate such information or allegations. 

Last Updated September 15, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Holmes R (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would: 

  • Designate a correctional facility as a designed voter agency under the National Voter Registration Act. 
  • Require correctional facilities to:
    • Provide mail ballot applications to eligible voters within the custody of the facility
    • Provide sufficient size envelopes and postage stamps upon request
    • Transmit completed mail ballot applications to the appropriate official and county upon request
    • Provide individuals being released with a voter registration form and a declination form, and offer that individual assistance in filling out the appropriate form. 
  • Allow incarcerated individuals to pre-register to vote in anticipation of discharge.
  • Require the superintendent or administrator of the state or county correctional facility notify an incarcerated individual prior to their release that their voting rights are being restored upon discharge.
  • Require the superintendent of the state correctional facility and each administrator of a county correctional facility to transmit two lists to the secretary of the commonwealth by the 15th day of each month. The first list must include information about individuals who have become ineligible to vote because of their incarceration and the second list must contain information about individuals who have become eligible to vote because of their discharge from incarceration.
  • Require the secretary of state to:
    • Ensure that all voters eligible to vote upon discharge are added to the statewide voter registration database
    • Ensure that individuals who have become eligible to vote upon discharge face no continued barriers to registration or voting resulting from their felony convictions
    • Ensure that registrants who submitted registration forms upon anticipation of release be pre-registered until finally discharged
    • Implement a program to educate attorneys, judges, election officials, superintendents and administrators of correctional facilities, correctional facility officials, parole and probation officers, and members of the public about disenfranchisement and restoration of voting rights. 

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated April 17, 2025

data.author: Meschino (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow 17-year-olds who are pre-registered to vote to vote in primary elections if they will be 18 by the corresponding general election. Under existing law, otherwise eligible citizens may pre-register to vote starting at age 16 but cannot vote in any election until they turn 18.

Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Pease (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would prohibit the secretary of the commonwealth from sending any additional mailings related to ballot requests for individual elections other than the one mailing sent to every registered voter before each statewide election during the calendar year unless mandated by federal law. Under existing law, there is no prohibition.

Last Updated March 14, 2025

data.bill_name: Early Voting and Vote By Mail

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Peisch (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow the mayor of a city, board of selectmen, or select board of a town to reduce or eliminate early voting if 25 percent or more of the votes cast in the prior state election in any municipality were by mail. 

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Ramos (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow same-day registration until 5 p.m. on Election Day and no later than 5 p.m. on the day of a special town meeting. Under existing law, registration ends at 5 p.m. on the 10th day before Election Day and no later than 5 p.m. on the 10th day before a special town meeting. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this provision.

Last Updated March 26, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Ramos (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would require that all voters be mailed an official voting ballot and "the accompanying papers" with prepaid postage and return postage guaranteed. The bill would also allow voters to request that their ballot be delivered to them by calling the office of the clerk, with the exception of voters at designated health care facilities who already have a designated process under law to have ballots delivered. Under existing law, Massachusetts does not have universal vote by mail.


Last Updated March 26, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Rogers D (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would end disenfranchisement due on felony convictions other than convictions for manslaughter; indecent assault and battery on a child under 14; attempted murder; or assault with intent to commit rape for which the individual may be sentenced to life in prison. Under existing law, all felony convictions are disenfranchising for the duration of incarceration.

Last Updated April 16, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Tyler (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would remove from statute provisions disenfranchising citizens incarcerated for felony convictions. This would not impact the state constitutional provision to this effect, which would remain in place unless the constitution is amended. A proposal to amend the state's constitution is also being considered by the legislature but would require approval from the voters.

Last Updated April 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Uyterhoeven (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would restore voting rights to individuals convicted of felony convictions. Specifically, the bill would repeal the provision listing "incarceration in a correctional facility due to a felony conviction" as a factor for being ineligible to vote. The bill would leave in place the existing requirement that individuals temporarily or permanently disqualified by law because of corrupt practices in respect to elections be ineligible to vote.

Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Vaughn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Require the registrars to provide the secretary of state with a copy of all voting lists and that those voting lists be public record. 
  • Require the secretary of state to respond to any request for regional or statewide voting lists and would require that those requests be governed by the state's public records laws. Under existing law, the secretary of state maintains a central registry of voters, containing various information about each voter. Under existing law, the names and addresses in the central registry are not a matter of public record but are available to state party committees, statewide candidate committees, state ballot question committees, the jury commissioner, adjutant general, and anyone else that the secretary of state designates under regulation.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Vitolo (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would require mail ballots to be sent or delivered to voters no more than 30 days before the date of the election. Under existing law, ballots are sent to voters as soon as materials are available.

Last Updated April 16, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5311

data.author: Whipps Lee (U)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow a voter to establish, change, or cancel their political party affiliation prior to 8 p.m. on the 20th day before a primary election by sending the board of registrars of voters a certificate signed by the voter under penalty of perjury or by appearing in front of the board personally. Under existing practice, voters can change their political party by re-registering to vote. Under existing law, the registration deadline is 5 p.m. on the 10th day before Election Day for applications submitted in person. Online applications must be submitted before 11:59 p.m. on that day and mailed registration forms must be postmarked by that day.

If a voter changes their affiliation after this time frame, it would not become effective until after the primary. Massachusetts has open primaries with optional partisan registration. Individuals who do not affiliate with a party can choose which ballot they want when voting.

Last Updated August 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order H5253

data.author: Arena-DeRosa (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 31, 2025, would:

  • Shorten the in-person early voting period for biennial general elections by moving the first day from the seventeenth day before an election to the tenth.
  • Move forward the deadline for voter registration by requiring that if the deadline fell on a Saturday, applications must be received by the preceding day. Under existing law, applications must be received by the preceding day if the deadline falls on a Sunday or holiday, but the deadline can be a Saturday.

Last Updated April 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Joint Committee on Election Laws
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on November 19, 2025, would create new requirements for polling places in order to ensure compliance with federal and state laws concerning accessibility for voters with disabilities. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require an agent of the secretary of state to inspect each polling place at least once every four years to evaluate compliance with federal and state accessibility standards and to report in writing any failures.
  • Require responsible town officials to take immediate action to ensure access for voters with disabilities upon receiving any report and would require them to submit a written compliance plan.
  • Require the secretary of state to promulgate regulations that define the minimum compliance standards for polling places and early voting sites.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide an annual report in writing to the state legislature regarding compliance and failures from the past year.
  • Allow the attorney general to bring a civil action in the superior court to enforce compliance with accessibility laws.


Last Updated November 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: Placed on file

data.author: Mariano (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2026, would establish same-day voter registration on Election Day. Individuals could register at their precinct during the hours of voting by presenting proof of residence and signing an attestation, then cast a regular ballot. Proof of residence could be any official document including the registrant's name and address. Voters without proof of residence would be allowed to cast a provisional ballot that would be counted if they provided proof of residence to the clerk within six business days of a general election or within two business days of a state primary or municipal election.

The bill would also establish that upon receiving credible information or allegation of illegal registration, or credible information or allegation of illegal multiple voting, the attorney general or proper district attorney must conduct an investigation.

status_actions.summary: See H5001

data.author: Mariano (D)
data.public_commentary:
This committee report from the legislature declined to advance an initiative petition that would have established same-day registration. Under the state’s initiative petition process, the legislature may approve or reject the petition as submitted, but may not amend it. The report explains that the petition would allow otherwise eligible voters to register and cast a ballot on the same day, including during early voting periods and on Election Day. Existing law does not currently provide same day registration.

Last Updated June 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: Placed on file

data.author: Miranda (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025, proposes an amendment to the Massachusetts Constitution to end all disenfranchisement based on criminal convictions. Under existing law, citizens convicted of felonies are disenfranchised while incarcerated and have their voting rights restored upon release from incarceration.

In order to go into effect, this bill must be passed by both chambers of the legislature in this legislative session and the following one then approved by a majority of the electorate in the next state election.

Last Updated February 12, 2025

data.bill_name: Election Day Registration at Polling Places

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Brady (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Create same-day registration. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Require individuals registering on Election Day to present proof of residence, which is defined as one of the following:
    • A valid photo ID, including but not limited to, a Massachusetts driver's license or other state-issued ID card
    • Other documentation that includes their name and the address where the applicant seeks to register, including, but not limited to a copy of a current utility bill, bank statement, government check, residential lease agreement, wireless telephone statement, paycheck, or other government document or correspondence, a current student fee statement, or other document from a post-secondary educational institution that verifies the student's current address. Under existing law, voters do not need to provide proof of residency. 
  • Require an individual registering on Election Day who cannot provide proof of residency to vote a provisional ballot and return within two business days of a state primary or municipal election or six days following a state election to present sufficient ID to the local election official.
  • Eliminate the requirement that for all elections held prior to June first in any year, an individual must be registered in the ward or precinct where they resided on January first of the previous year and instead would require that a voter be registered where they reside. 
  • Move up the registration deadline from 8 p.m. on the 10th day before an election to 5 p.m. that day.
  • Extend the amount of time individuals living in towns of 1,500 voters or less can register to vote on the last day of registration before an election by one hour. Under existing law, registration must be available from at least 9 a.m. until 11 a.m. and from 3 p.m. to 5 p.m. The bill would extend afternoon registration to start at 2 p.m. and end at 5 p.m.
  • Require the registry of motor vehicles and any other state agency permitted by law to provide data to the secretary of state for purposes of maintaining accurate and complete voter lists to provide to the Electronic Registration Information Center. Those agencies would be required to electronically transmit certain voter information at least every two months to the secretary of state. 
  • Require the creation of an advisory committee on the implementation of same-day registration to consider the costs and feasibility.
  • Require the attorney general or district attorney having jurisdiction to investigate allegations of illegal multiple motiving upon credible information or allegation. 

This bill's effective date would be July 1, 2025.


Last Updated April 15, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Collins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would eliminate the ability of individuals whose domicile is in Massachusetts but will be absent from the state on Election Day for reasons other than military service or incarceration to request an "absentee" ballot. However, under existing law that would remain unchanged, all voters are able to request and cast "early voting by mail" ballots without a qualifying excuse.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Creem (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Repeal the requirement that a voter be removed from the voter list if they fail to respond to the annual census for two consecutive years and would instead provide that an individual who fails to respond even once may receive a fine.
  • Repeal the requirement that a voter be moved from the active voter list to the inactive voter list if they are not entered in the annual register for two consecutive years and fail to vote in any election during that time. Instead, the bill would require the registrar to move a voter to the inactive voter list if the registrar receives information that the voter no longer lives in the city or town from permanent change-of-address information supplied by the U.S. Postal Service or the Electronic Registration Information Center.
  • Require the registrar to update a voter's address in the register upon receiving information that the voter has moved and would require the registrar to notify the voter in writing. Existing law does not require the registrar to update a voter's registration unless notified by the voter that they have moved.
  • Eliminate the requirement that the registrar send notice to voters moved to the inactive list before the first Monday of June in each year. While the bill would still require the registrar to send these notices, there is no timeframe specified.
  • Permit voters on the inactive list to vote a regular ballot upon their written affirmation of their continued residence in the city or town.
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar make all inquiries and investigations necessary to identify every individual listed on the annual register.
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar prepare a list of the names of voters not entered on the annual register that is available for public inspection and may be posted in the newspaper. 
  • Require that the registrar only remove a voter from the register if: 
    • The voter requests so in writing
    • The registrars receive notice from the city or town clerk or a death record from the department of public health indicating that the voter has died
    • The registrars receive official written notice that the voter is: 
      • incarcerated for a felony conviction
      • disqualified by law because of corrupt practices in respect to elections
      • under guardianship that prohibits voting
    • The registrars receive official written notice that the voter is registered to vote in another jurisdiction
    • The registrars determine that the voter is illegally or incorrectly registered after complaint, notice, and hearing as required under existing law
    • The registrars determine that the voter no longer resides in their city or town.
    • Under existing law, the registrar must remove a voter from the register if:
      • The registrar "objects to" that individual
      • The individual resides at a licensed inn, lodging house, or public lodging house 
  • Repeal the requirement that the registrar send an annual notice on or before the first Monday of June to each voter of the preceding year whose name has not been entered in the annual register of the current year that notifying them that they may be removed from the voting list if they fail to respond to the notice and do not vote in the next two biennial state elections following the mailing of the notice.
  • Repeal the requirement that all voters make an affidavit of registration when registering to vote.

Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Creem (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 10, 2025 would:

  • Require an agent of the secretary of state to inspect each polling place and voting site at least once every four years to ensure compliance with federal and state laws concerning accessibility for voters with disabilities.
  • Require the inspecting agent to promptly and specifically report in writing to the secretary of state and city or town clerk every failure to comply with state or federal law and what action must be taken to ensure access. The city or town clerk would be required to submit a written plan to comply with the secretary of state within five days. 
  • Allow the secretary of state to order a city or town to comply with the law if the city or town failed to take action to to comply with the law. 
  • Require the secretary of state to report in writing to the clerks of the senate and house of representatives on all activities under this bill no later than December 31 of each year.
  • Allow the attorney general to bring a civil action to enforce compliance with accessibility laws, a written plan, or with an order from the secretary of state. 


Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on WAYS AND MEANS

data.author: Creem (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would create same-day registration on Election Day and during the early voting period. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow a voter to register at a polling place by presenting proof of residence and completing and signing an affidavit attesting to their eligibility. Under existing law, the deadline for voter registration is 10 days before an election, and no registration is permitted at a voting location. 
  • Specify accepted forms of proof of residence:
    • Valid photo ID including, but not limited to, a Massachusetts driver's license or state ID card
    • Copy of a current utility bill, bank statement, government check, residential lease agreement, telephone statement, paycheck, other government document, or a document from a post-secondary educational institution verifying a student's current address
  • Permit same-day registrants who do not present proof of residence to vote a provisional ballot, which would be counted if the individual presents proof of residence to the city or town clerk within two business days after a primary or within six days after a general election.
  • Expressly prohibit changing party enrollment at a primary through the same-day registration process. 
  • Expressly require that upon credible information or allegation of illegal voter registration or multiple voting, registrars carry out existing procedures for investigation. The bill would also require the attorney general or appropriate district attorney to investigate such information or allegations.

Last Updated September 15, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied Study Order S2567

data.author: Durant (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Require the secretary of state to provide free photo ID cards to voters for voting purposes only. 
  • Create an ID requirement when voting. Under existing law, voters do not have to present ID when voting unless they registered to vote in the city or town by mail after January 1, 2003 and have not previously voted in an election for federal office. Specifically, the bill would require voters to present an ID containing the voter's name and photo that has not been expired for more than five years. Voters 65 years of age and older could use an ID that is expired by more than five years. The following forms of ID would be accepted: 
    • A Massachusetts driver's license
    • An ID card issued by any state
    • A U.S. armed services ID card
    • A U.S. passport or card
    • A voter ID card
    • A challenged voter affidavit. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Allow a voter to update their address while voting if the address is in the same town or ward. The ballot clerk would be required to record the change of address on the checklist and the supervisor of the checklist would be required to cause the voter registration database to reflect the change.
  • Require all voters to provide their address when voting. Under existing law, voters must provide their address "if requested."
  • Require the secretary of state to develop and make available an information pamphlet explaining the procedure for obtaining a free voter ID card.
  • Allow an individual who cannot provide a photo ID when voting to cast a provisional ballot.


Last Updated April 16, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on RULES

Under existing law, the board cannot have more than two members from any established political party or designation.

data.author: O'Connor P (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow individuals who have pre-registered to vote at 17 years of age to vote at any preliminary or primary election to be held if they would be 18 years at the time of the subsequent general election. status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on ELECTION LAWS: Accompanied by Bill S2936

data.author: Rausch (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would:

  • Push back the registration deadline from the 10th day before an election to the seventh day. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this change.
  • Create a permanent mail voting list. The bill would allow voters to sign up for the permanent mail voting list when registering to vote or through an online portal. Under existing law, voters can sign up to receive mail ballots for that calendar year only. Voters would be permitted to remove themselves from the list by filing a form with the secretary of state or by using the online portal. This bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this change.
  • Require voters who have been registered through automatic voter registration to receive a form to join the permanent mail voting list with postage prepaid.
  • Require the state secretary to mail all registered voters a form that they could fill out to join the permanent mail voter list no later than June 30, 2026. 
  • Move back the deadline by which mail ballots must be received from 5 p.m. on the third day after Election Day to 5 p.m. on the seventh day after Election Day.
  • Eliminate the process of election officials comparing the signature on a voter's ballot with the signature on their application and would instead require that election officials "examine the completion" of the mail ballot affidavit. The election official would be required to reject the ballot if the ballot was not complete and to notify the voter, as is required under existing law. Under existing law, the election official must reject the ballot if the ballot is improperly executed or if the signatures do not match.
  • Eliminate the requirement that a voter sign an external envelope when submitting a ballot during in-person early voting and would eliminate the requirement that an official examine a voter's ballot envelope prior to submission.
  • Move state primaries from the seventh Tuesday preceding biennial state elections and the fourth Tuesday preceding special state elections to the second Tuesday in June in even-numbered years and the fourth Tuesday preceding special state elections, except that special primaries for senators or representatives in congress would be held on the sixth Tuesday preceding the election.
  • Require the secretary of state to maintain a public facing website for the confirmation of receipt and acceptance of early voting ballots.
  • Require each municipality to maintain at least one secured municipal drop box in a public location that is accessible to voters for no less than 12 hours each day, including business hours. Under existing law, there is no requirement as to how many drop boxes must be available or where they must be located. The bill would require municipalities with more than 25,000 registered voters to maintain at least one drop box for each 25,000 registered voters. A municipality with fewer than 5,000 voters in excess of any 25,000 registered voter increment would be permitted to apply to the state secretary for a waiver of the requirement to maintain additional drop boxes.
  • Require that, to the extent possible, drop box locations be selected by:
    • Proximity to public transportation and availability of parking
    • Equitable distribution across population centers
    • Access for voters with disabilities
    • Use of public buildings
  • Require that drop boxes be maintained from the earliest date that ballots become available through the hour fixed for the closing of polls. 
  • Require that for a biennial state election and any city or town election held on that day, the following municipalities conduct in-person early voting for the following amounts of time:
    • Municipalities with less than 1,000 registered voters must offer no less than eight total hours, including at least two weekend hours. Under existing law, municipalities with less than 5,000 registered voters must offer no less than four hours each weekend with at least one day per weekend, and for no less than two hours on a weekend day in which voting is conducted.
    • Municipalities with at least 1,000 registered voters but no more than 5,000 registered voters must offer no less than 16 total hours, including at least three weekend hours.
    • Municipalities with at least 5,000 registered voters but no more than 25,000 registered voters must offer no less than 24 total hours, including at least four weekend hours. Under existing law, municipalities of this size must offer at least six hours each weekend, with at least one day per weekend, for not less than three hours on a weekend day in which voting is conducted.
    • Municipalities with at least 25,000 registered voters but no more than 40,000 registered voters must offer no less than 40 total hours, including at least eight weekend hours. Under existing law, municipalities of this size must offer no less than four hours each weekend day.
    • Municipalities with at least 40,000 registered voters but no more than 75,000 registered voters must offer no less than 40 total hours, including at least eight weekend hours. Under existing law, municipalities of this size must offer no less than six hours each weekend day.
    • Municipalities with at least 75,000 registered voters must offer at least 48 total hours, including at least 10 weekend hours. Under existing law, municipalities of this size must offer no less than eight hours each weekend day.
  • Require that for any presidential or state primary and any city or town election held on that day, or any primary or election to fill a vacancy for senator or representative in congress and any city or town election held on the same day, in-person early voting be conducted no more than 10 days and no less four days preceding the election as follows:
    • Municipalities with less than 1,000 registered voters must offer no less than four total hours, including at least one weekend hour. 
    • Municipalities with at least 1,000 but no more than 5,000 registered voters offer no less than eight total hours, including at least two weekend hours.
    • Municipalities with at least 5,000 but no more than 25,000 registered voters offer no less than 12 hours, including at least three weekend hours.
    • Municipalities with at least 25,000 but no more than 40,000 registered voters offer no less than 16 total hours, including at least four weekend hours.
    • Municipalities with at least 40,000 or more registered voters offer no less than 20 total hours, including at least five weekend hours. Under existing law, in-person early voting hours do not vary by election type.
  • Require that an early voting ballot cast by a voter who was eligible to vote at the time the ballot was cast must be counted even if that voter moved to a different residence within the commonwealth after.
  • Repeal the requirement that the state secretary deliver early voting ballots to each city and town no later than 30 days before an election and instead require that the secretary prepare early voting materials by that time.
  • Allow, rather than require, town officials to detail police officers to each building that contains a polling place for at least one precinct. Under existing law, this is mandatory.
  • Specify that voted early ballots may be tabulated upon receipt and that city and town clerks would be permitted to remove early voting mail ballots from their envelopes and deposit them into tabulators on any day leading up to the close of the voter registration period.
  • Require the state secretary to conduct comprehensive, culturally competent, and linguistically diverse voter education outreach both digitally, and in paper copy no later than May 1 of every even numbered year.
  • Require the state secretary to approve and implement upgrades to the central registry and voter registration system and to authorize the use of electronic poll books no later than December 31, 2025.
  • Require the creation of a special commission to examine the automatic voter registration processes of the commonwealth, including the processes implemented via the registry of motor vehicles. The commission would be required to issue a report on its findings about automatic voter registration process improvements no later than April 15, 2026.
  • Require the state secretary to create, implement and maintain a centralized digital system into which each city and town clerk must input preliminary results for each presidential or state primary or election. The results would be publicly available on the secretary's website.
  • Make changes to the information contained in the central registry of voters. The bill would require the registry to contain a voter's political party enrollment or designation and any former names and would repeal the requirement that the registry include the voter's occupation, nationality, or veteran status. The bill would also allow the registry to contain the following information about residents age 16 and over:
    • Full name
    • Former name, if any
    • Residential address on January 1 in the current year
    • Residential address on January 1 of the preceding year
    • Date of birth
    • Occupation
    • Veteran status
    • Nationality, if not a citizen of the United States
  • Make the information contained in the central registry available to certain state party committees and political groups at a fair and reasonable cost. The state secretary must also provide this information to election officials at each polling place in digital format. The names and addresses contained in the registry would not be a matter of public record.
  • Require the state secretary to maintain an agreement with the Electronic Registration Information Center, Inc. (ERIC). Massachusetts is already a member of ERIC. 
  • Require the state secretary, rather than the "appropriate local election official" to send mail ballots to voters as soon as they are ready.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated April 23, 2025

status_actions.summary: From JOINT Committee on JUDICIARY: Accompanied Study Order S2886

data.author: Tarr (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would allow administrative decisions made by the secretary of state or any administrative body to be appealed to the superior courts. Existing law has no such requirement. Under existing law, the supreme judicial and superior courts have jurisdiction to require the certification of any application for mail ballot that has been unreasonably rejected, to order the counting of any ballot improperly rejected, or to enjoin the counting of any ballot which cannot be identified as the ballot of a person who is lawfully registered to vote. Under existing law, no proceeding can be filed after the date by which the canvass for state offices must be complete.

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 428

data.author: Wilkins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 12, 2026:

  • Establishes a process to automatically activate the voter registration for individuals released from state correctional facilities. The Maryland State Board of Elections and the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services (the department) would work jointly to develop an electronic transmission system to facilitate the process. Prior existing law, which the bill leaves in place, voting rights were restored automatically when people are released from incarceration, but there was no system in place requiring coordination between the department and the state board.
  • Requires the department to send to the state board, on a monthly basis, a list of individuals released from incarceration at state correctional facilities.
  • Requires the state board to restore the individual's registration at their new residential address and assign the individual active voter status. Prior law required restoration of voting rights following incarceration but the law did not require automatic activation of the registration upon release.
  • Prohibits information regarding the fact that a voter was registered through the Department of Public Safety and Correctional Services from being disclosed to the public.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on RULES

data.author: Feldmark (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 9, 2026, would:

  • Create a misdemeanor offense for certain deceptive deepfakes in elections. The bill would prohibit knowingly or with reckless disregard creating, using, or disseminating a deepfake to produce materially false information intended to influence voting or election-related activity. Existing law does not regulate election-related deepfakes.
  • Establish exceptions for deepfakes used for satire, parody, news, and by certain broadcasters, and with protections for service providers.
  • Require the state administrator of elections to disseminate accurate information upon receiving credible reports of misinformation or disinformation. The state auditor would be authorized to seek injunctions and issue subpoenas.
  • Allow the state board of elections to bring civil actions against individuals or entities that create or disseminate election disinformation or, with reckless disregard, misinformation, and to seek damages, costs, and attorney’s fees.

Last Updated April 14, 2026

data.bill_name: Election Law and Affiliating with a Party
data.bill_location: Senate Education, Energy, and the Environment Committee

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on EDUCATION, ENERGY, AND THE ENVIRONMENT

data.author: Fair (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 10, 2026, would:

  • Permit voters who are unaffiliated with a political party to request to affiliate with a party at an early voting center during a primary election or at a precinct polling place on Election Day. Under existing law, party affiliation changes must generally be made prior to the close of voter registration.
  • Require election judges to verify that the voter resides in the county where they seek to vote and, if so, issue the voter a voter authority card and a provisional ballot for the voter’s chosen political party. Existing law does not allow unaffiliated voters to affiliate with a party at the polling place in order to vote in that party’s primary. 
  • Add a voter’s request to change party affiliation during early voting or on primary election day as a basis for issuing a provisional ballot and establish procedures for determining whether the provisional ballot should be counted.
  • Require the State Board of Elections to adopt regulations and procedures governing the administration of party affiliation changes during early voting and on election day.
  • Establish an enactment date of January 1, 2027, and specify that the provisions apply beginning with the statewide primary election in 2028.

Last Updated March 12, 2026

data.bill_location: House Government, Labor, and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: Hearing Scheduled 2/11 at 2:00 p.m

data.author: Wims (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 19, 2026, would establish a new state Voting Rights Act that applies to counties and municipal corporations. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit a county or municipal corporation from imposing or applying a method for electing its governing body that impairs the ability of members of a protected class to elect candidates of their choice or to influence the outcome of an election due to vote dilution or abridgment. Existing law does not provide a standalone state-law cause of action addressing vote dilution in local elections.
  • Establish a legal standard for proving a violation by requiring a showing that local elections exhibit racially polarized voting and that the challenged method of election dilutes or abridges the voting strength of a protected class, as demonstrated by the existence of one or more alternative election methods that could be constitutionally adopted and would likely mitigate the dilution
  • Authorize the Office of the Attorney General or any other person to bring a civil action to enforce the state Voting Rights Act, grant courts broad authority to order tailored remedies to address violations, allow preliminary relief for upcoming elections when feasible, and authorize courts to award attorney’s fees and litigation costs to prevailing private plaintiffs.


Last Updated February 5, 2026

data.bill_name: Election Law Voter Registration Eligibility US Citizens
data.bill_location: House Government, Labor, and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: Hearing Scheduled 2/11 at 2:00 p.m

data.author: Hornberger (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 23, 2026, would:

  • Require the state administrator of elections to enroll the state in the federal Systematic Alien Verification of Entitlements (SAVE) program and require local boards of elections to verify U.S. citizenship through SAVE when processing voter registration applications, including applications submitted during early voting or on Election Day. Existing law requires applicants to attest to citizenship but does not require verification through the SAVE program.
  • Require election officials to review existing voter registration records through the SAVE program and to take action when SAVE reports indicate a registrant may not be a U.S. citizen.
  • Establish notice and appeal procedures for individuals identified by the SAVE program as noncitizens, including requiring written notice, an opportunity to submit documentary proof of citizenship, and review by the state board of elections before a registration is rejected or canceled.
  • Require coordination between the state administrator of elections, local boards of elections, and U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services to implement SAVE-based verification and transmit relevant information for enforcement and list maintenance. Existing law does not require coordination with USCIS for voter registration verification.

Last Updated January 26, 2026

data.author: Williams (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2026, would create "curbside voting", a process of voting at a designated outside location under the observation of election judges. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require each local board of elections to designate a location for curbside voting at least six months before each statewide primary election. The bill would require the location to be outside of one early voting center during early voting and on Election Day. Electioneering and canvassing would be prohibited within the curbside voting area.
  • Require the curbside voting process to enable voters to mark their ballots secretly and without assistance unless the voter requests help. Curbside voters would use paper ballots, except that if a voter eligible for curbside voting wished to use a ballot marking device, the bill would require such a device to be brought to the voter.
  • Require local boards to post signage at curbside voting locations informing voters of its location and how to request the service.

Last Updated February 2, 2026
