data.author: Stewart (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2026, would modify Maryland’s existing automatic voter registration (AVR) system by establishing new procedures for voter registration during REAL ID transactions at the Motor Vehicle Administration (DMV equivalent). Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the DMV to implement an "enhanced automatic voter registration" system by July 1, 2029. The system would send registration information to the State Board of Elections within five days of REAL ID transactions for individuals who are: not already registered, U.S. citizens, at least 16 years old, and Maryland residents. Existing law requires AVR through the DMV and other agencies but does not differentiate based on REAL ID or citizenship documentation. Eligible residents are automatically registered to vote when completing applications for services at voter registration agencies if they do not opt out during the transaction.
  • Prevent the transmission of voter registration information to the State Board if the individual has provided documentation as part of the REAL ID transaction showing they are not a U.S. citizen. Existing law does not include such a restriction.
  • Require the DMV to give voters a chance to select a political party affiliation during the REAL ID transaction and require the State Board to send an “enhanced automatic voter registration notice” if the individual is not registered, or an “update notice” if the person is already registered but has changed their name or address. If the applicant does not respond to the registration notice within 21 days, the individual would be registered automatically. 
  • Specify that individuals whose documentation during REAL ID transactions does not confirm U.S. citizenship would not be registered through the enhanced system but would remain eligible for the existing AVR process. Existing law does not distinguish between registration systems based on citizenship documentation.
  • Authorize the State Board of Elections to identify and work with other agencies to implement similar enhanced AVR procedures if those agencies can "reliably verify" U.S. citizenship.

data.bill_location: House Government, Labor, and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: Hearing Scheduled 2/25 at 1:00 p.m

data.author: Grammer (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 6, 2026, would establish the "Secure the Vote Act of 2026." Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require documentary proof of U.S. citizenship to register to vote. Existing law does not require proof of citizenship to register to vote.
  • Require voters to present photo ID when voting. The bill would also allow a person to provide a voter notification card and one of the following: current utility bill, bank statement, government check, paycheck, or other government document with the voter's name and current address.
  • Require the state board to allow individuals to obtain a required photo ID at any public library in the state.
  • Restrict mail voting to individuals who have certain excuses. Under existing law, all voters may request a mail ballot without an excuse. Excuses would include:
    • Absence from the state
    • Illness or physical disability
    • Religious observance
    • Pregnancy
    • Caregiving
    • Jury duty
    • Incarceration
    • Military service
    • Participation in the political process
    • Dislocation due to a natural disaster or other emergency
  • Eliminate ballot drop boxes by repealing statutory provisions governing their placement, security requirements and use. The bill would remove the requirement that drop boxes be available at the Baltimore City centralized booking facility.
  • Require a comprehensive audit of the statewide voter registration list before each primary election. The audit would need to be completed at least 90 days before the primary, and a report describing the results would be published online.

Last Updated March 13, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 467

data.author: Jones (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 12, 2026, would:

  • Requires police officers on duty at polling places to obey the order of the state administrator, the deputy administrator, and a local election director, in addition to election judges.
  • Specifies that police officers who make arrests under the order of a local board employee are fully protected in doing so as if the officers received a valid warrant. Under prior law, the authority only applied to orders of election judges.
  • Grants the state administrator and the deputy administrator authority to maintain order and enforce elections laws. Specifically the bill allows them to:
    • Keep the peace
    • Order the arrest of any person who breaches the peace, preaches any provision of the state's election law, or interferes with the work of the election judges in conducting and carrying out their assigned tasks
  • Authorizes a local board employee to protect challengers or watchers in the the exercise of their rights or eject a challenger or watcher who violates the law.
  • Expands who may designate accredited challengers and watchers and where they may serve. Prior law limited designation authority to entities such as the State Board of Elections, local boards, candidates, political parties, and voter groups, and generally limits challenger and watcher activity to polling places. Specifically, the bill:
    • Adds political committees and persons required to file campaign finance reports under state law as entities authorized to designate challengers and watchers.
    • Expands service locations to include early voting centers and canvassing locations, in addition to polling places.
  • Specifies that authorized observers may observe board of canvassers and the staff of a local board while the officials are completing each part of the canvass.
  • Authorizes the candidates, political parties, political committees, and certain individuals required to file reports under state law to designate a registered voter to serve as an observer at a counting center.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

data.author: Fair (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 13, 2026, would:

  • Specify that, in the event of federal action, absentee votes for non-federal contests should be counted under the existing state requirements . Existing law does not address separate canvassing treatment for federal and non-federal contests. Under existing law, which the bill would leave in place, ballots returned by mail must be postmarked no later than Election Day, and received by 10 a.m. on the second Friday after the election.
  • Require, in the event of federal action, the state board to adopt regulations for the conduct of the absentee ballot canvass that comply with federal requirements and provide notice to voters of the new requirements. Under the bill, the notice should encourage absentee voters to mail their ballot at least one week before Election Day or place their ballot in a drop box on or before Election Day.
  • Require standardized terminology for absentee ballots in public communications. The bill would require the State Board of Elections and local boards to refer to absentee ballots as "mail-in ballots" and refer to absentee ballots delivered electronically as "print-at-home ballots" in communications with voters and the public.

Last Updated April 16, 2026

data.bill_name: Absentee Ballots and Notice of Timely Receipt
data.bill_location: House Government, Labor, and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: Hearing Scheduled 3/04 at 1:00 p.m

data.author: Rosenberg (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 13, 2026, would:

  • Require the state administrator of elections to establish a uniform tabulation policy for absentee ballots for federal election contests if a federal court action preempts state law or regulations governing the timely receipt of absentee ballots. The policy would need to comply with the federal action. The provisions would apply only to primary and general elections in 2026 and 2028. Under existing law, ballots returned by mail must be postmarked no later than Election Day, and received by 10 a.m. on the second Friday after the election.
  • Specify that, in the event of federal action, votes for state and local contests on an absentee ballot that was mailed on or before Election Day (and timely received) should be canvassed under State law even if federal contests on the same ballot are subject to a different federal deadline. Existing law does not address separate canvassing treatment for federal and non-federal contests on the same ballot in response to federal court action.

data.bill_name: Information on Candidates and Voting Procedures

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 523

data.author: Tomlinson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 12, 2026, requires municipalities to submit to the State Election Board information on how to vote and where to vote in the municipality and a list of each qualified candidate for office in the municipality within 10 business days after the candidate filing deadline. The state board must publish the information on its website immediately after receiving it.


This bill includes additional provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

data.bill_name: Election Law Petitions and Ballot Questions

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 439

data.author: Kagan (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 12, 2026:

  • Requires summaries printed on petition signature pages and condensed statements appearing on the ballot for questions be written in plain language that voters can easily understand and prohibit the use of legal jargon, passive voice, and double negatives. Under the bill, the state board must include a brief statement explaining the practical outcome of each voting choice. Prior law required descriptions and summaries of ballot questions but did not impose these detailed plain language standards or require a practical-outcome statement.
  • Modifies deadlines for preparing and certifying ballot question language by requiring the designated official to prepare and certify ballot question information on or before July 1 before a general election, instead of no later than the 95th day. The bill further establishes a 15-day public comment period on proposed ballot question language, requires review and revisions following the comment period, and requires submission of certified plain text for publication. Prior law set certification deadlines but did not require a public comment period or plain-language review process.
  • Expands public notice requirements by requiring the state board and applicable local boards to post the complete text of ballot questions and related legislation in a manner widely accessible to the public for at least 90 days before a general election and to provide links to relevant state legislature or county webpages. Prior law required posting and public inspection of ballot questions but did not require 90-day online posting or linked legislative text.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

data.bill_name: Absentee Ballots State and Federal Requirements

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 440

data.author: Kagan (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 13, 2026, would:

  • Specifies that, in the event of federal action, absentee votes for non-federal contests should be counted under the existing state requirements. Existing law does not address separate canvassing treatment for federal and non-federal contests. Under existing law, which the bill would leave in place, ballots returned by mail must be postmarked no later than Election Day, and received by 10 a.m. on the second Friday after the election.
  • Requires, in the event of federal action, the state board to adopt regulations for the conduct of the absentee ballot canvass that comply with federal requirements and provide notice to voters of the new requirements. Under the bill, the notice should encourage absentee voters to mail their ballot at least one week before Election Day or place their ballot in a drop box on or before Election Day.
  • Requires standardized terminology for absentee ballots in public communications. The bill requires the State Board of Elections and local boards to refer to absentee ballots as "mail-in ballots" and refer to absentee ballots delivered electronically as "print-at-home ballots" in communications with voters and the public.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Paquette (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would require the secretary of state to provide the Department of Natural Resources with voter registration applications. The department would then be required to make those applications available to the public (both in person and on the department's website), and would be permitted to ask applicants for hunting, fishing, or fur harvesting licenses whether they wish to receive a voter registration application. The department would not be permitted to assist with completing the application.

Last Updated February 27, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Hoadley (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 12, 2025, would require removal of voters from the voter list if they have not voted for eight years and fail to confirm their registration. Existing law prohibits removal from the list solely for non-voting, and has no confirmation requirement to remain on the list related to non-voting. Under the new removal process:

  • The secretary of state would be required to send notice 180 days after each general November general election to all voters who have not voted in the past eight years or more, requesting confirmation of address and a signature to remain registered.
  • Voters wishing to remain registered would be required to respond to the notice, vote, or engage in "voting-related activity," including requesting a mail ballot or updating their registration, by the first business day after the second general November election following the notice. 
  • If the voter fails to return the completed confirmation at least 15 days before the next election, they would be required to affirm their address at the voting site before receiving a ballot. 
  • On receipt of a completed confirmation, clerks would be required to verify the voter's identity by signature matching the form with the voter's signature on file. If the signatures do not match, the clerk must notify the voter that the registration is considered challenged, and provide instructions for verifying their identity. 
  • If a confirmation notice is returned as undeliverable, the secretary must designate the voter's registration as challenged. As with failure to respond for any other reason, registration for these voters would be cancelled if they fail to engage in voting-related activity by the first business day after the second general November election that follows. 


Last Updated June 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Wooden (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 6, 2025, would create a new process under which certain events would trigger a possible removal of a registered voter who is not a US citizen. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the secretary of state, if the secretary receives a legal presence document from a person which indicates that the person is not a citizen and determines that the person is registered to vote, to compare the information in the qualified voter file against any database or program the secretary has access to in order to verify the person's citizenship.
  • Require that if the secretary receives notice from any such database or program confirming that the person is not a citizen, the secretary notify the person accordingly and advise them that they have 60 days to prove their citizenship before their voter registration will be canceled.

Last Updated May 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Koleszar (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 6, 2025, would permit the removal of registered voters from the rolls for non-voting and would shorten the amount of time a voter has to respond to an address confirmation notice. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit the department of state to interpret a voter's failure to vote for a period of 20 years as evidence that the voter has moved and initiate an address confirmation process that may result in the voter's removal from the rolls. Existing law does not permit the cancelation of a voter's registration for failure to vote for any period of time.
  • Shorten the amount of time that a voter has to respond to a confirmation notice. Under existing law, when the department of state believes a voter has moved, the department will send the voter a confirmation notice which the voter has 30 days to return. Under the bill, such notice must be returned within 15 days.
  • Provide that a confirmation notice returned by a voter must be postage prepaid and preaddressed.

Last Updated May 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Xiong (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 6, 2025, would require the secretary of state to biennially review the standard voter registration form to ensure that the form describes the citizenship requirement for voter registration and specifies that a person who is not a citizen may not register to vote.

Last Updated May 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Woolford (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 14, 2026, would create a proof of citizenship requirement for anyone registering to vote in Michigan. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require any person registering to vote to present proof of United States citizenship. This is not required under existing law. The bill defines proof of citizenship as one of the following documents:
    • A driver license or personal identification card issued by Michigan or another state, if the license or card indicates U.S. citizenship.
    • A birth certificate that verifies United States citizenship, which may be accompanied by a marriage license or other documentation that shows a name change.
    • A U.S. passport
    • U.S. naturalization documents
    • A certificate of citizenship
    • An American Indian Card issued by the United States Department of Homeland Security with the classification "KIC"
    • A Bureau of Indian Affairs card number, Indian Census Number, tribal treaty card number, or tribal enrollment number
    • A Certification of Report of Birth or Consular Report of Birth Abroad
  • Make conforming changes to statutes concerning automatic voter registration and electronic voter registration to specify that proof of citizenship is required during those transactions.
  • Modify voter registration forms to state that an application will not be processed until the applicant has provided proof of United States citizenship.
  • Specify that proof of United States citizenship contained in a voter registration record is exempt from disclosure.
  • Create a "federal-only voter" designation for individuals whose citizenship cannot be verified. Under the bill, the applicant would only be eligible to vote only in federal elections until citizenship is confirmed. Existing law requires applicants to attest to citizenship but does not require verification or create a separate federal-only voter category.

Last Updated April 17, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Pavlov (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on August 26, 2025, would require the Department of Corrections to provide individuals being released from prison with a document, prepared by the secretary of state, with information regarding voter registration, election processes, voting processes, election dates, and an express statement that individuals who are otherwise eligible may register and vote after release from confinement.

The bill also contains additional provisions that are not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Bruck (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 5, 2026, would impose new requirements on donations for election administration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a person, other than a government entity, who enters into an agreement to provide funding for election administration to certify to the secretary of state, no later than ten business days before the donation, that they are not knowingly a recipient of foreign donations.
  • Require such a person to annually renew the certification, and to update within five business days if the person becomes aware of a change in circumstances that impacts the certification.
  • Require governmental entities to quarterly provide the secretary with information on any entity that provides the entity with funding for election administration.
  • Create a misdemeanor offense for any person who knowingly fails to provide an accurate or updated certification as required by the bill.

Last Updated February 20, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Wortz (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 12, 2026, would:

  • Require township boards to enter into a contract with an individual accredited to conduct elections, who resides within a reasonable proximity to the township, to perform duties related to voter registration, nominations, and elections when the township clerk and any deputy are unavailable. Existing law requires the township board to appoint a qualified registered elector of the township to perform these duties but does not expressly authorize contracting with an accredited election professional.
  • Grant the contracted individual the same powers and authority as a deputy township clerk for election-related duties.
  • Require the contracted individual to report to the township board at each regularly scheduled meeting summarizing the work performed.

Last Updated April 30, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Wortz (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 22, 2026, would require all electronic poll book flash drives used in a primary or election to be preserved and allow their destruction only after 22 months. Existing law requires retention and sets preservation timelines for various election materials, including ballots and returns, but does not expressly regulate electronic poll books.

Last Updated April 27, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Wortz (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2026, would:

  • Transfer chief election officer authority when the secretary of state is a candidate for another office and make the deputy secretary of state the chief election officer for the election in which the secretary is a candidate. Existing law law does not provide for this transfer of authority when the secretary of state is a candidate.
  • Expressly prohibit the secretary of state from supervising or administering an election in which they are a candidate for another office.
  • Specify that transfer of authority does not apply to the secretary of state's role in conducting election audits and that the secretary retains powers and duties not related to supervising or administering elections.

Last Updated May 15, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Hoskins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2026, would establish a "State Voting Rights Act" and create certain protections in recognition of the right to vote and other constitutional guarantees, and would assert that the state intends all election law to be construed in a manner that encourages electoral participation and intends that members of minority groups have equal access to participate. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Assert that all laws of the state are intended to be construed in a manner that favors the lawful exercise of the vote by eligible voters, the counting of those votes, and accessibility of the electoral process.
  • Prohibit any agency or local government of the state from imposing any qualification on a person's ability to vote, adopting any prerequisite to voting or any regulation or ordinance impacting the administration of elections, or imposing any standard, practice, procedure, or policy inconsistent with the goals of electoral participation and equal access to the process. The bill creates a rebuttable presumption that certain activities, including failure to provide translated voter materials, closing a polling place in a manner likely to disproportionately impact a protected class, or changing a local government (including through annexation or dissolution) in a way likely to disproportionately impact a protected class.
  • Prohibit any local government from using any method of election that has the effect of impairing the opportunity of protected class members to elect candidates of the protected class member's choice, or otherwise diluting their votes. The bill provides guidelines and general principles for how to conclude whether this has occurred.
  • Provide general guidelines for how to determine if a protected class has had their right to vote impaired, when viewing the totality of the circumstances. The bill also provides procedures for commencing an action against a local government alleging a violation of a right guaranteed under the bill. The bill would also permit local governments to adopt resolutions to remedy alleged violations. Such a resolution would be sent to the secretary of state to determine if the remedy proposed would solve the problem.
  • Require covered jurisdictions to obtain preclearance for covered policies from the secretary of state or the court of claims. The secretary would make an annual determination of which jurisdictions are covered for this purpose based on a set of rules intended to encompass recent violators of the bill or local governments otherwise engaging in activities inconsistent with the bill's stated goals. A covered policy would be any new or modified qualification for admission as an elector, prerequisite to vote, or any law or regulation of any kind impacting access to the ballot.
  • Permit a voter with disabilities, or an organization that advocates for such voters, to bring an action requesting that a monitor be appointed for elections conducted by a local government having recently engaged in activity that negatively impacts access to the ballot for voters with disabilities. The monitor, if appointed, would be granted authority to take actions to protect the voting rights of these individuals within the local government's jurisdiction.

The bill would take effect only if separate, specified bills are also passed into law.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Young (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 13, 2026, would require the secretary of state, not later than November 5, 2027, to enter into an agreement with one or more public universities in Michigan to create a voting and elections database and institute. The purpose of the database and institute would be to aggregate all elections and voting data available to the public from local governments (who would be required to share their data) and to foster research on existing laws and on election best practices. All data would be maintained electronically and all relevant election and voting data and records for at least the previous 12-year period would be required to be made available for study.


The bill would only take effect if the legislature simultaneously passes two separate, specified bills.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Phillips (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2026, would:

  • Permit a voter who is unable to enter a polling place to request to vote their ballot curbside at a polling place by requesting that assistance in advance or sending another person inside the polling place to request assistance on their behalf, or through any other method deemed appropriate by election officials. The bill would:
    • Require that two election officials from opposing political parties deliver the voter's ballot, wait while the voter casts the ballot, then take it inside to be tabulated and return to confirm the tabulation to the voter.
    • Require curbside voting to be offered at all hours a polling place or early voting site is open.
  • Provide that an individual is permitted to provide food, entertainment, warmth, or other necessities to persons waiting in line to vote.
  • Permit a voter to have language assistance while voting from any individual chosen by the voter to assist the voter in participating in the electoral process.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide notice on the secretary's website within five days of receiving notice from a local government of any of the following:
    • a change in location of a polling place, ballot dropbox, or other voting location
    • any change in voting hours during regular or early voting, or changes to an early voting plan
    • the results of an election audit, selection of a voting system, establishment of a counting board, or changes to a district
  • Repeal a provision of law requiring a primary election ballot to be spoiled, and the voter prevented from voting in that primary, if the voter shows the ballot to another person in a way likely to reveal how the voter voted.

The bill would take effect only if separate, specified bills are also passed.


The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including notice requirements for certain ballot language or for the inspection of election equipment.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTION INTEGRITY

data.author: Koleszar (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 4, 2026, would create an income tax deduction for compensation paid to election inspectors. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow a taxpayer, for tax years beginning on January 1, 2026, to deduct 100% of any compensation paid by the state or a political subdivision to the taxpayer for serving as an election inspector during an election period. Existing law does not provide a specific income tax deduction for compensation earned by election inspectors.
  • Define "election inspector” as an individual who assists with conducting elections under Michigan election law, but would exclude election challengers, election observers, and full-time employees of a county, city, or township clerk’s office.
  • Define the “election period” to include the date of a state, local, or federal election, including primary and general elections, the early voting period for that election, and any dates before or after the election period when an election inspector is required to attend trainings or meetings.

Last Updated June 8, 2026

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE.  Read second time and substituted. (H-1 ) To third reading

data.author: Posthumus (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as amended on April 30, 2025, would propose an amendment to the state constitution requiring verification of U.S. citizenship for all registration applicants and currently registered voters, among other changes. Under existing law, registration applicants must sign a statement certifying the truth of their eligibility, including their citizenship status, but further verification is not required. Specifically, the resolution would:

  • Require each new applicant for voter registration, beginning in December 2026, to prove U.S. citizenship by one of two methods:
    • Presentation of documentary proof of United States citizenship at the time of registration or afterwards to the secretary of state or clerk
    • Verification of citizenship by the secretary of state
  • Require the secretary of state to carry out an annual "systematic" process using "all reasonable efforts" to verify whether each registered voter in the current voter file is a citizen. If the secretary's research indicates that a person is not a citizen, they must issue notice to the voter within seven days of the discovery; if the voter fails to respond or provide evidence of citizenship within 60 days, the secretary would be required to cancel their registration. 
  • Authorize the secretary of state to conduct an "individualized review" of a single voter at any time, employing the same efforts to determine whether an individual registered voter is a citizen.
  • Require the statewide voter registration file be updated to reflect whether a voter has presented proof of citizenship.
  • Require the secretary of state to verify a person's citizenship status before automatically registering the person as a voter as a result of conducting business with the secretary. The secretary would not be permitted to automatically register the person if the secretary determines the person is not a citizen.
  • Require voting by provisional ballot only for any registered voter whose citizenship has not been verified prior to voting, and require such voters to provide proof of citizenship by the sixth day after an election for the ballot to be counted.
  • Require the secretary to report annually to the legislature on the removal of registered voters pursuant to the list maintenance program adopted under the resolution.
  • Require voting by provisional ballot for any voter appearing in person who does not have voter ID, and require such voters to provide an accepted form of ID by the sixth day after an election for the ballot to be counted. Under existing law, a voter unable to present photo identification may execute an affidavit to verify their identity and still vote a regular ballot. 
  • Require applicants for a mail ballot to provide their driver's license number, state ID card number, or the last four digits of their Social Security number, in addition to their signature, if applying by any method other than in person, and require verification of both the ID number and signature matching. Under existing law, such mail ballot applications require a signature and signature matching only. 
  • Instruct the legislature to provide a process for individuals to claim financial hardship and obtain a voter ID card at no cost.

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires passage by two-thirds of the legislature and approval by voters at the next general or special election.

Last Updated May 15, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE. Passed HOUSE

data.author: Smit (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as passed by the Michigan House on February 5, 2026, encourages the federal government to pass legislation requiring documentary proof of citizenship to vote in federal elections, such as the SAVE Act.

Last Updated February 17, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Outman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on May 21, 2026, would urge Congress to propose and send to the states for ratification an amendment clarifying that states may set reasonable limits on spending money to influence state elections and that Congress may set reasonable limits on spending money to influence federal elections. The resolution would further state that, when setting campaign spending limits, states and Congress should be able to distinguish between natural persons and artificial entities, including corporations, unions, and artificial intelligence.

Last Updated May 22, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Lindsey (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 5, 2025, would modify the procedures and requirements for a person voting in person without identification. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a voter who appears to vote in person, but does not possess identification for election purposes, to execute an affidavit and present one document as proof of identity and one document as proof of state residence in order to cast a regular ballot. Under existing law, such a person is permitted to vote a regular ballot upon execution of the affidavit. A voter who is unable to present the required documents would vote a provisional ballot, which would only be counted if they present the required documents within six days after the election.
  • Define the following documents as proof of identity for the purposes of the bill:
    • a marriage license issued in the United States
    • a divorce decree issued in the United States
    • a United States court order for a name change
    • a United States military discharge or separation document
    • a Michigan driver education certificate
    • a government issued adoption record
    • a certified copy of a birth certificate
    • a Consular Report of Birth Abroad
  • Define the following documents as proof of state residence for purposes of the bill:
    • a utility bill or credit card bill issued within the last 90 days
    • an account statement, including an electronic account statement, from a bank or other financial institution issued within the last 90 days
    • a Michigan high school, college, or university report card or transcript issued within the last 90 days
    • a current mortgage, lease, or rental agreement
    • a pay stub or earnings statement issued within the last 90 days that contains the name and home address of the employee
    • a life, health, automobile, or home insurance policy issued within the last 90 days
    • a Michigan title and registration that shows the current residential address


Last Updated March 6, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Cavanagh (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on September 9, 2025, would create a civil penalty for persons who make certain misleading statements about an election, or an entity that employs such a person. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a civil penalty, in an amount of up to $1,000 per violation, for any person who intentionally makes a false statement to another person concerning:
    • the time, place, or manner of an election
    • the qualifications for, or restrictions on, voter eligibility
    • the criminal penalties associated with voting
    • an individual's voter registration status or eligibility
  • Impose the above penalty only if the individual knew the falsity of the statement and made it with intent to impede the other person's ability to participate in an election.
  • Create a separate civil penalty in an amount of up to $10,000 for an organization that employs, for an election-related purpose, a person who incurs the civil penalty.
  • Exempt an organization from the penalty if the organization can demonstrate that it had no prior knowledge of the employee's plan to violate this law. The bill would also create a rebuttable presumption that a local government did not have prior knowledge.

Last Updated September 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Wojno (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 10, 2026, would:

  • Increase the capacity for consolidated precincts from 5,000 to 15,000 active registered voters.
  • Require officials who are determining whether to consolidate an election precinct to take into consideration the distance between election precinct polling places and the access voters have to public transportation in addition to existing factors. Existing factors, which the bill leaves in place include: the percentage of voters who voted in the last similar election, intensity of voter interest in the jurisdiction, and the number of choices a voter must make.

Last Updated June 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS AND ETHICS

data.author: Bellino (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 11, 2026, would:

  • Require township boards to enter into a contract with an individual accredited to conduct elections, who resides within a reasonable proximity to the township, to perform duties related to voter registration, nominations, and elections when the township clerk and any deputy are unavailable. Existing law requires the township board to appoint a qualified registered elector of the township to perform these duties but does not expressly authorize contracting with an accredited election professional.
  • Grant the contracted individual the same powers and authority as a deputy township clerk for election-related duties.
  • Require the contracted individual to report to the township board at each regularly scheduled meeting summarizing the work performed.

data.author: McBroom (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 7, 2026, would establish tabulation audits that must be completed before certification of election results. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change the secretary of state’s audit authority from discretionary to mandatory by requiring the secretary to audit election precincts after each election.
  • Require the secretary of state to randomly select precincts from each county for a tabulation audit that equals one percent of registered voters in the county, or three precincts in the county, whichever is greater. Each tabulation audit would require a hand tally of at least one race in each selected precinct, and for each presidential election, the audit would require a hand tally of the presidential contest in each selected precinct. Existing law requires audits but does not establish specific county-level sample sizes.
  • Require each county clerk or their designee to submit a report of the tabulation audit results to the board of county canvassers and the secretary of state before the board of canvassers certifies the results. Under existing law, audits are conducted after certification.
  • Prohibit the county board of canvassers from concluding the canvass until the tabulation audit is complete. The bill would retain the general requirement that the county board of canvassers complete the canvass by the 14th day after the election.
  • Specify that if there is a discrepancy between the tabulation audit results and the unofficial election returns that cannot be resolved, the secretary of state or the county clerk may require tabulation audits for additional precincts.


Last Updated May 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: In SENATE.  Read second time.  To third reading

data.public_commentary:

  • Prohibit any agency or local government of the state from imposing any qualification on a person's ability to vote, adopting any prerequisite to voting or any regulation or ordinance impacting the administration of elections, or imposing any standard, practice, procedure, or policy inconsistent with the goals of electoral participation and equal access to the process. The bill creates a rebuttable presumption that certain activities, including failure to provide translated voter materials, closing a polling place in a manner likely to disproportionately impact a protected class, or changing a local government (including through annexation or dissolution) in a way likely to disproportionately impact a protected class.
  • Prohibit any local government from using any method of election that has the effect of impairing the opportunity of protected class members to elect candidates of the protected class member's choice, or otherwise diluting their votes. The bill provides guidelines and general principles for how to conclude whether this has occurred.
  • Provide general guidelines for how to determine if a protected class has had their right to vote impaired, when viewing the totality of the circumstances. The bill also provides procedures for commencing an action against a local government alleging a violation of a right guaranteed under the bill. The bill would also permit local governments to adopt resolutions to remedy alleged violations. Such a resolution would be sent to the secretary of state to determine if the remedy proposed would solve the problem.
  • Require covered jurisdictions to obtain preclearance for covered policies from the secretary of state or the court of claims. The secretary would make an annual determination of which jurisdictions are covered for this purpose based on a set of rules intended to encompass recent violators of the bill or local governments otherwise engaging in activities inconsistent with the bill's stated goals. A covered policy would be any new or modified qualification for admission as an elector, prerequisite to vote, or any law or regulation of any kind impacting access to the ballot.
  • Permit a voter with disabilities, or an organization that advocates for such voters, to bring an action requesting that a monitor be appointed for elections conducted by a local government having recently engaged in activity that negatively impacts access to the ballot for voters with disabilities. The monitor, if appointed, would be granted authority to take actions to protect the voting rights of these individuals within the local government's jurisdiction.

The bill would take effect only if Senate Bills 962, 963, and 964 are also passed into law.

status_actions.summary: In SENATE.  Read second time.  To third reading

data.author: Moss (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 13, 2026, would require the secretary of state, not later than November 5, 2027, to enter into an agreement with one or more public universities in Michigan to create a voting and elections database and institute. The purpose of the database and institute would be to aggregate all elections and voting data available to the public from local governments (who would be required to share their data) and to foster research on existing laws and on election best practices. All data would be maintained electronically and all relevant election and voting data and records for at least the previous 12-year period would be required to be made available for study.


The bill would only take effect if Senate Bills 961 and 963 are also passed.

data.author: Chang (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2026, would require local governments to provide language assistance to voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require local governments to provide language assistance to voters if one of the following conditions is met:
    • Before January 1, 2030, at least 5% of eligible voters or at least 10,000 eligible voters in that local government's jurisdiction speak a non-English language and have limited English proficiency
    • Beginning January 1, 2030, at least 600 eligible voters in that local government's jurisdiction (or at least 100 if that comprises at least 2.5% of the voting-eligible population in that government) that speak a non-English language and have limited English proficiency
  • Require a county to provide language assistance to voters if a local government required to do so enters into an agreement with the county under which the county will conduct early voting for the local government.
  • Require the secretary of state to track and share which jurisdictions are covered by these provisions, and the relevant languages, at least biannually. The secretary would also be required to create the necessary documents for language assistance for elections in all covered languages. A language access advisory council would be created within the secretary's office to advise on how to implement the law. The council would annually approve a list of voter-facing materials that must be translated into each designated language by a certified translator.
  • Instruct the director of elections to keep local governments up to date with whether they are required to provide language assistance under the law.
  • Require a prospective plaintiff to notify the local government or secretary of state, as appropriate, before filing a civil action alleging that a local government failed to provide effective language assistance to attempt to create a written plan to address the issue. Such a lawsuit could be filed by the attorney general, an affected voter, or an affected entity. The local government would then be required to provide effective language assistance in that language (including translated materials and live interpretation if possible), and the attorney general, an affected voter, or an affected entity could bring an action in court to request appropriate remedies if the jurisdiction failed to provide effective language assistance.

The bill would only take effect if Senate Bill 961 and Senate Bill 962 are also passed.

Last Updated May 18, 2026

data.author: Geiss (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2026, would:

  • Permit a voter who is unable to enter a polling place to request to vote their ballot curbside at a polling place by requesting that assistance in advance or sending another person inside the polling place to request assistance on their behalf, or through any other method deemed appropriate by election officials. The bill would:
    • Require that two election officials from opposing political parties deliver the voter's ballot, wait while the voter casts the ballot, then take it inside to be tabulated and return to confirm the tabulation to the voter.
    • Require curbside voting to be offered at all hours a polling place or early voting site is open.
  • Provide that an individual is permitted to provide food, entertainment, warmth, or other necessities to persons waiting in line to vote.
  • Permit a voter to have language assistance while voting from any individual chosen by the voter to assist the voter in participating in the electoral process.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide notice on the secretary's website within five days of receiving notice from a local government of any of the following:
    • a change in location of a polling place, ballot dropbox, or other voting location
    • any change in voting hours during regular or early voting, or changes to an early voting plan
    • the results of an election audit, selection of a voting system, establishment of a counting board, or changes to a district
  • Repeal a provision of law requiring a primary election ballot to be spoiled, and the voter prevented from voting in that primary, if the voter shows the ballot to another person in a way likely to reveal how the voter voted.

The bill would take effect only if Senate Bills 961 and 963 are also passed.


The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including notice requirements for certain ballot language or for the inspection of election equipment.

Existing law provides that the lieutenant governor, secretary of state, and attorney general are nominated by party conventions.

This would begin January 1, 2027. Existing law provides that members of these boards are elected as provided by law.

This bill, as introduced on February 13, 2025, would create new criminal penalties related to mail ballots and mail ballot applications, and would increase the punishment for others. Specifically, the bill would:

The bill also modifies the offense to include mail ballots in addition to mail ballot applications, and includes denying compensation for delivery of applications and ballots under the same circumstances as prohibited conduct.
    • Increasing a person's compensation for collecting a certain number of mail ballot applications. The bill also modifies the offense to include mail ballots in addition to mail ballot applications, and includes increasing compensation for delivery of applications and ballots under the same circumstances as prohibited conduct.

data.author: Heintzeman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced February 24, 2025, would change the state's voter ID requirements. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require documentary proof of identity, in the form of voter ID, in many situations where it is not currently required, by:
    • Requiring photo ID and proof of residence to vote in person. Under existing law, voters affirm their identity and residence under penalty of perjury but are not required to provide documentation.
    • Requiring anyone lacking proof of ID or proof of residence when voting in person to cast a provisional ballot. Provisional ballots would only be counted if a voter presents photo ID and documents proving their residence within seven days after Election Day, unless the voter attempts to procure these documents but is unable to and attests to that fact.
    • Requiring photo ID for anyone registering to vote on Election Day. Under existing law, photo ID is typically required but can be waived if a registered voter or residential facility employee signs an oath affirming the voter's identity, based on the affiant's personal knowledge.
    • Requiring photo ID for any person applying to vote at the time they return an absentee ballot or a mail ballot. This provision would require that the voter show the witness signing the voter's absentee ballot photo ID to demonstrate their identity. Under existing law, the voter needed to only show the witness proof of residence. This provision would also require the witness to provide their own driver's license number, state ID number, voter ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these numbers. Ballots lacking this information would be rejected.
    • Expanding the types of ID numbers required on absentee ballot applications, absentee ballot envelopes, and mail ballot envelopes to include voter ID numbers. Under existing law, applicants must provide their driver's license number, state ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these ID numbers. This bill would require that the voter's ID number be verified and reject any application with an invalid ID number.
    • Providing free voter ID cards to any person who lacks a driver's license or state ID card. Applicants would be required to provide information including the last four digits of the applicant's social security number (or a certification that they do not have one), proof of citizenship, and proof of name, date of birth, identity, and residence address before obtaining a free voter ID card. Voter ID cards would have a unique ID number, the person's name, date of birth, address, signature, and photograph, and the card would have to be replaced if the voter's name or address changed. This bill would also provide that people who need a certified vital record can obtain the record for free if the person attests that it is to obtain a voter ID card.
    • Requiring the secretary of state to conduct a public education campaign regarding the new ID requirements of this bill and the availability of free voter ID.
  • Require an individual assisting a voter voting in person with their ballot to provide their name, address, and telephone number, and create a felony offense for assisting a voter with a ballot in a manner not authorized by law.
  • Add unduly influencing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of unduly influencing a voter, and increasing the punishment for that offense from a misdemeanor to a felony.
  • Add bribing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of bribing a voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to annually report to legislative leadership their proposed legislation, which, according to this bill, should only be legislation that would codify matters that would otherwise be adopted through administrative rulemaking.
  • Extend the timeframe for canvassing ballots after primaries and elections.
  • Make additional conforming changes to other sections of the election code.

Last Updated February 28, 2025

data.bill_location: House Elections Finance and Government Operations Committee

data.author: Quam (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would make various changes to voter registration requirements, including eliminating the requirement that voters provide proof of residency when registering to vote through same-day registration or when submitting a mail ballot. Under existing law, these methods of registration require either documentation or personal vouching of a voter's current residence. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change same-day voter registration processes by:
    • Eliminating the requirements that a same-day voter registration applicant must make an oath and provide proof of residency or have their residence personally vouched for, and instead require only the standard registration form (which an applicant must certify under penalty of perjury). The bill would also require same-day registrants to vote a newly created "provisional ballot," and set out procedures for casting, verifying, rejecting, and counting these ballots. The provisional ballots of same-day registrants would be counted if the registration application was accepted, and voters whose ballots were rejected would receive notice following the election. Existing law permits voters who meet the requirements for same-day registration to vote a regular ballot, and does not provide for the use of provisional ballots generally.
    • Requiring that any same-day registration applications be entered into the voter registration system within three days after Election Day. Under existing law, they must be entered within 42 days after Election Day.
  • Change requirements for registering to vote by mail by:
    • Eliminating the requirement that applicants who register to vote by mail must include their driver's license number, state ID number, or the last four digits of their social security number.
    • Changing the process for correcting incomplete registration applications submitted by mail. Under existing law, if a mail registration applicant (1) has not provided a form of documentation required for same day registration, (2) has not voted previously in the state in a federal election, and (3) officials are unable to verify their driver's license, state ID, or Social Security number, the applicant must either register in person or meet the requirements for same day registration (including documentation or personal vouching of residency). The bill would simplify the process by requiring, where a mail applicant's eligibility to vote cannot be verified, that the individual submit a general registration application in person, on Election Day, or with a completed mail ballot.
  • Change processes for voters who simultaneously return a mail ballot and apply to vote by:
    • Eliminating the requirement that voters who are simultaneously registering to vote and returning an absentee ballot present proof of residence to the witness for the absentee ballot.
    • Requiring election officials to open ballot envelopes to determine if there is a voter registration application in the envelope. If so, then the application must be removed and the envelope resealed. The auditor would then promptly process the application. Under existing law, the voter registration application is processed by the absentee ballot board during absentee ballot processing.
  • Expressly limit the optional "alternative procedure" for in-person early voting at the county auditor's office in the seven days before an election to only those voters who are already registered to vote. As a result, voters would not be permitted to do same-day registration for this form of voting. Existing law is silent as to whether this procedure may be offered to unregistered voters.
  • Change requirements for challengers by allowing challengers to prove their residency by presenting a student ID or a student fee statement in addition to the other forms of ID already named in statute.
  • Repeal the provision concerning postsecondary institutions submitting enrollment lists to election officials to facilitate proof of residency requirements for students, and the provision requiring election officials to record the number of same-day registration applications that are rejected due to lack of residency proof.
  • Require the secretary of state to conduct a public awareness campaign to encourage people to register to vote before Election Day.
  • The bill also contains provisions that are not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including changes to the time for canvassing primary and general elections.


Last Updated February 27, 2025

Existing law already requires this to happen with an application submitted electronically, but this bill would require this process to take place for all application types.

If the voter cannot be verified, the voter's status must be changed to "challenged" and the voter removed from the permanent mail voter list.

data.author: Quam (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced February 26, 2025, would create new photo ID requirements for in-person voting, same-day registration, provisional ballots, and mail ballot voting, among other changes. Existing law does not require voters to show ID to vote in person, and has less onerous documentation requirements for other voting-related activities than those set out in the bill. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Define accepted forms of "documentary proof of identity and residence" for use as voter ID. Accepted forms of ID would be limited to:
    • an unexpired state driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence
    • valid US military ID, with photo and current residence
    • unexpired tribal ID, with photo and current residence
    • receipt for a driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence (if receipt lacks photo, this must be accompanied by (1) a license, state ID, or voter ID that is expired or shows prior address, (2) a US passport, (3) a photo ID issued by the state or federal government, (4) a student ID issued by a postsecondary institution that has provided an enrollment list to election authorities, or (5) a tribal ID)
    • a US passport, tribal ID, or US military card that has a photo but does not have the voter's current address, plus one of the following with their address: (1) utility bill or work services order, (2) bank statement, (3) high school transcript, (4) college or university transcript issued by institution in the state, (5) unemployment stub, (6) statement from long-term health care facility, (7) insurance policy, (8) federal or state tax return, (9) property tax statement, (10) vehicle title, (11) property deed or title, (12) SSI statement, (13) mortgage document, (14) lease agreement, (15) unexpired professional license, (16) unexpired Selective Service card, (17) current military orders, (18) cell phone bill, or (19) game or fishing license.
    • for students, a state license, state ID, or voter ID card issued by any state that includes a photograph, and a current fee statement showing state residence
    • for residential facility residents, a license or state ID card, and a certification of residence through the state's official form
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID to vote in person. Under existing law, in-person voters must affirm their identity and residence under penalty of perjury, but are not required to provide documentation.
  • Require in-person voters who lack an accepted form of photo ID to cast a provisional ballot, and set out procedures related to provisional ballot voting and verification. A provisional ballot would only be counted if the voter presents required photo ID within seven days after Election Day, unless they have attempted to procure the documents and were unable to, and attest to that fact. Existing law does not provide for the use of provisional ballots.
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID for same-day registration on Election Day. Under existing law, same-day registrants are required to provide proof of residence only, by presenting a driver's license, state ID, student fee statement, or other document approved by the secretary of state, but this documentation requirement can be waived if a registered voter or residential facility employee signs an oath affirming the voter's identity, based on the affiant's personal knowledge. Under the bill, same day registrants who could not provide acceptable photo ID would only be permitted to vote a provisional ballot.
  • Require mail ballot voters to show an accepted form of photo ID to their ballot witness, who must note the specific type on the ballot certificate. Voters who have attempted to procure accepted ID and been unable to do so would be permitted to execute an affidavit asserting the explanation, which must be signed in the presence of the witness. Under existing law, mail ballot voters who are already registered must provide an ID number on the ballot certificate, but are not required to present any documentation to their witness. For mail voters who are applying to register along with their completed ballot, existing law requires these voters to provide proof of residence only. The bill would also require a ballot witness to provide their own driver's license number, state ID number, voter ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these numbers. Ballots lacking this information, or any information required on the ballot certificate, would be rejected.
  • Expressly require election administrators to verify the identification numbers provided by a mail ballot applicant, and reject any application with a number that is not verified. Existing law requires verification of ID numbers by the Secretary of State for electronic mail ballot applications, but does not have an express requirement for applications otherwise submitted.
  • Provide for free voter ID cards to any person who lacks a driver's license or state ID card. Applicants would be required to provide information including a social security number (or a certification that they do not have one), proof of citizenship, and a photo ID (unless they have a religious objection) to obtain a free voter ID card. Voter ID cards would have a unique ID number, the person's name, date of birth, address, signature, and photograph, and would have to be replaced prior to use for voting if the voter's name or address change. The bill would also provide that people who need a certified vital record can obtain the record for free if the person attests that it is to obtain a voter ID card. Use of a voter ID card number would be permitted throughout election provisions where a driver's license or state ID number is accepted under existing law.
  • Require the Secretary of State to conduct a public education campaign regarding the new photo ID requirements and availability of free voter ID cards.
  • Require the Secretary of State to report to legislative leadership, by January 2026, proposed legislation to codify any election-related matters currently subject to administrative rulemaking that could be enacted into statutory law. For matters that are not practicable in statutory law, the Secretary must propose legislation that directs specific changes in administrative rules such that no interpretation of the law is required of the Secretary and the good cause rulemaking exception would be appropriate if authorized by the legislature.
  • Extend the timeframe for canvassing ballots after primaries and elections.
  • Make various conforming changes to other sections of the election code.
  • The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated February 27, 2025

data.author: Quam (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 10, 2025, would make a number of minor changes to election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a state court administrator to provide the secretary of state, on a quarterly basis, with the names of every person who declined jury service on the grounds of not being a citizen. The secretary of state would then compare this data against the voter registration list and remove noncitizens as necessary.
  • Change the timeline for the delivery of mail ballots. Under existing law, mail ballots must be sent at least 46 days before most elections, and at least 30 days before a town election held in March. Under the bill, this would be changed to no more than 46 days and no more than 30 days, respectively.
  • Prohibit an election judge from vouching for the residence of a voter who is registering at a polling place while the judge is working. Under existing law, a same-day registrant must proof residency, and one of the methods of doing so is being vouched for by another registered voter. This bill would not permit an election judge working at the polling place from being the person to vouch for the voter.
  • Require that each major political party be represented by at least one election judge in each precinct. The bill would also require the secretary of state to develop a form for parties to use to recruit potential judges, including judges willing to serve in precincts outside their home jurisdiction or on ballot boards.
  • Clarify that voters may receive assistance from election judges in marking ballots or be permitted to vote curbside at any location where ballots may be cast.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide guidance to residential facilities on the "vouching" process.
  • Provide that no more than half the election judges in a place where ballots are being counted, recounted, or reviewed may be affiliated with the same party. This already applies to precinct polling places under existing law.
  • Differentiate between election challengers and election observers, who may observe an election without issuing challenges.
  • Require the governing body of each municipality to create chain of custody procedures for ballots.
  • Require party chairs to provide names of potential election judges to serve as alternates or replacements, as necessary.
  • Require the secretary of state to operate a telephone number election judges may call to receive advice on resolving issues.
  • Provide clarity on list maintenance procedures.

The bill also contains changes to provisions of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including election equipment and candidate affidavits.

Last Updated March 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTIONS FINANCE AND GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Freiberg (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 10, 2025, would make various minor changes to election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require mail ballots to be delivered earlier. Under existing law, mail ballots must be sent no later than 14 days before an election. Under this bill, they must be sent no later than 28 days before an election.
  • Change voter registration forms to remove the option to join the permanent mail voter list, and instead require this option to be added to an application for a mail ballot. Under existing law, mail ballot applications may include this option, but it is not required.
  • Repeal a provision of law under which the operator of a residential facility may provide a list of its employees to the county auditor for purposes of facilitating same-day registration. Under existing law, a person registering to vote during same-day voter registration may prove their residence by having an employee of a residential facility located in the precinct sign an oath vouching that the employee personally knows that the voter resides in the facility. Under the bill, this would still be possible, but the employee would be personally responsible for proving their employment at the facility, instead of their name being on a list provided by the facility's operator.
  • Clarify that students registering to vote during same-day voter registration must be permitted to provide their proof of residence during early voting and on election day.
  • Require county auditors to create a detailed chain of custody plan for each election and file it with the secretary of state. The plan would be required to account for both the physical security and cybersecurity of elections materials.
  • Require that if a federally recognized Native American tribe has off-reservation Tribal lands in a county, the county auditor must establish an early voting polling place on those lands. Existing law only requires establishment of a polling place on a reservation in the county.
  • Require each political party to provide more information about individuals who have indicated willingness to serve as an election judge, including by ensuring that their interest is recent and by providing more thorough contact information. The bill would also move the date by which parties have to supply this information from May to March of an election year, and specify that all election judges must have received training.
  • Require each appointing authority to contact all registered voters in the jurisdiction to ascertain their interest in serving as an election judge.
  • Create new procedures for the segregation of mail ballot envelopes by precinct, verifying the number of ballots received after each day of early voting, and requiring two members of the ballot board to be responsible for counting and recording ballots. 
  • Require a city canvass to begin between the 3rd and 14th day after an election, instead of the 3rd and 10th day after as under existing law.
  • Permit the use of a new location as a combined polling place in a school board election under limited circumstances.

The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including rules for candidates.

Last Updated March 28, 2025
