data.bill_location: House Elections Finance and Government Operations Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTIONS FINANCE AND GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Dotseth (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 10, 2025, would create new photo ID requirements for in-person voting, same-day registration, provisional ballots, and mail ballot voting, among other changes. Existing law does not require voters to show ID to vote in person, and has less onerous documentation requirements for other voting-related activities than those set out in the bill. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Define accepted forms of "documentary proof of identity and residence" for use as voter ID. Accepted forms of ID would be limited to:
    • an unexpired state driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence
    • valid US military ID, with photo and current residence
    • unexpired tribal ID, with photo and current residence
    • receipt for a driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence (if receipt lacks photo, this must be accompanied by (1) a license, state ID, or voter ID that is expired or shows prior address, (2) a US passport, (3) a photo ID issued by the state or federal government, (4) a student ID issued by a postsecondary institution that has provided an enrollment list to election authorities, or (5) a tribal ID)
    • a US passport, tribal ID, or US military card that has a photo but does not have the voter's current address, plus one of the following with their address: (1) utility bill or work services order, (2) bank statement, (3) high school transcript, (4) college or university transcript issued by institution in the state, (5) unemployment stub, (6) statement from long-term health care facility, (7) insurance policy, (8) federal or state tax return, (9) property tax statement, (10) vehicle title, (11) property deed or title, (12) SSI statement, (13) mortgage document, (14) lease agreement, (15) unexpired professional license, (16) unexpired Selective Service card, (17) current military orders, (18) cell phone bill, or (19) game or fishing license.
    • for students, a state license, state ID, or voter ID card issued by any state that includes a photograph, and a current fee statement showing state residence
    • for residential facility residents, a license or state ID card, and a certification of residence through the state's official form
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID to vote in person. Under existing law, in-person voters must affirm their identity and residence under penalty of perjury, but are not required to provide documentation.
  • Require in-person voters who lack an accepted form of photo ID to cast a provisional ballot, and set out procedures related to provisional ballot voting and verification. A provisional ballot would only be counted if the voter presents required photo ID within seven days after Election Day, unless they have attempted to procure the documents and were unable to, and attest to that fact. Existing law does not provide for the use of provisional ballots.
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID for same-day registration on Election Day. Under existing law, same-day registrants are required to provide proof of residence only, by presenting a driver's license, state ID, student fee statement, or other document approved by the secretary of state, but this documentation requirement can be waived if a registered voter or residential facility employee signs an oath affirming the voter's identity, based on the affiant's personal knowledge. Under the bill, same day registrants who could not provide acceptable photo ID would only be permitted to vote a provisional ballot.
  • Require mail ballot voters to show an accepted form of photo ID to their ballot witness, who must note the specific type on the ballot certificate. Voters who have attempted to procure accepted ID and been unable to do so would be permitted to execute an affidavit asserting the explanation, which must be signed in the presence of the witness. Under existing law, mail ballot voters who are already registered must provide an ID number on the ballot certificate, but are not required to present any documentation to their witness. For mail voters who are applying to register along with their completed ballot, existing law requires these voters to provide proof of residence only. The bill would also require a ballot witness to provide their own driver's license number, state ID number, voter ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these numbers. Ballots lacking this information, or any information required on the ballot certificate, would be rejected.
  • Expressly require election administrators to verify the identification numbers provided by a mail ballot applicant, and reject any application with a number that is not verified. Existing law requires verification of ID numbers by the Secretary of State for electronic mail ballot applications, but does not have an express requirement for applications otherwise submitted.
  • Provide for free voter ID cards to any person who lacks a driver's license or state ID card. Applicants would be required to provide information including a social security number (or a certification that they do not have one), proof of citizenship, and a photo ID (unless they have a religious objection) to obtain a free voter ID card. Voter ID cards would have a unique ID number, the person's name, date of birth, address, signature, and photograph, and would have to be replaced prior to use for voting if the voter's name or address change. The bill would also provide that people who need a certified vital record can obtain the record for free if the person attests that it is to obtain a voter ID card. Use of a voter ID card number would be permitted throughout election provisions where a driver's license or state ID number is accepted under existing law.
  • Require the Secretary of State to conduct a public education campaign regarding the new photo ID requirements and availability of free voter ID cards.
  • Require the Secretary of State to report to legislative leadership, by January 2026, proposed legislation to codify any election-related matters currently subject to administrative rulemaking that could be enacted into statutory law. For matters that are not practicable in statutory law, the Secretary must propose legislation that directs specific changes in administrative rules such that no interpretation of the law is required of the Secretary and the good cause rulemaking exception would be appropriate if authorized by the legislature.
  • Add unduly influencing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of unduly influencing a voter, and increasing the punishment for that offense from a misdemeanor to a felony.
  • Add bribing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of bribing a voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to annually report to legislative leadership their proposed legislation, which, according to this bill, should only be legislation that would codify matters that would otherwise be adopted through administrative rulemaking.
  • Extend the timeframe for canvassing ballots after primaries and elections.
  • Make various conforming changes to other sections of the election code.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated March 11, 2025

data.bill_location: House Elections Finance and Government Operations Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTIONS FINANCE AND GOVERNMENT OPERATIONS

data.author: Virnig (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 17, 2025, tightens the deadline for submission of a mail ballot application under certain circumstances, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require an application for a mail ballot to be submitted earlier if not submitted in person. Under existing law, an application for a mail ballot may be submitted any day up until the day before the election. Under the bill, a request for a mail ballot that is submitted by any method other than in person must be received seven days before the day of the election. Voters with disabilities and emergency responders would be exempt from this deadline.
  • Require mail ballot applicants applying using the website designed for that purpose to provide the number of their Minnesota driver's license or state ID card, and the last four digits of their social security number. If they do not possess both types, they must certify a statement to that effect. Under existing law, applicants are only required to provide one of those ID numbers.
  • Instruct county auditors and clerks to inform voters whose applications for a mail ballot are received after the deadline of the alternative opportunities to vote in the election within one day of receiving an untimely application.
  • Require a person delivering an application for a mail ballot on behalf of a voter to return it within seven days after it has been dated by the voter and no later than seven days before the date of the election. Under existing law, these deadlines are ten days and six days, respectively.
  • Require that third parties who send mail ballot applications or sample ballots to voters include a statement informing the voter that the mailing is not sent by or at the request of election officials, and that the sample ballot is not an official ballot. The bill also prohibits third parties from prefilling absentee ballot applications.
  • Permit two employees at a health care facility or hospital to deliver mail ballots in place of election judges, if necessary for health and safety reasons. Both employees must be present during the entire process and return the ballot to the election judges.
  • Exempt county elections from the party balance requirement in the appointment of election judges if the election is not held in conjunction with a state election.
  • Permit electronic rosters to be combined as needed to operate a combined polling place.

Last Updated April 1, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE. Second Reading

data.author: Tabke (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 17, 2025, would change procedures for mail ballots, including requiring many applications to be returned earlier to be accepted. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require an application for a mail ballot to be submitted earlier if not submitted in person. Under existing law, an application for a mail ballot may be submitted any day up until the day before the election. Under the bill, a request for a mail ballot that is submitted by any method other than in person must be received seven days before the day of the election. Voters with disabilities and emergency responders would be exempt from this deadline.
  • Instruct county auditors and clerks to inform voters whose applications for a mail ballot are received after the deadline of the alternative opportunities to vote in the election within one day of receiving an untimely application.
  • Require a person delivering an application for a mail ballot on behalf of a voter to return it within seven days after it has been dated by the voter and no later than seven days before the date of the election. Under existing law, these deadlines are ten days and six days, respectively.
  • Require mail ballot applicants applying using the website designed for that purpose to provide the number of their Minnesota driver's license or state ID card, and the last four digits of their social security number. If they do not possess both types, they must certify a statement to that effect. Under existing law, applicants are only required to provide one of those ID numbers.
  • Provide procedures for the handling of mail ballots, including segregating them by precinct and ensuring that all steps of the process are handled by two members of the ballot board acting in concert.

Last Updated March 26, 2025

data.author: Hussein (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 20, 2025, would facilitate voter registration and mail voting from persons in a county jail. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Instruct each county sheriff to develop a plan under which eligible persons confined in jail on the day of a primary or general election could vote by mail. Each sheriff would need to designate a staff member to facilitate the process, and report to the legislature on its success every other year. An eligible voter who expresses interest in voting by mail must be permitted to contact the county auditor by phone to request materials or assistance.
  • Allow same-day voter registration on election day at a county jail. The bill would specify that a confined person's address for purposes of voter registration is the address on their intake form.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

This bill, as amended on March 26, 2026, would place a restriction on election judges who assist voters with their ballots outside the polling place. Under existing law, no restriction is placed on how many such ballots an election judge may handle at one time. Under the bill, a judge would be limited to handling one voted ballot at a time.

data.author: Freiberg (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 13, 2026, would make various changes to election administration processes and voting process.

This bill would:

  • Expand the third party individuals who may receive an absentee ballot for a voter to include a designated agent for any individual,
  • Permit election officials to not use the statewide voter registration system for town elections held in March,
  • Make election administrative procedural changes for election officials regarding record keeping, and processing received absentee ballots,
  • Increase the days for canvassing election returns, and
  • Eliminate the secretary of state's ability to sell intellectual property rights associated with the statewide voter registration system to other states or to units of local government in other states.

Last Updated May 28, 2026

data.author: Quam (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 27, 2026, would require the voter registration form to be in compliance with any relevant federal law or federal requirements. The bill would require the secretary of state to ensure local and state election officials comply with federal requirements. The bill would require the secretary to submit an annual report detailing compliance with federal requirements.


The effective date of this bill, if signed, would be following the final enactment.

Last Updated April 30, 2026

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 24, 2025, would provide that all data in the voter registration database is public information, other than a voter's date of birth, driver's license number, state ID, number, military identification card number, passport number, or any part of their Social Security number. The voter's choice of party in a primary election would also be private, except that it could be provided to the party the voter chose. The voter's name could also be withheld if necessary for the safety of the voter or their family.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced February 7, 2025, would create new photo ID requirements for in-person voting, same-day registration, provisional ballots, and mail ballot voting, among other changes. Existing law does not require voters to show ID to vote in person, and has less onerous documentation requirements for other voting-related activities than those set out in the bill. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Define accepted forms of "documentary proof of identity and residence" for use as voter ID. Accepted forms of ID would be limited to:
    • an unexpired state driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence
    • valid US military ID, with photo and current residence
    • unexpired tribal ID, with photo and current residence
    • receipt for a driver's license, state ID, or voter ID card, with photo and current residence (if receipt lacks photo, this must be accompanied by (1) a license, state ID, or voter ID that is expired or shows prior address, (2) a US passport, (3) a photo ID issued by the state or federal government, (4) a student ID issued by a postsecondary institution that has provided an enrollment list to election authorities, or (5) a tribal ID)
    • a US passport, tribal ID, or US military card that has a photo but does not have the voter's current address, plus one of the following with their address: (1) utility bill or work services order, (2) bank statement, (3) high school transcript, (4) college or university transcript issued by institution in the state, (5) unemployment stub, (6) statement from long-term health care facility, (7) insurance policy, (8) federal or state tax return, (9) property tax statement, (10) vehicle title, (11) property deed or title, (12) SSI statement, (13) mortgage document, (14) lease agreement, (15) unexpired professional license, (16) unexpired Selective Service card, (17) current military orders, (18) cell phone bill, or (19) game or fishing license.
    • for students, a state license, state ID, or voter ID card issued by any state that includes a photograph, and a current fee statement showing state residence
    • for residential facility residents, a license or state ID card, and a certification of residence through the state's official form
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID to vote in person. Under existing law, in-person voters must affirm their identity and residence under penalty of perjury, but are not required to provide documentation.
  • Require in-person voters who lack an accepted form of photo ID to cast a provisional ballot, and set out procedures related to provisional ballot voting and verification. A provisional ballot would only be counted if the voter presents required photo ID within seven days after Election Day, unless they have attempted to procure the documents and were unable to, and attest to that fact. Existing law does not provide for the use of provisional ballots.
  • Require an accepted form of photo ID for same-day registration on Election Day. Under existing law, same-day registrants are required to provide proof of residence only, by presenting a driver's license, state ID, student fee statement, or other document approved by the secretary of state, but this documentation requirement can be waived if a registered voter or residential facility employee signs an oath affirming the voter's identity, based on the affiant's personal knowledge. Under the bill, same day registrants who could not provide acceptable photo ID would only be permitted to vote a provisional ballot.
  • Require mail ballot voters to show an accepted form of photo ID to their ballot witness, who must note the specific type on the ballot certificate. Voters who have attempted to procure accepted ID and been unable to do so would be permitted to execute an affidavit asserting the explanation, which must be signed in the presence of the witness. Under existing law, mail ballot voters who are already registered must provide an ID number on the ballot certificate, but are not required to present any documentation to their witness. For mail voters who are applying to register along with their completed ballot, existing law requires these voters to provide proof of residence only. The bill would also require a ballot witness to provide their own driver's license number, state ID number, voter ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these numbers. Ballots lacking this information, or any information required on the ballot certificate, would be rejected.
  • Expressly require election administrators to verify the identification numbers provided by a mail ballot applicant, and reject any application with a number that is not verified. Existing law requires verification of ID numbers by the Secretary of State for electronic mail ballot applications, but does not have an express requirement for applications otherwise submitted.
  • Provide for free voter ID cards to any person who lacks a driver's license or state ID card. Applicants would be required to provide information including a social security number (or a certification that they do not have one), proof of citizenship, and a photo ID (unless they have a religious objection) to obtain a free voter ID card. Voter ID cards would have a unique ID number, the person's name, date of birth, address, signature, and photograph, and would have to be replaced prior to use for voting if the voter's name or address change. The bill would also provide that people who need a certified vital record can obtain the record for free if the person attests that it is to obtain a voter ID card. Use of a voter ID card number would be permitted throughout election provisions where a driver's license or state ID number is accepted under existing law.
  • Require the Secretary of State to conduct a public education campaign regarding the new photo ID requirements and availability of free voter ID cards.
  • Require the Secretary of State to report to legislative leadership, by January 2026, proposed legislation to codify any election-related matters currently subject to administrative rulemaking that could be enacted into statutory law. For matters that are not practicable in statutory law, the Secretary must propose legislation that directs specific changes in administrative rules such that no interpretation of the law is required of the Secretary and the good cause rulemaking exception would be appropriate if authorized by the legislature.
  • Extend the timeframe for canvassing ballots after primaries and elections.
  • Make various conforming changes to other sections of the election code.
  • The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated February 10, 2025

data.author: Eichorn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced February 14, 2025, would change the state's voter ID requirements. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require documentary proof of identity, in the form of voter ID, in many situations where it is not currently required, by:
    • Requiring photo ID and proof of residence to vote in person. Under existing law, voters affirm their identity and residence under penalty of perjury but are not required to provide documentation.
    • Requiring anyone lacking proof of ID or proof of residence when voting in person to cast a provisional ballot. Provisional ballots would only be counted if a voter presents photo ID and documents proving their residence within seven days after Election Day, unless the voter attempts to procure these documents but is unable to and attests to that fact.
    • Requiring photo ID for anyone registering to vote on Election Day. Under existing law, photo ID is typically required but can be waived if a registered voter or residential facility employee signs an oath affirming the voter's identity, based on the affiant's personal knowledge.
    • Requiring photo ID for any person applying to vote at the time they return an absentee ballot or a mail ballot. This provision would require that the voter show the witness signing the voter's absentee ballot photo ID to demonstrate their identity. Under existing law, the voter needed to only show the witness proof of residence. This provision would also require the witness to provide their own driver's license number, state ID number, voter ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these numbers. Ballots lacking this information would be rejected.
    • Expanding the types of ID numbers required on absentee ballot applications, absentee ballot envelopes, and mail ballot envelopes to include voter ID numbers. Under existing law, applicants must provide their driver's license number, state ID number, the last four digits of their social security number, or a statement that they do not have any of these ID numbers. This bill would require that the voter's ID number be verified and reject any application with an invalid ID number.
    • Providing free voter ID cards to any person who lacks a driver's license or state ID card. Applicants would be required to provide information including the last four digits of the applicant's social security number (or a certification that they do not have one), proof of citizenship, and proof of name, date of birth, identity, and residence address before obtaining a free voter ID card. Voter ID cards would have a unique ID number, the person's name, date of birth, address, signature, and photograph, and the card would have to be replaced if the voter's name or address changed. This bill would also provide that people who need a certified vital record can obtain the record for free if the person attests that it is to obtain a voter ID card.
    • Requiring the secretary of state to conduct a public education campaign regarding the new ID requirements of this bill and the availability of free voter ID.
  • Require an individual assisting a voter voting in person with their ballot to provide their name, address, and telephone number, and create a felony offense for assisting a voter with a ballot in a manner not authorized by law.
  • Add unduly influencing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of unduly influencing a voter, and increasing the punishment for that offense from a misdemeanor to a felony.
  • Add bribing a person to register to vote or to apply for a mail ballot to the definition of the offense of bribing a voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to annually report to legislative leadership their proposed legislation, which, according to this bill, should only be legislation that would codify matters that would otherwise be adopted through administrative rulemaking.
  • Extend the timeframe for canvassing ballots after primaries and elections.
  • Make additional conforming changes to other sections of the election code.


Last Updated February 21, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 5, 2025, tightens the deadline for submission of a mail ballot application under certain circumstances, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require an application for a mail ballot to be submitted earlier if not submitted in person. Under existing law, an application for a mail ballot may be submitted any day up until the day before the election. Under the bill, a request for a mail ballot that is submitted by any method other than in person must be received seven days before the day of the election. Voters with disabilities and emergency responders would be exempt from this deadline.
  • Require mail ballot applicants applying using the website designed for that purpose to provide the number of their Minnesota driver's license or state ID card, and the last four digits of their social security number. If they do not possess both types, they must certify a statement to that effect. Under existing law, applicants are only required to provide one of those ID numbers.
  • Instruct county auditors and clerks to inform voters whose applications for a mail ballot are received after the deadline of the alternative opportunities to vote in the election within one day of receiving an untimely application.
  • Require a person delivering an application for a mail ballot on behalf of a voter to return it within seven days after it has been dated by the voter and no later than seven days before the date of the election. Under existing law, these deadlines are ten days and six days, respectively.
  • Require that third parties who send mail ballot applications or sample ballots to voters include a statement informing the voter that the mailing is not sent by or at the request of election officials, and that the sample ballot is not an official ballot. The bill also prohibits third parties from prefilling absentee ballot applications.
  • Permit two employees at a health care facility or hospital to deliver mail ballots in place of election judges, if necessary for health and safety reasons. Both employees must be present during the entire process and return the ballot to the election judges.
  • Exempt county elections from the party balance requirement in the appointment of election judges if the election is not held in conjunction with a state election.
  • Permit electronic rosters to be combined as needed to operate a combined polling place.

Last Updated April 1, 2025

data.author: Westlin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 1, 2025, would make various minor changes to election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require mail ballots to be delivered earlier. Under existing law, mail ballots must be sent no later than 14 days before an election. Under this bill, they must be sent no later than 28 days before an election.
  • Change voter registration forms to remove the option to join the permanent mail voter list, and instead require this option to be added to an application for a mail ballot. Under existing law, mail ballot applications may include this option, but it is not required.
  • Repeal a provision of law under which the operator of a residential facility may provide a list of its employees to the county auditor for purposes of facilitating same-day registration. Under existing law, a person registering to vote during same-day voter registration may prove their residence by having an employee of a residential facility located in the precinct sign an oath vouching that the employee personally knows that the voter resides in the facility. Under the bill, this would still be possible, but the employee would be personally responsible for proving their employment at the facility, instead of their name being on a list provided by the facility's operator.
  • Clarify that students registering to vote during same-day voter registration must be permitted to provide their proof of residence during early voting and on election day.
  • Require county auditors to create a detailed chain of custody plan for each election and file it with the secretary of state. The plan would be required to account for both the physical security and cybersecurity of elections materials.
  • Require that if a federally recognized Native American tribe has off-reservation Tribal lands in a county, the county auditor must establish an early voting polling place on those lands. Existing law only requires establishment of a polling place on a reservation in the county.
  • Require each political party to provide more information about individuals who have indicated willingness to serve as an election judge, including by ensuring that their interest is recent and by providing more thorough contact information. The bill would also move the date by which parties have to supply this information from May to March of an election year, and specify that all election judges must have received training.
  • Require each appointing authority to contact all registered voters in the jurisdiction to ascertain their interest in serving as an election judge.
  • Require the secretary of state to develop and maintain an "election reporting system" - a computerized central statewide database for offices, candidates, ballot questions, and unofficial results.
  • Create new procedures for the segregation of mail ballot envelopes by precinct, verifying the number of ballots received after each day of early voting, and requiring two members of the ballot board to be responsible for counting and recording ballots. 
  • Require a city canvass to begin between the 3rd and 14th day after an election, instead of the 3rd and 10th day after as under existing law.
  • Permit the use of a new location as a combined polling place in a school board election under limited circumstances.

The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including rules for candidates.

Last Updated April 2, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 12, 2025, would make public information lists (lists containing the name, address, year of birth, and voting history of each registered voter) available with faster turnaround time. Under existing law, upon submission of a complete request for such a list, the county auditors and secretary of state are instructed to provide the requested list within ten days. Under the bill, this would be changed to two business days.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 12, 2025, would require that all activities of a ballot board be livestreamed, including the examining and acceptance or rejection of envelopes, depositing envelopes into a ballot box, and counting the ballots. All board members, ballots, and doors in the room must be under constant surveillance. If the livestream is disrupted, the bill would require the ballot board to cease its activities until recording resumes, unless election observers are also present.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 12, 2025, would provide that all data in the voter registration database is public information, other than a voter's date of birth, driver's license number, state ID, number, military identification card number, passport number, or any part of their Social Security number. The voter's choice of party in a primary election would also be private, except that it could be provided to the party the voter chose. The voter's name could also be withheld if necessary for the safety of the voter or their family.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 12, 2025, would tighten the timeline for mail voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require mail ballots to be sent to voters at least 28 days before each election. Under existing law, mail ballots are sent to voters at least 46 days before an election in most cases. UOCAVA voters would not be affected by this change. The bill would make additional conforming changes in various sections of the code to accommodate this.
  • Require mail ballots that are returned in person to be received by 3 p.m. on the day of the election to be counted. Under existing law, this deadline is 8 p.m.
  • Prohibit the public disclosure of unofficial election results if the results do not include all votes cast in the precinct, including mail voters.

Last Updated March 13, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would require certain criteria to be met before a person can be sent a mail ballot or remain on the permanent mail voter list. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all applications for a mail ballot to be electronically submitted by the county auditor or municipal clerk to the secretary of state to verify the application information matches the information in a government database associated with the applicant's driver's license number, state identification card number, or Social Security number. Existing law already requires this to happen with an application submitted electronically, but this bill would require this process to take place for all application types.
  • Require, before each primary and general election, that the secretary of state examine each person on the permanent mail voter list via this same database. If the voter cannot be verified, the voter's status must be changed to "challenged" and the voter removed from the permanent mail voter list.


Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would permit candidates, their representatives, or party official to observe the handling and counting of mail ballots. The bill would also make a recount open to public viewing.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would make a number of minor changes to election laws. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a state court administrator to provide the secretary of state, on a quarterly basis, with the names of every person who declined jury service on the grounds of not being a citizen. The secretary of state would then compare this data against the voter registration list and remove noncitizens as necessary.
  • Change the timeline for the delivery of mail ballots. Under existing law, mail ballots must be sent at least 46 days before most elections, and at least 30 days before a town election held in March. Under the bill, this would be changed to no more than 46 days and no more than 30 days, respectively.
  • Prohibit an election judge from vouching for the residence of a voter who is registering at a polling place while the judge is working. Under existing law, a same-day registrant must proof residency, and one of the methods of doing so is being vouched for by another registered voter. This bill would not permit an election judge working at the polling place from being the person to vouch for the voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide guidance to residential facilities on the "vouching" process.
  • Provide that no more than half the election judges in a place where ballots are being counted, recounted, or reviewed may be affiliated with the same party. This already applies to precinct polling places under existing law.
  • Differentiate between election challengers and election observers, who may observe an election without issuing challenges.
  • Require the governing body of each municipality to create chain of custody procedures for ballots.
  • Require party chairs to provide names of potential election judges to serve as alternates or replacements, as necessary.
  • Require the secretary of state to operate a telephone number election judges may call to receive advice on resolving issues.
  • Provide clarity on list maintenance procedures.

The bill also contains changes to provisions of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including election equipment and candidate affidavits.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would make various changes to voter registration requirements, including eliminating the requirement that voters provide proof of residency when registering to vote through same-day registration or when submitting a mail ballot. Under existing law, these methods of registration require either documentation or personal vouching of a voter's current residence. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change same-day voter registration processes by:
    • Eliminating the requirements that a same-day voter registration applicant must make an oath and provide proof of residency or have their residence personally vouched for, and instead require only the standard registration form (which an applicant must certify under penalty of perjury). The bill would also require same-day registrants to vote a newly created "provisional ballot," and set out procedures for casting, verifying, rejecting, and counting these ballots. The provisional ballots of same-day registrants would be counted if the registration application was accepted, and voters whose ballots were rejected would receive notice following the election. Existing law permits voters who meet the requirements for same-day registration to vote a regular ballot, and does not provide for the use of provisional ballots generally.
    • Requiring that any same-day registration applications be entered into the voter registration system within three days after Election Day. Under existing law, they must be entered within 42 days after Election Day.
  • Change requirements for registering to vote by mail by:
    • Eliminating the requirement that applicants who register to vote by mail must include their driver's license number, state ID number, or the last four digits of their social security number.
    • Changing the process for correcting incomplete registration applications submitted by mail. Under existing law, if a mail registration applicant (1) has not provided a form of documentation required for same day registration, (2) has not voted previously in the state in a federal election, and (3) officials are unable to verify their driver's license, state ID, or Social Security number, the applicant must either register in person or meet the requirements for same day registration (including documentation or personal vouching of residency). The bill would simplify the process by requiring, where a mail applicant's eligibility to vote cannot be verified, that the individual submit a general registration application in person, on Election Day, or with a completed mail ballot.
  • Change processes for voters who simultaneously return a mail ballot and apply to vote by:
    • Eliminating the requirement that voters who are simultaneously registering to vote and returning an absentee ballot present proof of residence to the witness for the absentee ballot.
    • Requiring election officials to open ballot envelopes to determine if there is a voter registration application in the envelope. If so, then the application must be removed and the envelope resealed. The auditor would then promptly process the application. Under existing law, the voter registration application is processed by the absentee ballot board during absentee ballot processing.
  • Expressly limit the optional "alternative procedure" for in-person early voting at the county auditor's office in the seven days before an election to only those voters who are already registered to vote. As a result, voters would not be permitted to do same-day registration for this form of voting. Existing law is silent as to whether this procedure may be offered to unregistered voters.
  • Change requirements for challengers by allowing challengers to prove their residency by presenting a student ID or a student fee statement in addition to the other forms of ID already named in statute.
  • Repeal the provision concerning postsecondary institutions submitting enrollment lists to election officials to facilitate proof of residency requirements for students, and the provision requiring election officials to record the number of same-day registration applications that are rejected due to lack of residency proof.
  • Require the secretary of state to conduct a public awareness campaign to encourage people to register to vote before Election Day.

The bill also contains provisions that are not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including changes to the time for canvassing primary and general elections.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Champion (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would facilitate voter registration and mail voting from persons in a county jail. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Instruct each county sheriff to develop a plan under which eligible persons confined in jail on the day of a primary or general election could vote by mail. Each sheriff would need to designate a staff member to facilitate the process, and report to the legislature on its success every other year. An eligible voter who expresses interest in voting by mail must be permitted to contact the county auditor by phone to request materials or assistance.
  • Allow same-day voter registration on election day at a county jail. The bill would specify that a confined person's address for purposes of voter registration is the address on their intake form.


Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Boldon (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would require notice of a closed polling place to be conspicuously posted at the location. The notice would be required to inform voters of the new location in as conspicuous a manner as possible, and must remain posted until the next general election or redistricting occurs.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 21, 2025, would require that any mail ballot application or sample ballot sent by anyone other than an election official must include a statement that it is not from the government, that it was not sent at the request of the government, and that any sample ballot is not an official ballot that may be cast. Mail ballot applications sent by third parties would not be permitted to be preprinted with the voter's information.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

data.author: Westlin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 7, 2026 would make multiple changes to election administration processes. This bill would:

  • Entitle students to provide the required additional residence documentation in the 18 days prior to the election;
  • Expand which Tribal lands an auditor may provide for an additional polling place to include off-reservation Tribal lands;
  • Change the process for opening and processing received absentee ballots;
  • Align vote total return statements of state and local elections;
  • Require the county auditor to develop a chain of custody plan for election related materials; and
  • Increase the time mail-in absentee voters have to receive their ballot by requiring the county auditor to send ballots 28 days before the election rather than 14 days as currently in code.

Certain parts of the bill's effective date would be after final enactment while other parts would be effective following certain legislative procedural rules.

data.author: Cwodzinski (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 17, 2026, would require the secretary of state to prepare and distribute a comprehensive voter information guide prior to each statewide general election. Existing law has no comparable provision. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the secretary of state to mail a printed copy of the guide to every household with at least one registered voter, as well as to all public libraries and public colleges and universities in the state. This mailing must occur no later than 53 days before the general election.
  • Require the secretary of state to post an electronic copy of the guide on their website no later than 53 days before the election.
  • Establish language and accessibility requirements. The guide must be written in English and the three most commonly spoken non-English languages in the state, as determined by the state demographer. The secretary of state must also prepare an English-language audio recording of the guide to be provided to any voter upon request without cost.
  • Establish content requirements for the guide. The guide must include:
    • Voter eligibility and registration requirements, and may also include a voter registration form or an application for a mail ballot.
    • Information on proposed state constitutional amendments, including the full text with clear indicators of added or deleted language, and the legislative vote totals broken down by political party.
    • Statements and photographs submitted by candidates for specified federal, state, and judicial offices.

The bill also contains provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab concerning certain requirements for candidate statements.

Last Updated March 25, 2026

data.author: Westlin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 23, 2026, would clarify several election administrative processes. The bill would:

  • Provide clerks discretion to use the statewide registration system as it relates to town elections;
  • change the procedures for opening and processing absentee ballots;
  • require candidates to sign an attestation to comply with the US Constitution qualifications to hold office;
  • clarify canvassing procedure for determining the winner of a contest;
  • Increase penalties for certain election crimes. The bill would increase penalties for interfering with election officials while working and interfering with the election process. The bill would change the penalty from a gross misdemeanor to a felony and would increase the fine from 1,000 up to 10,000.

Some provisions of the bill would become effective August 1, 2026.


Parts of this bill also covers topics that VRL does not track.

Last Updated April 29, 2026

data.author: Dibble (DFL)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 17, 2026, would permit the possible use of mobile voting in the state. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit the use of mobile voting technology if the voting system has been approved for use, uses encryption and other security features, and can display the correct ballot.
  • Permit a voter residing in a jurisdiction which has authorized the use of mobile voting technology to request that their balloting materials be delivered electronically, which the voter would then be able to vote and return electronically.

Last Updated March 25, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Westrom (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 9, would make significant changes to registration and voter ID. The bill would require individuals registering to vote on Election Day to provide evidence of US citizenship, identity, and residence and provides which documents satisfy these requirements. The bill would establish a challenge processes for individuals who fail to provide proof of citizenship. The bill would permit those who fail to provide documentation of US citizenship, identity, and residence may vote a provisional ballot. The bill would establish a free voter identification card process for eligible voters. The bill would require the Secretary of State to pay the DMV for every voter ID application submitted.


The bill would change requirements for certain absentee voters and witnesses to absentee voters. The bill would require a witness to a certain absentee voters to see documentation of citizenship, identity, and residence and record the type of documentation received before signing as a witness.


The bill would change early voting procedures to permit election officials to ask for documentation of citizenship and residence.


The bill would require any issued REAL ID to have the notation "US Citizen" for applicants that have provided proof of citizenship.

Last Updated April 20, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Koran (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 27, 2026, would require the voter registration form to be in compliance with any relevant federal law or federal requirements. The bill would require the secretary of state to ensure local and state election officials comply with federal requirements. The bill would require the secretary to submit an annual report detailing compliance with federal requirements.


The effective date of this bill, if signed, would be following the final enactment.

Last Updated April 30, 2026

data.author: Matthiesen (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would expand challenger access during the early voting period. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Authorize election challengers to be present at voting locations at any time ballots are being cast on Election Day or, in a first class or charter county, during the early voting period. Under existing statute, watchers are only entitled to be present until all ballots are cast on Election Day.
  • Provide clearer procedures for the appointment of watchers. Under the new procedures, appointments must be submitted to the local election authority at least four business days before the watcher is to appear at a voting location. The authority must evaluate the watcher's eligibility and give the appointing party time to name a substitute.

data.author: Nolte M (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 10, 2026, would:

  • Require the Department of Health and Senior Services to provide the secretary of state, on a monthly basis, with a report of every death certificate filed in the preceding month. Existing law requires monthly death reporting by state or local registrars of vital statistics but does not specifically require such data sharing from the Department of Health and Senior Services.
  • Require the secretary of state to notify appropriate local election authorities of deaths identified by the Department of Health and Senior Services within 10 days of receiving the report.
  • Require local election authorities to remove from the voter rolls any individuals identified as deceased through notifications from the secretary of state within 10 days of the notice.

Last Updated March 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: *****To GOVERNOR

data.author: McGaugh P (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 12, 2026, would:

  • Require that any list of applications for an absentee ballot be kept confidential and not posted or displayed in any area open to the general public or provided to any unauthorized individual.
  • Specify that existing ID requirements for regular in person voting also apply to individuals who appear to vote absentee in person. Under existing law, voters who appear at a polling place without specified form of ID must voter provisionally, but there is no specific requirement for absentee voting in person.
  • Specify that if an absentee voter is required to cast a provisional ballot, they must return to the election authority's office by 7 p.m. on Election Day with the required ID. Existing law does not include a specific requirement for in person absentee voters.
  • Narrow felony disenfranchisement for individuals on probation or parole by specifying that a person is not entitled to vote while confined under a sentence of imprisonment, and while on probation or parole after conviction of certain violent and sexual felony offenses. Existing law bars voting while a person is confined under a sentence of imprisonment, and while on probation or parole after conviction of all felony offenses, and any felony or misdemeanor connected with the right of suffrage, which the bill leaves in place.
  • Designate as August 12 as annual "Election Worker Appreciation Day" in the state and encourage citizens to participate in events and activities to celebrate the essential contributions of election workers and express appreciation for the role they play in elections.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates, lobbyist, and filling vacancies.

Last Updated June 9, 2026

data.author: Cook (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would eliminate an existing misdemeanor election offense that criminalizes a voter allowing their ballot to be seen by another person with the intent of revealing how the voter is about to vote or has voted.

data.author: Simmons J (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would prevent voters from changing their party registration to participate in a closed primary unless the voter changes their affiliation at least 23 weeks before the primary. Any change in affiliation closer to the primary would not be effective until after the primary election. New voters, however, would be able to submit a voter registration application and choose a party affiliation until the fourth Wednesday prior to the primary and be eligible to vote in the primary. Under existing law, primaries are open to all registered voters, not simply ones affiliated with the party conducting the primary. In addition, the fourth Wednesday before an election is the ordinary voter registration deadline. Finally, the bill would allow parties that wish to conduct a caucus or an open primary to do so at the party's own expense.

Last Updated January 22, 2026

data.author: Stinnett (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 3, 2026, would narrow the scope of disenfranchisement due to criminal convictions, by limiting general disenfranchisement to the period of incarceration. Existing law provides for disenfranchisement during incarceration, probation, or parole after a felony, as well as for any offense related to voting (which the bill leaves in place). 

Last Updated March 5, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on EMERGING ISSUES

data.author: Appelbaum (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would create automatic voter registration during transactions at many voter registration agencies. Missouri does not currently have automatic voter registration at any agency. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create automatic voter registration during certain transactions at a variety of state agencies, which include:
    • the department of revenue,
    • the department of social services,
    • local housing authorities,
    • the University of Missouri system,
    • the department of corrections,
    • the department of labor,
    • the department of health and senior services,
    • the bureau of vital records,
    • local health departments,
    • recorders of deeds, and
    • any other agency designated by the secretary of state.
  • Require the agencies performing automatic voter registration to provide a name, address, date of birth, proof of citizenship or attestation, ID number (driver's license number, state ID number, or last four digits of social security number), and the applicant's signature to the secretary of state for each applicant at least monthly. If the agency does not have the applicant's signature, the secretary of state will create a process to ensure that the person's signature is captured during the voting process.
  • Require the secretary of state to develop forms and processes for agencies, including a script to notify voters that registration is optional but a voter must expressly opt out to avoid being registered to vote.
  • Require the secretary of state to send a notice to all eligible applicants permitting the applicant to opt out of voter registration and explaining that a failure to respond within 45 days will result in the applicant being registered to vote. This notice would also be required to inform applicants that they must opt out if they do not meet voting eligibility requirements. The postage for the return form will be paid by the secretary of state.
  • Clarify that a voter does not commit a class one election offense simply because incorrect information about the voter is in the statewide voter registration system unless the voter knowingly or willingly supplied the incorrect information.

data.author: Weber (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would change requirements for soliciting voter registration applications and mandate provision of registration information during real estate sales and leases. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require each election authority to make a voter registration information packet, including a voter registration form, available to realtors and landlords, and require them to provide the information packet to home purchasers and tenants respectively, on closing a real estate sale or lease.
  • Eliminate the prohibition on compensation of individuals, other than by a governmental entity, for soliciting voter registration applications.
  • Require individuals to register as a voter registration solicitor if they are paid or otherwise compensated for soliciting ten or more voter registration applications, and require the registrant to indicate if they expect to be paid and if so, the identity of the payor. Under existing law, individuals may not be paid or otherwise compensated to solicit applications unless by a governmental entity, and must register as a voter registration solicitor only if they solicit ten or more applications. Note that this provision would, in effect, narrow the existing felony offense for failing to register as a registration solicitor of ten or more applicants, by making it apply only if the solicitor was compensated for 10+ applications.

data.author: Perkins (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2026, would:

  • Establish a proof of citizenship requirement, mandating that DMV officials only offer an opportunity to register to vote to individuals who provide documentation demonstrating U.S. citizenship. Under existing law, DMV officials must not offer an individual the opportunity to register to vote if their documentation affirmatively establishes noncitizenship. The bill would also add a provision that gives individuals whose documentation suggests noncitizenship to provide appropriate documentation that otherwise establishes citizenship.
  • Specify that the data shared between the secretary of state and the director of the department of revenue should include information pertaining to citizenship status.
  • Require the secretary of state and the director of the department of revenue to create a process for the DMV to use certain personal information to determine whether the statwide voter registration database indicates whether a person is already registered and whether address records match.

Last Updated February 9, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Simmons J (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 10, 2026, would:

  • Require the secretary of state to enter into and use the federal Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) program to verify the citizenship status of registered voters and applicants, including conducting checks at least twice annually and upon each registration when data indicates potential noncitizenship. Existing law does not require use of the SAVE program. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require notice to voters before officials can take any action based on SAVE data. Election officials would be required to provide voters at least 30 days to respond with acceptable proof of citizenship.
    • Prohibit removals within 90 days of a federal election, except at the voter's request or with a final judgment of in eligibility.
    • Require the secretary of state to establish uniform procedures, evidence standards, reporting requirements, and what what qualifies as acceptable evidence.
  • Require each circuit court clerk to submit monthly reports to the secretary of state identifying individuals who self-report noncitizenship during jury qualification. The secretary of state would subsequently send the names of those voters to the local election authority to remove those individuals from the list and mail them a notice of such action.. Existing law does not include a comparable reporting requirement.
  • Prohibit election authorities from “curing” absentee or mail ballots after they are returned, and require rejection of ballots missing a signature, notarization, attestation, or other required information. Election officials would be required to notify voters of rejection within four business days and inform them of options to vote in person or by provisional ballot. The bill defines curing "any post-return contact, outreach, or solicitation by an election authority or its agent inviting or permitting a voter to add, change, or supply information or a signature to make a returned absentee ballot or mail-in ballot countable." Missouri law does not provide a statewide process for curing problems with absentee ballots but the state also does not include a uniform prohibition on curing.
  • Establish the Office of Election Crimes and Security. The office would be designed to respond to notifications and complaints of election law violations, initiate investigations, and oversee a voter fraud hotline. Any violations would be submitted to the attorney general and the prosecuting attorney in the jurisdiction.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to recounts.

Last Updated March 18, 2026

data.author: O'Laughlin (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would require documentary proof of United States citizenship as a condition of voter registration, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all applicants for voter registration to submit documentary proof of United States citizenship at the time of application, regardless of the registration method, including in person, by mail, through voter registration agencies, or through the Department of Revenue. If an application is submitted without documentary proof of citizenship, election authorities must reject the application and notify the applicant that registration cannot be completed until such documentation is provided. If proof is submitted by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day, the applicant would be deemed registered as of the date the original application was received. Existing law allows applicants to register using certain forms of identification that do not necessarily establish citizenship and relies in part on attestation.
  • Specify that individuals already on the voter registration list, or seeking to transfer their registration within Missouri are not required to submit documentary proof of citizenship unless their registration has previously been stricken.
  • Define “documentary proof of United States citizenship” to include the following specified documents:
    • Any document that complies with the federal REAL ID Act and indicates that the individual is a United States citizen
    • United States passport
    • United States military ID, if accompanied by a United States military record of service showing United States birth
    • Photo ID card issued by the United States government or the state of Missouri showing that the individual’s place of birth was in the United States
    • Certified birth certificate meeting specified statutory requirements
    • Hospital birth record created at the time of the individual’s birth in the United States
    • Final adoption decree stating that the individual was born in the United States
    • Consular report of birth abroad or a certification of birth of a United States citizen issued by the United States secretary of state
    • Naturalization certificate or certificate of citizenship issued by the United States Department of Homeland Security or other proof of citizenship issued pursuant to the federal Immigration and Nationality Act
    • American Indian card issued by the federal Department of Homeland Security with the classification “KIC”
  • Prohibit the Department of Revenue from offering voter registration as part of a driver’s license or nondriver identification transaction when an applicant presents documentation establishing noncitizenship. Existing law does not expressly bar voter registration opportunities based on documentation presented during licensing transactions.
  • Require court clerks to to send a list to the secretary of state on a monthly basis containing the names of anyone who indicated they are not qualified to jury service due to noncitizenship.
  • Require local election authorities to provide an affidavit to the attorney general, secretary of state, and the appropriate prosecuting or circuit attorney if an ineligible individual voted or registered to vote.
  • Make conforming changes to reflect new requirements.

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to recounts and election equipment.

Last Updated January 14, 2026

data.author: Schroer (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would require documentary proof of United States citizenship as a condition of voter registration and make related changes to voter list maintenance, election law enforcement referrals, and ballot counting. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all applicants for voter registration to submit documentary proof of United States citizenship at the time of application, regardless of the registration method, including in person, by mail, through voter registration agencies, or through the Department of Revenue. If an application is submitted without documentary proof of citizenship, election authorities must reject the application and notify the applicant that registration cannot be completed until such documentation is provided. If proof is submitted by 7:00 p.m. on Election Day, the applicant would be deemed registered as of the date the original application was received. Existing law allows applicants to register using certain forms of identification that do not necessarily establish citizenship and relies in part on attestation.
  • Specify that individuals already on the voter registration list, or seeking to transfer their registration within Missouri are not required to submit documentary proof of citizenship unless their registration has previously been stricken.
  • Define “documentary proof of United States citizenship” to include the following specified documents:
    • Any document that complies with the federal REAL ID Act and indicates that the individual is a United States citizen
    • United States passport
    • United States military ID, if accompanied by a United States military record of service showing United States birth
    • Photo ID card issued by the United States government or the state of Missouri showing that the individual’s place of birth was in the United States
    • Certified birth certificate meeting specified statutory requirements
    • Hospital birth record created at the time of the individual’s birth in the United States
    • Final adoption decree stating that the individual was born in the United States
    • Consular report of birth abroad or a certification of birth of a United States citizen issued by the United States secretary of state
    • Naturalization certificate or certificate of citizenship issued by the United States Department of Homeland Security or other proof of citizenship issued pursuant to the federal Immigration and Nationality Act
    • American Indian card issued by the federal Department of Homeland Security with the classification “KIC”
  • Prohibit the Department of Revenue from offering voter registration as part of a driver’s license or nondriver identification transaction when an applicant presents documentation establishing noncitizenship. Existing law does not expressly bar voter registration opportunities based on documentation presented during licensing transactions.
  • Require all ballot counting, whether conducted by hand or by automatic tabulating equipment, to be recorded and live-streamed on the website of the relevant election authority. Existing law requires public counting but does not require electronic recording or live-streaming.
  • Make conforming changes to reflect new requirements.

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to recounts, election holidays, and election equipment.

Last Updated January 13, 2026

data.author: Mosley (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would establish a cure process for defective mail ballots. Under existing law, Missouri does not have a process by which a mail voter may cure a defect in their mail ballot. Under this bill, a voter whose mail ballot is required to be rejected for any reason must be notified of the rejection by the election authority. A voter whose ballot was rejected for a failure to complete a statement on the ballot envelope, to sign the oath, an invalid signature, or to have the signature verified in the manner provided by law would then have until the end of the period for verifying provisional ballots to submit a copy of their ID and an affidavit verifying that they cast the ballot. If the affidavit is sufficient, the ballot would then be counted.

data.author: Scott O (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 10, 2025, would establish same-day voter registration on Election Day. Under existing law, the voter registration deadline is 30 days before Election Day. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Authorize local election officials to designate one location within the jurisdiction for same-day registration through the Statewide Elections Management System. This system would be checked before entering the registrant as a new voter. A newly accepted voter would be sent an address confirmation notice to the new registrant at their residential address.
  • Require same-day registrants to:
    • appear in person at the designated location,
    • declare under oath that they had not previously voted in the election,
    • present an ID showing the applicant's residential address, and
    • show the required photo ID to vote in person (which could be the same as the residence-confirming document).
  • Allow same-day registrants to cast an "election day ballot," which would be in an envelope clearly designated as a same-day registration ballot and containing an affirmation that the voter had not previously voted and had accurately and truthfully registered. Election day ballots would be stored and transported separately but would be counted on Election Day in the same manner as absentee ballots.
  • Direct officials to mail address confirmation notices to same-day registrants. 


This bill would take effect on July 1, 2026

status_actions.summary: Died in committee

data.author: Scott O (D)
data.public_commentary:
The bill as introduced on January 7, 2026, would: 

  • Automatically restore voting rights to individuals disenfranchised because of certain criminal convictions upon completion of their sentence. The bill specifically prescribes these disenfranchising crimes as suspending an individual’s right to vote. Under existing law, voters convicted of any disenfranchising crimes are only eligible for rights restoration upon a 2/3 vote of the legislature, being granted a pardon, or being granted a restoration of rights by the governor.
  • Require the circuit clerk of each county to keep an updated list of disenfranchising crimes both in law and any crime interpreted as disenfranchising by an attorney general opinion. 
  • Require election administration process changes to reflect automatic restoration.
  • Establish new reporting requirements from the secretary of state.

data.author: Scott O (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would establish online voter registration for first-time voters. Under existing law, online voter registration services are only available for already-registered voters who need to update information in their existing voter record. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Authorize first-time registrants to use the existing system to register to vote, provided that the applicant has a current and valid Mississippi driver's license or photo ID card.
  • Grant applicants whose registration applications are rejected the same notice and review rights for their rejected applications as applicants who use existing registration methods.
  • Require local election officials to provide notice of successful registration to new voters by email, if an email address is on file, in addition to the requirement under existing law that notice be given by mail.
  • Specify that the date of registration for a first-time voter using this online registration proposed by this bill will be the date the completed application is submitted.
  • Require that instructions for online registration are provided by county registrars to each public school alongside paper registration forms, which they already are required to provide twice a school year.

data.author: Scott O (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 7, 2026, would automatically restore voting rights to people disenfranchised because of criminal convictions (including vote fraud) upon completion of their sentence. Under existing law, voters convicted of any disenfranchising crimes are only eligible for rights restoration upon a 2/3 vote of the legislature, being granted a pardon, or being granted a restoration of rights by the governor. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Restore voting rights to any person convicted of a disenfranchising conviction automatically after completion of their sentence (including parole for a disqualifying conviction). The bill would specify that the right to vote is restored immediately if a person is sentenced to probation only.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide training to election commissioners on the voting rights of people whose voting rights have been restored. The bill would also require related annual training for registrars.
  • Require the secretary of state to provide local election officials with regular reports of voters who have been convicted of disenfranchising crimes in the county and require the report to indicate whether the sentence imposed is a term of incarceration or probation.
  • Require the voter registration application and instructions to indicate that a person whose rights have been restored is not disqualified from registering to vote.
