data.bill_name: Donald J Trump Early Voting Act

status_actions.summary: Died in committee

data.author: Scott O (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2026, would establish in-person early voting and modify requirements for mail voting. Under existing law, only voters with a qualifying excuse may vote prior to Election Day, and such voters must do so by mail. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish an early voting period. Specifically:
    • Early voting would begin 14 days before Election Day and end at 5 p.m. on the Saturday before Election Day.
    • Voting would be available at registrars' offices during regular business hours, except that during the last full week before Election Day, registrars could choose to extend their office hours.
    • Registrars' offices would be required to open from 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. on the two Saturdays immediately preceding an election, although only the latter Saturday would fall within the early voting period.
    • Voting would be conducted in the same manner as Election Day voting, including the photo ID requirements, voter assistance, and the rights of poll watchers.
    • Registrars would be permitted to provide additional early voting locations, and required to do so for every 30,000 registered voters in the county and 10,000 registered voters in a municipality.
    • If a county registrar's office was poorly suited to accommodate early voting, the county could conduct early voting elsewhere, so long as they posted a notice at the registrar's office regarding the voting location.
    • Officials would be required to post notice of the early voting locations in at least three places in each county, including at the county courthouse.
    • Early voting would have to be conducted via a system that allows ballots to be examined by a candidate and for certification and audit purposes.
  • Repeal qualifying excuses for mail voting covering:
    • Voters outside of their county of residence on Election Day, instead requiring absence from the county on all days on which in-person voting is available.
  • Establish a new excuse for emergency response providers deployed during a state of emergency.
  • Establish a mail ballot application deadline of seven days before Election Day. Under existing law, there is no application deadline.
  • Adjust voter list maintenance timing to require that certain removals be completed prior the start of early voting.
  • Make a variety of conforming changes to reflect the addition of in-person early voting.

status_actions.summary: Died in committee

data.author: Osborne (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 9, 2026, would establish in-person early voting and modify requirements for mail voting. Under existing law, only voters with a qualifying excuse may vote prior to Election Day, and such voters must do so by mail. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish an early voting period. Specifically:
    • Early voting at the office of the registrar would begin seven days before each election and continue until 12:00 p.m. on the Saturday before Election Day. The bill would require registrar offices to remain open from 8:00 a.m. until 12:00 p.m. on the two Saturdays immediately preceding each election and permit extended office hours during the final full week before the election.
    • County or municipal election commissioners would be able to designate additional early voting polling places outside the office of the registrar and establish an expanded early voting period at those locations beginning fifteen days before each election, with voting conducted from 8:00 a.m. until 5:00 p.m. Monday through Friday, from 10:00 a.m. until 4:00 p.m. on the Saturday immediately preceding Election Day, and from 8:00 a.m. until 5:00 p.m. on the Monday immediately preceding Election Day.
    • Voting would be conducted in the same manner as Election Day voting, including the photo ID requirements, voter assistance, and the rights of poll watchers.
    • Registrars would be permitted to provide additional early voting locations, and required to do so for every 30,000 registered voters in the county and 10,000 registered voters in a municipality.
    • If a county registrar's office was poorly suited to accommodate early voting, the county could conduct early voting elsewhere, so long as they posted a notice on the door of the registrar's office regarding the voting location.
    • Officials would be required to post the early voting locations in at least three places in each county, including at the county courthouse.
    • Early voting would have to be conducted via a system that allows ballots to be examined by a candidate and for certification and audit purposes.
  • Repeal qualifying excuses for mail voting covering:
    • Voters outside of their county of residence on Election Day, instead requiring absence from the county on all days on which in-person voting is available.
    • Voters required to be at work during all Election Day voting hours.
  • Establish a new excuse for emergency response providers deployed during a state of emergency.
  • Establish a mail ballot application deadline of seven days before Election Day. Under existing law, there is no application deadline.
  • Repeal the requirement that the county clerk serve as the attesting notary for the ballot envelopes of mail voters, other than the disabled or elderly, who complete their ballot while physically in the county.
  • Repeal a requirement that mail voters serving in the armed forces must have their affidavits attested to by an officer no lower in rank than a sergeant.
  • Adjust voter list maintenance timing to require that certain removals be completed prior the start of early voting.
  • Make a variety of conforming changes to reflect the addition of in-person early voting.

data.author: McCray (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 12, 2026, would establish in-person early voting and modify rules for mail voting. Under existing law, there is no generally available method for voting prior to Election Day. Specifically the bill would:

  • Establish a one-week early voting period for general, primary, and municipal elections. Under this system:
    • Early voting would begin 10 days prior to the date of an election and end at 5 p.m. on the Saturday preceding the election
    • Early voting would be conducted in the office of the county registrar during regular business hours unless the location is insufficient or inconvenient to accommodate voting. In such cases, registrars could provide an alternate location in lieu of their office.
    • Registrars would designate one additional early voting per 30,000 registered county voters or 10,000 registered municipal voters.
    • Voter ID requirements and designation of observers would function as they do on Election Day.
    • Specify that a designation of a person to collect a mail ballot or mail ballot application at a nursing home who is not a relative of the voter must be done in writing. Existing law requires that such individuals be designated by the voter but does not require the designation to be in writing.
    • Numerous other conforming changes would be made in recognition of the existence of early voting.
  • Modify qualifying excuses for mail voting. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Narrow excuses requiring physical absence from the county on Election Day to require that the voter be absent both on Election Day and during the entire early voting period in order to qualify.
    • Eliminate a dedicated qualifying excuse for students, teachers, and administrators at educational institutions, in addition to their spouses, whose study or employment requires absence from their county of registration.
    • Establish a dedicated qualifying excuse for emergency response providers deployed due to a declared state of emergency during early voting and on Election Day.
  • Modify mail voting procedures in other ways. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Expand a requirement that voters requesting to vote by mail due to being permanently physically disabled voters obtain and supply a note from a physician or nurse practitioner to also apply to voters doing so based on a temporary physical disability.
    • Allow voters with a temporary physical disability to apply to additionally receive a ballot for any potential corresponding runoff via a single application.
    • Eliminate a requirement that voters who are neither over 65 years of age nor disabled completing their mail ballots within their county of registration use the registrar as their witness on the mail ballot envelope. Mail voters would continue to be required to have a witness sign their return envelopes.
    • Eliminate a requirement that a mail voter who is serving in the armed forces have their ballot envelopes witnessed by an officer.
    • Direct officials to send voters mail ballot applications prior to sending out the actual ballot, if time allows. Under existing law, applications and ballots are sent simultaneously upon receipt by officials of a request.
    • Repeal a provision directing the secretary of state to prepare instructions for mail voters to comply with ID requirements.
    • Add a disclaimer regarding penalties for voter fraud and voter intimidation to the mail ballot return envelope.
    • Repeal a provision prescribing the precise wording of the affidavit printed on mail ballot envelopes.
    • Clarify that ballots requested by a voter temporarily residing outside their county of registration are to be mailed to the voter's temporary address.
  • Raise the maximum fine for a voter intimidation conviction from $1,000 to $3,000. The maximum jail sentence would continue to be one year.

data.author: Nelson (D)
data.public_commentary:
The bill, as introduced on January 12, 2026 would: 

  •  Automatically restore voting rights to people disenfranchised because of certain criminal convictions upon completion of their sentence. The bill specifically prescribes these disenfranchising crimes as suspending an individual’s right to vote. Under existing law, voters convicted of any disenfranchising crimes are only eligible for rights restoration upon a 2/3 vote of the legislature, being granted a pardon, or being granted a restoration of rights by the governor.
  • Require the circuit clerk of each county to keep an updated list of individuals convicted of any crime interpreted as disenfranchising by attorney general opinion in addition to disenfranchising crimes under the law. 
  • Require election administration process changes to reflect automatic restoration.
  • Require new reporting requirements from the secretary of state.

data.author: Sanford (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 10, 2026, would:

  • Require a citizenship check on individuals who did not provide their driver's license and the Department of Public Safety is unable to pull the information;
  • Expand the state's limited proof of citizenship requirement. The bill would require applicants for voter registration to provide proof of citizenship if either the check against the state drivers license database or the federal SAVE database suggests the individual is not a U.S. citizen. Under existing law, registrants must provide proof of citizenship only if both of these checks produce flags.
  • Direct officials to compare the entire voter roll to the SAVE database twice a year, with at least one check occurring no later than 180 days before a federal election. Existing law required a one-time check against the SAVE database by January 2025, along with the checks for new registrants.

data.author: Simmons D (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 19, would establish in person early voting 15 days before an election or runoff. The bill would change certain processes for absentee mail voting for administration purposes.


This bill would:

  • Require in person early voting to be accessible for at least 10 hours a day and include 8:00 AM and 6:00 PM availability;
  • Require in person early voting on Saturdays and Sundays;
  • Permit additional in person early voting locations;
  • Require the clerk's office to be used for the conduct of in person early voting; and
  • Require public posting of voting locations no less than 8 days before the election.

This bill would amend the mail absentee ballot process. The bill would require the clerk to mail the voter their absentee ballot application and ballot. Under existing law, a voter may request an absentee ballot application and vote in person by absentee at the clerk's office. The bill would make multiple changes administratively to align with this new process.


This bill would add two excuses for mail in absentee ballot specifically for emergency response providers and Mississippi voters who would temporarily reside outside the county during the in person early voting period or Election Day. The bill would eliminate two excuses for students and academics, and individuals working on Election Day.


The bill would decrease the time an individual may request an absentee ballot to no later than 7 days before the election. Under current law, a voter may request an absentee ballot application until the Saturday immediately preceding the Election.



Last Updated February 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: Died in committee

data.author: Hickman (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 19, 2026, would establish 15 days of in-person early voting for all elections in the state.


The bill would:

  • Permit in-person early voting until 12 P.M. the Saturday immediately preceding the election
  • Require in-person early voting during regular business hours at the clerk's office
  • Permit clerk's to extend the hours of in-person early voting to include lunch time and until 7 P.M.
  • Require in-person early voting to be offered 8 - 12 PM on the 2 Saturdays immediately preceding the election
  • Make conforming election administrative changes for in-person early voting
  • Require that all mail ballots be delivered, rather than being available in person at the clerk's office
  • Add 2 absentee ballot application excuses for individuals temporarily residing outside of their locality and emergency responder deployed due to a state of emergency declared by the President of the United States
  • Eliminate 3 absentee ballot application excuses for individuals outside the county on Election Day, students, teachers, administrators at university or community college who would absent on Election Day; and individuals working on Election Day and unable to vote.
  • Eliminate the ability to vote in person absentee at the clerk's office and makes conforming changes.

This bill would be effective January 1, 2027 if signed.

Last Updated May 22, 2026

data.author: England (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 12, would establish in person absentee voting 22 days before an election or runoff. The bill would change certain processes for absentee mail voting for administration purposes. 


This bill would:

  • Require in person early voting to be accessible during business hours and permit clerks to include lunch hour and until 7:00 PM the week preceding the election; 
  • Require in person early voting on Saturdays from 8:00 AM - noon
  • Prohibit additional in person early voting locations;
  • Require the clerk's office to be used for the conduct of in person early voting or another facility; and
  • Require public posting of voting locations no less than 8 days before the election.

This bill would amend the mail absentee ballot process. The bill would require the clerk to mail the voter their absentee ballot application and ballot. Under existing law, a voter may request an absentee ballot application and vote in person by absentee at the clerk's office. The bill would make multiple changes administratively to align with this new process. 


This bill would add two excuses for mail in absentee ballot specifically for emergency response providers and Mississippi voters who would temporarily reside outside the county during the in person early voting period or Election Day. The bill would eliminate two excuses for students and academics, and individuals working on Election Day. 


The bill would decrease the time an individual may request an absentee ballot to no later than 7 days before the election. Under current law, a voter may request an absentee ballot application until the Saturday immediately preceding the Election. 


This bill would repeal absentee envelope and application for voters voting in person absentee.

Last Updated March 10, 2026

status_actions.summary: Signed by GOVERNOR

data.author: England (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 1, 2026, requires applicants for voter registration to provide proof of citizenship if their citizenship cannot be confirmed via Department of Public Safety (DPS) driver's license data. Specifically, the bill:

  • Requires applicants for voter registration to provide proof of citizenship if any of the following is true:
    • DPS information indicates the applicant is a non-citizen
    • The applicant does not provide a Mississippi driver's license number and officials are unable to retrieve one using provided information
    • The federal SAVE system indicates the individual is not a U.S. citizen. SAVE is only checked if the applicant's citizenship cannot be confirmed with DPS data.
  • Provides a 30-day period for applicants to provide proof of citizenship. If there is an election during this window, the individual must cast a provisional ballot ("affidavit ballot") and then show proof of citizenship within five days of Election Day in order for their vote to count.
  • Requires an annual audit of voter lists against the SAVE database no later than 180 days before a scheduled general federal election. Registered voters flagged as noncitizens would have 30 days to provide proof of citizenship as outlined above.
  • Establishes protections against removal by:
    • Prohibiting cancellation of voter registrations based solely on information from the SAVE system.
    • Prohibiting removals based on SAVE within 90 days of an election.

Proof of citizenship is defined under an existing code section as:

  • Birth certificate or a legible photocopy
  • U.S. passport, or a legible photocopy of the pertinent pages identifying the applicant and showing the passport number
  • U.S. naturalization documentation, a legible photocopy, or the number of the applicant's Certificate of Naturalization (provided the number is verified with U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services)
  • Any other document or method of proof established by the Federal Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986

Under prior law, registrants were only required to provide proof of citizenship if there were flags indicating they were not citizens from both the DPS system and the SAVE system. The secretary of state was required to conduct a one-time check of the voter list against the SAVE database by January 1, 2025.

Last Updated May 7, 2026

data.author: Warren H (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 13, 2025, would greatly diminish the efficacy of organized voter registration drives. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the state board to develop a "sample" voter registration form. The form would describe the information required on an official voter registration form, and would provide instructions on how to register, but would be visually distinct from the real form and would contain no space for an individual to fill in their information. An individual could not use the sample form to actually register to vote.
  • Require organized voter registration drives to use only the sample form, meaning that a voter registration drive would not be able to hand out actual voter registration applications. An individual or organization that violated this would commit a misdemeanor.
  • Require all organizations conducting organized voter registration drives to register with the state.

data.author: Dahle (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 1, 2025, would make a variety of minor changes to a wide variety of election laws, including extending the cure deadline, and permitting more time to count mail ballots. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Extend the cure deadline for mail ballots from the third business day after the election to the fourth (the Monday following the election in most cases).
  • Permit all mail ballots to be counted as early as 9 a.m. on election day. Under existing law, mail ballots are counted at 5 p.m. on election day, but UOCAVA ballots may be counted as early as 9 a.m. upon adoption of a resolution. Under the bill, all mail ballots could be counted during that earlier timeframe upon adoption of a resolution. The bill would also remove a requirement that counting continue unabated except for unavoidable necessity by replacing it with a provision under which the county board may not adjourn the meeting until counting is completed.
  • Remove the word "card" from the provisions of law describing acceptable voter ID for the purposes of ensuring that digital versions of ID are considered acceptable.
  • Clarify that a voter may not have their ballot challenged if the voter was qualified to cast the ballot at the time it was received by the county board.
  • Standardize the time for delivery of mail ballots. Under existing law, mail ballots must be delivered to the board no later than 50 days before the election and may be sent to voters immediately thereafter, but in statewide general elections in even-numbered years, this delivery may happen up to 60 days before the election. The bill would standardize this to 50 days for all elections other than second primary elections (which would now have a deadline of 45 days before the election instead of "as quickly as possible" as provided under current law). UOCAVA voters would have their ballots mailed 50 days before each election unless 45 days is specifically authorized by the state board.
  • Change the timing for the counting of early voting ballots. Under existing law, the count of electronic early voting ballots begins when polls close, and paper early voting ballots are counted starting at the same time as mail ballots. Under the bill, all counting of early voting ballots would begin at 5 p.m. on Election Day, and may begin as early as 9:00 a.m. upon adoption of a resolution. The bill would also provide for provisional early voting ballots to be reviewed during the same time period as review of mail ballots, and would require a final decision to be made on provisional ballots by the time of the county canvass.
  • Permit a person deployed by their employer away from the voter's residence to respond to a disaster to the definition of "covered voter."
  • Codify the existing practice of letting persons who are at least 16 years of age but not yet 18 years of age to preregister to vote.
  • Require party leaders to recommend four potential chief judge or judge candidates for each precinct, instead of two as required under current law. The bill would also require only a bipartisan majority vote (instead of unanimous consent as under existing law) of the county board to find additional registered voters to serve as election judges if the parties do not provide sufficient recommendations. Finally, the bill would also repeal a provision of law that limits precincts with fewer than 500 voters to a maximum of two assistants to the election judges.
  • Specify that the list of voters who have requested mail ballots is confidential information. The bill would accordingly clarify that the list of mail ballots applied for, which a county board of elections is required to maintain under current law, should only consist of those applications that were approved.
  • Expand a misdemeanor for prohibited petition activities by adding compensating a person for the number of signatures collected to the list of prohibited actions.
  • Specify that the law which provides for a person to vote in their precinct after residing there for 30 days also applies to a ward or other election district.
  • Provide for an audit of mail ballots and early voting ballots by the state board after each election.
  • Specify that a challenge of a ballot in a municipal primary or election must be made no later than the second business day after the election, instead of the fifth as provided under current law.
  • Require a county board to post notice of its meetings on its website, instead of in a newspaper as required under existing law.

The bill also would make extensive changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

Last Updated April 18, 2025

data.author: Rubin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 3, 2025, would protect voting rights by ensuring that no voter has their ballot discarded if the voter relied on procedures in effect at the time the ballot was cast. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Specify that voters have the right to cast a ballot and have their vote counted based on the laws and established election procedures in place on election day, and may not have their vote thrown out on the basis of a law or rule not in effect at that time.
  • Specify that no administrative body, court, or official may exclude a vote from canvass, fail to certify an election, withhold a certificate of election, or fail to seat a candidate by excluding votes based on a law, judicial ruling, or legal interpretation that was not in effect on election day or an election procedure that was not established by election day.
  • Provide rules for courts to apply in determining when a law or election procedure was in effect for purposes of implementing the bill's provisions.
  • Provide for expedited resolution of an action concerning an election procedure if instituted within 90 days of the date that mail ballots may first be sent.

Last Updated April 4, 2025

data.bill_location: House Rules, Calendar and Operations of the House Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on RULES, CALENDAR, AND OPERATIONS OF THE HOUSE

data.author: Buansi (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 8, 2025, would create automatic voter registration and mandate online voter registration, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create automatic voter registration at state agencies. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Create automatic voter registration at drivers license offices. A person applying for issuance, renewal or correction of a drivers license or state ID card would be informed that unless they affirmatively opt out, they will be registered to vote or have their voter registration updated, as applicable.
    • Create a similar automatic voter registration process at all other state agencies that accept voter registrations under existing law, which are agencies that accept:
      • applications for public assistance
      • applications for government programs engaged in providing services to people with disabilities (though such an agency would not be required to provide automatic voter registration when providing services at a person's home)
      • claims for unemployment benefits
    • Specify that existing provisions of law prohibiting the provision of false information on a voter registration application apply during automatic voter registration at these agencies.
    • Create a presumption that a person wrongfully registered through the automatic voter registration process was registered through no fault of their own.
  • Mandate online voter registration. Online voter registration is currently offered in North Carolina, but it is not required by statute. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Mandate that the state board of elections provide an online application for voter registration, with online assistance available. The site would also be required to accept updates to voter registration.
    • Provide for the procurement of an applicant's signature either by authorizing the sharing of one already on file with a state agency or by having the applicant submit it electronically.
    • Specify that online applications are subject to the same submission deadlines as those submitted in person or fax (25 days before the election).
  • Restore the voting rights of a person convicted of a felony earlier in the reentry process. Under existing law, voting rights are restored upon completion of the person's sentence, including any period of probation or parole. Under the bill, the person's voting rights would be restored:
    • upon release from active punishment to a period of post-release supervision or parole of a person whose sentence was not suspended by the court, or
    • upon the suspension of an active sentence resulting in the imposition of an intermediate or community punishment
  • Fully repeal Senate Bill 747, which passed in 2023. That bill made extensive changes restricting mail voting, expanding access for poll watchers, and prohibiting private funding of elections. Repealing the bill would, among other changes:
    • Extend the deadline for the receipt of mail ballots to the third day after the election, instead of the day of the election.
    • Permit the establishment of drop boxes for mail ballots.
    • Repeal provisions that provided for the cure of mail ballots that did not contain required documentation.
    • Repeal provisions that prohibited private funding of election administration.
    • Repeal provisions expanding the authority of poll watchers.
    • Decriminalize the following misdemeanor offenses:
      • impersonating an election official in the course of duties in registering voters or administering an election
      • affixing or printing an identifier to a mail ballot request form, unless the individual is a state or county board of elections employee
    • Repeal provisions simplifying the process of removing registered voters for non-citizenship.
  • Reduce the number of witnesses required for a mail ballot from two to one. The bill also repeals a provision under which a notary public can witness the ballot instead of two people.
  • Specify that a mail ballot application may be submitted in person or by mail, email, or fax.
  • Require the state board of elections to make reasonable efforts to establish polling places on college campuses.
  • Implement safeguards to ensure that a voter being removed from the rolls for the reason of death is in fact the person who died by comparing birth dates and the last four digits of the voter's social security number. The board would not be permitted to remove the registration until it has matched the complete date of birth and other identification information from the deceased records.
  • Give a voter 60 days to respond to a confirmation notice before permitting the voter's registration to be canceled. Existing statute does not specify a timeframe for responding, but only directs the removal of a voter who fails to respond. The bill would also permit the sending of a confirmation notice via email, which is not authorized under current law.
  • Specify that the returning of a confirmation notice as "undeliverable" would not be sufficient cause to remove a voter's registration without further investigation.
  • Require the implementation of a nonpartisan redistricting process. This portion of the bill would include a constitutional amendment.
  • Reinstate nonpartisan judicial elections.

The bill also contains provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, mostly concerning campaign spending and advertising.

Last Updated April 10, 2025

data.bill_location: House Rules, Calendar and Operations of the House Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on RULES, CALENDAR, AND OPERATIONS OF THE HOUSE

data.author: Echevarria (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 9, 2025, would shorten the early voting period and require more information from mail voters, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Shorten the early voting period. Under existing law, the early voting period starts on the third Thursday before Election Day. Under the bill, the first permissible day for early voting would be the second Thursday before Election Day.
  • Change the information a voter is required to provide when returning a mail ballot. Under current law, a voter who is unable to provide a photocopy of their ID must complete an affidavit attesting to that effect and supply their driver's license number, their state ID number, or the last four digits of their Social Security number (SSN). Under the bill, all voters completing such an affidavit would be required to provide the last four digits of their SSN, and would also need to provide one of their driver's license number, their state ID number, or their birthdate.
  • Change the information a voter is required to provide when requesting a mail ballot. Under current law, a voter requesting a mail ballot must provide their driver's license number, their state ID number, or the last four digits of their Social Security number. Under the bill, all voters applying for a mail ballot must provide the last four digits of their SSN, and would also need to provide one of their driver's license number or their state ID number.
  • Prohibit ranked choice voting statewide.

The bill would also make changes in an area of law concerning electioneering that is not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

Last Updated September 17, 2025

data.author: Blackwell (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on July 31, 2025, would establish new ID documentation requirements for UOCAVA voters; require a full Social Security number for voter registration for voters without North Carolina driver's licenses; require statewide post-election audits of all early voting and mail ballots; extend the mail ballot cure period; and make various other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Increase ID documentation requirements for UOCAVA voters and restrict eligibility for overseas voting. The bill would:
    • Require UOCAVA voters to provide a copy of their photo ID when returning their ballot, or an affidavit explaining why they are exempt, beginning on January 1, 2026. Existing law requires regular mail ballot voters to provide such ID, but has no comparable requirement for UOCAVA voters, though a recent decision by the state's Supreme Court did require it. Under the bill, accepted forms of ID for this purpose would be a copy of an unexpired:
      • U.S.-issued military ID
      • U.S. passport card or passport photo page
      • North Carolina driver's license
      • Permit cure of missing voter ID for UOCAVA ballots by mail, the State Board electronic system, or email to the county board, by the day of the county canvass. 
      • Eliminate eligibility for UOCAVA voter status for individuals born outside the U.S. if their parent or legal guardian was eligible to vote in the state prior to leaving the country, and the individual had never been registered in another state. A recent North Carolina Supreme Court decision resulted in such votes being tossed. 
  • Require the State Board to conduct a uniform statewide audit after each primary or election to determine whether any early voting or mail ballots were "ineligible," according to "official database records." The bill would require the Board to provide audit results to each county board by the fourth day before the canvass, and require the county to review the results by the following business day and serve a challenge to any identified voter. Existing law has no comparable requirement.
  • Increase ID number requirements for voter registration and specify timing for registration confirmation notices. The bill would:
    • Require voter registration applicants to provide their full Social Security number when applying. Under existing law, applicants are only required to provide a Social Security number if they do not have a North Carolina driver's license, and in such case must only provide the last four digits. Related to the new requirement, the bill would:
      • Require the State Board to update application forms to include a full Social Security number by July 1, 2026, and to reject applications without a full number beginning Jan. 1, 2027. 
      • Require the DMV to provide the State Board with the full Social Security number of any registered voter in its records by Jan. 1, 2026. 
    • Require officials to send an address confirmation notice to all registration applicants within five business days of receiving an application, and if notice is returned as undeliverable, to send a second notice within five business days of the return. Existing law requires issue of these notices, but does not specify deadlines. The bill would also require notices to include a pre-printed space to indicate the addressee does not reside at the location.  
  • Change the timing for the counting of Election Day and early voting ballots. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require ballots cast during in-person early voting to be counted between 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. on Election Day. Existing law requires such ballots to be counted after the polls close if counted electronically, and at the same time as mail ballots if they are paper ballots counted manually. 
    • Move the deadline later for counting of provisional ballots, from the third business day after the election to the fifth business day after. The bill would also permit preparatory steps for the review of provisional early voting ballots to take place before Election Day, if these steps do not reveal the result of the count before close of polls, and require that officials make a final determination for counting provisional ballots with potentially false affidavits by the date of the county canvass.
  • Slightly increase the cure period for mail ballots, and make other changes to mail ballot processing. The bill would:
    • Move the deadline later for cure of a mail ballot defect, to noon on the fifth business day after the election. Under existing law, the cure deadline is noon on the third business day after the election.
    • Eliminate the existing requirement that the counting of mail ballots be continuous except for "unavoidable necessity," and require instead that the county board not adjourn its meeting until counting is completed.
    • Move the start of mail ballot counting slightly earlier, to 9 a.m. on Election Day. Under existing law, the board convenes at 5 p.m., and by adoption of a resolution before the election may begin the count at 2 p.m. 
    • Expressly provide that a mail ballot can only be counted if transmitted to a county board in accordance with existing law.
    • Move the deadline later for announcing the mail ballot tally, from the third business day after the election to the fifth day after. 
    • Establish a pilot program for the use of signature verification software to verify mail ballots, for the 2026 primary election. The bill would set out criteria for the State Board to choose 10 counties for the program, and for reporting results to the legislature. 
  • Modify procedures related to ballot challenges. The bill would:
    • Expressly extend applicability of existing challenge procedures for mail ballots to early voting ballots. Existing law provides for challenges to early voting ballots, but statutory provisions for challenge procedures do not expressly apply to these ballots. The bill would also specify certain record keeping requirements for challenged mail ballots.
    • Specify that for ballots challenged due to the death of a voter, the challenge must be sustained if the voter died between casting the ballot and 6:30 a.m. on Election Day. Existing law had no comparable express provision. 
    • Repeal the existing provision that requires a challenged ballot to be counted for any ballot items that the voter is eligible to vote. 
  • Specify procedures related to the assignment, training, removal, and compensation of election administrators. The bill would:
    • Require each county to appoint a minimum of six emergency election day assistants for each county. Existing law authorizes the State Board to determine the number of such assistants as needed, by certain criteria, but has no minimum. 
    • Specify the content of State Board training for county board election officials. Existing law sets out requirements for frequency of training, but does not include express requirements for its content. 
    • Permit the removal of a precinct official by the county board for incompetency or failure to discharge the duties of office. The bill would also permit an official removed in this matter from being prohibited from serving in any subsequent election. Existing law authorizes the State Board to remove county board members on these grounds, but does not expressly address removal of precinct officials by county officials. 
    • Increase the compensation of county board members from $25 to $100 per meeting.  
  • Authorize the State Board to employ staff counsel or retain private legal counsel to assist in election litigation. Existing law authorizes legal counsel by the attorney general, and permits private counsel only on recommendation of the attorney general and approval from the General Assembly. The bill would also specify the particular circumstances under which the Board may retain private legal counsel.
  • Create and expand criminal offenses related to election activity. The bill would:
    • Create a felony offense for revealing the result of any count of ballots before the close of polls on Election Day.
    • Expand the criminal offense of disorderly conduct at a public building, to include prohibited activity at a location temporarily used for voting during voting hours.
    • Expand the existing misdemeanor criminal offense of prohibited petition activities to include compensating a person on the basis of the number of signatures collected for a petition.
    • Create a misdemeanor offense for a person to steal, deface, vandalize, or unlawfully remove political advertising from the protected area around a polling location.
  • Specify that any voter registration data received from another state pursuant to a data-sharing agreement does not constitute a public record.
  • Prohibit ranked choice voting statewide.

The bill would also make changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including campaign finance and party designations on ballots.


A previous version of this bill also contained additional provisions that were amended out, which would have required more early voting sites.

Last Updated May 6, 2026

data.author: Morey (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 12, 2026 would prohibit the presence of any troops, or armed persons in the civil, military, or naval service of North Carolina or the United States at any location used as a voting place beginning 17 days before the election. The bill would not prohibit the presence of these entities in the instance of armed enemies of the United States are present. This prohibition would not apply to law enforcement presence required by law or requested by the clerk.


The bill would establish remedies for violations and personal liability with a civil penalty.


The bill would expand what voter information is considered personally identifying information that cannot be shared publicly to include voter registration number, passport number, and telephone number. The bill would prohibit the State Board of Elections from disclosing any personally identifying information to the federal government without a court order or arrest warrant signed by a judge.


The bill would be effective upon becoming law. Relevant portions of the bill would be applicable to all elections held on or after date of signing.

Last Updated May 20, 2026

data.bill_location: Senate Rules and Operations of the Senate Committee

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on RULES AND OPERATIONS OF THE SENATE

data.author: Meyer (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 24, 2025, would expand access to same-day voter registration in the state, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit same-day voter registration on Election Day. Under existing law, same-day voter registration is only permitted during the early voting period. The bill would also permit a person to register at an early voting location and then decline to immediately vote.
  • Clarify that same-day registration is available for a person who will be 18 years of age by the date of a general election to register and vote in the associated primary even if the person is not yet 18 years of age.
  • Codify the existing practice under which persons who are at least 16 years of age but not yet 18 years of age may pre-register to vote. This practice is already permitted in the state without being in statute, but the bill would put it into law.

Last Updated March 27, 2025

data.bill_location: Senate Rules and Operations of the Senate Committee

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on RULES AND OPERATIONS OF THE SENATE

data.author: Chaudhuri (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 25, 2025, would:

  • Establish new crimes and civil penalties meant to protect voters. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Create a Class H felony for threatening or attempting to threaten a person for voting or attempting to vote, voting or attempting to vote for or against a particular candidate, registering to vote, urging or aiding any individuals to vote or attempt to vote as allowed by law, or exercising any lawful powers or duties as an election official or enlisting another person to do the same.
    • Create a Class H felony for knowingly challenging another's right to vote on fraudulent or spurious grounds, engaging in "mass, indiscriminate, and groundless" challenging of voters for the purposes of preventing or delaying their voting or interfering with the orderly administration of an election, or fraudulently advising any person that they are not eligible or registered.
    • Create a Class A1 misdemeanor for intimidating or coercing, or attempting to intimidate or coerce, any person for voting or attempting to vote, voting or attempting to vote for or against a particular candidate, registering to vote, urging or aiding any individuals to vote or attempt to vote as allowed by law, or exercising any lawful powers or duties as an election official or enlisting another person to do the same.
    • Create a Class H felony for various actions employers could take in order to influence the votes of their employees.
    • Establish a private cause of action allowing any victim of the established crimes to seek preventative relief and attorney's fees.
    • Establish a restitution fund which individuals convicted of violations of the above prohibitions could be ordered by a court to pay funds into. The fund would then be used for voter education campaigns.
    • Define "coerce," "intimidate," and "threaten" as used in this section, and establish that a person will be deemed to have committed such acts if they should have known or reasonably should have known that their actions would produce such an effect.
  • Create a felony for intimidating, threatening, or coercing an election worker with intent to interfere with their official duties, punishable by a fine of up to $100,000, imprisonment for up to five years, or both. Violators of this prohibition would also be civilly liable for any injury or loss to the election worker as a result of their intimidation, threats, or coercion. An election worker acting in good faith to prevent election interference or preserve ballot access could not be liable under this section.
  • Establish a class 1 misdemeanor, punishable by a fine of up to $10,000, for an election official who performs or communicates the intention to perform an official act in which that official would refuse to certify results of an election without substantial evidence.
  • Establish standards for post-election audits, including:
    • Being conducted by nonpartisan officials with expertise in elections
    • Being routine and conducted prior to state certification
    • Being transparent and open to the public
    • Preserving integrity of systems and equipment
    • Preserving ballot secrecy and voter privacy
    • Conducted according to statistically sound methodologies
    • Requiring that procedures governing audits be known prior to Election Day
    • Public officials would be prohibited from funding or participating in audits taht do not meet the established standards
  • Direct the state board of elections to conduct a "hand-to-eye" risk-limiting audit of a randomly selected sample of ballots to confirm the machine count. The results of the risk-limiting audit could not change the certified results.
  • Require election observers to complete training established by the state board of elections at least every two years, complete an oath, and wear clear identification. Under existing law, no training is required, an oath is prohibited, and no identification is required.
  • Establish a process by which election workers can apply to the board of elections requesting that their personally identifiable information be withheld from public records.
  • Grant the judiciary the authority to review general assembly determinations regarding contested elections. Existing law prohibits such judicial review.
  • Provide funding for the state board of elections to establish, in coordination with the department of public safety, a new threat-monitoring capability to monitor and analyze threats to election officials and their families by monitoring social media and other websites and maintaining a database of complaints from officials regarding threats.
  • Provide funding for security training for election officials.


Last Updated March 26, 2025

data.author: Meyer (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 24, 2025, would

  • Modify voter registration procedures at DMVs (starting January 1, 2026) and all other voter registration agencies (starting January 1, 2027) in a process it calls "automatic voter registration." Voters who do not opt out of registration would be asked to provide information requested on the voter registration application as well as a signature and would be informed that wilfully and knowingly, and with fraudulent intent, giving false information during the registration process is a Class I felony. Under existing law, there is an optional voter registration section on driver's license applications and other agencies forms.
  • Designate the State Board of Community Colleges as a voter registration agency
  • Require that disenfranchised individuals whose rights were restored must be informed of "automatic voter registration" in addition to standard registration procedures.

data.author: Hollo (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 19, 2026 would reduce the number of early in-person voting days to 10 consecutive days for all elections. The bill would move the start of early in-person voting from the third Thursday before the election to the second Thursday before an election. Existing law establishes early in-person voting to be available for between 13 - 17 days, depending on the county, beginning the third Thursday before Election Day and ending on the Saturday before. Counties are required to offer early voting on the Saturday before an election and have discretion on the four other weekend days during the early voting period.


This bill would be effective immediately upon becoming law and would apply to any election held on or after the date of passage.

Last Updated May 20, 2026

The bill also would clarify that the transmission of an individual's information for voter registration purposes pursuant to a DMV transaction does not constitute an illegal registration by a person ineligible to register unless they willingly took steps to attempt to register to vote while knowing they were ineligible. In addition, the bill would create a misdemeanor for an officer who uses voter registration records for any purpose other than voter registration, election administration, or enforcement of the Election Act.

data.author: Holdcroft (NP)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would eliminate no-excuse mail voting and online voter registration, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Eliminate no-excuse mail voting. To vote a mail ballot, a voter would have to have one of the following excuses:
    • Absence from the county
    • Eighty years of age or older
    • Physical inability to go to the polling place, including hospitalization
    • Caretaker of a person who is hospitalized, ill, or disabled
    • Religious obligation
    • Confined by public order
    • Legal blindness
    • Confidentiality of the voter's residence address
    • Jury duty
    • The voter qualifies to vote a mail ballot under federal law
  • Require voter registration applications to provide photo ID at the time of registration. The bill would also require all voter registration applications to be made in person by the applicant, eliminating the options to register by mail, to register online, or to have another person deliver the application.
  • Delay the sending of mail ballots for statewide elections. Existing law requires mail ballots to be ready for delivery at least 35 days before a statewide general or primary election and 15 days before any other election. Under the bill, they may not be made ready for delivery earlier than 22 days before any election.
  • Standardize the length of the early voting ("in-person absentee") period at 30 days. Under existing law it is 15 days for an election other than a statewide general or primary election.
  • Require ballot drop boxes to have constant video surveillance, be conspicuously marked, and be located in secure, well-lit areas. The bill would also enact other law to ensure the security of drop boxes.
  • Permit an election official to hand count ballots, and require that at least a quarter of ballots are hand counted to verify an electronic tabulation. Voting or counting systems would be required to be re-approved before each statewide primary election.
  • Clarify that an employer or agent of an employer of a voter is generally not permitted to act as an agent to request a mail ballot on the voter's behalf.
  • Require all ballots to be watermarked.
  • Allow candidates to request a recount be done by hand count.

Last Updated February 3, 2025

data.bill_location: Government, Military and Veterans Affairs Committee

data.author: Spivey (NP)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would require each election commissioner or county clerk to develop and implement a plan to ensure that "restricted individuals" are able to vote. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Define a restricted individual as a person unable to vote in-person due to an inability to travel. The bill defines a facility as any city or county jail, detention center, assisted-living facility, nursing home, rehabilitation center, or hospital that houses restricted individuals.
  • Require each election commissioner or county clerk to develop and implement a plan to facilitate voting for restricted individuals in facilities who are eligible voters.
  • Require that a plan developed under the bill ensure that restricted individuals have access to voter information materials, registration, voting services, and ballot cure.
  • Provide that a sheriff or designated jail staff member who fails to comply with the requirements imposed by the bill be subject to a civil penalty.

Last Updated January 31, 2025

data.bill_name: Proof of United States Citizenship for Indigent Voters

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 68

data.author: Lynn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 18 2026, will establish a process to assist indigent voters who may be unable to present proof of citizenship, as is required to register to vote.


Specifically, the bill:

  • Permits a person unable to provide reasonable documentary proof of citizenship to request that an election official contact the secretary of state to attempt to verify the person's citizenship
  • Provides that if the secretary of state is unable to establish the person's citizenship, that a person who claims they are unable to obtain citizenship documentation due to indigence may sign an affidavit to that effect. If an election officer reviews the affidavit and confirms that the person is unable to obtain a citizenship document due to indigence, the person will be provided with a voucher to be used at an appropriate state agency to obtain a copy of the person's birth certificate or other sufficient citizenship documentation.

The bill will be effective July 17, 2026

Last Updated May 26, 2026

data.author: Flynn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would:

  • Prohibit officials from conducting automatic voter registration or updates during Motor Vehicle Commission (MVC) transactions without the applicant providing proof of citizenship. Existing law does not require proof of citizenship for automatic voter registration or updates at the MVC.
  • Require the chief administrator of the MVC to conduct an audit of voters registered after May 1, 2021, to confirm registrants satisfy all voter requirements.
  • Require the chief administrator to conduct a yearly audit that must be completed 30 days before the primary election. Findings would need to be submitted to the governor, the legislature and the secretary of state.

Last Updated April 16, 2026

data.bill_name: John R. Lewis Voter Empowerment Act of New Jersey

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on BUDGET AND APPROPRIATIONS

data.author: Reynolds-Jackso (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 19, 2026, would create the "John R. Lewis Voter Empowerment Act of New Jersey," establishing comprehensive voting rights protections and a preclearance system for certain election policies. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit vote dilution and suppression. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Prohibit state agencies, local election offices, or political subdivisions from enacting or implementing any voting qualification, prerequisite, or policy that results in a denial or abridgement of the right to vote for members of a protected class.
    • Prohibit the use of any election method that impairs the equal opportunity or ability of protected class members to participate in the political process, elect candidates of their choice, or influence election outcomes.
    • Prohibit any policy or practice that places a material burden on the right to vote on the basis of a voter's disability, unless the policy is narrowly tailored to promote a substantial state interest.
    • Broaden the existing prohibition on interfering with the right to vote to include deceiving or attempting to deceive an individual with the intent to interfere with the right to vote.
    • Establish specific factors for courts to consider when determining if a violation has occurred, including the history of discrimination in the political subdivision, the extent to which protected class members have been elected to office, and the use of overt or subtle racial appeals in political campaigns.
  • Create a new preclearance system for covered voting policies. The bill would:
    • Require "covered entities" to obtain preclearance from the newly established Division of Voting Rights before enacting or implementing any "covered policy".
    • Define "covered entities" to include political subdivisions that have a history of voting rights or civil rights violations within the past 15 years, or that meet certain demographic thresholds combined with high arrest rates or low voter turnout among protected classes.
    • Define "covered policies" to include changes to methods of election, annexation, consolidation, list maintenance methodology, polling or drop box locations and hours, election dates, voter registration, language assistance, districting or redistricting, and any additional topics designated by the Division of Voting Rights.
    • Establish a process for the Division of Voting Rights to review preclearance submissions, including periods for public comment and specific timelines for the Division to grant or deny preclearance.
    • Permit a covered entity to enact or implement a covered policy immediately if preclearance is granted. If preclearance is denied, the covered policy may not be enacted or implemented.
    • Allow affected political subdivisions to appeal a denial of preclearance to the Law Division of the Superior Court of New Jersey.
  • Establish a "notification letter" process prior to litigation. The bill would require prospective plaintiffs to send a written notice to a political subdivision at least 50 days before commencing a judicial action under the vote suppression or vote dilution provisions. The political subdivision may then pass a resolution affirming its intent to enact a remedy, which grants the political subdivision 140 days to implement the remedy before the plaintiff can file suit.
  • Expand language assistance requirements. The bill would:
    • Require local election offices and political subdivisions to provide voting assistance and materials in languages other than English if more than two percent (but not fewer than 100 individuals) or more than 4,000 eligible voters in the jurisdiction are limited English proficient and speak a shared language.
    • Require translations to be provided in a manner that conveys the intent and essential meaning of the original text, and prohibit the sole reliance on automatic electronic translation services.
    • Require the Division of Voting Rights to conduct a study every two years to identify political subdivisions that may require changes in language access coverage.
    • Specify that no newly designated language assistance requirement may apply to any election occurring fewer than 120 days after formal designation by the Division.
    • Require the Division to establish and administer a centralized translation and ballot terminology clearing house to assist political subdivisions in complying with language assistance requirements.
  • Create a new Division of Voting Rights within the Department of the Treasury, which would be responsible for enforcing these provisions. The Division's duties would include conducting investigations, promulgating rules, litigating cases, and issuing advisory opinions.
  • Require extensive public hearings for municipal and county redistricting. The bill would require political subdivisions proposing redistricting plans to hold a specified number of public hearings, varying from two to four depending on the population of the political subdivision, both before and after drafting the plans.
  • Establish a central "Voting and Elections Database." The bill would require the Division of Elections to maintain a publicly accessible database containing elections and voting data from all local election offices and political subdivisions. Political subdivisions would be required to transmit specific election results and district configuration data to the division within 120 days after each election.
  • Create a civil cause of action and penalties. The bill would:
    • Allow aggrieved persons or organizations to file civil actions in Superior Court for violations of the Act.
    • Hold any party that violates the Act liable for damages, including nominal, compensatory, and punitive damages.
    • ﻿Require the court to award reasonable attorneys' fees and litigation expenses to the prevailing plaintiff.

Last Updated March 27, 2026

data.bill_name: John R. Lewis Voter Empowerment Act of New Jersey

status_actions.summary: From ASSEMBLY Committee on APPROPRIATIONS as amended

data.author: Reynolds-Jackso (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 8, 2026, would create the "John R. Lewis Voter Empowerment Act of New Jersey." Specifically the bill would:

  • Prohibit any state agency, local election office, or political subdivision from enacting or implementing any voting qualification, prerequisite to voting, law, ordinance, standard, or policy that results in a denial or abridgement of the right of members of a protected class to vote. It would specifically prohibit policies that burden the right to vote for any individual on the basis of disability.
  • Prohibit any local election office or political subdivision from using any method of election that would impair the equal opportunity of protected class members in the political process.
  • Prohibit local governments from taking any action or failing to take any action, including implementing, imposing, or enforcing any qualification for qualification for eligibility to vote or any law, ordinance, regulation, standard, practice, procedure, or policy regarding the administration of elections that is intended to result in impairing voter participation among a protected class.
  • Create a set of factors to be analyzed to determine if violations of the bill have occurred.
  • Authorize courts to implement appropriate remedies for abridgment of the rights of protected classes, including transitioning to an alternative method of election, changes to the days, locations, and methods of elections, additional voter education, expanded voter registration, and adding voters back to voter rolls. Courts may retain jurisdiction as necessary to ensure the remedies are implemented.
  • Require localities to give members of the public opportunities to participate in the redistricting process. Localities must also conduct additional outreach to limited English proficient communities so that they may participate in the process.
  • Give members of protected classes and organizations composed of members of protected classes standing to sue for enforcement of the act's provisions.
  • Require potential plaintiffs to send a locality a letter at least 50 days prior to filing suit, during which time the locality may take steps to remedy the alleged violation.
  • Require localities to provide voting materials in languages other than English if more than two percent of the voting age population, but no fewer than 100 individuals, speak a language other than English and have limited proficiency in English or more than 4,000 individuals speak a language other than English and have limited proficiency in English.
  • Require "covered entities" to preclear regulations about many election-related practices with the newly established Division of Voting Rights within the Department of the Treasury (the "Division."). The bill defines covered entities as any political subdivision that has been subject to court orders related to civil rights violations in the last 15 years, localities with certain arrest rate data, localities with certain dissimilarity indexes, localities with certain poverty rates, localities with certain voter participation rates, localities that failed to provide voter data to the New Jersey Voting and Elections Institute in the last three years, and localities that enacted a policy without obtaining preclearance in the past 25 years.
  • Create a process for a covered entity to obtain preclearance from the Division.
  • Provide the attorney general with authority to investigate or enforce provisions of the bill.
  • Require the attorney general to engage in public education efforts to inform the voting eligible population about their voting rights, including which populations are protected classes, which languages access materials are available, and any causes of action and avenues of redress available under the bill.
  • Prohibit any person from intimidating, deceiving, or obstructing the rights of voters.
  • Require the attorney general to provide an annual report on voting rights and election-related matters, including summaries of actions taken to enforce the provisions of the bill.
  • Specify that the provisions of the bill would take effect on July 1, 2026.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated April 15, 2026

data.author: Wainstein (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 6, 2026, permits a municipality to adopt an ordinance to lengthen the early voting period of the May municipal election. Under prior law, that voting period began on the fourth calendar day before the election. Under the bill, a municipality can adopt an ordinance that would start the early voting period on the eighth calendar day before the election.

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 4, 2026, would:

  • Prohibit an individual from assisting more than three qualified mail-in voters in an election, except that a person may assist up to five qualified mail-in voters if they are immediate family members residing in the same household. Existing law permits assistance to mail-in voters but does not impose this specific number limit on assistance. 
  • Expand the categories of individuals who can provide assistance to mail voters by allowing any individual who is a register voter in the same county to provide assistance. Existing law limits assistance to family members of mail voters.

data.author: Stewart (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 7, 2026, would enable incarcerated voters to apply for and receive mail ballots. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the secretary of state to coordinate with the commissioner of the department of corrections in providing mail ballot applications to qualified voters who are detained or incarcerated reasons other than a conviction for an indictable offense. Existing law does not require coordination between departments in providing mail ballot applications to voters.
  • Authorize detained voters to list the location where they are detained or incarcerated as a secondary address to which the ballot can be sent.
  • Require the department of corrections to ensure delivery of the mail ballot to a voter in the event that a voter who requested a mail ballot is subsequently transferred to another location. Existing law does not include a similar provision.

Last Updated May 11, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 2025-258

data.author: Karabinchak (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on January 12, 2026, permits post-election audits to be conducted using an independent third-party machine, among other changes. Specifically, the bill:

  • Permits the use of a third-party machine for post-election audits. Under previous law, an independent audit team is appointed after each election to conduct a hand-to-eye count of ballots for post-election audit purposes. Under the bill, the team would also be permitted to use independent third-party electronic machines for this purpose.
  • Specifies procedures for the conduct of a post-election audit using electronic machines that mostly mirror existing procedures for hand-counted audits. Specifically, the bill:
    • Requires the machines to be certified by a National Institute of Standards and Technology certified laboratory or a U.S. Election Assistance Commission accredited voting system test laboratory, and requires them to be publicly tested prior to use for a post-election audit.
    • Requires the machine to conduct its tally by ballot type.
    • Prescribes procedures to ensure all voters' privacy is maintained throughout the audit process.
  • Moves responsibility for selecting the election districts in which post-election audits must be conducted. Under previous law, this authority lays with the secretary of state. Under the bill, the authority is transferred to county boards of elections.

Last Updated January 15, 2026

status_actions.summary: Substituted by S4835

data.author: Calabrese (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 8, 2026, would expand the notice and cure process. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow voters to cure mail ballots due to the voter failing to include or seal the mail ballot's inner or outer envelope or failing to complete the certificate of mail-in voter on the inner envelope. Under existing law, ballots with those deficiencies are rejected and those voters are not given the opportunity to cure their ballots.
  • Require voters whose ballots contain those deficiencies to receive cure notices. Under existing law, election officials must send voters who have submitted ballots with curable defects a notice within 24 hours informing them that their ballot has been rejected and informing them how to cure it.
  • Allow voters who receive a cure notice to vote a provisional ballot in-person during the early voting period or on Election Day. Under existing law, voters who receive a cure notice for a mail ballot are only permitted to vote by curing their mail ballot.
  • Specify that the initial decision of whether to accept a mail ballot does not require a vote of the full board, but that the decision may only be made by trained elections employees designated by the county board of elections, subject to supervisory review by the board. The bill also specifies that watchers must be permitted to observe this process.
  • Require any cure letter, cure form, or replacement ballot sent to a voter to be maintained as part of the voter's registration record.

Last Updated January 9, 2026

data.bill_name: Early Voting Period for Regular Municipal Elections

status_actions.summary: Substituted by A4745

data.author: Stack (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 19, 2026, would permit a municipality to adopt an ordinance to lengthen the early voting period of the May municipal election. Under existing law, that voting period begins on the fourth calendar day before that election. Under the bill, a municipality could adopt an ordinance that would start the early voting period on the eighth calendar day before the election.

Last Updated March 23, 2026

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would establish a link on the website of the secretary of state for voters to report irregularities with mail ballots they have received. The bill identifies the intended recipient of the ballot not residing at that address or being deceased as two potential choices for the voter to report. The bill would also establish a "vote by mail study commission" to investigate irregularities pertaining to the mail voting process.

Last Updated January 15, 2026
