data.bill_name: Voter Registration System and Ineligible Noncitizen
data.bill_location: Senate State Government, Wagering, Tourism and Historic Preservation Committee

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT, WAGERING, TOURISM AND HISTORIC PRESERVATION

data.author: Pennacchio (R)
data.public_commentary: Summary pending as of 4/21/2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 2025-204

data.author: Zwicker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on January 12, 2026, changes New Jersey's "front-end opt out" automatic voter registration process to a "back-end opt out" process when applying for certain forms of ID, expands the list of agencies who can conduct automatic voter registration, and makes other changes to automatic voter registration procedures. Specifically, the bill:

  • Allows any state agency, except for the Motor Vehicle Commission ("MVC"), that the secretary of state verifies already collects sufficient information, including through documents or database verification, that would provide proof of eligibility to establish automatic voter registration. The bill eliminates the requirement that the secretary of state comply with these requirements but would require each county commissioner of registration to comply with them. Under previous law, only an agency that collects documents that provide proof of eligibility could serve as an automatic voter registration agency.
  • Requires the secretary of state and chief administrator to jointly develop a process by which the MVC, upon obtaining an individual's full name, date of birth, driver's license or state ID number, and address, uses the information from the statewide voter registration system to determine whether that individual is already registered to vote in the state.
  • Requires the MVC and any other state agency that has implemented automatic voter registration to send any change of name or residence information received during a transaction with a registered voter to the secretary of state for the purpose of updating the voter's registration. Under previous law, change of residence information was required to be sent in written format to the commissioner of registration, made via phone call to the commissioner, or by completing a change of residence notice online from the secretary of state. Specifically, the bill requires:
    • The secretary of state to send that information to the commissioner of registration in the individual's county of residence
    • The county commissioner of registration to update that voter's registration information and to transfer that individual's registration file if inactive to the active file, and to send that voter by forwardable mail, a notice of the change and a postage paid pre-addressed return form by which the voter could correct the information.
    • The county commissioner, upon receiving a notice from a voter that a change was made in error, to immediately correct the voter's information in the statewide voter registration system.
  • Changes the MVC's front-end opt out automatic voter registration process to a back-end opt out process when applying for certain forms of ID. Specifically, the bill requires the MVC to automatically transmit sufficient information, including the voter's signature as a digitized image, to the secretary of state to conduct voter registration for each individual who completes an application for a REAL ID license, REAL ID card, and each related update, renewal, or change of address. The MVC may not transmit information for any applicant who has provided documentation that they are not a U.S. citizen. Specifically, the bill requires the secretary of state to:
    • Register individuals who applied for a REAL ID license, REAL ID card, or updated their information, based on information received from the MVC or other participating agencies unless the individual has demonstrated that they are not eligible to vote or are a member of the Address Confidentiality Program. 
    • Send the voter a notice by non-forwardable mail informing them they have been registered with instructions on how to declare a party affiliation, apply for mail-in ballots, or decline registration and request that their registration be removed from the statewide voter registration system. The notice would state the penalties for submission of a false application and state that if the individual declines registration that the request will remain confidential. The notice must be in English, Spanish, and any other language spoken by at least five percent of the registered voters in the county. The voter's registration would be deemed effective as of the date of their application with the MVC.
    • Electronically transmit a voter's information to the appropriate county commissioner of registration. If the information transmitted is an updated name or change of address for a registered voter, the county commissioner of registration would be required to update that voter's registration information. 
  • Requires the commissioner of registration to remove a voter's registration from the statewide voter registration system if that individual returns a notice declining to be registered. If that individual votes in an election after the transfer of their record but before the notice is returned, that individual would remain registered to vote.
  • Repeals the requirement that an individual's notice form declining to be registered not be considered a government record pursuant to the law concerning access to government records.
  • Changes the existing front-end opt out automatic voter registration system, which would only apply to individuals applying for standard licenses, standard ID cards, examination permits, probationary driver's licenses, and related updates or renewals. The bill requires that individuals engaging in those transactions affirmatively agree or decline the automatic voter registration. Under previous law, an individual would be registered unless they specifically declined the automatic voter registration.
  • Requires that upon implementation of online voter registration, voter registration information received from an application online for the renewal of a driver's license or ID card be collected and automatically transmitted to the secretary of state.
  • Requires the Department of Corrections, the secretary of state, and the MVC to coordinate to ensure that eligible individuals scheduled to be released from incarceration who are provided an opportunity to receive a non-driver ID card in advance of release are also provided voter registration opportunities as part of the ID card application. That individual would be allowed to preregister to vote if they have identified a place of residence within the state where they will reside following their release from incarceration. That individual would not be registered until they were released from incarceration.
  • Repeals a provision requiring a voter to receive a confirmation notice in the mail if they update their party affiliation when updating an existing voter registration.

Previous versions of the bill contained provisions that were amended out of this version. Those provisions would have:

  • Required the MVC to automatically transmit sufficient information to conduct voter registration for each person who completed a voter registration transaction with the MVC to the secretary of state.
  • Required the secretary of state and the chief administrator to jointly determine which documents are acceptable to demonstrate that an individual is a United States citizen.
  • Required the secretary of state to develop procedures for processing electronic records received from an agency without a digitized image of the applicant's signature. An individual whose registration record did not contain a digitized image of their signature would not be allowed to vote until a signature was obtained.
  • Required notices sent to voters who were automatically registered to vote to provide information and procedures for any individual wishing to prevent disclosure of their street address.
  • Provided that an individual who was not entitled to vote and who became registered to vote as a result of a transaction with the MVC and voted or attempted to vote would be presumed to have an affirmative defense to prosecution.
  • Extended back-end opt out automatic voter registration to the Division of Medical Assistance and Health Services in the Department of Human Services. Specifically, the bill would have required the Division of Medical Assistance to electronically transmit voter registration information to the secretary of state for each individual who applied to the New Jersey Medical Assistance and Health Services Program who was of sufficient age and was "externally verified" as a U.S. citizen as part of an application with the agency.

Last Updated January 15, 2026

data.author: Amato (R)

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 2025-322

data.author: Sarlo (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on January 20, 2026, expands the notice and cure process. Specifically, the bill:

  • Allows voters to cure mail ballots due to the voter failing to include or seal the mail ballot's inner or outer envelope or failing to complete the certificate of mail-in voter on the inner envelope. Under prior law, ballots with those deficiencies were rejected and those voters were not given the opportunity to cure their ballots.
  • Requires voters whose ballots contain those deficiencies to receive cure notices. Under prior law, election officials were required to send voters who have submitted ballots with curable defects a notice within 24 hours informing them that their ballot has been rejected and informing them how to cure it.
  • Allows voters who receive a cure notice to vote a provisional ballot in-person during the early voting period or on Election Day. Under previous law, voters who received a cure notice for a mail ballot were only permitted to vote by curing their mail ballot.
  • Specifies that the initial decision of whether to accept a mail ballot does not require a vote of the full board, but that the decision may only be made by trained elections employees designated by the county board of elections, subject to supervisory review by the board. The bill also specifies that watchers must be permitted to observe this process.
  • Requires any cure letter, cure form, or replacement ballot sent to a voter to be maintained as part of the voter's registration record.

Last Updated April 16, 2026

data.bill_name: Public Peace Health Safety and Welfare

status_actions.summary: Chaptered.  Chapter No. 58

data.author: Duhigg (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 9, 2026:

  • Makes it a felony offense for any person acting under color of law or otherwise to order or bring troops or armed forces to a polling place or within fifty feet of a ballot drop box beginning 28 days before an election through Election Day, unless necessary to repel armed enemies of the United States. Prior law did not include a similar prohibition.
  • Prohibits officials from imposing voter qualifications or election procedures contrary to state law or otherwise interfering with election administration or voters.
  • Authorizes the attorney general, secretary of state, county clerks, and affected voters to bring civil actions for violations. Courts can grant injunctive relief, impose civil penalties of $5,000 to $50,000 per violation, and award attorney’s fees to prevailing voter plaintiffs. 
  • Establishes emergency election procedures allowing the secretary of state to authorize county clerks in affected counties to develop contingency plans following a declared emergency, including relocating polling places, deploying mobile voting units, and electronically sending ballot materials to voters who submit emergency mailed ballot requests.

Last Updated April 6, 2026

data.bill_name: Election Commissioner Authorization Act of New York

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Paulin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 28, 2025, would shift election authority by enabling individual commissioners of a board of elections to order a voting machine audit when certain criteria exist, instead of requiring the entire board to order the audit. Because boards of election are equally bipartisan, this change would enable a commissioner of one political party to order an audit without the agreement of a commissioner of the other party. The bill would also empower any commissioner to order an audit of a particular contest.

Last Updated February 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Weprin D (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would require the Queens County Board of Elections to offer election materials including, but not limited to, ballots, signs, voter mailings, employee and volunteer training materials, and information on its website in Bengali, Punjabi, and Hindi. In addition, the election board would be required to produce and disseminate countywide a booklet that includes a voter registration form and mail ("absentee") ballot information with instructions in those languages, and general voter information in those languages. The bill would authorize the election board to include other languages on its website and in those booklets. 


Last Updated January 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Jackson C (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would create a cure process for provisional ballots ("affidavit ballots"). Specifically, when the local board of elections determines at or before the time of the canvass that a provisional ballot envelope is incomplete or otherwise defective, the board must mail notice to the voter within one day. If the board has the voter's phone or email, the board must give notice through those means as well. The voter could cure the defects by signing and returning an affirmation within seven business days of the board mailing the notice. The board must include the affirmation form with the notice. If a provisional ballot defect is not curable, the board must be sent notice within three business days of their ballot's rejection.


Last Updated January 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Jackson C (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would create same-day registration on Election Day, require all voters to be mailed ballots for all elections without changing early voting or Election Voting locations, ostensibly shift control of voter registration lists from the counties and New York City to the state, and change when mail ballots are brought by election officials to nursing homes and similar institutions to be voted in person. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create same-day registration on Election Day. The bill states that a same-day registrant must complete the ordinary voter registration form and provide the ordinary proofs to support it when registering on Election Day. Existing law does not require an ID document to register or to vote, although it does require a person to provide a driver's license number, a state ID number, or a Social Security number, or affirm that the applicant has none. 
  • Require local boards of election to mail all registered voters ballots at their registration address for all elections for which they are qualified to vote. Existing law requires voters to request mail ballots and to justify their request with an excuse authorized by statute.
  • Allow people who expect to be away from their registration address to request to have their ballot sent to a different address. The bill would require the State Board of Elections to make a standard application but would require local boards to accept any request that substantially complied with the state form. Under existing law, voters requesting mail ballots may request that the ballot be sent to an address different from their registration address. 
  • Eliminate the requirement that 25 people in a residential care facility request absentee ballots before a local board of elections can bring ballots to the facility to assist voters with casting ballots, and require ballots be brought to all such facilities. 
  • Clarify that the use of titles, initials, or customary abbreviations of given names in a voter's or witness's signature on an application to redirect the mail ballot, or on the mail ballot envelope, cannot be the basis for invalidating the application or ballot for a signature mismatch. 
  • Require the State Board of Elections to implement, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner, a single, uniform, official, centralized, interactive, computerized statewide voter registration database system. The system would be defined, maintained, and administered at the state level, contain the voter registration lists maintained by each county board of elections, and assign a unique identifier to each legally registered voter. Although counties would be allowed to maintain lists in a parallel system, they would need to facilitate the state's maintenance of the state system. The bill would require the state system to offer various features to assist in election administration, including signature verification. Existing law, not addressed by the bill, requires the State Board of Elections to maintain an interactive, statewide, computerized, voter registration list.

Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, Section 2 authorizes only people who are out of their county or out of New York City, as applicable, and people who are unable to appear to vote in person due to illness or physical disability to choose to vote by mail. Art. 2, Section 5 requires people to register to vote a minimum of 10 days before an election.

The bill would also specify that a nonpartisan poll monitor is permitted to assist a voter upon personal request from the voter and upon the monitor taking an oath. The bill would not prevent non-electioneering communication with voters outside of a polling place.

data.author: Steck (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would establish universal mail voting by requiring election officials to mail all registered voters a ballot for each election in which they are qualified to vote, by amending the mail ("absentee") ballot section of the election code. As a result, the bill would make most elections mail ballot elections, although certain specialty elections, like school board elections, would not be affected. 

In addition, the bill would restrict who a voter could choose to return their completed, sealed ballot by forbidding people from returning more than one ballot other than their own. Thus a voter could choose anyone to return their ballot, provided that person had not returned anyone else's. Existing law does not include a similar restriction.

data.author: Slater (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would create a photo ID requirement for in-person and mail ("absentee") voting. In-person voters would need to present current and valid acceptable ID when voting, and mail voters would need to include a copy of current and valid acceptable ID when returning their ballots. Under existing law, voters are not required to present an ID document to vote. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Make the following forms of photo ID acceptable: 
    • NY state driver's license or state ID
    • U.S. passport

    • Government-issued medical card
    • Any ID card issued by the U.S. or the state of NY
  • Require any person without photo ID to use fail-safe voting. Their ballots would only count if the local board of elections can determine the validity of the ballot. 
  • Make individuals who are exempted by federal law exempt from its requirements.

Under existing law, counties outside of New York City may pay poll workers any minimum amount the law otherwise allows, while New York City must pay poll workers at least $130 and election coordinators at least $200. Statewide, poll workers of all types must be paid at least $25 per training session.

data.bill_name: Voting Rights and Access for Incarcerated Individuals

status_actions.summary: From ASSEMBLY Committee on RULES

data.author: Walker (D)
data.legal_commentary:
Bill version 1/15/25This bill, as introduced on January 15, 2025, and entitled the "Democracy During Detention Act", would create new opportunities for detained eligible voters to participate in elections. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow local boards of elections to authorize in-person early voting locations to serve eligible voters inside correctional facilities. Authorized county facilities would offer at least three hours of early voting per day on the 10th and 9th day before Election Day. Authorized New York City facilities would offer at least six hours of early voting per day on the 10th, 9th, and 8th day before Election Day. The bill would also require New York City election officials to conduct at least one site visit to each correctional facility between 45 and 30 days prior to Election Day to conduct voter registration.
  • Require local boards of elections in counties with at least 100,000 registered voters to develop an absentee voting plan for each correctional facility in the county holding at least 75 individuals. The board would need to submit a plan concerning procedures, staffing, and security no later than 90 days prior to the first election in which it will be used.
  • Require New York City officials to conduct at least one site visit at each facility between 45 and 30 days prior to each primary or general election to conduct voter registration and ensure adequate allocation of resources, including assistive and interpretive resources, based on historical and current data.
  • Require local boards of elections in counties with fewer than 100,000 registered voters to provide incarcerated individuals sufficient access to online and paper voter registration opportunities and to ensure that each incarcerated registered voter has access to an absentee ballot application no later than 21 days before Election Day.
  • Require election officials to work with federal detention facilities to make their best efforts to provide access to registration and voting. These efforts must include direct biennial engagement with leadership at each federal facility no later than February 15 of each even-numbered year.
  • Codify certain rights for incarcerated eligible voters, including an opportunity to register and request ballots for each election and a duty for correctional officials to cooperate with election officials to ensure the provision of these rights. 

(KenDrell, 2/10/25)(original by Daniel)

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Colton (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 17, 2025, would require boards of election to provide election materials in multiple languages. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Specify that if the director of the census determines, based on census data, that more than five percent of voting-age citizens in a political subdivision are members of a single language minority and are limited-English proficient, or more than ten thousand of its voting age citizens are members of a single language minority and are limited-English proficient, then the political subdivision is a "covered jurisdiction" and must provide translated materials. However, a political subdivision will not be covered if it has less than five percent voting age limited-English proficient citizens of each language minority which comprises over five percent of the statewide limited-English proficient voting-age citizen population.
  • Require covered jurisdictions to provide any registration or voting notices, forms, instructions, assistance, or other materials or information relating to the electoral process, including ballots, in the language of the applicable minority group as well as in the English language. Provided that where the language of the applicable minority group is oral or unwritten or in the case of Alaskan natives and American Indians, if the predominant language is historically unwritten, the political subdivision would be required to furnish oral instructions, assistance, or other information relating to registration and voting.
  • Authorize a covered jurisdiction that seeks to provide English-only registration or voting materials or information, including ballots, to seek a declaratory judgment permitting such provision. The court must grant the requested relief if it determines that the illiteracy rate of the applicable language minority group within the state or political subdivision is equal to or less than the national illiteracy rate. Note, this bill appears to be patterned on a federal bill. The bill references covered states and the provision relating to a declaratory ruling speaks of litigating against the United States in federal court.

Last Updated January 27, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Cunningham (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would increase the notices given to voters as part of the address confirmation process, when their voter registration status is changed to inactive or canceled. Specifically, the bill would increase the notice to voters when a local board of elections:

  • Attempts to confirm a voter's address. The bill would require the board of elections to send the required notices by first class forwardable mail, and to any email addresses and telephone numbers that are in the board of elections' registration records for the voter. In addition, the bill would require these notices to be made using standardized statewide forms issued by the State Board of Elections. Existing law specifies that at least some address confirmation notices are sent by first-class forwardable mail, but the section of code the bill would amend is silent on the type of mail to be used. Existing law does not require email or phone notice.
  • Changes a voter's registration from active to inactive. The bill would require the board of elections to notify voters immediately when making a voter's registration inactive, using first-class forwardable mail addressed to where the board believes the voter now lives, first-class forwardable mail to the address where the voter is registered, and to any email or phone numbers in the board's registration records for the voter. The notice would be given using uniform, statewide forms issued by the State Board of Elections, and would explain why the voter's registration has been made inactive. Existing law does not require such notice.
  • Cancels a voter's registration. The bill would require the board of elections to give voters the pre-cancellation and post-cancellation notices existing law requires using first-class forwardable mail addressed to where the board believes the voter now lives, first-class forwardable mail to the address where the voter is registered, and to any email or phone numbers in the board's registration records for the voter, using a statewide, uniform form created by the State Board of Elections. Existing law does not require email or phone notice, and requires the mail notice to be sent only to the voter's last registration address. Existing law does not require the notice form to be uniform statewide.

Last Updated February 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Brown (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would require boards of elections to conduct citizenship checks using DMV records and require voters to present government issued ID to vote. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Newly require boards of elections to conduct citizenship checks using DMV records for all registered voters and individuals attempting to register.
  • Require local boards to forward to the district attorney any statements or testimony made by voters or witnesses who are deemed to have made false statements regarding qualifications or have given false testimony at any hearing affecting registration. Existing law does not include a similar requirement.
  • Repeal provisions of existing law that limit certain voter ID requirements to initial voters who have not previously voted in a federal election in the jurisdiction. Under existing law, such individuals can provide a current and valid photo identification or other documents, including a copy of their: utility bill, bank statement, government check, paycheck, or a government document that shows their name and address. Under this bill, only current and valid government issued photo ID would be accepted and all voters would be required to provide such ID. Currently New York voters are not required to present an ID document to vote, early or on Election Day.
  • Specify that a valid and current government issued photo ID includes the following: 
    •  New York driver's license or non-driver ID
    • U.S. passport
    • ID card issued by a U.S. educational institution
    • U.S. military ID card
    • Government issued medical medical card
    • Any ID card issued by the U.S. or state of New York
  • Require voters who do not have any forms of required ID to cast an provisional ("affidavit") ballot and present to the local board of elections a valid and current photo ID within 3 days of casting the ballot. Under existing law, such ballots are counted if the individual is determined to be an eligible voter and the ballot complies with the law.
  • Provide free identification cards for adult citizens with incomes that qualify for Medicaid.

Last Updated February 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Carroll (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 23, 2025, would establish a "vote center" model for Election Day voting in the state. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Allow a voter to vote at any location in their county of registration, rather than requiring them to vote at a location within their election district. 
  • Require Election Day polling locations to have certain equipment, such as ballot on-demand printers, to facilitate voting at vote centers.
  • Allow voters in New York City to vote at any location in the city during the early voting period. Current law allows these voters to vote early at any location in their county of registration. 
  • Require polling place accessibility for voters with disabilities. Current law allows a voter unable to access a polling place in their district due to accessibility issues to vote in another election district.

data.author: DiPietro D (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 27, 2025, would shorten the mail ballot return deadline and require provisional voters to prove residency to receive a provisional ballot. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Shorten the time a voter has to return their mail ballot (called "absentee ballot" in New York) by changing the deadline to postmarked by the first day of early voting, which is ten days before Election Day. Under existing law, ballots must be postmarked on or before Election Day. Under both the bill and existing law, election officials must receive the properly postmarked ballot on or before the seventh day after Election Day.
  • Require voters who are required to vote provisional ballots (called "affidavit ballots" in New York) to prove residency before receiving the affidavit ballot using the documents existing law allows voters to use to prove residency when registering to vote. The bill would require that documents such as utility bills must be no older than 120 days, and that electronic-only documents are not acceptable. Under existing law, voters who qualify for provisional ballots are issued them without proving residency. Election officials verify their qualifications to vote using the information the voter includes in the affidavit on the provisional ballot envelope before counting their ballots.

Last Updated March 18, 2025

  • Require in-person voters would need to present current and valid acceptable ID when voting, and mail voters would need to include a copy of current and valid acceptable ID when returning their ballots. Under existing law, voters do not present an ID document to vote, early or on Election Day. The bill would make the following forms of photo ID acceptable:
    • NY state driver's license or state ID
    • U.S. passport

    • U.S. military ID

In-person fail-safe ballots would only count if the voter provides the local board with acceptable photo ID within three days of casting the ballots. Absentee fail-safe ballots would only count if the local board of elections can determine the validity of the ballot. The bill would make individuals who are exempted by federal law exempt from its requirements.
  • Waive fees for any voter obtaining an ID card if the voter qualifies for Medicaid.

Bill version 1/2725: This bill, as introduced on January 27, 2025, would make changes to locations eligible to be polling locations. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit public schools from serving as early voting locations.
  • Repeal existing language that prohibits the use of buildings in which alcoholic beverages are sold on the day of registration or voting as polling locations.
  • Add buildings owned by entities receiving more than $1 million in annual state grant funding to the list of acceptable locations in the same or contiguous election district if a sufficient public building cannot be determined to host early voting. The owner of the building would have an opportunity to show that using the building for early voting is significantly incompatible with the building's main function when applicable.

(KenDrell, 2/9/25)(original by daniel)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 27, 2025, would make changes to locations eligible to be polling locations. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit public schools from serving as early voting locations.
  • Repeal existing language that prohibits the use of buildings in which alcoholic beverages are sold on the day of registration or voting as polling locations.
  • Add buildings owned by entities receiving more than $1 million in annual state grant funding to the list of acceptable locations in the same or contiguous election district if a sufficient public building cannot be determined to host early voting. The owner of the building would have an opportunity to show that using the building for early voting is significantly incompatible with the building's main function when applicable.

data.bill_name: Deceptive Practices and the Suppression of Voters

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Lavine (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 27, 2025, would criminalize communicating deceptive information that prevents or deters an individual from voting, and would separately criminalize voter suppression. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a new misdemeanor for spreading false information, or its attempt, regarding any of the following:
    • The time, place, or manner of any election
    • The qualifications for on voter eligibility, including any penalties associated with voting by ineligible voters
    • Information regarding a voter's registration status or eligibility
    • The political party affiliation of any candidate
  • Create a new misdemeanor for voter suppression, or its attempt. The bill would define "suppress" as using force, authority or an abuse of power to prevent, restrain, inhibit or compel another from acting in his or her own interests or intentions, or into not acting at all.
  • Elevate the new offenses to a felony charge for a second or further conviction.
  • Allow any individual impacted by the dissemination of the deceptive information to seek a court order for preventative relief, or apply for a permanent or temporary injunction, restraining order, declaratory judgment, or other order.

Last Updated January 28, 2025

data.bill_name: Enrollment in the Address Confidentiality Program

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on GOVERNMENTAL OPERATIONS

data.author: Lavine (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 27, 2025, would allow election officers to enroll in the state's address confidentiality program and require the secretary of state to designate county boards of elections to assist election officers in applying to be program participants. Under existing law, the program is available to victims of various crimes as well as reproductive health care service providers (and their immediate family), employees, patients, and volunteers.

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Simon (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2025, would require a county or city board of election inspectors to exhaust the prepared list of additional inspectors in the case of a vacancy before appointing any other qualified individual not on the list. The bill would require vacancies to be filled by a qualified individual affiliated with the same party as the absent inspector.

Last Updated March 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Simon (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2025, would expand the authority of the state and county boards of election to react to disasters or states of emergency that impact elections. The bill would:

  • Remove the existing threshold requiring that fewer than 25 percent of voters participate in an election in order to allow election officials to provide an additional day of voting after Election Day. The bill would allow officials to make a judgment call that the ability of voters to vote has become, or will become, impossible and such impossibility cannot be mitigated in the ordinary schedule for voting.
  • Allow officials to schedule an extra day of voting for any elections. Existing law limits this option to general elections.
  • Allow officials to provide extra time for election-related filings during a state of emergency
  • Allow officials to reschedule a party caucus when needed during a state of emergency
  • Establish notice requirements when officials schedule an additional day of voting or change voting days due to a state of emergency

Last Updated March 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: From ASSEMBLY Committee on RULES

data.author: Raga (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 1, 2026, would:

  • Require all public higher education institutions to develop a website to provide students with voting information, including registration information, key election dates, and details about mail voting, early voting, and Election Day voting.
  • Require each higher education institution in the state to have a student voting coordinator, who would be responsible for developing and implementing a student voting action plan. The plan would require:
    • Hosting at least three voter outreach events during each academic year, one of which must occur within 45 days of a primary or general election
    • Providing informational materials on registration and voting
    • Supporting student organizations engaged in voter registration and voter outreach activities
    • Creating a policy to excuse students from classes or jobs to allow time for voting on Election Day
  • Require public and private higher education institutions to submit an annual report to the State Board of Elections detailing efforts to promote student voter registration and participation. The board would also be required to make reports publicly available on its website.

If enacted, the bill would take effect one year after becoming law.

Last Updated June 9, 2026

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Solages (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 31, 2025, would:

  • Require challengers to take an oath affirming that they are challenging the voter's right to vote in good faith and are aware of the consequences of bad faith challenges. Under existing law, a challenged voter is required to take an oath but the challenger is not.
  • Require challengers to complete a written form with the challenger's name and address and name of the voter challenged
  • Require challengers to present the necessary oath to a challenged voter in written form. If the challenger signs the written oath, they will be deemed to have taken the oath and will be eligible to vote. Challengers would be permitted to administer the oath orally if the challenged voter has difficulty reading or requests the oath be administered orally.

Last Updated March 18, 2025

Existing law requires voters to request a mail ballot before receiving one, and justify that request with a statutorily-authorized excuse.

This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would establish the "Voter Registration Integrity Act" requiring first-time voter registration applications to include proof of citizenship in addition to currently required documentation of New York residency. Existing law does not require voters to show proof of citizenship. Under the bill, first-time voter registration applications would have to include one of the following:

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Molitor (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would criminalize "ballot harvesting" as both a felony and a misdemeanor, depending on whether the ballot harvesting was willful. Ballot harvesting would be defined as:

  • Submitting an mail ("absentee") ballot application signed by the voter if the person submitting it is not a family member of the voter or does not have a notarized authorization to submit the application. Even with a notarized authorization, a person can only assist one voter per election.
  • Collecting, distributing, or submitting to the board of elections a voter's ballot unless the person submitting the ballot is the voter's family member or a caregiver who has a notarized authorization certifying the person as a caregiver of the voter, or an election official, postal worker, or courier, in each case acting within their official duties.


Last Updated March 11, 2025

data.bill_name: Election Workers and Polling Places Protection Act

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Lavine (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would create new misdemeanor and felony offenses intended to protect voters, election officials, poll watchers, and election equipment. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a felony to prohibit a person, whether or not acting under color of law, from willfully physically damaging or threatening to physically damage any physical property being used as a polling place or tabulation center or other election infrastructure, with the intent to interfere with the administration of an election or the tabulation or certification of votes.
  • Create a misdemeanor to prohibit a person, whether or not acting under the color of law, by force or threat of force, or violence, or threat of harm to any person property, from willfully intimidating, interfering with, or attempting to do either, the ability of any person or any class of persons to qualify or act as a poll watcher, or any legally authorized election official or any person who is, or is employed by, an agent, contractor, or vendor of a legally authorized election official assisting in the administration of an election.
  • Create a misdemeanor to prohibit a person from using force or violence (or the threat thereof) to intimidate or interfere with a person's ability to vote or qualify to vote in any primary, special, or general election. The bill would also make attempted intimidation or interference a misdemeanor.
  • Create a misdemeanor to prohibit interference with any election official in the discharge of their duties and to prohibit inducing an election official to violate or refuse to comply with their duty or the law.
  • Authorize the attorney general, a county attorney, or an election official to bring civil actions to enforce the intended protections.
  • Define, for the purpose of these offenses:
    • "Election worker" as any election inspector, poll clerk or election coordinator.
    • "Election infrastructure" as any office of an election official, staff, worker, or volunteer or any physical, mechanical, or electrical device, structure, or tangible item used in the process of creating, distributing, voting, returning, counting, tabulating, auditing, storing, or other handling of voter registration or ballot information.

Last Updated February 18, 2025

data.author: Dinowitz (D)
data.legal_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 10, 2025, would expand existing crimes to include election officers among the specified victims. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Expand the list of victims targeted by the existing felony of assault in the second degree to include election officers as victims.
  • Expand the existing misdemeanor of obstructing governmental administration in the second degree to include intentionally preventing or attempting to prevent an election officer from performing an official act.
  • Create a new way to commit the misdemeanor of harassment in the second degree to include "with the intent to harass, annoy, threaten or alarm an election officer, he or she strikes, shoves, kicks, or otherwise subjects another person to physical contact, or attempts or threatens to do the same while such election officer is performing his or her official duties."

(KenDrell, 2/11/25)(reintro of NY A 1884)

  • Expand the existing misdemeanor of obstructing governmental administration in the second degree to include intentionally preventing or attempting to prevent an election officer from performing an official act.
  • Create a new way to commit the misdemeanor of harassment in the second degree to include "with the intent to harass, annoy, threaten or alarm an election officer, he or she strikes, shoves, kicks, or otherwise subjects another person to physical contact, or attempts or threatens to do the same while such election officer is performing his or her official duties."

data.author: Gibbs (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 10, 2025, would:

  • Repeal statutory provisions canceling the registrations of individuals convicted of felonies. Under existing law, residents who have been convicted of felonies are disenfranchised while they are incarcerated.
  • Require the Department of Corrections and Community Supervision to collaborate with boards of election to facilitate the registration and voting by incarcerated individuals. Existing law does not require a similar voter registration or voting program but does require correction facilities to notify everyone prerelease that their voting rights will be restored upon release, and provide registration forms and assistance. Existing law also requires the Department of Corrections and Community Supervision to notify people on community supervision of their voting rights and provide registration assistance.
  • Require the registration and voting program the agencies create to include access to and assistance with voter registration forms and a mechanism for voting, including but not limited to mail ballots ("absentee ballots").

Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, Section 3 requires the Legislature to enact laws disenfranchising people who have been convicted of bribery or of any infamous crime.

Last Updated March 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: DeStefano (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 12, 2025, would create a strict photo ID requirement to vote in person or vote by mail, and a process by which people without ID could obtain one for free. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a voter to show a current and valid government issued photo ID to vote in person. Current law does not require voters to present an ID document to vote, early or on Election Day.
  • Require a voter to enclose a copy of a current and valid government issued photo ID to apply for an mail ("absentee") ballot. Under existing law a voter must sign the ballot envelope, and their signature will be matched to their record, but no special identifying information is required.
  • Define "current and valid government issued photo ID" to include the following:
    • New York state driver's license or non-driver ID
    • United States Passport
    • Identification card issued by a United States educational institution
    • United States military identification card
    • Government-issued medical card or any identification card issued by the United States or the state of New York.
  • Create a process for people who lack ID to obtain one for free. To obtain a free ID, people who have sufficient documentation to receive a non-driver's ID would present that to the DMV along with an affidavit in which the voter attests that they lack a necessary ID to vote and that the election is at least seven days away. If the voter does not have the documentation to obtain a non-driver's ID but was born in New York, the bill would allow them to use the affidavit to get a free birth certificate. The birth certificate and affidavit would be sufficient to receive a free ID. Finally, the bill also provides that the DMV can issue the free ID if the voter presents the affidavit, "and a certificate of birth or any other form of documentation accepted by the [DMV] to prove the person's identity." Presumably that is how a voter born outside New York could obtain such an ID. The free IDs would be good for four years.

Last Updated March 7, 2025

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 18, 2025, would make various changes concerning assistance with mail ("absentee") voting. The bill would:

  • Define "authorized agent" to mean spouse, parent, child or sibling of the voter, a member of the voter's household, or a holder of a voter's power of attorney. Existing law is silent on this issue.
  • Limit an authorized agent to receive no more than five ballots per election.
  • Allow a voter's authorized agent to apply for a mail ballot online.

This data source would be in addition to existing law's requirement that lists of deceased people be sent at least monthly from the state's and New York City's departments of health to boards of election.

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Carroll (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 20, 2025, would lower the voting age to 16 years and create a voter registration and civics education program for high school students. The bill would make conforming changes to voter registration forms and other parts of the election code. Under existing law, people may preregister to vote at 16, but cannot vote until they turn 18. The New York Constitution Art II, section 1 requires voters to be 18 years old, however, the bill references a proposed an amendment to change that requirement. The bill expressly delays implementation of the voting age provision until the amendment is adopted, but the registration and civics education program would take effect upon enactment.

The voter registration and civics program the bill would create would:

  • Require high schools to annually:
    • Distribute a pre-filled voter registration form and a voter registration opt-out form to each student who will be 16 years old on or before December 31 of the year. School administrators would be required to complete all information on the voter registration forms for each student, except for their citizenship status, party affiliation, signature, and date the form is signed. Students would be directed to correct any errors on the form.
    • Give students classroom time to complete whichever form they wish and then turn in both. Schools would be required to dispose of the voter registration forms for students who opted out of registration.
  • Require schools to teach high school students civics education for at least eight full class periods each year, in accordance with the New York Department of Education's social studies framework for those grades.

Last Updated February 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Solages (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 20, 2025, would create same-day registration at every early voting and election-day polling place. The process would be called "conditional registration", and conditional registrants would be required to cast provisional ballots (called "affidavit ballots" in New York.) These ballots would count if the voter registration information is verified through matching at the DMV, the Social Security Administration, or otherwise, either before or during the canvass. Same day registration is currently available in New York on the first day of early voting only.


Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, section 5 requires that people register at least ten days before Election Day.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

data.bill_name: Disrupted Voting Times and Locations During Elections

status_actions.summary: Substituted by S4602B

data.author: Gibbs (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 1, 2026, would:

  • Require the local board of elections to toll the scheduled time frame for voting if early voting or Election Day voting at one or more polling places or election districts is disrupted for more than one hour. Existing law does not include a similar tolling provision.
  • Specify that "disruption" means wan obstruction or interruption at a polling place that causes voters of at least one election district to be unable to vote for a period of time exceeding one hour during a scheduled timeframe for voting.
  • Specify that, as a remedy for the time lost due to the disruption, the scheduled time frame for voting would be adjusted for each polling place or election district and compensated on that day or the following day, but not later than election day.
  • Require the state board of elections to publicize any remedial action taken if there is a delay or disruption that results in an extension of voting hours.

Under existing law, election inspector or poll clerk appointments in each district must be equally divided between major political parties.

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Carroll (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 27, 2025, would convert all Election Day polling places to vote centers without allowing polling place consolidation, change rules relating to poll watcher appointment and powers, improve voter education relating to upcoming elections, and make numerous transparency and election security changes that Voting Rights Lab does not track. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all polling places on Election Day to be vote centers at which any voter in the county can vote their appropriate ballot and prohibit boards of election from justifying a reduction in the number of polling places based on vote center availability. Under existing law, voters are required to vote at their assigned polling place, except that if a voter casts a provisional ballot ("affidavit ballot" in New York) at a polling place in their county and state assembly district, all the races on that ballot that the voter was eligible to vote for will be counted. By requiring all polling places in a county to offer voters their correct ballot, voters should no longer risk having parts of their ballots invalidated because they appeared at the wrong polling location unless the voter attempts to vote in the wrong county.
  • Require all early voting sites to be vote centers, eliminating the option to assign voters to a particular early voting location.
  • Expand who may appoint poll watchers, change how many watchers may be appointed, and what poll watchers may explicitly observe. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Authorize the committee on open government to appoint poll watchers from a pool of New York residents who apply to be watchers. The committee would be allowed to appoint as many watchers as it deemed appropriate, and if that number is fewer than the number of applicants, would be required to select the most qualified applicants without regard to the watchers' partisan affiliation. These watchers would be allowed to serve at polling places, mail ballot processing locations, and during the post-election audit. Under existing law, the committee does not have the power to appoint watchers.
    • Change the number of poll watchers per polling place that other authorized entities may appoint from three per election district to four per polling place. Because polling places may have more than one election district assigned to them, this change might result in fewer authorized watchers at a given location.
    • Require poll workers to give a watcher who is present a copy of each tabulator's results tape, provided they submitted their request in writing in advance to the local board of elections or asked a poll worker in person at least a half hour before polls close.
    • Allow watchers to observe, if they request, the number of voters who have signed in at each table, the summary report of voters who have signed in, and the public and protective counter numbers for each scanner, as well as verify that the electronic poll book router is working correctly. Poll watchers would also be allowed to confirm that the ballot-marking device was working correctly and that access compliant with the Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990 to the polling location is available.
  • Revise a requirement that boards of election give notice on their websites of upcoming special elections to require such notice before general and village elections as well, and to expand the required content, timing, and distribution of that notice. Specifically, boards would be required to post notice of the upcoming general, special, or village election on their websites and social media channels beginning 30 days before the election, and beginning 25 days before the election, use their websites and social media to notify and direct voters to the information contained in the online notice. The information must be available in all major languages spoken in the board's jurisdiction. Existing law requires the notice of a special election to be posted on the board's website not less than two weeks before the election and does not require social media posting or the translation of information. In addition, the bill would require the notice to include the:
    • day of the election,
    • hours of the election,
    • offices to be voted on,
    • voter registration deadline,
    • mail ballot request deadline,
    • mail ballot postmark deadline,
    • days and times of early voting,
    • a mechanism by which voters can find their polling places, including information on the accessibility of such polling places, and
    • sample ballots for each polling place.
  • Existing law requires the online notice to include only the day and hours of the election, and the offices to be voted upon, with a link, if available, to a polling place lookup tool.
  • Require boards of election, at least 30 days before a primary, to prominently publish a notice on their websites and through their social media channels regarding the upcoming primary, and beginning 25 days before the primary, use their websites and social media to notify and direct voters to the information contained in the online notice. The information must be available in all major languages spoken in the county. Existing law requires publication of a more limited notice in two newspapers in the board's jurisdiction the week before the election and on Election Day and does not have a translation requirement. The notice the bill would require must specify the:
    • day of the primary,
    • hours of the primary,
    • public offices and party positions included in the primary,
    • voter registration deadline,
    • mail ballot request deadline,
    • mail ballot postmark deadline,
    • days and times of early voting,
    • a mechanism by which voters can find their polling places, including information on the accessibility of such polling places, and
    • sample ballots for each polling place.
  • Require the online ballot tracking system to include, for voters whose ballots have been rejected, instructions on how they may cure their ballots.
  • Require every board or body authorized to designate places for registration shall consistently have voter registration instructions prominently displayed on its website.
  • Require boards of elections to publicly announce polling place locations and the form and content of ballots and ballot envelopes, including candidates and questions on the ballots, at least 30 days before an election, and accept public comment. The public comment would be advisory only, and the boards would retain their power to change a polling site up to five days before an election if the required notice is given to affected voters. The bill would amend the existing notice requirement to add notice by email and text to any affected voters with such contact information available to them.
  • Make numerous changes to the election code that Voting Rights Lab does not track, including increasing the frequency and transparency of results reporting, strengthening the chain of custody procedures, strengthening election equipment testing and documentation, mandating certain post-election public hearings, and requiring certain paper ballots.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the code.

Last Updated April 4, 2025

status_actions.summary: Amended in ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 4, 2025, would:

  • Require individuals sending mailers to voters for the purpose of later challenging their right to vote to file copies of the mailer, the list of recipients and identifying information relating to the sender with the State Board of Elections within two business days of the mailing. Failure to file would create a presumption against using the mailing to challenge a voter. Existing law has no similar requirement.
  • Require challengers to complete a specified challenge affidavit for each voter they challenge.
  • Specify what questions are to be asked of a challenged voter, under oath, to verify their eligibility. If the voter failed to answer any of the questions fully, they would not be allowed to vote.
  • Provide that a failure of a voter to certify that they are a citizen shall be considered an innocent error and not considered as attempting to register to vote.
  • Create additional recordkeeping requirements for poll workers relating to challenges.
  • Create two new misdemeanors relating to challenge affidavits. One would prohibit willfully losing altering, destroying, or mutilating a challenge affidavit or challenge report, and the other would prohibit making a material false statement in a challenge affidavit.

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Reyes (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 6, 2025, would outline automatic voter registration and pre-registration procedures specific to DMV transactions, Medicaid enrollment, and New York City Housing Authority applications. Note: New York enacted an automatic voter registration law in December 2020. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Specify automatic voter registration procedures for individuals who submit applications for a driver's license, driver's license renewal, ID card, or any renewal, recertification, or re-examination transaction, or any change of address or name.
    • Require DMV officials to electronically transmit certain personal information to the state board of elections if during a transaction they conclusively determine a person is old enough to register or pre-register to vote and is a U.S. citizen.
    • The personal information would include: name, date of birth, driver's license or state ID number, residence, mailing address, citizenship status, copy of signature, date of the transaction. The bill would also require transmission of party affiliation, if provided.
    • The bill prohibits DMV officials from transmitting information of participants in the address confidentiality program.
    • Specify that an individual will be registered for an election if their DMV transaction occurs by the 12th day before the election and the information is transmitted by the 10th day prior.
  • Require DMV officials and the state board of elections to jointly determine which documents conclusively demonstrate United States citizenship. Nothing under the bill would be construed to require voters to present proof of citizenship for voter registration.
  • Require county or city election officials to promptly send a notice to automatically registered or pre-registered voters informing them of their option to decline such registrations and that a decision to decline would remain confidential.
    • Officials would be required to cancel the registration of anyone who declines.
    • The notice must offer voters the opportunity to enroll with a political party and advise them that only enrolled members of a party may vote in that party's primaries.
    • This notice would need to be combined with other existing notices and include information on how to keep one’s address confidential.
  • Repeal a provision in existing law that limits an "application for services or assistance" to an application for a driver's license, a driver's license renewal, or an identification card issued by the DMV.
  • Specify automatic voter registration procedures for individuals who apply for or re-enroll in Medicaid.
    • Require officials at the department of health and local social services departments to electronically send personal information of verified U.S. citizens to the state board of elections.
    • The information includes name, date of birth, Social Security last 4 digits, address, gender, phone, email, and party enrollment if indicated.
    • Specify procedures for how updates should be handled for already registered voters.
  • Specify automatic voter registration procedures specific to New York City Housing Authority applications applicants who reach the eligibility interview or reexamination stage, similar to Medicaid procedures.
  • Specify that if necessary to comply with federal law, before sending a person's information to the board of elections, agencies must notify the person by mail within 15 days of the transaction and give them 17 days to opt out using a pre-paid form.
  • Require officials to mail a second notice regarding party affiliation if no response is received within 45 days after the first notice is sent to automatically registered voters who did not designate a political party.
  • Require the state board of elections to release monthly public reports, excluding personally identifying information and categorized by gender and age, regarding the number of voters who were automatically registered and pre-registered, changed their registration, and declined registration.

Last Updated June 9, 2025

data.bill_name: Modernized Voter Registration Act of New York

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 20, 2025, would require automatic voter registration, same-day voter registration, and online voter registration. Note, New York enacted an automatic voter registration law in December 2020. However, the system this bill would enact differs in significant ways from the enacted system. Notably, this bill does not amend the existing automatic registration system. Instead, this bill creates new sections of code. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Create automatic voter registration at the DMV, all existing voter registration agencies, and as part of certain events, such as signing up for classes at a city or state university or regaining eligibility after a felony conviction. The DMV and voter registration agencies are already part of the enacted automatic voter registration system. Because this bill does not reference the enacted legislation it is unclear how locations covered by both this bill and enacted law would function.
  • Create same-day voter registration on both Election Day and during early voting. Same day registration is currently available in New York on the first day of early voting only. Note, the bill does not specify any particular process to be followed. The New York constitution Art II, section 5 requires people to register 10 days before an election.
  • Require online voter registration. Existing law already requires online voter registration, and it is currently available to voters. The system the bill would require would have these features:
    • An online application for voter registration
    • Online assistance with applying to register to vote
    • Online completion and submission by applicants of the mail voter registration application including assistance with providing a signature in electronic form
    • Online receipt of completed voter registration applications
  • Require boards of election to offer those same registration services by automated telephone process.
  • Require voter registration applications to enable voters to provide an email address and receive future voting information by email.

Last Updated April 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Bichotte (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 20, 2025, would:

  • Require the New York City Board of Elections to provide interpreters for all languages that are designated city-wide languages in the New York City code or other applicable statute at all poll sites that contain an election district with at least fifty voting age residents whose limited English proficiency language is a designated city-wide language. Existing law does not include a comparable provision.
  • Require the Board of Elections to designate which polling places need such interpreters using the most recent United States census data or American community survey data, whichever is more recent. Beginning on January 1, 2028, the Board would need to re-review that data every two years.
  • Require the interpreters to be inside the poll site, except that any interpreter that engaged in electioneering would be relieved of their duties.
  • Require the State Board of Elections to set standards for the number of interpreters required at each polling location based on the number of voters who potentially need the service.

Note: The bill does not name New York City, instead addressing its requirements to a city of one million or more residents. Only New York City is that large.

Last Updated April 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 20, 2025, sets out a timetable for local boards of elections to mail ballots to voters who have applied for them. Existing law does not specify a timeline. Under the bill, local boards would mail ballots:

  • No later than the 46th day before Election Day, to voters who applied for a mail ballot (called "absentee ballot" in New York) at least 60 days before.
  • No later than the 42nd day before Election Day, to voters who applied after the 60th day through the 46th day before.
  • Within four days of receiving the application for applications received after the 46th day through the 30th day before the election.
  • Within three days of receiving the application for applications received after the 30th day through the 15th day before the election.
  • Within two days of receiving the application for applications received after the 15th day through the 7th day before the election. Existing law, which the bill would not amend, provides that applications to receive a ballot by mail must be received by the board of elections by the 15th day before an election.
  • On the day the application was received if every reasonable effort enables it, if the application was timely postmarked and received after the seventh day before the election. Existing law, which the bill would not amend, provides that applications to receive a ballot by mail must be received by the board of elections by the 15th day before an election.
  • If the application is delivered in person after the seventh day before the election through the day before the election, the board would be required to make every reasonable effort to deliver the ballot on the day the application is received. Existing law allows in-person delivery of a mail ballot through the day before Election Day.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.public_commentary:

Under the bill, the booklet could include any other languages the board chooses.
