data.bill_name: Modernized Voter Registration Act of New York

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 11, 2025, would establish a new "automated voter registration" system, expand online and same-day registration, enhance data privacy and security standards, and require new voter protections. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish automated voter registration for individuals who interact with designated government agencies, including the DMV, social service agencies, public universities, housing authorities, and parole offices, provided they consent to register. Agencies must electronically transmit voter registration data—including name, address, date of birth, citizenship, and a signature image—to election officials for inclusion in the statewide voter registration list. New York previously enacted legislation requiring multiagency automatic voter registration with varying implementation dates.
  • Require online registration through official government websites and an automated phone-based system, with provisions for providing an electronic signature. Existing law allows online registration only through the DMV and does not expressly authorize a phone-based system.
  • Mandate same-day registration at polling places during early voting and on Election Day. Under current law, same-day registration is available in New York on the first day of early voting only.
  • Implement new voter list maintenance and security standards, including defining matching rules and record retention policies, limiting access to confidential information, and requiring agencies to transmit change-of-address information within 24 hours unless the person opts out.
  • Require signature verification for absentee ballots and prohibit processing of ballots that do not match the voter’s registration signature. Under existing law, officials check signatures to confirm they match the registration record.
  • Prohibit "voter caging" by banning the use of returned or undeliverable mail, unverified match lists, or immaterial errors to justify a voter registration challenge, unless corroborated with independent evidence. Existing law does not explicitly prohibit these practices.
  • Require poll worker training to cover accessibility, non-discrimination, and election security, and allow state employees paid leave to serve as poll workers.
  • Allocate $5 million to fund research and pilot programs to develop accessible paper ballot systems for voters with disabilities and limited English proficiency.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights lab related to audits. 

Last Updated April 15, 2025

data.bill_name: State Board of Elections and Local Boards of Elections

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Slater (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 11, 2025, would prohibit the state board of elections and all local boards from accepting any donation of private funds for election administration. Under the bill, private funds would include money from individuals, businesses, non-profit organizations, and other non-public entities. Existing law does not expressly prohibit acceptance of such funds. 

Last Updated April 14, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 11, 2025, would require correctional facilities in New York to establish in-person polling places for eligible incarcerated voters and expand voter education and status tracking requirements for detained individuals. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require boards of elections to establish polling places in jails and prisons, known as "correctional polling places," open to eligible detained voters for the full duration of early voting and Election Day. Existing law does not require in-person voting sites in detention facilities.
  • Mandate that detained individuals who are otherwise eligible to vote be assigned "active eligible detainee" status and be automatically assigned to a correctional polling place for the election in their district of residence or last domicile. Existing law does not create or define a special voter status for detained individuals.
  • Authorize the use of experimental voting machines so long as such machines from interfering with the duty of election officials to ensure equitable voting access for detained voters compared to non-detained voters
  • Require sheriffs to regularly report detained individuals' intake, transfer, and release data to update voter status. Reporting must occur at least monthly and increase in frequency leading up to an election. Existing law does not require these reporting procedures for voter registration purposes.
  • Require sheriffs to distribute nonpartisan voting rights materials and hold educational sessions, led by detained individuals and supported by community groups, in correctional facilities. Existing law does not mandate voter education in correctional settings.
  • Direct boards of elections to develop public plans to implement correctional polling places, including staffing, equipment, voter outreach, and coordination with detention facilities. These plans must be publicly posted and distributed to local public defenders and community organizations. 
  • Prohibit obstruction of correctional polling places and eligible detained voters' right to vote, and create a private right of action with judicial remedies, including damages and court-ordered relief. The bill would also authorize the attorney journey to file related legal actions. 

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to visitor access rules for correctional facilities.


Last Updated April 14, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Slater (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 11, 2025, would change existing language concerning early voting locations to require each county to place at least one early voting location in a city in the county or the county seat. Current law requires each county to place an early voting location at the largest city in the county.

This resolution, as introduced on May 1, 2025, would propose amending the state constitution to require government-issued photo identification for all in-person voters. Voters would be required to provide, in addition to their signature, an approved form of photo ID, such as a New York driver's license or state ID, U.S. passport, military ID, or a student ID from a U.S. educational institution, government issued medical card, or any other ID issued by the federal or state government. The requirement would apply to all voters, whether they register in person or not, except in cases of illiteracy or physical disability. Under existing constitutional law, which the bill leaves in place, voters need only identify themselves by their signature.


If approved, the amendment must pass the next legislative session after the general election and be publicly published for three months before taking effect.

data.bill_name: Electronic Absentee Ballot Applications for Military

status_actions.summary: Amended in ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Carroll (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 18, 2026, would:

  • Allow military and special federal voters to submit completed mail ballot applications through the electronic absentee ballot application transmittal system or other electronic means such as email or fax. Under existing law, voters may use the system, but federal post card forms are not expressly authorized for this method.
  • Provide that ballot applications submitted electronically through the state’s absentee ballot system are considered original applications, eliminating the need to separately submit a paper version.
  • Eliminate the requirement that voter registration applications from military voters be returned by mail or in person.
  • Specify that military voters are entitled to military ballots in each subsequent election unless their registration is cancelled or placed in inactive status, they no longer meet qualifications for such a ballot, or the they request otherwise. Existing law provides that a voter who submits a military ballot application is entitled to a military ballot through the next two general elections in even numbered years (including runoffs) only.
  • Change the deadline for registering military voters who apply for a military ballot from 35 days before an election to 10 days before an election.
  • Move the window for mailing ballots to military and special federal voters, requiring boards of elections to send ballots no earlier than 90 and no later than 75 days before a primary or general election. Under existing law, the window is 60 to 40 days before an election.
  • Require institutions of higher education to provide students studying abroad during an election with an electronic federal post card application form (or a link to it) and clear voting instructions at least one week before their classes start. Under existing law, there is no specific obligation for schools to provide voting materials or guidance to students abroad.

Last Updated March 19, 2026

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Walker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 13, 2025, would:

  • Require that any application for an absentee ballot that does not include a qualifying excuse be processed as a request for an early mail ballot. The bill would require application forms to include notice of this requirement. Existing law does not allow for processing absentee applications as early mail ballot requests by default.
  • Clarify that early mail ballots are for voters who do not qualify for an absentee ballot but still wish to vote by mail. Under current law, an absentee ballot is for voters who are absent from their county, ill, incarcerated, or in a VA hospital on Election Day, while an early mail ballot is for any voter choosing to vote by mail without providing an excuse.
  • Standardize envelope instructions, affirmation statements, and delivery procedures across different election types, including general, primary, school district, and special town elections for absentee and early mail ballots. Existing law provides for similar materials but does not harmonize envelope formats and instructions across ballot types.
  • Expand the application options for military and special federal voters to clarify ballot eligibility duration and ballot delivery instructions. Existing law already allowed these voters to apply electronically but did not specify some of the new application timing and ballot delivery provisions.


Last Updated May 14, 2025

This provision would be an exception to existing law limits election inspector or poll clerk service to residents of the county in which they serve.
  • Specify that if located within the city of New York, the students may be certified in any county there as long as they are registered to vote in the state, do not hold elective office, are not a candidate for office in the district of service, and can speak, read, and legibly write English.

data.author: Molitor (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 2, 2025, would permit election commissioners of the major political parties in a county to jointly designate alternative locations for an early voting site, providing an option to substitute the otherwise required site in the largest city or municipality in the county if both commissioners agree. Under existing law, each county must designate at least one early voting site in the largest city or, if there is no city, the most populous municipality in the county, except in Washington County where it must be in the county seat.

Last Updated June 3, 2025

Voters who have neither must provide a statement that they are qualified and registered to vote. Under exiting law, mail ballot applications only require a voter's name, address, date of birth, and signature.

data.bill_name: Ballots Mailed Into the Board of Elections

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Assembly Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 27, 2026, would:

  • Clarify that an absentee or early mail ballot is deemed time received before the close of polls on Election Day if it is returned in an envelope with a U.S. Postal Service or foreign postal service postmark dated no later than Election Day. However, ballots that otherwise would not be counted under federal timing rules would only be eligible to be counted for nonfederal elections. Existing law allows absentee and early mail ballots postmarked by Election Day to be counted if received within seven days after the election.
  • Apply the same rule to military ballots by deeming them timely received before the close of polls on Election Day if it is returned in an envelope with a qualifying postmark dated no later than Election Day, but ballots otherwise barred from counting under federal-election timing rules would only be eligible to be counted for nonfederal elections. Existing law counts military ballots postmarked or signed and dated by Election Day if received within seven days after a primary election or 13 days after a general or special election.
  • Create a fallback rule if a court limits federal ballot counting by specifying that if a court determines that only ballots received by Election Day may be counted for federal elections, otherwise timely ballots may still be counted for nonfederal elections.

Last Updated June 10, 2026

data.bill_name: Receipt of Ballots by Board of Election

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Assembly Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 27, 2026, would:

  • Require every early mail, absentee, special, or other ballot that may be returned by mail to include a written notice explaining all lawful return methods (including mail, express delivery, delivery to a board of elections, early voting site, Election Day poll site, or ballot drop box). Existing law allows these return methods but does not require this standardized notice.
  • Require the notice to advise voters to return ballots as soon as possible, consider alternatives to domestic mail if returning a ballot close to Election Day, and seek a hand cancellation date when mailing. Existing law does not require this specific timing guidance.
  • Require the state board of elections, county boards, and New York City Board of Elections to post the notice on their websites, send it to available email addresses or phone numbers for voters who requested early mail ballots, and post it on official social media accounts.
  • Authorize the state board of elections to issue rules and regulations necessary to implement the notice requirement.

Last Updated June 10, 2026

data.bill_name: Military or Armed Government Presence at Polls

status_actions.summary: From ASSEMBLY Committee on RULES

data.author: Assembly Rules Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 29, 2026, would:

  • Create criminal offense for troops or armed government presence at polling places. Under the bill, it is punishable by a fine, imprisonment for up to five years, or both, for an officer of the army or navy, or another person in the civil, military, or naval service of New York or the United States, to order, bring, keep, or control troops or armed persons at a place where an election is held, unless necessary to repel armed enemies of the United States and where the person acts in violation of federal law. Existing law does not include a similar offense.
  • Specify that the prohibition does not prevent members of the armed forces from voting and does not apply to law enforcement responding to a specific emergency or disturbance at the request or authorization of the presiding election officer, law enforcement presence otherwise required by law, or state or national guard members activated by the governor to perform election-related duties under election officer supervision.
  • Create criminal offense punishable by up to five years in prison for election interference by armed forces. The prohibited behavior includes: interfering with elections by prescribing voter qualifications, preventing qualified voters from voting, compelling election officials to accept votes from unqualified voters, imposing different election regulations, or interfering with election officials’ duties. Existing law does not create this state-level offense.
  • Create criminal penalties for election interference by certain government employees. The bill would make it punishable imprisonment for up to one year, or both, for a federal, state, or local administrative employee connected to an activity financed in whole or in part by federal or state loans or grants to use official authority to interfere with the nomination or election of a candidate for federal, state, or local office, where the person acts in violation of federal law. Existing law does not create this state-level offense.
  • Allow a person impacted by a violation to seek civil relief and recover reasonable attorney’s fees, expert fees, litigation expenses, and costs if successful. The bill would also authorize election officers and the attorney general to bring actions for equitable relief, including temporary or permanent injunctions, restraining orders, or other orders. Existing law does not provide this specific civil enforcement framework.
  • Authorize the attorney general jurisdiction to prosecute offenses created by the bill and require civil actions brought under the bill to receive expedited pretrial and trial proceedings and an automatic calendar preference. Existing law does not provide these specific enforcement procedures.

Last Updated June 9, 2026

data.bill_name: Automatic Voter Registration and Pre Registration

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Gianaris (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 8, 2026, would outline automatic voter registration and pre-registration procedures specific to DMV transactions and Medicaid enrollment. Note: New York enacted an automatic voter registration law in December 2020. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Specify automatic voter registration procedures for individuals who submit applications for a driver's license, driver's license renewal, ID card, or any renewal, recertification, or re-examination transaction, or any change of address or name. Specifically, the bill would:
  • Require DMV officials to electronically transmit certain personal information to the state board of elections if during a transaction they conclusively determine a person is old enough to register or pre-register to vote and is a U.S. citizen. The personal information would include: name, date of birth, driver's license or state ID number, residence, mailing address, citizenship status, copy of signature, date of the transaction. The bill would also require DMV officials to collect a person's gender, email, phone number, if available. Choice of party enrollment would also be included, if affirmatively provided by the voter. For an applicant who is already registered to vote in New York state who does not affirmatively indicate a choice of party enrollment, their existing party affiliation status would remain unchanged as part of any registration update. The bill would prohibit DMV officials from transmitting information of participants in the address confidentiality program.
  • Require DMV officials and the state board of elections to jointly determine which documents conclusively demonstrate United States citizenship. Nothing under the bill would be construed to require voters to present proof of citizenship for voter registration.
  • Prohibit automatic voter registration or preregistration from occurring when an applicant provides documentation or agency-held data conclusively demonstrating ineligibility due to noncitizenship or age. While existing law already provides that no voter registration application may be submitted if an applicant declines registration or fails to sign the integrated application, this bill would newly bar the Department of Motor Vehicles from offering voter registration or transmitting registration information for individuals who present documentation conclusively demonstrating they are not U.S. citizens, require the DMV and the state board of elections to jointly determine which documents establish noncitizenship, and prohibit all designated agencies from transmitting registration information for individuals who are demonstrably ineligible based on age or citizenship data received in the ordinary course of agency operations.
  • Require county or city election officials to promptly send a notice to automatically registered or pre-registered voters informing them of their option to decline such registrations and that a decision to decline would remain confidential. Officials would be required to cancel the registration of anyone who declines. Under the bill, the notice must offer voters the opportunity to enroll with a political party and advise them that only enrolled members of a party may vote in that party's primaries. The notice would also include information for voters who wish to prevent disclosure of their address.
  • Repeal a provision in existing law that limits an "application for services or assistance" to an application for a driver's license, a driver's license renewal, or an identification card issued by the DMV.
  • Specify that New York City Housing Authority applicants who reach an eligibility interview or complete a reexamination transaction will be automatically registered or pre-registered. New York enacted an automatic voter registration law in December 2020, but implementation has been delayed and existing law does not extend automatic voter registration to public housing authority applications.
  • Specify automatic voter registration procedures for individuals who apply for or re-enroll in Medicaid. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require officials at the department of health and the county and city departments of social services to automatically and electronically send certain personal information to the state board of elections to facilitate registration and pre-registration of individuals to vote if they are of sufficient age and are externally verified as U.S. citizens. The personal information would include: name, date of birth, driver's license or state ID number, residence, mailing address, citizenship status, copy of signature, date of the transaction. The bill would also require DMV officials to collect a person's gender, email, phone number, if available. Existing law declares these departments as designated automatic voter registration agencies beginning January 1, 2024.
    • Specify that if necessary to comply with federal law, before sending a person's information to the state board of elections, department officials must notify the person by mail within 15 days of the transaction that they may decline their registration by using the postage paid pre-addressed return form. If a person returns the notice within 17 days then their voter registration information would not be sent to the state board of elections. A voter would be registered to vote for an election if their information is sent to the county or city board 10 days before an election.
    • Require officials to mail a second notice regarding party affiliation if no response is received within 45 days after notice is sent to automatically registered voters who did not designate a political party.
    • Require the state board of elections to release monthly public reports, excluding personally identifying information and categorized by gender and age, regarding the number of voters who were automatically registered and pre-registered, changed their registration, and declined registration.

If enacted, the bill would generally take effect on January 1, 2028, except for provisions barring automatic voter registration or preregistration when agency-held documentation or data conclusively demonstrates ineligibility and establishing joint document-determination authority for the department of motor vehicles and the state board of elections, which would take effect immediately.

Last Updated January 12, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Rhoads (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would require voters to show a photo ID when voting in person, and enclose a copy of such ID when applying for and returning a mail ballot. The bill would also create a process to receive a free voter ID that is more expansive than the free ID system the DMV already offers. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require in-person voters to show a current and valid government-issued photo ID to vote in person. Under existing law, voters are identified by reciting their name and address and signing the poll book. The poll book signature is then compared to their registration record.
  • Require mail voters to enclose a copy of a current and valid government-issued photo ID with both their ballot application and their ballot return envelope.
  • Define current and valid government-issued photo identification as including the following: 
    • New York state driver's license or non-driver ID
    • United States Passport
    • Identification card issued by a United States educational institution
    • United States military identification card
    • Government-issued medical card or any identification card issued by the United States or the state of New York.
  • Create a process for people without voter ID to obtain a free one from the DMV. Specifically:
    • A person without voter ID could get a free non-driver ID from the DMV if they give the DMV the required documentation for a state ID plus a specific affidavit created by the bill, provided that the request is made at least seven days before Election Day.
    • A person without voter ID and without the documentation required for a non-driver state ID could complete the affidavit created by the bill, present it to the appropriate clerk of the New York jurisdiction in which they were born or the State Department of Health, and receive a certified copy or certified transcript of the individual's record of birth at no cost to the requesting individual if the clerk or department has access to the individual's record of birth. No later than the seventh day before the election, if the individual presents their record of birth and the signed affidavit to the DMV, the DMV would issue the individual a non-driver identification card at no cost.
    • A notary who administers the oath for the affidavit would not be permitted to collect a fee for collecting the required documents.
    • The State Board of Elections would create and distribute the relevant affidavit as specified by the bill.

Last Updated January 13, 2025

data.bill_name: Voting Rights and Access for Incarcerated Individuals
data.bill_location: Senate Crime Victims, Crime and Correction Committee

status_actions.summary: Amended in SENATE Committee on CRIME VICTIMS, CRIME AND CORRECTION

data.author: Myrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would improve voting rights for incarcerated individuals by providing the eligible residents the ability to register and vote, enforceable in court. The bill would create two methods for eligible incarcerated voters to vote by mail ballot and authorize local boards of election to offer two days of early voting at correctional facilities.


Specifically, the bill would guarantee the right of eligible incarcerated residents to vote by:

  • Declaring that residents who are eligible to register to vote and vote and who are detained, confined, or committed to a correctional facility are "entitled to the free exercise and enjoyment of the elective franchise without discrimination or preference."
  • Requiring the Department of Corrections to issue regulations directing each facility to ensure the timely delivery, and to facilitate the timely return if applicable, of all official election mail, forms, notices, or communications to any individual detained or confined at such facility and that non-partisan plain language educational materials about the voting rights of individuals currently incarcerated and formerly incarcerated are included in the inmate handbook or similar materials and reentry resources.
  • Requiring the rules and regulations governing correctional facilities recognize the right of qualified detained or incarcerated individuals to register and vote. Such rules would be required to allow access to electronic personal voter registration or voter registration by application, and allow access to mail balloting services ("absentee balloting services") through the electronic mail ballot application transmittal system or by paper application. The rules for both voter registration and mail voting would be required to best effect this spirit and intent while being consistent with the proper discipline and management of the correctional facility.
  • Requiring the superintendent of each correctional facility to cooperate with the board of elections in developing and implementing a plan to facilitate at least one method of voter access for all individuals eligible to vote who are detained or confined at each such facility, including making available space and reasonable accommodations.
  • Requiring the same registration and voting access be given to people who are in solitary confinement as other eligible incarcerated voters.
  • Authorizing aggrieved individuals to enforce their rights in court.
  • Creating a presumption that if any superintendent, employee, or agent of a correctional facility by commission or omission intentionally frustrates the ability of eligible incarcerated people to register and vote, an election law violation has occurred that may be enforced in court by any aggrieved persons, an organization whose membership includes aggrieved persons or members of a protected class, an organization whose mission, in whole or in part, is to ensure voting access, or the attorney general. That section of code allows multiple remedies including nominal, compensatory, or punitive damages.

The bill would facilitate registration and voting by incarcerated eligible people through three different measures. One involves expanding an existing program in which election workers bring mail ballots to residential facilities, distribute them, collect them, and return them to the board of elections, as well as provide any needed assistance to the people voting them. Another prescribes registration and mail voting processes facilities not using that in-person mail ballot program, while the third authorizes local boards of elections to offer an early voting polling place for incarcerated eligible voters. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Expand an existing program in which election workers bring mail ballots to nursing homes and other residential facilities. Some of the changes to the program would be specific to correctional facilities, and some would apply to all residential facilities. The bill would:
    • Add correctional facilities to the named facilities that can participate in the program.
    • Require people who bring mail ballots to any residential facilities, including correctional facilities, to be trained election inspectors. Existing law does not explicitly require this training.
    • Require boards of elections to contact the specified residential and correctional facilities at least 45 days before an election to arrange days and times election workers will go to the facility. If a residential facility that is not one of the named types but has 25 or more residents apply for mail ballots, the local board of elections would be required to contact them to make similar arrangements when that threshold was reached. Existing law requires this contact to be made at least 20 days before the election, or when a sufficient number of applications have been received.
    • Expand the window of time in which boards of elections may bring mail ballots to residential facilities to start 21 days before Election Day and expand the possible hours to between 7 a.m. and 8 p.m. Existing law allows the process to occur beginning thirteen days before Election Day and sets the possible hours as between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m.
    • Expand the supplies that the election workers bring to residential facilities to include voter registration forms, mail ballot applications, and sufficient equipment to print and issue mail ballots or all the ballots addressed to voters at the facilities who applied for them. In addition, the election workers would be required to bring assistive devices and any reasonable accommodations for disabled voters, as well as interpretive resources and personnel for those in need of language assistance. Completed mail ballot applications would be treated as an application to receive a ballot in person, rather than to have a ballot mailed to the applicant.
    • Require boards of elections, when serving a correctional facility in this program, to provide voter registration forms and mail ballots for voters not eligible to vote in the jurisdiction covered by the local board, in accordance with a written procedure the board would be required to adopt. Those procedures would include, but not be limited to, the delivery and retrieval of forms and ballots. The correctional facility's superintendent, administrator or director, or their designated democracy officer or agent would be required to countersign the procedures, and the local board of elections would be required to submit them to the State Board of Elections at least 90 days before the election. The State Board would be required to approve them, with any necessary amendments, by 75 days before the election. These procedures would be public records.
    • Require boards of elections, with regard to correctional and local correctional facilities with at least 75 or more detainees in counties with at least 100,000 registered voters, to go to such facilities and offer voting by mail ballot without regard to how many people at the facility applied for one. Existing law authorizes boards of elections to send election workers to all residential facilities in the existing program in their jurisdiction, and requires them to go to any facility for which it has received at least 25 mail ballot applications.
    • Require new related recordkeeping. Specifically, the board of elections would be required to keep a list of the days and times inspectors go to each facility, the number of mail ballot applications received from the facility, and the number of mail ballots returned by voters at the facility. This list would be a public record, and the local board would be required to deliver a copy to the State Board of Elections within seven days after certifying the election. Existing law does not require tracking the number of applications or ballots returned, and does not require delivering the list to the State Board.
  • Create a mail voting program for eligible incarcerated people and require boards of elections, with regard to any correctional or local correctional facility that does not meet the requirements for mandatory inclusion in the residential in-person mail voting program except those exclusively detaining people under 18 years old, to either include the facility in that program or provide the mail voting program created by the bill. That program would require the board of elections to coordinate with the appropriate person at the correctional facility to facilitate voter access by:
    • Ensuring that all eligible citizens are given a voter registration form.
    • Ensuring all eligible voters are provided with a mail ballot application at least 21 days before Election Day.
    • Including in the inmate handbook or similar materials and reentry resources non-partisan educational materials in plain language about the voting rights of individuals currently or formerly incarcerated, as drafted by the State Board of Elections. The bill would expressly allow facilities to also include or distribute non-partisan educational materials in additional resources provided to or made available to incarcerated or formerly incarcerated individuals.
    • Complying with the duties and obligations imposed by the guarantee of voting rights.
  • Allow local boards of elections generally to open early voting polling places at correctional facilities and local correctional facilities instead of offering a mail balloting program. The polling place would be open for at least three hours beginning on the tenth day before Election Day and ending on and including the ninth day before. The board of elections in New York City would be authorized to open the polling place for at least six hours beginning on the tenth day before Election Day and ending on and including the eighth day before. These early voting sites would be in addition to, not instead of, any others required by law, nor would they impact siting decisions for those required sites. Only voters who are detained, confined, or employed by the facility and eligible to vote in the county in which the facility is located (or in New York City, if the facility is in New York City) would be allowed to vote in the polling place. All other eligible people or voters would be given voter registration forms and mail ballot applications.

Last Updated January 7, 2025

data.author: May (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would enable the governor and the State Board of Elections (SBOE) to remove a commissioner of a local board of elections "for incompetence, misconduct, or other good cause" by majority vote. Because the SBOE is evenly bipartisan, a majority vote would necessarily be bipartisan. Before the SBOE could vote or the Governor could act, the local commissioner would be given the charges against them in writing, and have an opportunity to defend themselves. 

Under existing law, the SBOE does not have authority to remove local election commissioners, and the Governor must exert removal power "in the same manner as a sheriff".

Last Updated January 15, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Krueger (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would specify requirements regarding which buildings may be used as polling places and early voting locations. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require officials to designate any public building as a polling place "to the extent practicable." The bill would expressly prohibit public school buildings from being used as early voting locations. Existing law requires buildings that are exempt from taxes to be used whenever possible as polling places. If additional polling places are needed, the bill would authorize a building that is tax exempt or owned by an entity receiving over a million dollars in annual state grant funding to be used as a polling place if it is in the same or a contiguous election district. The bill would create an exception for building owners who demonstrate that use of the building as a polling place is significantly incompatible with the primary function of the entity. 
  • Remove an existing provision that prohibits designating as an early polling place any place of business licensed to sell alcohol "for on premises consumption" during any day of local registration or voting.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Hoylman (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would lower the voting age to 16 years and create a voter registration and civics education program for high school students. The bill would make conforming changes to voter registration forms and other parts of the election code. Under existing law, people may preregister to vote at 16, but cannot vote until they turn 18. The New York Constitution Art II, section 1 requires voters to be 18 years old, however, a separate resolution would propose an amendment to change that requirement to 16 years. 

The voter registration and civics program the bill would create would:

  • Require high schools to annually:
    • Distribute a pre-filled voter registration form and a voter registration opt-out form to each student who will be 16 years old on or before December 31 of the year. School administrators would be required to complete all information on the voter registration forms for each student, except for their citizenship status, party affiliation, signature, and date the form is signed. Students would be directed to correct any errors on the form.
    • Give students classroom time to complete whichever form they wish and then turn in both. Schools would be required to dispose of the voter registration forms for students who opted out of registration.
  • Require schools to teach high school students civics education for at least eight full class periods each year, in accordance with the New York Department of Education's social studies framework for those grades.


Last Updated January 10, 2025

data.bill_name: Deceptive Practices and the Suppression of Voters

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Stewart-Cousins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 8, 2025, would criminalize communicating deceptive information that prevents or deters an individual from voting, and would separately criminalize voter suppression. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a new misdemeanor for spreading false information, or its attempt, regarding any of the following:
    • The time, place, or manner of any election
    • The qualifications for on voter eligibility, including any penalties associated with voting by ineligible voters
    • Information regarding a voter's registration status or eligibility
    • The political party affiliation of any candidate
  • Create a new misdemeanor for voter suppression, or its attempt. The bill would define "suppress" as using force, authority or an abuse of power to prevent, restrain, inhibit or compel another from acting in his or her own interests or intentions, or into not acting at all.
  • Elevate the new offenses to a felony charge for a second or further conviction.
  • Allow any individual impacted by the dissemination of the deceptive information to seek a court order for preventative relief, or apply for a permanent or temporary injunction, restraining order, declaratory judgment, or other order.

Last Updated January 21, 2025

data.author: Borrello (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 9, 2025, would criminalize "ballot harvesting" as both a felony and a misdemeanor, depending on whether the ballot harvesting was willful. Ballot harvesting would be defined as:

  • Submitting an mail ("absentee") ballot application signed by the voter if the person submitting it is not a family member of the voter or does not have a notarized authorization to submit the application. Even with a notarized authorization, a person can only assist one voter per election.
  • Collecting, distributing, or submitting to the board of elections a voter's ballot unless the person submitting the ballot is the voter's family member or a caregiver who has a notarized authorization certifying the person as a caregiver of the voter, or an election official, postal worker, or courier, in each case acting within their official duties.

data.bill_name: Election Workers and Polling Places Protection Act

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: May (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 10, 2025, and entitled the "Election Workers and Polling Places Protection Act", would create several new criminal offenses designed to protect election workers from harassment and intimidation and election equipment from unauthorized tampering. All of the new offenses would be class A misdemeanors except the one related to election equipment, which would be a class D felony. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit the following acts, punishable as criminal offenses:
    • Interfering with an election official completing their lawful duty or inducing an official to fail to comply with their lawful duty.
    • Using a threat of force or harm to intimidate or interfere with a poll watcher.
    • Using a threat of force or harm to intimidate or interfere with a voter.
    • Using a threat of force or harm to intimidate or interfere with an election official, including acts of retaliation.
    • Damaging or threatening damage to election equipment at a polling place or tabulation center.
    • Obstructing, impeding, or interfering with access or egress from a location where voting, tabulation, or canvassing is conducted.
  • Authorize the attorney general, county attorneys, and election officials to bring civil actions to enforce the prohibitions created by the bill.


Last Updated January 29, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Borrello (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 10, 2025, would shorten the mail ballot return deadline and require provisional voters to prove residency to receive a provisional ballot. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Shorten the time a voter has to return their mail ballot ("absentee ballot") by changing the deadline to postmarked by the first day of early voting, which is ten days before Election Day. Under existing law, ballots must be postmarked on or before Election Day. Under both the bill and existing law, election officials must receive the properly postmarked ballot on or before the seventh day after Election Day.
  • Require voters who are required to vote provisional ballots ("affidavit ballots") to prove their residency before receiving the provisional ballot using the documents existing law allows voters to use to prove residency when registering to vote. The bill would require that documents such as utility bills be no more than 120 days old, and prohibit electronic-only documents. Under existing law, voters who qualify for provisional ballots are issued them without proof of residency. Election officials verify their qualifications to vote using the information the voter includes in the affidavit on the provisional ballot envelope before counting their ballots.

In-person voters would need to present current and valid acceptable ID when voting, and mail voters would need to include a copy of current and valid acceptable ID when returning their ballots.   • Require any person without photo ID to use fail-safe voting. Their ballots would only count if the local board of elections can determine the validity of the ballot. 
  • Make individuals who are exempted by federal law exempt from its requirements.

data.bill_name: Notification of Death by County Boards of Elections

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on HEALTH

data.author: Murray (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 15, 2025, would revise certain voter registration list maintenance practices relating to removing deceased voters from the list. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the State Board of Health and the New York City Board of Health to give the State Board of Elections information about deceased people immediately, and not later than within seven business days.
  • Require the State Board of Elections to give local boards of elections the list of deceased people within seven business days of receiving it.
  • Require local boards of elections to cancel such voters' registrations. 

Existing law requires the state board and any local board of elections that receives information about deceased voters to transfer that information to the relevant local board of elections each month, but does not specifically address forwarding on the lists received or impose a similar deadline. Similarly, existing law requires the registrations of deceased voters to be canceled, but does not include the specific language the bill would include.

This bill, as amended on June 1, 2026, would:

  • Require all public higher education institutions to develop a website to provide students with voting information, including registration information, key election dates, and details about mail voting, early voting, and Election Day voting.
  • Require each higher education institution in the state to have a student voting coordinator, who would be responsible for developing and implementing a student voting action plan. The plan would require:
    • Hosting at least three voter outreach events during each academic year, one of which must occur within 45 days of a primary or general election
    • Providing informational materials on registration and voting
    • Supporting student organizations engaged in voter registration and voter outreach activities
    • Creating a policy to excuse students from classes or jobs to allow time for voting on Election Day
  • Require public and private higher education institutions to submit an annual report to the State Board of Elections detailing efforts to promote student voter registration and participation. The board would also be required to make reports publicly available on its website.

If enacted, the bill would take effect one year after becoming law.

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Kavanagh (D)
data.legal_commentary:
Bill version 1/24/25: This bill, as introduced on January 24, 2025, would create new procedures to bring clarity to the voter challenge process. Specifically, the bill would:

Require people sending mailers to voters for the purpose of later challenging their right to vote to file copies of the mailer, the list of recipients, and identifying information relating to the sender with the State Board of Elections within two business days of the mailing. Failure to file creates a presumption against using the mailing to challenge a voter. Existing law has no similar requirement.

Require challengers to complete a specified challenge affidavit for each voter they challenge.

Specify what questions are to be asked of a challenged voter, under oath, to verify their eligibility. If the voter failed to answer any of the questions fully, they would not be allowed to vote.

Provide that a failure of a voter to certify that they are a citizen shall be considered an innocent error and not considered as attempting to register to vote.

Create additional recordkeeping requirements for poll workers relating to challenges.

Create two new misdemeanors relating to challenge affidavits. One would prohibit willfully losing altering, destroying, or mutilating a challenge affidavit or challenge report, and the other would prohibit making a material false statement in a challenge affidavit.

(KenDrell, 2/9/25)(reintro of NY S 2987)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 28, 2026, would create new procedures to bring clarity to the voter challenge process. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require people sending mailers to voters for the purpose of later challenging their right to vote to file copies of the mailer, the list of recipients, and identifying information relating to the sender with the State Board of Elections within two business days of the mailing. Failure to file creates a presumption against using the mailing to challenge a voter. Existing law has no similar requirement.
  • Require challengers to complete a specified challenge affidavit for each voter they challenge.
  • Specify what questions are to be asked of a challenged voter, under oath, to verify their eligibility. If the voter failed to answer any of the questions fully, they would not be allowed to vote.
  • Provide that a failure of a voter to certify that they are a citizen shall be considered an innocent error and not considered as attempting to register to vote.
  • Create additional recordkeeping requirements for poll workers relating to challenges.
  • Create two new misdemeanors relating to challenge affidavits. One would prohibit willfully losing altering, destroying, or mutilating a challenge affidavit or challenge report, and the other would prohibit making a material false statement in a challenge affidavit.

Existing law requires absentee ballot envelopes for local elections to include a voter attestation referencing disqualifications related to certain criminal convictions, but that language no longer reflects current law regarding voting eligibility.

A previous version of the bill included a provision that was amended out of the current text. The provision would have changed the mail voter statement on ballots for school district and town elections to reflect that voters who have been discharged from parole or whose maximum term of incarceration has expired may vote by absentee ballot.

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 3, 2025, would facilitate voting by people whose native language is Haitian Creole. Specifically, the bill would require the New York City Board of Elections to provide all the information it provides in English in Haitian Creole, including registration or voting notices, forms, instructions, voting systems, ballot marking devices, and ballots in Haitian Creole.

The New York City Board of Elections would also be required to disseminate citywide a booklet that includes: a voter registration form in English with instructions in Haitian Creole, instructions in Haitian Creole regarding the criteria and application process for absentee voting, and a section with general voter information in Haitian Creole. Finally, the board of elections would be required to provide one or more Haitian Creole-speaking interpreters at each polling location in any county that has a population comprised of five percent or more of people who identify as being Haitian according to the most recent United States census. Existing law does not include similar requirements.

Last Updated February 19, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 3, 2025, would require a board of elections in a city with a population of over one million to provide the same information in Haitian Creole that it provides in languages other than English on its website. In addition, it would be required to disseminate citywide a booklet that includes: a voter registration form in English with instructions in Haitian Creole, instructions in Haitian Creole regarding the criteria and application process for obtaining a mail ("absentee") ballot, and a section with general voter information in Haitian Creole including frequently asked questions.

Last Updated February 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Comrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would:

  • Require that any time an election official sends an address confirmation notice, places a voter's registration in inactive status, or cancels a voter's registration, the election official must send notice to that voter:
    • Via forwardable first class mail at the address where officials believe the voter currently resides and
    • At the voter's registered address.
    • Via any email addresses or telephone numbers in the voter's registration records. Under existing law, whenever the board of elections has reason to believe that a voter is no longer qualified, it is required to notify the voter by first class forwardable mail.
  • Require the state board to develop a uniform statewide form for these notices.

Last Updated March 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: Opinion referred to JUDICIARY

data.author: Comrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, proposes an amendment to the New York Constitution to repeal a provision suggesting that voters must have an excuse in order to vote "absentee." The state has already enacted and implemented legislation allowing all voters the option of voting early by mail.

In order to go into effect, the proposed amendment would have to be adopted by a majority in both legislative chambers in this session and the legislative session beginning in 2027 and then receive a majority vote at an election. A similar amendment was rejected by voters in 2021.

Last Updated February 26, 2025

status_actions.summary: Opinion referred to JUDICIARY

data.author: Rhoads (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would propose amending the state constitution to require government-issued photo identification for all in-person voters. Voters would be required to provide, in addition to their signature, an approved form of photo ID, such as a New York driver's license or state ID, U.S. passport, military ID, or a student ID from a U.S. educational institution, government issued medical card, or any other ID issued by the federal or state government. The requirement would apply to all voters, whether they register in person or not, except in cases of illiteracy or physical disability. Under existing constitutional law, which the bill leaves in place, voters need only identify themselves by their signature.

If approved, the amendment must pass the next legislative session after the general election and be publicly published for three months before taking effect. 

Last Updated February 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Comrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 7, 2025, would create a cure process for provisional ballots ("affidavit ballots"). Specifically, when the local board of elections determines at or before the time of the canvass that a provisional ballot envelope is incomplete or otherwise defective, the board would be required to mail notice to the voter within one day. If the board has the voter's phone or email, the board would need to give notice through those means as well. The voter could cure the defects by signing and returning an affirmation within seven business days of the board mailing the notice. The board must include the affirmation form with the notice. If a provisional ballot defect is not curable, the board must be sent notice within three business days of their ballot's rejection.

Last Updated February 18, 2025

data.bill_name: Disrupted Voting Times and Locations During Elections

status_actions.summary: Passed ASSEMBLY

data.author: Gounardes (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 1, 2026, would:

  • Require the local board of elections to toll the scheduled time frame for voting if early voting or Election Day voting at one or more polling places or election districts is disrupted for more than one hour. Existing law does not include a similar tolling provision.
  • Specify that "disruption" means wan obstruction or interruption at a polling place that causes voters of at least one election district to be unable to vote for a period of time exceeding one hour during a scheduled timeframe for voting.
  • Specify that, as a remedy for the time lost due to the disruption, the scheduled time frame for voting would be adjusted for each polling place or election district and compensated on that day or the following day, but not later than election day.
  • Require the state board of elections to publicize any remedial action taken if there is a delay or disruption that results in an extension of voting hours.

Last Updated June 9, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on TRANSPORTATION

data.author: Weik (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 10, 2025, would:

  • Require anyone applying to register to vote at DMV to include a Social Security number as part of their application. Under existing law, Social Security numbers are not required as part of any voter registration process.
  • Establish "driving privilege" licenses that can only be used for operating a motor vehicle distinct be distinct from commercial driver's licenses, and prohibit any government entity from accepting driving privilege licenses as proof of personal identification.
  • Alter the drivers' license type that may be obtained by people who do not document their legal right to be in the United States.



Last Updated March 11, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Sepulveda (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 18, 2025, would significantly change voter registration list maintenance by keeping inactive registrations on the inactive list perpetually, rather than removing them after the voter takes no voting-related action in two consecutive general election cycles. Removal for any reason of ineligibility, such as death, felony conviction, mental incapacity, moving out of state, refusing to take a challenge oath, or any other reason for ineligibility, would remain. The bill would further provide that if an inactive voter casts a provisional ballot ("affidavit ballot"), their status would be restore to active and the ballot would count if it was otherwise eligible to count. Under existing law such ballots are counted.

Last Updated February 19, 2025

data.bill_name: Photographs of Self and Ballot While Voting

data.author: Sepulveda (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 18, 2025, would allow voters to photograph their own ballot and themselves while in a privacy booth for voting and to display the photo on social media. The bill would create a misdemeanor for forcing an individual to photograph their own ballot or post it on social media. The bill would also add language exempting such "ballot selfies" from an existing misdemeanor prohibiting a voter from showing their ballot or revealing its contents.

data.author: Comrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 19, 2025, would allow the governor to suspend or delay an election in a jurisdiction subject to a state or federal disaster emergency. Under the bill, if the election is delayed or suspended, it must be rescheduled as soon as practicable. In addition, in an election in a jurisdiction affected by an emergency within 14 days of the election, voters would be permitted to vote by mail without further excuse. Finally if a voter appears at the wrong polling place in the right county because of the emergency, they may cast an affidavit (provisional) ballot and have their ballot count for any races they are qualified to vote in.

The bill would require the same signature affirmation for early mail voters and special presidential voters. If enacted, the bill would go into effect one year after becoming law.

Existing law allows the legislature to direct how officers are appointed but does not include an express provision regarding authority for determining qualifications. 

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires passage by the current legislature and approval again in the next legislative session after the general election and publication for three months before taking effect.

This bill, as introduced on February 20, 2025, would allow a voter to choose to vote by mail if they may be unable to personally appear at their polling place due to religious observance.

Note: The New York Constitution Art. II Section 2 limits the ability to choose to vote by mail to voters who may be absent from their county or New York City, as applicable, or who may be unable to appear personally at the polling place because of illness or physical disability.

These qualifications would include general requirements for serving as a public officer, such as not having been convicted of certain categories of public corruption crimes, as well as having experience in election administration or other forms of administrative, operations, or management experience. Under existing law that would remain unchanged, local boards of elections are appointed by local governing bodies.

The bill also purports to create an online mail ballot application portal. In 2021, effective April 1, 2022, New York enacted legislation to create an online absentee ballot application portal.

This bill, as introduced on February 28, 2025, would expand existing crimes to include election officers among the specified victims. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Expand the list of victims targeted by the existing felony of assault in the second degree to include election officers as victims.
  • Expand the existing misdemeanor of obstructing governmental administration in the second degree to include intentionally preventing or attempting to prevent an election officer from performing an official act.

This resolution, as introduced on February 28, 2025, proposes an amendment to the state constitution to remove existing language requiring voters to complete registration 10 days before an election.

Last Updated March 4, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Gianaris (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 28, 2025, would establish same-day voter registration for new registrants only at early voting and Election Day polling locations. The bill would allow same day registrants who provide proof of residency to vote a regular ballot, while same day registrants who do not provide proof of residency would vote a provisional ("affidavit") ballot subject to verification of the registrant's residency and eligibility.

Last Updated April 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 4, 2025, would create same-day registration on Election Day, require all voters to be mailed ballots for all elections without changing early voting or Election Voting locations, ostensibly shift control of voter registration lists from the counties and New York City to the state, and change when mail ballots are brought by election officials to nursing homes and similar institutions to be voted in person. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create same-day registration on Election Day. The bill states that a same-day registrant must complete the ordinary voter registration form and provide the ordinary proofs to support it when registering on Election Day. Existing law does not require an ID document to register or to vote, although it does require a person to provide a driver's license number, a state ID number, or a Social Security number, or affirm that the applicant has none. 
  • Require local boards of election to mail all registered voters ballots at their registration address for all elections for which they are qualified to vote. Existing law requires voters to request mail ballots and to justify their request with an excuse authorized by statute.
  • Allow people who expect to be away from their registration address to request to have their ballot sent to a different address. The bill would require the State Board of Elections to make a standard application but would require local boards to accept any request that substantially complied with the state form. Under existing law, voters requesting mail ballots may request that the ballot be sent to an address different from their registration address. 
  • Eliminate the requirement that 25 people in a residential care facility request absentee ballots before a local board of elections can bring ballots to the facility to assist voters with casting ballots, and require ballots be brought to all such facilities. 
  • Clarify that the use of titles, initials, or customary abbreviations of given names in a voter's or witness's signature on an application to redirect the mail ballot, or on the mail ballot envelope, cannot be the basis for invalidating the application or ballot for a signature mismatch. 
  • Require the State Board of Elections to implement, in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner, a single, uniform, official, centralized, interactive, computerized statewide voter registration database system. The system would be defined, maintained, and administered at the state level, contain the voter registration lists maintained by each county board of elections, and assign a unique identifier to each legally registered voter. Although counties would be allowed to maintain lists in a parallel system, they would need to facilitate the state's maintenance of the state system. The bill would require the state system to offer various features to assist in election administration, including signature verification. Existing law, not addressed by the bill, requires the State Board of Elections to maintain an interactive, statewide, computerized, voter registration list.

Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, Section 2 authorizes only people who are out of their county or out of New York City, as applicable, and people who are unable to appear to vote in person due to illness or physical disability to choose to vote by mail. Art. 2, Section 5 requires people to register to vote a minimum of 10 days before an election.

Last Updated April 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 3, 2024, would:

  • Repeal statutory provisions canceling the registrations of individuals convicted of felonies and require the Department of Corrections and Community Supervision to collaborate with boards of election to facilitate the registration and voting by incarcerated individuals.
  • Require the registration and voting program the agencies create to include access to and assistance with voter registration forms and a mechanism for voting, including but not limited to mail ("absentee") ballots. Under existing law, people who have been convicted of felonies are disenfranchised while they are incarcerated. Existing law does not require a similar voter registration or voting program but does require correction facilities to notify everyone prerelease that their voting rights will be restored upon release, and provide registration forms and assistance. Existing law also requires the Department of Corrections and Community Supervision to notify people on community supervision of their voting rights and provide registration assistance.

Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, Section 3 requires the Legislature to enact laws disenfranchising people who have been convicted of bribery or of any infamous crime.

Last Updated March 5, 2025

data.bill_name: Modernized Voter Registration Act of the State

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 5, 2025, would require automatic voter registration, same-day voter registration, and online voter registration. Note, New York enacted an automatic voter registration law in December 2020. However, the system this bill would enact differs in significant ways from the enacted system. Notably, this bill does not amend the existing automatic registration system. Instead, this bill creates new sections of code. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Create automatic voter registration at the DMV, all existing voter registration agencies, and as part of certain events, such as signing up for classes at a city or state university or regaining eligibility after a felony conviction. The DMV and voter registration agencies are already part of the enacted automatic voter registration system. Because this bill does not reference the enacted legislation it is unclear how locations covered by both this bill and enacted law would function.
  • Create same-day voter registration on both Election Day and during early voting. Same day registration is currently available in New York on the first day of early voting only. Note, the bill does not specify any particular process to be followed. The New York constitution Art II, section 5 requires people to register 10 days before an election.
  • Require online voter registration. Existing law already requires online voter registration, and it is currently available to voters. The system the bill would require would have these features:
    • An online application for voter registration
    • Online assistance with applying to register to vote
    • Online completion and submission by applicants of the mail voter registration application including assistance with providing a signature in electronic form
    • Online receipt of completed voter registration applications
  • Require boards of election to offer those same registration services by automated telephone process.
  • Require voter registration applications to enable voters to provide an email address and receive future voting information by email.


Last Updated March 11, 2025

status_actions.summary: Opinion referred to JUDICIARY

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on March 5, 2025, proposes amending the state constitution to specify voting rights protections and prohibit certain restrictions on voter registration and access. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit state and local government from imposing voting qualifications, prerequisites, or procedures that result in denying or abridging the right to vote based on race, color, religion, gender preference, or disability.
  • Ban certain voting requirements, including literacy tests, educational qualifications, moral character assessments, poll taxes, government-issued photo ID requirements at polling sites, documentary proof of citizenship for registration, and voter vouching systems.
  • Protect third-party voter registration efforts by preventing the state or local governments from restricting organizations or individuals who register voters in good faith.

To take effect, this resolution must be approved in two consecutive legislative sessions and then approved by voters.

Last Updated March 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 25, 2025, would require the New York City Board of Elections to provide interpreters for all languages that are designated city-wide languages in the New York City code or other applicable statute at all poll sites that contain an election district with at least fifty voting age residents whose limited English proficiency language is a designated city-wide language. The Board of Elections would be required to designate which polling places need such interpreters using the most recent United States census data or American community survey data, whichever is more recent. Beginning on January 1, 2028, the Board would need to re-review that data every two years. The bill would require the interpreters to be inside the poll site, except that any interpreter that engaged in electioneering would be relieved of their duties. The bill would require the State Board of Elections to set standards for the number of interpreters required at each polling location based on the number of voters who potentially need the service. Finally, the bill clarifies that it would set a floor for providing interpreters, and that the boards of elections could provide more.

Note: The bill does not name New York City, instead addressing its requirements to a city of one million or more residents. Only New York City is that large. The next largest city is Buffalo, which has less than 300,000 residents.

Last Updated March 6, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Parker (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 6, 2025, would establish new protections against voter suppression, enhance election transparency, and implement oversight measures for changes to voting laws. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Create a felony offense for voter intimidation, which includes using or threatening force, violence or any tactic of coercion to induce or compile a person not to vote, register to vote, or vote a certain way. 
  • Create a felony offense for voter suppression, which includes knowingly attempting to prevent or deter someone from voting or registering through fraudulent or deceptive means, including falsely challenging voter eligibility, providing misleading information to discourage registration or voting, or giving false details about election dates, times, locations, or procedures.
  • Require election officials to post a "Voter’s Bill of Rights" at polling places and distribute election procedure manuals to poll workers. The bill would mandate that these materials outline key voter rights, address language assistance, and provide guidance on handling election-day disputes, ballot security, and emergency protocols.
  • Prohibit voting qualifications or procedures that restrict access to voting based on race, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, or disability.
  • Mandate preclearance for any changes to voting qualifications or election procedures, requiring approval from the attorney general or a court before implementation. 

Last Updated March 7, 2025

data.bill_name: Applications for Registration and Enrollment of Voters

Under existing law, such voters may only personally register at the board of elections office in their county and vote only in a general election held at least 10 days later.

Last Updated April 11, 2025

This bill, as introduced on March 17, 2025, would prohibit officials from designating public school buildings as early voting locations. Existing law does not include a similar prohibition.

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 24, 2025, would prohibit a court from changing the date of a primary election which results in the bifurcation of the offices and positions to be elected and nominations to be made that statute requires to be part of the same primary election, except that this restriction would not apply to a presidential primary.
