Last Updated April 1, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on FINANCE

data.author: Gonzalez (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 28, 2025, would:

  • Require that any application for an absentee ballot that does not include a qualifying excuse be processed as a request for an early mail ballot. The bill would require application forms to include notice of this requirement. Existing law does not allow for processing absentee applications as early mail ballot requests by default.
  • Clarify that early mail ballots are for voters who do not qualify for an absentee ballot but still wish to vote by mail. Under current law, an absentee ballot is for voters who are absent from their county, ill, incarcerated, or in a VA hospital on Election Day, while an early mail ballot is for any voter choosing to vote by mail without providing an excuse.
  • Standardize envelope instructions, affirmation statements, and delivery procedures across different election types, including general, primary, school district, and special town elections for absentee and early mail ballots. Existing law provides for similar materials but does not harmonize envelope formats and instructions across ballot types.
  • Expand the application options for military and special federal voters to clarify ballot eligibility duration and ballot delivery instructions. Existing law already allowed these voters to apply electronically but did not specify some of the new application timing and ballot delivery provisions.

Last Updated April 15, 2025

data.bill_name: Poll Worker Income Earned on an Election Day

This bill, as introduced on March 28, 2025, would:

  • Exempt from state income tax:

Last Updated March 31, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Cleare (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 3, 2025, would require local boards of election to send a mail ("absentee") ballot application to all voters who filed a mail ballot application for the previous election, and would create a list of voters who would receive a mail ballot application for each election. Any voter would be allowed to join or leave the list at any time by communicating with their board of elections electronically or by mail. Under existing law, voters who claim permanent illness or physical disability can sign up to receive a mail ballot for every election.


If enacted, the bill would take effect immediately, except for Section 2 (establishes the permanent ballot application list), which would take effect upon voter approval of the related resolution to amend the constitution.

Last Updated April 4, 2025

data.bill_name: Time Period For Mailing of Absentee Ballots

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Gianaris (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 4, 2025, sets out a timetable for local boards of elections to mail ballots to voters who have applied for them. Existing law does not specify a timeline. Under the bill, local boards would mail ballots:

  • No later than the 46th day before Election Day, to voters who applied for a mail ballot (called "absentee ballot" in New York) at least 60 days before.
  • No later than the 42nd day before Election Day, to voters who applied after the 60th day through the 46th day before.
  • Within four days of receiving the application for applications received after the 46th day through the 30th day before the election.
  • Within three days of receiving the application for applications received after the 30th day through the 15th day before the election.
  • Within two days of receiving the application for applications received after the 15th day through the 7th day before the election. Existing law, which the bill would not amend, provides that applications to receive a ballot by mail must be received by the board of elections by the 15th day before an election.
  • On the day the application was received if every reasonable effort enables it, if the application was timely postmarked and received after the seventh day before the election. Existing law, which the bill would not amend, provides that applications to receive a ballot by mail must be received by the board of elections by the 15th day before an election.
  • If the application is delivered in person after the seventh day before the election through the day before the election, the board would be required to make every reasonable effort to deliver the ballot on the day the application is received. Existing law allows in-person delivery of a mail ballot through the day before Election Day.

Last Updated April 7, 2025

data.bill_name: Delivery of an Absentee Ballot or Application Therefore

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on CODES

data.author: Cleare (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 21, 2025, would require courts to inform defendants who are at least 18 years old of their right to vote while detained pre-trial. In addition, all local correctional facilities would be required to post information regarding voting eligibility for individuals in pre-trial detention in a location easily accessible by all such defendants.

Last Updated April 23, 2025

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Skoufis (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on March 13, 2026, would:

  • Allow military and special federal voters to submit completed mail ballot applications through the electronic absentee ballot application transmittal system or other electronic means such as email or fax. Under existing law, voters may use the system, but federal post card forms are not expressly authorized for this method.
  • Provide that ballot applications submitted electronically through the state’s absentee ballot system are considered original applications, eliminating the need to separately submit a paper version.
  • Eliminate the requirement that voter registration applications from military voters be returned by mail or in person.
  • Specify that military voters are entitled to military ballots in each subsequent election unless their registration is cancelled or placed in inactive status, they no longer meet qualifications for such a ballot, or the they request otherwise. Existing law provides that a voter who submits a military ballot application is entitled to a military ballot through the next two general elections in even numbered years (including runoffs) only.
  • Change the deadline for registering military voters who apply for a military ballot from 35 days before an election to 10 days before an election.
  • Move the window for mailing ballots to military and special federal voters, requiring boards of elections to send ballots no earlier than 90 and no later than 75 days before a primary or general election. Under existing law, the window is 60 to 40 days before an election.
  • Require institutions of higher education to provide students studying abroad during an election with an electronic federal post card application form (or a link to it) and clear voting instructions at least one week before their classes start. Under existing law, there is no specific obligation for schools to provide voting materials or guidance to students abroad.


Last Updated March 16, 2026

data.bill_name: New York Election Officer Protection Act

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Gonzalez (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 9, 2026, would:

  • Create a misdemeanor offense for knowingly disseminating restricted personal information of an election officer with the intent or knowledge that it will be used to threaten, intimidate, or facilitate violence against the election officer. Existing law does not specifically criminalize the targeted dissemination of election officers’ personal information.
  • Expand eligibility for the state's address confidentiality program to include election officers, allowing them to use designated substitute addresses to protect against threats of violence. Existing law limits eligibility primarily to victims of domestic violence, stalking, and related offenses, and certain other protected groups.
  • Prohibit any person from intimidating or attempting to intimidate an election officer based on the officer’s performance of election duties, assistance to voters, or status as a past or present election officer, including through the use or threat of force, violence, restraint, duress, or other conduct that reasonably interferes with the execution of election duties. Existing law does not contain a standalone prohibition specifically addressing intimidation or obstruction of election officers in the election law.
  • Prohibit obstruction or interference with access to polling places, election offices, or voting equipment when such conduct reasonably prevents election officers from carrying out their duties. Existing law addresses certain forms of election interference but does not expressly frame obstruction as a civil violation tied to election officer protection.
  • Create a rebuttable presumption of intent to intimidate or interfere with election administration when a person visibly possesses a firearm, rifle, or shotgun at a polling place in violation of existing weapons restrictions, with exceptions for law enforcement and peace officers acting within the scope of their duties. Existing law regulates weapons at polling places but does not create a presumption related to election officer intimidation.
  • Authorize election officers, boards of elections, the state board of elections, the attorney general, and certain organizations to bring civil actions for violations, and authorize courts to impose tailored remedies. Existing law does not provide a dedicated civil enforcement mechanism for election officer intimidation claims.

Last Updated January 13, 2026

data.author: Bottcher (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 21, 2026, would lower the voting age to 16 years and create a voter registration and civics education program for high school students. The bill would make conforming changes to voter registration forms and other parts of the election code. Under existing law, people may preregister to vote at 16, but cannot vote until they turn 18. The New York Constitution Art II, section 1 requires voters to be 18 years old, however, the bill references a proposed an amendment to change that requirement. The bill expressly delays implementation of the voting age provision until the amendment is adopted, but the registration and civics education program would take effect upon enactment.

The voter registration and civics program the bill would create would:

  • Require high schools to annually:
    • Distribute a pre-filled voter registration form and a voter registration opt-out form to each student who will be 16 years old on or before December 31 of the year. School administrators would be required to complete all information on the voter registration forms for each student, except for their citizenship status, party affiliation, signature, and date the form is signed. Students would be directed to correct any errors on the form.
    • Give students classroom time to complete whichever form they wish and then turn in both. Schools would be required to dispose of the voter registration forms for students who opted out of registration.
  • Require schools to teach high school students civics education for at least eight full class periods each year, in accordance with the New York Department of Education's social studies framework for those grades.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Gonzalez (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 15, 2026, would create a felony prohibiting any person or entity, other than a board of elections, from erecting, displaying, or maintaining a mail ballot drop-off box or receptacle. Under existing law, voters are allowed to return their mail ballots by hand to voting locations or boards of elections offices, and by mail.

data.bill_name: Ballots Capable of Return by Mail

status_actions.summary: To ASSEMBLY Committee on ELECTION LAW

data.author: Skoufis (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 22, 2026, would:

  • Require every early mail, absentee, special, or other ballot that may be returned by mail to include a conspicuous written notice, in a form approved by the State Board of Elections, explaining all lawful methods for timely return. The notice would need to include methods for return, including: mail, express delivery, delivery by the voter or an agent to the appropriate board of elections, delivery to an early voting site or Election Day poll site, and deposit in a ballot drop box. Existing law allows these return methods but does not require this standardized notice to accompany every ballot capable of return by mail.
  • Require the notice to advise voters to return ballots as soon as possible, recommend using alternatives to domestic mail if returning a ballot within several days of the election, remind voters using domestic mail to seek a hand cancellation date so the mailing date can be determined, and advise overseas voters to consider delivery times for their chosen delivery service.
  • Require the State Board of Elections, county boards of elections, and the New York City Board of Elections to prominently post the notice on their official websites, send it to any email address or phone number maintained for voters who requested early mail ballots, and post it on official social media accounts.

Last Updated May 26, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on ELECTIONS

data.author: Zellner (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 29, 2026, would create same-day registration at every early voting and election-day polling place. The process would be called "conditional registration", and conditional registrants would be required to cast provisional ballots (called "affidavit ballots" in New York.) These ballots would count if the voter registration information is verified through matching at the DMV, the Social Security Administration, or otherwise, either before or during the canvass. Same day registration is currently available in New York on the first day of early voting only.


Note: The New York Constitution Art. II, section 5 requires that people register at least ten days before Election Day.

data.bill_name: Military or Armed Government Presence at Polls

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on RULES

data.author: Myrie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 1, 2026, would:

  • Create criminal offense for troops or armed government presence at polling places. Under the bill, it is punishable by a fine, imprisonment for up to five years, or both, for an officer of the army or navy, or another person in the civil, military, or naval service of New York or the United States, to order, bring, keep, or control troops or armed persons at a place where an election is held, unless necessary to repel armed enemies of the United States and where the person acts in violation of federal law. Existing law does not include a similar offense.
  • Specify that the prohibition does not prevent members of the armed forces from voting and does not apply to law enforcement responding to a specific emergency or disturbance at the request or authorization of the presiding election officer, law enforcement presence otherwise required by law, or state or national guard members activated by the governor to perform election-related duties under election officer supervision.
  • Create criminal offense punishable by up to five years in prison for election interference by armed forces. The prohibited behavior includes: interfering with elections by prescribing voter qualifications, preventing qualified voters from voting, compelling election officials to accept votes from unqualified voters, imposing different election regulations, or interfering with election officials’ duties. Existing law does not create this state-level offense.
  • Create criminal penalties for election interference by certain government employees. The bill would make it punishable imprisonment for up to one year, or both, for a federal, state, or local administrative employee connected to an activity financed in whole or in part by federal or state loans or grants to use official authority to interfere with the nomination or election of a candidate for federal, state, or local office, where the person acts in violation of federal law. Existing law does not create this state-level offense.
  • Allow a person impacted by a violation to seek civil relief and recover reasonable attorney’s fees, expert fees, litigation expenses, and costs if successful. The bill would also authorize election officers and the attorney general to bring actions for equitable relief, including temporary or permanent injunctions, restraining orders, or other orders. Existing law does not provide this specific civil enforcement framework.
  • Authorize the attorney general jurisdiction to prosecute offenses created by the bill and require civil actions brought under the bill to receive expedited pretrial and trial proceedings and an automatic calendar preference. Existing law does not provide these specific enforcement procedures.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on COMMERCE AND LABOR

data.author: Klopfenstein (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 18, 2025, would prohibit employers from discharging, threatening to discharge, or disciplining permanent employees who are absent from work to serve as precinct election officials or attend required training, as long as they provide reasonable notice. The bill would also prohibit employers from requiring such employees to use annual, vacation, or sick leave for their service. Existing law does not include similar provisions.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: LaRe (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 15, 2025, would establish new documentary proof of citizenship requirements, modify mail and provisional ballot procedures, and increase list maintenance requirements. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require applicants to provide proof of U.S. citizenship before being added to the voter registration rolls. Existing law does not require documentary proof of citizenship to register to vote. Acceptable documents would include:
    • U.S. passport
    • Birth certificate
    • Certificate of naturalization
    • Ohio driver’s license or ID number, if the secretary of state verifies citizenship using information obtained from the DMV that the individual has submitted documentation that indicates U.S. citizenship.
    • Current or expired driver's license or state ID issued on or after April 7, 2023, or a copy of the front and back of that ID if the ID does not notate whether the individual is a non-citizen. (Note: Ohio IDs currently include "noncitizen" notations.)
  • Expand the use of provisional ballots for voters whose citizenship status or identity cannot be confirmed at the time of voting, and update procedures for verifying and curing those ballots. Existing law allows provisional ballots but does not require one for lack of citizenship documentation.
  • Require officials to verify citizenship through available records or follow up with a mailed confirmation notice if citizenship cannot be confirmed. If a voter does not respond with valid proof of citizenship within 14 days, officials would cancel the registration, send the individual notice of the cancellation, and refer the matter to the attorney general for "further investigation and possible prosecution."
  • Eliminate the use of drop boxes ("secure receptacles") for returning absentee ballots by repealing provisions that previously required at least one receptacle per county and allowed for additional receptacles at the discretion of the county board of elections. Existing law requires at least one monitored drop box per county, located at the board of elections.
  • Require designated third parties assisting voters with returning mail ballots to complete a "delivery attestation," which the election official must attach to the ballot return envelope. The attestation would be made under penalty of election falsification, specify the relationship to the voter, and the date of delivery. Existing law does not include attestation requirements for third parties.
  • Direct more frequent and detailed list maintenance procedures, including regular cross-checking against state and federal records, and authorize certain voter data to be made publicly available. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the secretary to review at least monthly the statewide voter registration database to verify the citizenship of individuals on the list. Existing law allows list maintenance but does not require monthly reviews or specify public data access in the same detail.
    • Repeal provisions that prohibit the secretary of state from conducting reviews 90 days before a primary or general election for federal office. 

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to initiative petitions and petition circulation requirements.


Last Updated April 16, 2025

data.bill_name: Elector to Register as Member of Political Party

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GOVERNMENT OVERSIGHT

data.author: Lear (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 3, 2025, would:

  • Create closed primaries, requiring voters to register as members of a political party in order to vote in that party’s primary election. Under the bill, a voter may vote a political party's ballot at a primary election only if they are affiliated with that political party by the 30th day before the primary. Existing law does not require registration with a party to participate in a primary.
  • Define party affiliation based on a voter's most recent registration form or, for existing voters, the party whose primary ballot they most recently cast in the current or previous two calendar years.
  • Update voter registration forms to include a list of recognized political parties and a space for selecting one, along with an option to remain unaffiliated. 
  • Require county boards of elections to include party affiliation information in the statewide voter registration database and voter notifications.Existing law does not require this level of party affiliation tracking.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates. 


Last Updated June 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Rader (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced July 24, 2025, would:

  • Create a permanent list for voters to receive mail ballot applications. Existing law does not include provisions for any permanent mail voting lists.
  • Allow voters to request to join the list by applying directly, during voter registration or updates (either in writing or online), or when applying for a mail ballot.
  • Require local boards to mail the the mail ballot applications to all voters on the list for each election until the voter requests to be removed from the list or the voter's registration is canceled. Under the bill, applications must be mailed 45 days before the voter registration deadline.

Last Updated July 28, 2025

data.bill_name: Create an Automatic Voter Registration System

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Upchurch (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on September 23, 2025, would create opt-out automatic voter registration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create automatic voter registration at the Bureau of Motor Vehicles (BMV) and Department of Medicaid, requiring officials to electronically transmit records of individuals who appear eligible to vote to the secretary of state each business day. Information transmitted would include name, residence, date of birth, driver’s license or state ID number or last four digits of Social Security number, citizenship status, and a signature image. Automatic voter registration is not available under existing law.
  • Direct the secretary of state to forward this information to the county board of elections, which must register individuals to vote. Individuals would receive a mailed notice, with prepaid return postage, explaining eligibility requirements, their precinct, and the procedure to decline registration or preregistration or submit corrections.
  • Require county boards to treat a post-registration declination as a cancellation of the registration. If a voter's information is erroneously updated through automatic registration, the board must restore the prior registration record.
  • Establish public reporting requirements for the secretary of state, including monthly publication of the number of individuals registered or pre-registered through the automatic system and the number of individuals who declined.
  • Expand the existing felony offense under the election falsification statute to include individuals whose registration or pre-registration is processed through the new automatic voter registration system. A person registered or pre-registered through AVR would only violate the law if they knowingly provide false information with the intent of registering or updating a registration, or if they knowingly cause another ineligible person to be registered or pre-registered through AVR using false information. 
  • Make conforming changes throughout the Code to integrate the new system.

Last Updated September 24, 2025

data.bill_name: Waive Fees for Homeless Individuals Identification Card

status_actions.summary: *****To GOVERNOR

data.author: Cockley (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 11, 2026, would require photo identification to cast mail ballots, create a secure online portal for mail ballot applications, and waive certain identification card and vital records fees for individuals experiencing homelessness. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require photo ID for mail ballot applications and returned ballots. Beginning with the November 2, 2027 general election, the bill would require mail voters to provide photo ID when applying for mail voter’s ballots or, if they do not provide photo ID at application, their driver's license or state ID number or the last four digits of their Social Security number. Such voters would still need to provide photo ID when returning their voted ballot. Existing law allows voters to use an Ohio driver’s license or state ID number, the last four digits of a Social Security number, or a copy of photo ID for mail ballot identification.
  • Create exceptions and alternative identification procedures. The bill would allow certain voters to use alternative ID instead of a copy of photo identification, including uniformed services and overseas voters, voters with religious objections to being photographed, voters with severe medical conditions, physical disabilities or infirmities, voters confined in jail or workhouse, voters who lack mobility to obtain photo ID, and voters with another material obstacle. Existing law does not require this alternative-identification process because photo identification is not generally required for mail ballots.
  • Require the secretary of state to establish a secure online portal beginning with September 3, 2027, for the November 2, 2027 general election. The portal would allow electors, including uniformed services and overseas voters, to apply for absent voter’s ballots electronically and would require applicants to submit identifying information and verification through photo identification, live capture or upload, or permitted alternative identification. Existing law generally requires absentee ballot applications to be submitted in writing by mail or in person, with separate electronic procedures for uniformed services and overseas voters.
  • Make conforming changes to the absentee ballot application, identification envelope, curing, processing, and counting procedures to reflect the new photo identification requirements. 
  • Require public libraries, the secretary of state, boards of elections, and the registrar of motor vehicles to provide electors with a free copy of their photo ID upon in-person request for purposes of casting mail ballots by mail. Existing law does not include this free-copy requirement for mail voting.

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to individuals experiencing homelessness.

Last Updated June 12, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Ferguson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on November 5, 2025, would:

  • Require voters casting absentee ballots by mail, including military and overseas ballots, to include a copy of their photo identification with their ballot materials or a signed affidavit of religious objection to being photographed. Existing law requires voters to provide either a driver’s license number, state ID number, the last four digits of their Social Security number, or a copy of a specified ID when voting by mail but does not require submission of photo identification. 
  • Require public libraries, high schools, vocational schools, county treasurers, boards of elections, deputy registrars, and the secretary of state to provide voters with free copies of photo identification for the purpose of casting absentee ballots by mail. Existing law does not provide such a requirement.
  • Require the secretary of state to include information about the new photo ID requirement for mail voting in voter registration materials and educational brochures distributed by election offices and other designated agencies.
  • Add multifactor authentication requirements for individuals using the online voter registration system to verify identity before submission. Existing law does not require multi-factor authentication for online voter registration.

Last Updated November 6, 2025

This bill, as introduced on November 10, 2025, would:

Existing law prohibits public officials (with the exception of the secretary of state) from sending unsolicited absentee ballot applications but does not extend that restriction to private individuals or organizations.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Willis (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on November 18, 2025, would shorten the mail ballot return deadline and establish a proof of citizenship requirement for individuals flagged as potential noncitizens during the registration process or during new monthly reviews.


Regarding mail ballot return, the bill would: 

  • Make the mail ballot deadline earlier by requiring mail ballots to be received by the county board of elections by the close of polls on Election Day in order to be counted. Existing law allows ballots postmarked before Election Day and received by the fourth day after the election to be counted.

Regarding citizenship verification, the bill would: 

  • Expand the definition and required use of a confirmation notice by authorizing boards of elections to send a notice to confirm not only a voter’s address but also the voter’s date of birth, United States citizenship, driver’s license or state ID number, the last four digits of the voter’s Social Security number, or any other information required for registration.
  • Enumerate documents constituting adequate proof of citizenship, including:
    • A birth certificate, certification of report of birth, consular report of birth abroad, or a copy of one of the documents
    • A current U.S. passport or passport card, a copy of the ID page of the passport, or a copy of the front and pack of the passport card
    • A certificate of naturalization, a certificate of citizenship, or a copy of one of those documents
    • An Ohio driver's license or state ID number, if the secretary of state verifies it using previously submitted DMV documentation indicating the applicant is a U.S. citizen
    • A current Ohio driver's license, state ID card, or interim identification form issued on or after April 7, 2023, or a copy of the front and back of that document
  • Specify that an applicant's proof of citizenship is not subject to disclosure under the state's public record requirements.
  • Require the secretary of state to review and provide regular reports to each board of elections based on information provided in the statewide voter registration database, the DMV database, and the U.S. social security administration database, in order to identify individuals whose ID number or last four digits of their social security number does not match. 
  • Require the secretary of state to conduct a review of the statewide database at least monthly to verify the citizenship of individuals whose citizenship has not been verified. The secretary of state must cross-reference those records with DMV records and the systematic alien verification for entitlements (SAVE) program. The secretary of state would then need to report any noncitizen registrations to local boards, which must promptly cancel those registrations. Under existing law the secretary of state is only required to identify individuals who appear to be noncitizens when conducting the annual review. 
  • Require the Election Integrity Unit to conduct further investigation of any individuals who are identified as noncitizens.
  • Require proof of citizenship in order for a provisional ballot to be counted when it is cast by an individual who has changed their name or moved within the state without submitting a change of address or change of name notice and who has not previously submitted proof of citizenship.

Last Updated November 20, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Miller J (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 17, 2026, would:

  • Permit voters or authorized individuals to return a completed mail ballot on Election Day to any polling place within the county where the voter resides. Existing law requires mail ballots to be returned to the county board of elections office only, and does not expressly allow return at any polling place.
  • Require polling places to store mail ballot return envelopes in a secure container for delivery to the board of elections after polls close.
  • Prohibit officials from requiring individuals who are returning their mail ballots to wait in line or complete additional forms to submit their ballot.
  • Require polling locations to display signage indicating that mail ballots may be returned at that location and clarifying that only ballots from voters within the same county may be accepted.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the code implementing the provisions of the bill.

Last Updated March 18, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Glassburn (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 12, 2026, would require each county board of elections to create a plan for each polling place to minimize the time that voters who have difficulty standing for a prolonged period must stand while waiting to sign the pollbook or cast a ballot. Existing law requires polling places to meet certain accessibility standards and allows voters with disabilities who cannot enter the polling place to vote from a vehicle or at the door, but does not require a plan to reduce standing time inside or outside the polling place.

Last Updated May 14, 2026

data.bill_name: Enact Safeguard American Voter Eligibility Act

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Richardson (R)
data.public_commentary: Summary pending as of 3/6/2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Gavarone (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on June 18, 2025, would create the Election Integrity Unit within the Office of the Secretary of State. Specifically, the bill would:

  •  Assign the unit the following duties:
    • Investigate allegations of election fraud and voter suppression on its own initiative or upon receiving a complaint. The division would have the power to administer oaths, issue subpoenas, summon witnesses, compel the production of evidence, and hold hearings.
    • Allow the public to submit allegations of election fraud and voter suppression.
    • Refer allegations, as appropriate, to a prosecutor, a law enforcement agency, or another state or federal agency for further investigation or prosecution.
    • Submit a report to the governor and the legislature each January summarizing and providing statistics regarding the allegations and investigations with respect to the previous calendar year.
  • Establish deadlines for responding to Election Integrity Unit referrals and the transfer of prosecutorial power to the attorney general. Under existing law, the attorney general may prosecute election crimes if a prosecutor requests them to do so, or if the prosecutor has not made charges in "a reasonable time." In addition to this existing authority, the bill would:
    • Require prosecutors to respond to referrals from the Unit within 12 months by either prosecuting alleged crimes, or providing a written explanation for declining or to request more evidence. 
    • After receiving additional evidence, prosecutors would have 180 days to prosecute or provide a statement as to why they decline. 
    • If the prosecutor again declines or fails to provide the statement by the deadline, the bill authorizes the Unit to refer prosecution to the attorney general.


Last Updated June 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on GENERAL GOVERNMENT

data.author: Gavarone (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 19, 2025, would establish a proof of citizenship requirement for individuals flagged as potential noncitizens during the registration process or during new monthly reviews. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Requires officials to verify voters' citizenship before they can vote. While existing law does not include a documentary proof of citizenship requirement, applicants using the print voter registration form are required to present an Ohio driver's license, state ID number, or the last four digits of their Social Security number.
  • Enumerate documents constituting adequate proof of citizenship, including:
    • A birth certificate, certification of report of birth, consular report of birth abroad, or a copy of one of the documents
    • A current or expired U.S. passport or passport card, a copy of the ID page of the passport, or a copy of the front and pack of the passport card
    • A certificate of naturalization, a certificate of citizenship, or a copy of one of those documents
    • An Ohio driver's license or state ID number, if the board of elections verifies it using previously submitted documentation indicating the applicant is a U.S. citizen
    • A current or expired Ohio driver's license, state ID card, or interim identification form issued on or after April 7, 2023, or a copy of the front and back of that document
    • A driver's license or non-driver ID number from any state, or a copy of the back and front of the document, if the state indicates on the document that the individual is a U.S. citizen
  • Require the secretary of state to provide regular reports to each board of elections based on information provided in the statewide voter registration database, the DMV database, and the U.S. social security administration database in order to identify individuals whose information does not match or is missing. Additionally, the secretary of state would be required to conduct a review of the statewide voter database at least monthly (and daily beginning on the 46th day before an election and ending the day before the election) to verify the citizenship of individuals whose citizenship has not been verified. Under existing law the secretary of state is only required to identify individuals who appear to be noncitizens when conducting the annual review.
  • Require local boards to send applicants a notice when a registration is incomplete and specify the information needed to complete the registration. Local boards would be required to reject any applications and refer the matter to the county prosecuting attorney if they determine an applicant is ineligible to vote. Officials would note that a voter must cast a provisional ballot if their citizenship status has not been verified. Current law does not include such a requirement for local boards but requires the secretary of state to the attorney general any instances of the DMV receiving documentation indicating noncitizenship if the person has not responded to a second notice after a designated time.
  • Require proof of citizenship in order for a provisional ballot to be counted when it is cast by an individual who has changed their name or moved within the state without submitting a change of address or change of name notice and who has not previously submitted proof of citizenship.
  • Specify that an applicant's proof of citizenship is not subject to disclosure under the state's public record requirements.
  • Eliminate the use of mail ballot drop-off locations by repealing existing provisions that authorize officials to place one secure receptacle outside of the of the board of election.
  • Require individuals assisting voters to complete a "delivery attestation" when they deliver the voter's mail ballot. Existing law authorizes specified relatives to assist voters, which the bill would leave in place, but does not include an attestation requirement.

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to petitions.

Last Updated November 20, 2025

data.author: Reynolds (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on September 16, 2025, would change the party affiliation process, create closed primary elections, and expand the availability of the general registration process in certain state courts. Under existing law, Ohio voters are designated with a party affiliation when they request a ballot for a party primary, and maintain this affiliation for two calendar years following that election. To vote in a specific primary, a voter must either be currently affiliated with the party, or unaffiliated (i.e., they have not voted in another party's primary in the last two calendar years). Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change information required from voters on voter registration applications and registration update forms by adding the following: a political party affiliation field, a list of recognized political parties, and a box for applicants who do not wish to be affiliated with any party. The forms would be required to include a notice that the applicant may select or write the name of only one recognized political party, and that there is no requirement to choose an affiliation. The bill would further require the secretary of state to maintain affiliation information in the statewide voter database, and require that the information be provided in pollbooks for party primaries.
  • Expressly prohibit a voter from voting in a political party's primary election if they are not affiliated with that political party 30 days before the primary election.
  • Expressly provide that voters' political affiliation would be determined based on the designation in their most recent voter registration application or "voter registration update forms." Alternatively, affiliation would be based on the political party the voter most recently cast a ballot for in a primary election held on or after May 3, 2022. The bill specifies that if the voter cast an issues-only ballot, they are unaffiliated.
  • Add another method for registering to vote by allowing an individual to submit a registration application to a probate court or a court of common pleas. The board of elections would be required to provide the forms to the courts and the courts must provide forms to anyone 18 years or older who has their name changed or applies for a marriage license. Existing law requires the courts to distribute and return the change of residence and change of name forms only, not new registration applications.
  • Make conforming changes throughout the code to replace statutory references to change of residence or change of name forms with "voter registrations forms," and to reflect new affiliation requirements for registration applications.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to candidate requirements, a topic that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated September 18, 2025

data.author: Timken (R)
data.public_commentary:
This joint resolution, as adopted on June 10, 2026, proposes a constitutional amendment to require voter identification in Ohio. Specifically, the amendment would:

  • Constitutionally require photo identification for in-person voting. The proposed amendment would require electors to provide photo identification to vote in person at a polling place or other voting location. The amendment would allow the general assembly to provide an alternative ID verification method for voters who do not have photo identification because of a sincere religious objection to being photographed. Existing law requires photo identification for in-person voting, but this requirement is not currently in the state constitution.
  • Require voters to provide at minimum, either photo ID or, if authorized by law, a signature and at least one other unique identifier. Existing law allows absentee voters to use identifying information such as a driver’s license or state ID number, the last four digits of a Social Security number, or a copy of photo identification, but these requirements are not currently in the state constitution.
  • Define photo identification to include an unexpired Ohio driver’s license, state identification card, qualifying temporary BMV document, U.S. passport or passport card, U.S. military identification card, Ohio National Guard identification card, or U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs identification card. Existing statutory law includes a similar definition of acceptable photo identification for voting purposes.

Final adoption of the proposed amendment requires approval by voters at the November 3, 2026 general election. 

Last Updated June 12, 2026

data.author: Lepak (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 22, 2025, would add hurdles for certain state officials to implement federal elections directives or accept federal funding for elections. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit the secretary of the state election board or of a county board from implementing a new federal directive or guidance related to election administration or voter registration issued without prior approval of the legislature, or of the speaker and president pro tem of the senate if the legislature is not in session. Notice of a new directive or guidance should be provided to the legislature within ten days of issuance. The secretaries would also be required to inform the governor, the speaker, the president pro tem, and the attorney general of any possible litigation threatened by the federal government due to state election laws.
  • Provide that all new federal election funds must be approved by the legislature before they may be accepted or used by the secretary of the state election board or of a county election board (or by legislative leaders if the legislature is not in session).
  • Require the secretary of the state election board or of a county board to inform the legislature if they intend to implement a directive or guidance due to a court order.
  • Expand a misdemeanor under which any member or employee a county election board or absentee voting board, or any precinct official, commits an offense if they fail to perform a lawful duty by having it also apply to members or employees of the state election board.

This bill, as amended on February 24, 2025, would end no-excuse mail balloting in Oklahoma. Specifically, the bill would:

    • illness or disability
    • employment or education obligations
    • military service
    • religion
    • service as an election official
    • being 75 years of age or older
    • jury duty

data.author: Wolfley (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 20, 2025, would impose new requirements on notaries public who notarize mail ballot affidavits. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Impose new requirements on notaries publics. Under existing law, notaries who witness mail ballot affidavits are required to keep a log of them, and some notaries public are authorized to witness more than twenty such affidavits in a single election. Under the bill, notaries with that authorization would be required to submit a copy of their log to the county election board no later than the fifth business day after an election. Such a log would be a public record.
  • Create civil and criminal penalties for notaries public. A notary who fails to submit their log to the board as required by the bill, or who notarizes more than twenty affidavits in a single election without authorization, would commit a misdemeanor and would also have their notary public appointment revoked for eight years.

data.author: Boren (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 3, 2025, would extend the in-person early voting period ("in-person absentee voting" in Oklahoma), among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change the early voting period. Under existing law, in-person early voting is conducted for two to four days, depending on the type of election, and begins no earlier than the Wednesday before an election and ends no later than the Saturday before the election. Under the bill, early voting would always be available on the Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday preceding an election, and would also be available on each of the three Saturdays preceding a general, primary, runoff primary, or presidential preference primary election.
  • Require more early voting sites in certain counties. Under existing law, a county with more than 25,000 registered voters or an area of more than 1,500 square miles is permitted to designate more than one early voting site. Under the bill, such a county would be required to designate one location for each 15,000 registered voters in the county.
  • Clarify that a person who is in line to apply for in-person early voting at the end of the period is still eligible to do so.

Last Updated February 4, 2025

data.author: Paxton (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 25, 2025, would change the manner of appointment of the members of the state election board. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Change what officials have appointment power over the members of the board. Under existing law, the board has three members and two alternates, all of whom are appointed by the governor with the advice and consent of the senate. Under the bill, beginning on July 1, 2027, the composition would change. The governor would retain the ability to appoint three members with the advice and consent of the senate, one of whom must be a registered Republican, one a registered Democrat, and one not registered with either party. The remaining two members - the chair and the secretary of the board - would be appointed by the speaker of the house and the president pro tem of the senate.
  • Require the Republican and Democratic state committees to submit nominees to the governor, as under existing law.
  • Provide that if a member must be replaced or a vacancy filled, that this be done in the same manner and by the same appointing authority as the initial appointment.
  • Permit, on January 1, 2026, the State Election Board to appoint a person as the board's secretary with the advice and consent of the senate. Under existing law, the secretary of the senate serves in this role.

data.author: Bergstrom (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 17, 2026, would make several changes to the state's voter registration process. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the state voter registration form to include a space for applicants to indicate if they have previously been registered in a different state or county and to provide their previous address.
  • For anyone who registers to vote in Oklahoma and indicates they have been previously registered in a different state, require the secretary of the state election board to send a notice to the chief election official of the state of that person's previous registration.
  • Stipulate that by registering to vote in Oklahoma, the applicant requests that their previous registration in a different state or county be canceled.
  • Require the secretary of the state election board to publish an annual report detailing voter registration statistics. Specifically, the report must include:
    • The number of notices sent to a chief election official of another state providing information about a voter in that state who is registered to vote in Oklahoma.
    • The number of notices sent to each state.
    • The number of notices received from a chief election official of another state providing information that a voter from Oklahoma registered to vote in that other state.
    • The number of notices received from each state.
    • The number of voter registrations canceled in Oklahoma due to information provided by the chief election official of another state.

status_actions.summary: Chaptered. Chapter No. 00130

data.author: Jama (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 4, 2026, makes changes to the presidential elector process. Specifically, this bill:

  • Requires each presidential elector to vote for the candidates of the political party that appointed the elector. If an original candidate dies or withdraws before the meeting of presidential electors, the elector must vote for the replacement candidate selected by the party that appointed the elector. Current law is silent on this issue.
  • Requires the presidential elector pledge to specify that the elector will vote for the candidates of the political party that appointed the elector. Current law does not specify that the elector must vote for the presidential candidate affiliated with the party that appointed the elector.
  • Requires a presidential elector to be registered to vote in Oregon and to be affiliated with the political party that appointed the individual to serve as an elector. Current law does not restrict who may serve as a presidential elector.
  • Requires the secretary of state to examine each elector's ballot to ensure it has been cast in accordance with the elector's pledge. Current law is silent on this issue.
  • Clarifies the process for filling vacancies, stipulates that an elector who violates the pledge has vacated the office of elector, and updates relevant language in the certificate of ascertainment. Current law includes a vacancy process but not for faithless electors.
  • Requires the secretary of state to prepare, sign, and transmit certificates of vote in accordance with federal law. Current law more broadly requires the secretary of state to act in accordance with federal law.

Last Updated April 9, 2026

data.author: Conklin (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on April 14, 2025, would:

  • Authorize election officials to begin pre-canvassing mail ballots seven days before Election Day. Under existing law, pre-canvassing may start no earlier than 7 a.m. on Election Day.
  • Change an election administration grant program to require pre-canvassing to begin no later than 7 a.m. on Election Day and continue until all timely mail-in and absentee ballots are pre-canvassed. Current law require pre-canvassing begin at 7 a.m. on Election Day.

data.author: Diamond (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, proposes an amendment to the state constitution to limit voting by mail to excuse-required absentee voting, and require all other voting to be in-person at polling places, including early in-person voting. Under existing law, Pennsylvania offers both excuse-based and no-excuse mail voting options, and early voting is conducted as in-person mail ballot voting at election officials' offices. Because the elimination of no-excuse mail voting would limit who could cast a mail ballot in person to those with an excuse, the amendment would end early voting unless the Legislature enacted legislation creating it.

This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2025, would create curbside voting for voters who are unable to enter a polling place safely due to a disability. The voter could provide evidence of the disability at the polling location. A bipartisan team of election officers would administer curbside voting.

data.author: Solomon (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would permit unaffiliated ("unenrolled") voters to vote in a party's primary election by requesting the ballot of that party. Under existing law, only voters affiliated with a party may vote in that party's primary. An unaffiliated voter would request the ballot of the party whose primary in which they wish to vote, and would be considered a member of the party for the purposes of that ballot only. Accordingly, the bill would also repeal a misdemeanor offense for voting in a party primary if the voter is not affiliated with that party.

data.author: Maloney (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 23, 2025, would require residents registering to vote to provide proof of citizenship. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Establish new proof of citizenship requirements for residents registering to vote. The bill would allow people lacking proof of citizenship to obtain a non-driver's license photo ID or a birth certificate at no cost.
  • Exempt voters who are already registered from providing proof of citizenship.
  • Enumerate documents constituting adequate proof of citizenship. The bill would require one of the following, or a legible photocopy of the following:
    • State driver's license or non-driver ID, if the ID indicates citizenship
    • U.S. passport 
    • U.S. birth certificate, U.S. Department of State birth certificate, hospital record of birth, final adoption decree showing a U.S. birthplace, official U.S. military record showing a U.S. birthplace, or consular report of birth abroad of a U.S. citizen
    • Naturalization documents or certificate of nationalization number, or other proof of citizenship issued by the federal government under the Immigration and Nationality Act
    • Tribal ID
    • Other evidence deemed satisfactory by the State Election Board
  • Establish the State Election Board, as a subdivision of the Department of State, which would be charged with assessing evidence of citizenship to make determinations.
  • Create a process for review of voter registration applications when accompanied and not accompanied by proof of citizenship, including a hearing for people lacking specified documents and an appeals process.

data.author: Bizzarro (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would create same-day registration on Election Day. Under existing law, same-day registration is available during part of the early voting period only, ending 15 days before Election Day. To use the process, a person would appear at the correct polling place for their residence, complete a voter registration application, and provide documentary proof of identity and residence. The person would then vote a provisional ballot. The Department of State would be required to approve or reject the registration within 10 days and notify the applicant. If approved, the ballot would be counted.

These identification documents would be sufficient, but the bill makes clear they are examples and that other documents could be used:

  • A valid driver's license or identification card issued by the Department of Transportation.
  • A valid identification card issued by another agency of the Commonwealth.
  • A valid identification card issued by the United States Government.
  • A valid United States passport.
  • A valid United States Armed Forces identification card.

This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would require incarcerated individuals to either complete a voter registration application or complete a form declining to register, before discharge or release. Under existing law, individuals are prohibited from registering to vote within five years of incarceration for a felony offense. In 2000, a state court struck down the waiting period, and individuals have generally had their voting eligibility restored upon release. There is no existing requirement that released or discharged individuals register or decline to register to vote.

data.bill_name: Applications for Official Mail in Ballots

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Webster (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would eliminate grounds for rejecting timely mail ballots cast by eligible voters, authorize and regulate ballot drop boxes, change the deadline to apply to receive a ballot in the mail, and rename the list voters may join to receive a ballot application each year without otherwise changing the rules relating to the list. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Eliminate grounds for rejecting mail ballots. Specifically, so long as the ballot certificate is otherwise properly executed, the bill would require election officials to count:
    • Ballots with unsigned ballot certificates. Currently, some counties attempt to contact voters and allow them to address this issue by the close of polls.
    • Ballots returned without a secrecy envelope (colloquially known as "naked ballots"). Currently, some counties attempt to contact voters and allow them to address this issue by the close of polls.
    • Ballots with undated or misdated signatures on ballot certificates. Currently, some counties attempt to contact voters and allow them to address this issue by the close of polls.
  • Authorize and regulate ballot drop boxes, but would not require their use. Existing statutes do not address drop boxes, however, the Secretary of the Commonwealth issued guidance relating to them in 2020 and they have been used in some counties. If a county board of elections decides to offer ballot drop boxes, the bill would:
    • Require notice to voters at least 30 days before Election Day with signage at the county board's office and other locations, on the board's website, and in the materials mailed to voters with their ballots. These notices would include information about each location, its hours of operation, accessibility, and other topics.
    • Require each drop box to be fully ADA-compliant and in a well-lit, fixed location. Boxes would also need to be tamper-resistant, locked, and secured.
    • Require county boards of election to monitor the boxes when they are open either in person or by video and require video recordings to be retained for a year.
    • Allow county boards of elections to decide when their drop boxes would be open, subject to certain minimum parameters. Specifically, boxes would need to open no later than 30 days before Election Day and be open on Election Day, and in the five days before Election Day must be open at least one weeknight and at least one weekend time period.
    • Require ballots to be collected from the boxes by two county employees, at least one of whom is assigned to the board of elections. The other may be a member of law enforcement. A chain of custody log would be required.
  • Shorten the time to apply to receive a ballot by mail by requiring such applications to be received by the board of elections by the 12th day before Election Day. Existing law allows voters to apply for mail ballots through 5 p.m. on the Tuesday before Election Day. The bill would keep that deadline for in-person applications for mail ballots.
  • Rename the permanent absentee voter list and the permanent mail-in voter list the "annual absentee voter list" and "annual mail-in ballot list".

Last Updated February 18, 2025

data.author: O'Mara (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 19, 2025, would create a designated early voting period and specify the conduct of early voting. Under existing law, a voter is permitted to vote an absentee ballot (which includes both excuse-only "absentee ballots" and no-excuse "mail-in ballots" in Pennsylvania) in-person, but there is no specific designated process for early voting. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Create an early voting period beginning on the thirtieth day before Election Day and ending on the day before Election Day, including every intervening Saturday and Sunday.
  • Establish early voting hours of either 8:30-4:30 or 9:00-5:00 on weekdays, 9:00-12:00 on Saturdays, and 10:00-4:00 on Sundays, except that beginning eight days before Election Day, the early voting locations must be open from at least 9:00 a.m.-7:00 p.m. every day. Early voting locations must remain open for at least 8 hours on any holidays during the early voting period.
  • Establish minimum numbers of early voting locations. In a county with a population over 250,000, there must be at least one permanent early voting location in each of the three largest municipalities in the jurisdiction, except that any municipality with over 80,000 residents must have at least two permanent early voting locations during the early voting period. Counties with between 100,000 and 250,000 residents must have at least one permanent early voting location. The bill also requires that any county containing a municipality with a population over 100,000 must establish at least permanent polling places.
  • Set standards for early voting locations. The county may use the county board's main office or other sites, provided that all early voting locations must be at least 1.5 miles apart, except in municipalities with over 500,000 residents. Public buildings must be made reasonably available as early voting locations without charge. In addition, counties may establish temporary polling places for early voting, which are not subject to the days and hours schedule required of other early voting locations.
  • Create Election Day-style voting by requiring that each county provide voting booths with voting machines and other suitable equipment during the early voting period.
  • Require that each county board maintain a list of voters who vote during the early voting period, which list must be delivered to the judges of election in each election district.
  • Require that early voting locations and schedules be published in a newspaper and posted physically and online at least a week before the early voting period begins and weekly during the early voting period.

Last Updated February 27, 2025

data.author: Owlett (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 3, 2025, would create a new voter ID requirement in the state. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require voters to present photo ID. Under existing law, Pennsylvania has a voter ID statute, but it is not enforced after a court decision in 2012 and voters are generally not required to present ID. Under the bill, the following forms of ID would be accepted.
      • driver's license
      • a voter registration card containing a scannable ID number and the voter's signature
      • an ID document issued by a branch of the armed forces that does not expire and indicates current membership or veteran status with the armed forces
      • a voter identification card issued by the secretary of the commonwealth
      • an affidavit under which the voter affirms their identity and provides the last four digits of their social security number
      • an unexpired document showing the voter's name and photograph, issued by the US government, Pennsylvania government, a municipality of Pennsylvania to an employee, a university in Pennsylvania, or a care facility in Pennsylvania
      • in the case of a voter who has a religious objection to being photographed, the bill would permit only a copy of a valid-without-photo driver's license or a valid-without-photo identification card issued by the Department of Transportation
    • Direct the secretary of the commonwealth to begin producing voter ID cards and issuing them to voters without charge on request.
    • Direct county boards to begin producing and issuing scannable voter registration cards to voters on new or updated registration, or upon the voter's request

Last Updated April 14, 2025

status_actions.summary: Rereferred to HOUSE Committee on APPROPRIATIONS

data.author: Mehaffie (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 6, 2025, would create voter ID requirements for voting in person and a new statewide requirement for notifying mail ballot voters when their identity cannot be verified, beginning in 2027. While Pennsylvania statutory law currently has a general photo ID requirement for in-person voting, these provisions were struck down by a state court in 2014 and are not enforced. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a voter ID requirement for voting in person, allowing for use of photo ID, non-photo ID, personal voucher, or personal affirmation. Under the bill:
    • Voters would generally be required to present one of the following forms of photo ID:
      • State driver's license or ID card
      • ID issued by the federal government or tribal government
      • ID issued by the military indicating current membership or veteran status
      • ID issued by a county, municipality, or school district in the state
      • Firearm permit
      • Student ID 
      • Employee ID
    • Voters who do not have an accepted form of photo ID would be permitted to present one of the following:
      • For voters with religious objections to being photographed, a non-photo state license or ID
      • Non-photo ID issued by the state or a local entity, including a voter ID card
      • Non-photo ID issued by the federal government or tribal government
      • Care facility ID 
      • Utility bill, including a bill from a cellular telecommunications provider
      • Bank statement, paycheck or paystub, or government check.
      • Proof of insurance document
      • Tax filing or document
      • Registration, fee statement or transcript from an institution of higher education
      • Mortgage document
      • Residential lease
    • Voters who do not have an accepted form of photo or non-photo ID would be permitted to verify their identity with an affirmation from a qualified voter, confirming the voter's name and that the voucher personally knows them, and the bill would require the secretary of state to develop a form for doing so.
    • Voters who have no accepted form of ID and are unable to provide a voucher affirmation would be permitted to affirm their identity using a form developed by the secretary of state. The bill would require the form to disclose penalties for perjury.
    • Voters who are unable to produce any accepted form of identification would be required to vote a provisional ballot.
  • Require election officials to issue notice to a voter by email, phone, or text, if their mail ballot is missing required proof of identification, informing the voter of how to submit an accepted form of ID via email, text, fax, mail, or in person. Existing law does not expressly require notice to a mail voter when their ballot lacks required identification, but does provide for a cure process within six days of the election.
  • Eliminate the availability of free voter ID by repealing the provisions requiring the Department of Transportation to provide such ID to individuals who do not have an accepted document. 


Last Updated July 22, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Daley M (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 28, 2025, would codify Pennsylvania's existing online voter registration system, which has been in place administratively since 2015. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the Department of State (the Department) to develop an electronic voter application on a secure computer system that allows residents to submit a voter registration application or make changes to an existing registration.
  • Require the system to be developed within 180 days of the bill going into effect.
  • Mandate the online voter registration application to comply with all the existing paper form requirements.
  • Require methods for individuals with disabilities to submit registration forms.
  • Require that an applicant provide one of the following forms of identifying information:
    • The last four digits of their Social Security number.
    • Driver's license number or state-issued ID number.
    • Any other information required by the Department to verify their identity, if no Social Security number or ID is available.
  • Require the application to attest to the truth of the information they provide.
  • Require the Department to obtain an electronic copy of an applicant's signature from their ID. If a signature is not available, the Department must require the applicant to provide a signature at the polling place or on the absentee ballot application the first time they vote.
  • Expressly allow an applicant to print, sign, and mail the completed voter registration application to the appropriate commission.
  • Allow an eligible resident to vote in an election if their online voter registration application is submitted no later than 11:59 p.m. on the 15th day before Election Day. An application submitted before the deadline would be transferred through Pennsylvania's Statewide Uniform Registry of Electors (SURE) system to the appropriate commission.
  • Provide that changes to information in an elector's voter registration be effective once the Department is able to confirm their signature or they print, sign and mail the form. The department must inform the applicant if an electronic signature cannot be verified.
  • Require the Department to establish security measures to ensure the security of the voter registration applications or changes to voter registration.

Last Updated March 4, 2025

data.author: Warner (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 26, 2025, would:

  • Require residents to provide documentary proof of U.S. citizenship when registering to vote using a state voter registration application. Existing law does not require proof of citizenship. The bill defines documentary proof of citizenship to include the following:
    • Valid U.S. passport
    • Official U.S. military identification card, together with a United States military record of service showing U.S. birth
    • Valid government-issued photo identification card issued by a Federal, state, or tribal government showing U.S. birth
    • A valid government-issued photo identification card issued by a Federal, state or tribal government other than one listed above, if presented together with one or more of the following:
      • A certified birth certificate issued by a state, local government, or tribal government that:
      • Was issued by the state, local government, or tribal government in which the applicant was born
      • Was filed with the office responsible for keeping vital records in the state
      • Includes the applicant’s full name, date of birth, and place of birth
      • Lists the full names of one or both parents
      • Has the signature of an authorized official
      • Includes the date the certificate was filed with the office responsible for vital records
      • Has the official seal of the issuing authority
    • Extract from a United States hospital record of birth created at the time of birth showing the applicant’s place of birth in the United States
    • Final adoption decree showing the applicant’s name and place of birth in the United States
    • Consular report of birth abroad of a citizen of the United States or a certification of the applicant’s report of birth issued by the Secretary of State
    • Naturalization certificate or certificate of citizenship issued by the Secretary of Homeland Security, or any other document or method of proof of United States citizenship issued under specified federal law
    • American Indian Card issued by the Department of Homeland Security with the classification “KIC”
  • Establish a private right of action, allowing any person to bring a civil cause related to violation of the new citizenship verification requirement. The bill would authorize courts to award damages up to $10,000 per violation. 
  • Require officials to cancel the voter registration record of an individual who is found to not be a U.S. citizen. Existing law requires removal of the record upon voter request, death, confirmation of the voter moving outside the county, and under a voter removal program. 

Last Updated March 27, 2025

data.author: Fink (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 7, 2025, would: 

  • Revise the definition of "canvass" to include “gathering ballots after the election and counting, computing, and tallying the votes." The current definition requires canvassing after the final "pre-canvas" meeting.
  • Repeal the term "pre-canvassing," which previously allowed counties to begin tallying (but not recording or publishing) mail-in and absentee ballots before polls closed. 
  • Clarify procedures for voters who requested but did not cast a mail-in or absentee ballot. These voters would be authorized to vote provisionally in person if they direct the request to be voided and complete an affidavit. Voters could cast a regular ballot at the polling place if the uncast absentee ballot is voided and the voter signs an affidavit.  
  • Require counties to provide complaint forms for voters who believe a mail-in or absentee ballot was requested in their name without authorization and to refer such complaints to the district attorney. Existing law does not include this complaint mechanism.
  • Prohibit counties from compensating election judges who fail to return supplies such as mail-in ballots and absentee ballots, in addition to provisional ballots. 


Last Updated April 16, 2025
