data.author: Zimmerman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on April 15, 2025, proposes a constitutional amendment to revise voter qualifications in Pennsylvania. Specifically, the amendment would:

  • Add a requirement that all voters present valid government-issued identification before voting, and provide such ID and a matching signature when voting by mail. Existing law does not impose a constitutional voter ID requirement. Note: A 2012 voter ID law was declared unconstitutional by a Pennsylvania appellate court. The law has not been repealed by the General Assembly, but it is not enforced. Pennsylvania has reverted to the rules that were in place prior to the passage of the voter ID law, which require only those voters who are voting for the first time in an election district to present ID.
  • Require voters to register at least 30 days prior to an election and direct voter registration officials to verify eligibility before allowing registration. Existing law sets the voter registration deadline by statute, not in the constitution.
  • Reduce the state residency requirement for voting from 90 to 30 days before the election and the precinct residency requirement from 60 to 30 days. Existing law requires 30 days of residency in both the state and precinct, but this language would be added to the constitution.

The amendment must pass the legislature in two consecutive sessions before going to voters for approval.

Last Updated April 16, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Grove (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 15, 2025, would end the permanent mail-in voting list; impose drop box restrictions; create a statewide early voting period; create a voter fraud hotline; prohibit curb-side voting; establish a Voter’s Bill of Rights; and codify online voting, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • End the permanent mail-in voting list and require voters to apply for mail ballots each election.
  • Place new restrictions on ballot drop-off locations, limiting them to the seven days before an election and requiring bipartisan monitoring, video surveillance, and geographic distribution. Statutes are currently silent on the use of drop boxes, but the secretary of state has issued guidance on their use.
  • Create a statewide early voting period running from the second Friday through the first Wednesday before Election Day, with specific operating rules for early voting centers and satellite offices. Under existing law, a voter may apply for, receive, and return a mail-in ballot in the office of the county board of elections without providing an excuse.
  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to maintain an election fraud hotline for reporting any known or suspected election fraud or intimidation of election officials and observers. The complaints would be automatically shared with an independent prosecutor and county election integrity officer. Existing law does not require an election fraud hotline.
  • Expressly prohibit several election practices, including: drive-through or curb-side voting, automatic mailing of ballots to voters, ranked choice voting, registering voters without them submitting an application, and participation in the National Popular Vote compact.
  • Create a Voter’s Bill of Rights, along with specific rights for seniors and voters with disabilities, which must be posted at each polling place.
  • Prohibit local and state election officials from accepting private funding for election operations unless distributed by the secretary of state with approval from the Election Law Advisory Board.
  • Require new training for poll watchers.
  • Provide a new election equipment grant program through the Commonwealth Financing Authority and repeal the existing voting apparatus bond program.
  • Establish a new article on voter registration, detailing qualifications, processes for registration (including online voter registration), and revising the SURE system and signature requirements. The bill would authorize an electronic signature for the online application. Online voter registration is currently available but not required by statute. 
  • Require each county to use electronic poll books, provide durable voter ID cards (free of charge) with signature likeness and barcode, and conduct monthly voter roll maintenance, including removing deceased voters and investigating citizenship claims.

The bill includes additional provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to audits, petitions, judicial intervention, county party coordination, and election equipment.


Last Updated April 17, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Grove (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 15, 2025, would: 

  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to maintain an election fraud hotline for reporting any known or suspected election fraud or intimidation of election officials and observers. The complaints would be automatically shared with an independent prosecutor and county election integrity officer. Existing law does not require an election fraud hotline.
  • Establish an “independent prosecutor” appointed by the attorney general and approved by the state Senate to review election complaints and issue post-election reports with investigation summaries and policy recommendations. Existing law does not specify an independent prosecutor position for election oversight.
  • Designate each county’s district attorney or designee as the “election integrity officer” responsible for working with local election officials to ensure proper chain of custody, internal control procedures, and fraud prevention tools. Existing law does not specify a comparable role.
  • Require that authorized representatives have “meaningful access” to observe ballot pre-canvassing and canvassing, including viewing any ballot or envelope text, and that meetings be audio- and video-recorded and released after polls close. Existing law allows observers but does not define meaningful access or mandate recordings.
  • Require the Department of State and counties to investigate all reported concerns from observers and share the results with the independent prosecutor. Existing law does not mandate this level of investigation or information-sharing.


Last Updated April 16, 2025

This bill, as introduced on April 28, 2025, would:

data.author: Davidson (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 28, 2025, would:

  • Create a new misdemeanor offense prohibiting offering or providing money, goods, or other valuable things, including a chance to win a lottery or prize-drawing contest, to induce a person to register to vote. Under existing law, there is no specific offense related to offering inducements for voter registration.
  • Amend the existing offense of bribery at elections to specify that offering or providing a "chance to win a lottery or similar prize-drawing contest" is included within the meaning of giving a gift, money, goods, or "other valuable thing" to influence a person’s vote. Under existing law, bribery at elections is punishable as a felony.


Last Updated April 29, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: McClinton (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 12, 2025, would make extensive changes to election law, including expanding voter access by establishing a statewide Election Day-style in-person early voting process; reducing grounds for mail ballot rejection and creating a statewide notice and cure process for mail ballots; requiring the provision of drop boxes for mail ballot return; allowing for an earlier start to ballot processing; and codifying automatic voter registration at the DMV, among other changes. 


Regarding early voting, the bill would:

  • Create a uniform statewide period of Election Day-style in-person early voting, with voting booths and electronic voting systems. Under existing law, the early voting process is limited to casting a mail ballot in person at the county board of elections. The bill's early voting provisions would:
    • Require that in-person early voting be available from the 11th day before Election Day through the Sunday before Election Day, for 8-12 hours a day between the hours of 6:00 a.m. to 8:00 p.m., except for certain permitted emergency closures. Under existing law, in-person early voting by mail ballot is available at the county board office from the issuance of mail ballots - generally 50 days before an election - through 5:00 p.m. the Tuesday before Election Day, and is generally available during business hours only on weekdays.
    • Establish requirements for early voting polling places. The bill would:
      • Require counties to provide at least one early voting location, which may be at the office of the county board, and provide an early voting location for every 100,000 registered voters. The bill would require at least one early voting polling place to be located in the same municipality as the county seat, and would permit counties to provide additional polling places at their discretion. 
      • Require early voting be provided in locations eligible to be selected as an Election Day polling place and be accessible to disabled voters. The bill would also specify that counties must consider locations' proximity to public transportation, the availability of parking, travel times, commuter traffic patterns, geographic features affecting access and convenience, population density, existing locations that serve a large number of electors, use of public buildings, cost savings from public buildings, and security considerations. Locations must be published in a newspaper and online no later than 30 days before the election. A list of all locations must also be provided to the secretary of the commonwealth.
      • Permit counties to establish temporary early voting locations with different days and hours of operation than those required for permanent early voting locations. The schedules and locations of such temporary voting locations must be posted on the county board's website.
      • Set out requirements related to early voting place poll workers and poll watchers, and prohibitions related to electioneering and voter privacy.  
  • Permit voters to cast an early ballot at any early voting location in their county. 
  • Require counties to record every voter who votes during early voting and update poll book information to indicate they have cast a ballot. 


Regarding mail ballot voting, the bill would:

  • Create a statewide notice and cure process for missing signatures on mail ballots. While some counties currently provide for notice and cure, existing statutory law has no general requirement or procedure. The bill would:
    • Require election officials to identify mail ballots with missing signatures during pre-processing. 
    • For ballots with missing signatures, require officials to provide notice to the voter within 24 hours by electronic communication or phone if possible, and by letter. The bill would also require counties to publicly post a list of voters whose ballots require cure.
    • Require officials to develop a cure form that can be completed on paper, or by electronic means or telephone. The form must allow the voter to provide a state driver's license number, or last four digits of their Social Security number, or a copy of the following: 
      • Official federal, state, county or municipal document which lists the voter's name
      • Student identification document issued by an institution of higher education in the state
      • Utility bill, telephone bill, bank statement, government check, paycheck or tax or rent receipt which lists the voter's name
    • Set the cure deadline at noon on the sixth day after an election. 
  • Move the deadline earlier for applications for a mail ballot, to 5 p.m. on the 14th day before an election. Under existing law, the application deadline is 5 p.m. on the Tuesday before the election. The bill also modifies the deadline for challenging the validity of a mail ballot application, from 5 p.m. on the Friday before an election, to the earlier of either that time or prior to the pre-canvass of the voter's mail ballot. 
  • Create a permanent mail voter list ("permanent mail-in ballot list"). Under existing law, voters may sign up to automatically receive a mail ballot application each year, which will provide them with a mail ballot for elections in that year. Under the bill, voters on the list would receive mail ballots directly for all future elections, unless they request removal or become ineligible to vote. 
  • Eliminate the date requirement for mail ballot declarations, and reduce grounds for rejecting mail ballots due to markings or damage. The bill would:
    • Eliminate the requirement the mail ballot declarations be dated. Under existing law, declarations must be signed, and following a 2024 court ruling, mail ballots with undated or misdated declarations may not be counted. 
    • Eliminate a ground for mail ballot rejection, by repealing the requirement that a mail ballot must be rejected if it includes any text, mark, or symbol that reveals the voter's identity, political affiliation, or candidate preference. The bill would also provide that ballots that are not sealed in an official envelope must be counted.
    • For mail ballots that are damaged or defective and cannot be counted by tabulation equipment, require officials to create a clearly labeled duplicate ballot to allow for counting.  
  • Require counties to provide drop boxes for mail ballot return, and specify rules for their use. Existing statutory law is silent as to drop boxes, but they have been widely used in past elections by some counties under guidance issued by the secretary of state. The bill would:
    • Require all counties to provide at least two two drop boxes, and require counties with populations over 100,000 to provide additional drop boxes equal to at least one additional drop box for every 50,000 registered voters over 100,000. Counties also may, but are not required to, provide additional drop boxes. Existing guidance has no criteria for numbers of boxes provided.
    • Require drop boxes to be available beginning 30 days before an election, with at least one box available 24 hours a day through the election. Existing guidance has no comparable provisions.  
    • Expressly prohibit counties from prohibiting or inhibiting the use of drop boxes.  
    • Set out requirements for public notice of drop box locations and deadlines for changing locations. Existing guidance has no comparable provisions.
    • Require counties to forward ballots returned to a box outside the voter's county to the proper jurisdiction, and require acceptance of such ballots until 8 p.m. on Election Day. Existing guidance has no comparable provision. 
    • Require video surveillance of drop boxes while they are available, among other security requirements, and require the secretary of state to promulgate additional rules regarding security. Existing guidance requires monitoring of drop boxes, but does not specifically require video surveillance. 
    • Set out requirements related to removal of ballots from drop boxes and chain of custody documentation, generally consistent with existing rules under agency guidance. 
    • Specify the content of signage for drop boxes, including notice of existing restrictions on third party return of mail ballots, criminal penalties for certain prohibited activity with a mail ballot, and notice regarding deposit of a mail ballot outside of a voter's county of residence and potential impact on whether it is counted as timely returned. These requirements expand slightly on existing requirements under agency guidance.
    • Expressly provide that the existing misdemeanor offense for hindering or delaying performance of a duty includes interference with removal of ballots from a drop box or with the deposit of a ballot into a drop box.
  • Provide for paid postage return of mail ballots return and mandate ballot tracking. The bill would:
    • Require prepaid postage for the outer return envelope for mail ballots. Under existing law, counties may, but are not required to, provide prepaid postage. 
    • Require use of USPS Intelligent Mail barcodes on ballots to facilitate ballot tracking.
    • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to develop a mail ballot tracking system. While ballot tracking is currently available, existing statutory law does not require such a system.  


Regarding polling places procedures, the bill would:

  • Eliminate the existing challenge process at polling places, which allows a voter, election officer, overseer, or poll watcher to challenge a voter as to their identity or residency. Under existing law, voters facing such a challenge must present another registered voter to act as witness to affirm their qualification.
  • Allow for the use of digital representation of an accepted form of voter ID, if issued by a system maintained by the state or subdivision, or by an institution of higher education. Existing law requires presentation of ID when voting at a polling place for the first time, and is silent as to the use of digital forms of ID. 
  • Require officials, in the event that a provisional ballot is cast in the wrong election district by a voter registered in the state, to count the ballot as to races for the voter's correct district. Under existing law, a provisional ballot cast in the wrong district is only counted for applicable races if the voter is registered to an address in that county; if the voter is registered elsewhere, the ballot is rejected.
  • Require that county boards of election use electronic poll books that allow real-time tracking of voters by December 31, 2026, at which time they will be used for voter certificates, voting check lists, numbered lists of voters, district registered, and management of voter rolls during elections. The secretary of state would be responsible for creating specifications for electronic poll books and the bill appropriates $7 million to the department of state for counties' procurement of electronic poll books.


Regarding ballot processing, the bill would:

  • Move the start of ballot pre-canvassing earlier, to begin between 9 a.m. on the seventh day before an election, and no later than 7 a.m. on Election Day. Existing law prohibits ballot processing before 7 a.m. on Election Day. The bill would also specify that the pre-canvass meeting may continue until 8 p.m. on Election Day.  
  • Move the start of canvassing earlier, from no later than the third day after an election, to the day after the election.


Regarding voter registration and voter list maintenance, the bill would:

  • Codify the existing availability of automatic voter registration (AVR) at the DMV, and establish new requirements for the process. While existing statutory law provides only for opt-in registration at the DMV, executive action in 2023 established a front-end AVR system at the agency in which applicants are registered unless they affirmatively opt out. For the new statutory AVR process, the bill would:
    • Require all DMV transactions to serve as automatic voter registration for eligible individuals, unless they present documentation or an affirmation demonstrating that they are not a U.S. citizen, or they decline to register. 
    • Require officials to mail notice to all individuals after registration at the DMV, providing the opportunity to choose party affiliation or decline registration. 
    • Make conforming changes related to transmission of data from the DMV to election officials, and modify requirements related to certain address updates. 
  • Simplify procedures for the transfer of voter registration between counties, by eliminating existing requirements for voters to provide notice to election officials and providing for transfer directly between election authorities in certain circumstances. 
  • Designate state institutions of higher education as voter registration agencies, and permit additional offices as designated by the secretary of state. The bill would also amend the registration information form provided by agencies to omit the statement that individuals are not eligible to vote if they have been incarcerated for a felony within the last five years. This eligibility requirement was struck down in 2000. 
  • Expand sources for identification of deceased voters for removal from the voter list. The bill would:
    • Require the Department of Health to provide information to election officials on deaths for residents age 16 and older, and expressly require removal of deceased pre-registered individuals from the voter roll. Existing law requires notice of death for residents age 18 and above and requires removal of registered voters only. 
    • Add ERIC to the additional sources, beyond the Department of Health, that election officials may use to identify deceased voters.  


The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including changes to requirements for election equipment and election audits.



A previous version of the bill also contained additional provisions that were amended out. They would have:

  • Lowered the age for pre-registration to age 16. Under existing law, otherwise eligible individuals may pre-register if they will be 18 by the next general election. 

Last Updated May 22, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Pugh (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 7, 2025, would:

  • Specify return options for no-excuse mail (mail-in) ballots. Under existing law, ballots are to be returned by mail, postage prepaid, except where franked, or delivered in person to the county board of election counties. Acceptable methods under the bill would be limited to:
    • Delivery through the U.S. Postal Service to the county board of elections under existing law, mail-in ballots may be returned by mail but are not limited to USPS delivery.
    • In-person delivery to the permanent county election office during regular business hours.
    • Delivery to a ballot return location. Currently, the code is silent as to drop boxes.  
  • Establish drop box requirements. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Limit ballot box locations to a permanent office of the board of elections or the county courthouse.
    • Require ballot return locations to be monitored by a bipartisan pair of election inspectors. 
    • Prohibit operation of a return location on days when both inspectors are not available.
    • Require inspectors to verify voter identification and check for a completed declaration (signature and date) before accepting a ballot.
    • Require inspectors to record and separate any ballots they believe may violate election law, for review by the county board of elections.
    • Mandate video surveillance of ballot return locations during hours of operation, with recordings retained for two years and made publicly available.
    • Designate ballot return locations as polling places for the purposes of accessibility, observer access, and political activity restrictions.

Last Updated September 29, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Dawkins (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 21, 2025, would:

  • Allow individuals who are incarcerated in a county prison awaiting trial or after conviction for a misdemeanor or summary charge, whether awaiting sentencing or serving a sentence, to either use the prison address for voter registration and voting, or to vote by mail ("absentee") using their pre-incarceration residence. Under existing law, incarcerated individuals remain residents of their jurisdiction prior to confinement for voting purposes and may not register to their prison address.
  • Require any county of the first class to provide an additional polling place in the county prison for individuals who are registered to the prison address. A county of the first class is one with 1.5 million residents or more (i.e., Philadelphia).

Last Updated May 22, 2025

data.author: Cooper (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 30, 2025, would require each county registration commission to establish a process for owners and lessees of any individual residence to report the registration of anyone listed at that address who is not currently residing at the home. The commission would then send a cancellation notice to any registered voter who is reported and attempt to contact them at an alternative address. While existing law does not include a reporting procedure for owners and lessees, each commission must establish a voter removal program for identifying registered voters whose address may have changed. The provisions of this bill would be in addition to and not an alternative to the existing program.

Last Updated July 7, 2025

data.author: Rowe (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on October 10, 2025, would add offices that provide applications for hunting and fishing licenses to the list of agencies that must distribute voter registration applications with each application, reapplication, renewal, or change of address; assist applicants with completing the registration form (unless assistance is refused); accept completed registration applications; and transmit them to the appropriate election commission. Existing law requires the secretary of the commonwealth to administer voter registration through offices that provide public assistance, county clerks of orphans’ courts (including marriage license bureaus), offices offering state-funded programs for individuals with disabilities, and armed forces recruitment centers only.

Last Updated October 14, 2025

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on December 3, 2025, would specify that the definition of "overseas voter" (for registration and voting purposes) includes a citizen residing abroad who was born outside of the United states, is not registered to vote in another state, has not voted in another election, has a parent or legal guardian whose last domicile was in Pennsylvania, and is eligible to vote. Existing law defines an "overseas voter" as a qualified elector who is outside of the United States.

Last Updated December 9, 2025

data.author: Shusterman (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 5, 2026, would:

  • Establish the Election Fellowship Pilot Program within the Department of State of the Commonwealth (the Department). Under the bill, the purpose of the program is to provide eligible individuals with experience in managing and administering elections. Participating counties would be randomly selected.
  • Require program to begin August 1 of the year the General Assembly appropriates money to implement the program and conclude three years after it commences.
  • Require the Department to submit a report to the public and various executive and legislative branch officials after the program concludes highlighting the outcomes and recommendations about whether it should be expanded to designated executive and legislative leaders.

Last Updated March 18, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Inglis (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 30, 2026, would:

  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to develop and maintain a uniform naming convention for election districts that all county boards of elections must follow, including updating the convention as needed. Existing law does not require a standardized naming convention for election districts.
  • Require county boards of elections to assign and input election district names in accordance with the naming convention established by the secretary of the commonwealth.

Last Updated March 31, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Pielli (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 30, 2026, would:

  • Extend the deadline for receipt of completed absentee and mail-in ballots by requiring officials to count mail ballots as timely if received by county boards of elections no later than 5:00 p.m. on the seventh day following an election. Existing law requires mail ballots (including both excuse-required absentee ballots and no-excuse mail-in ballots) to be received in the office of the county board of elections no later than 8:00 p.m. on Election Day.
  • Prohibit rejection based on postmark issues if a voter declares under penalty of perjury that their ballot was timely submitted. Under the bill, the ballot may not be rejected solely because it has a late, missing, or unreadable postmark.

Last Updated March 31, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Sanchez (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 9, 2026, would:

  • Require courts to prioritize and decide appeals from county boards of elections no later than seven days after filing. Existing law requires courts to hear these appeals but does not impose a specific deadline for resolution.
  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth to certify election results no later than the fifth Tuesday after Election Day and to provide notice to legislative leaders at least seven days in advance if the deadline may not be met, including reasons for the delay. Existing law requires certification but does not impose a uniform deadline or require advance notice of delays.
  • Require courts to decide election contest cases within seven days of filing and to send certified decisions to the secretary of state within five days.
  • Create an expedited appeal process to the Pennsylvania Supreme Court, allowing appeals of election contest decisions to be filed within one day and require the state supreme court to issue a decision within six days, or no later than seven days before the meeting of presidential electors, whichever is earlier. Existing law does not establish these specific timelines for supreme court review

Last Updated April 30, 2026

data.author: Street (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 29, 2025, would:

  • Expand the definition of "qualified absentee elector" to include those who are confined in a correctional institution but not convicted of a felony. Pennsylvanias existing excuse-required process known as "absentee voting" does not list confinement as an excuse to receive a mail ballot, but the state does have a parallel no-excuse mail voting process called "mail-in voting."
  • Specify voter registration requirements for determining the residence and assigned precinct for individuals who are homeless or lack a specific physical address. Under the bill, people without a physical address, including incarcerated individuals who were homeless prior to confinement, may submit a description such as a map or coordinates indicating where they reside.
  • Require the Secretary of the Commonwealth to issue rules on acceptable forms of non-physical addresses.
  • Require the Department of Corrections to establish civic education policies and provide correctional institutions with:
    • Information about voter registration, absentee ballots, mail-in ballots, and eligibility requirements
    • Necessary forms and applications
    • Necessary training for obtaining and casting a ballot
  • Require the Department of Corrections to create procedures for state and county correctional institutions to inspect voter registration applications and absentee ballots for contraband without opening or reading them.
  • Require the warden or authorized person in charge of a state or county correctional institution to collect data that includes inmates demographic information and the number of inmates who registered to vote, applied for a ballot, and submitted a completed ballot. A report of the data would be required within 30 days after the general election.

Last Updated March 27, 2025

data.author: Coleman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would create a new misdemeanor offense for an election official who knowingly makes a false statement, representation or certification in a list, record or other document required to be maintained under or who fails to complete a duty related to pre-election logic and accuracy testing of election equipment. The bill also makes changes in areas of law not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, including requirements to preprint ballots.

data.bill_name: Distribution of Unofficial Mail In Form

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Brooks (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would create a misdemeanor offense for distributing unofficial mail-in forms to voters. Under the bill, unofficial mail-in forms would include absentee ballots, mail-in ballots, and applications for such ballots not issued under law by the state, a county election board or their agents. The offense would not apply to distribution of a document that is designed for informational purposes, clearly and conspicuously states the document is unofficial, identifies the distributor, does not include a return mailing address, and does not use stationary mimicking that of an elected official, the state, or county election board.

Last Updated February 27, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Laughlin (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 30, 2025, would:

  • Permit unenrolled electors to vote in a recognized political party’s primary election by choosing a party ballot at the polling place. The voter's choice would be recorded for that primary only, and the voter would remain unenrolled for future elections. Under existing law, only registered party members may vote in their party’s primary. The bill would define an “unenrolled elector” as a voter who is registered with no party affiliation or as “independent.”
  • Prohibit unenrolled voters from voting for party officers or members of party committees on the primary ballot.
  • Require the secretary of the commonwealth and county boards of elections to:
    • Prepare voter education and ballot materials for unenrolled voters.
    • Train poll workers on the new process.
    • Adjust absentee and mail-in ballot applications to reflect the new option.
    • Modify the SURE voter registration system to record primary party selection without changing a voter’s unaffiliated status.

Last Updated May 1, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Boscola (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 14, 2025, would establish same-day voter registration on Election Day and create related criminal enforcement measures. Under existing law, same-day registration is only available during the early voting period. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Permit a person to register to vote on the day of any primary, municipal or general election by appearing at the polling place in the district where they reside, completing a registration card, making an oath, and proving their residence by presenting a valid driver's license or approved documentation.
  • Make registration cards and forms for oaths available at each polling place and require the completed registration cards to be sent to the county board.
  • Require judges of elections to conduct same-day registration, and require judges to record the number of people who attempt to register but cannot provide proof of residence.
  • Require written notice to be mailed to a voter if a county board finds that the voter's Election Day registration is "substantially deficient." The bill specifies that a registration is not deficient solely because the person who provided proof of residence was ineligible to do so.
  • Require county boards to send notice to a random sample of at least 25 percent of voters registered on Election Day within 10 days of the election. County boards would also be required to mail registration verification notices to all voters registered on Election Day as soon as practicable and notify the district attorney and the Department of State if they do not receive satisfactory proof of a person's eligibility to vote.
  • Create new second degree misdemeanor offenses for conduct related to improper voter registration. Existing law includes various misdemeanor and felony offenses for improper registration by applicants and officials processing applications, some of which overlap with the new offenses set out in the bill. Specifically, the bill would create offenses for:
    • Causing an ineligible person's name to be registered to vote.
    • Causing a person's name to be registered in order to vote in multiple elections or to vote in an election district where the person does not reside.
    • Misrepresenting a person's identity when attempting to register to vote.
    • A judge of elections allowing an unregistered voter to vote in any election.
    • Aiding, abetting, counseling or procuring another person to commit one of these crimes.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Boscola (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 14, 2025, would slightly alter the "motor voter" process of registering people to vote through a driver's license or state ID application. When a person's voter registration is rejected because they are not yet 18, the bill would require the DMV (Department of Transportation in Pennsylvania) to add them to a list. When people on the list turn 18, the bill would require DMV to notify them that they have become eligible to vote and include voter registration materials with that notice.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Collett (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 21, 2025, would permit an individual who is 16 years of age but not yet 18 years of age to pre-register to vote. Under existing law, pre-registration is allowed only if the individual will be 18 by Election Day. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Add definitions for "preregistrant" and "preregistration" to the Code that expressly permit pre-registration beginning at age 16. Current law does not include either term.
  • Require each registration commission to enter pre-registration information into Pennsylvania's Statewide Uniform Registry of Electors (SURE) system when the pre-registration is received.
  • Mandate automatic registration activation for pre-registrants no later than their 18th birthday.
  • Require the registration commission to cancel an individual's pre-registration if their death is reported by the Department of Health. Under existing law, this cancellation process applies only to registered voters.

Last Updated March 24, 2025

data.author: Kane (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 4, 2025, would: 

  • Require all public employers, including state and municipal entities, to observe Election Day as a legal holiday.
  • Permit counties and school districts to also observe Election Day as a legal holiday, though they would not be required to do so.
  • Require private employers to provide at least two hours of unpaid leave on Election Day for employees to vote.
  • Impose a civil penalty of at least $1,000 for any private employer that fails to comply with the leave requirement, with penalties paid to the county board of elections.
  • Direct the Department of State to enforce these provisions and to adopt any necessary implementing regulations.

Last Updated April 16, 2025

data.author: Dush (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 9, 2025, would reduce the options for a person returning a mail ballot in person. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require a person who returns a mail ballot in person to do so at the primary office of the county board of elections. Under existing law, this can be done at any office of the county board of elections.
  • Specify that the delivery must be made to an employee of the board. This provision appears to be intended to eliminate the option of returning a ballot to an unstaffed drop box.

data.bill_name: Applications for Official Mail in Ballots

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Stefano (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 30, 2025, would:

  • Repeal provisions that allow voters to request permanent mail-in voter status and directs counties to send mail-in ballot applications annually to those voters. Under existing law, voters on the permanent list automatically received mail-in ballot applications each year unless they became ineligible.
  • Prohibit any person or entity other than the Pennsylvania Department of State or the voter’s county board of elections from sending a mail-in ballot application to that voter. Under existing law, there was no restriction on third-party distribution of mail-in ballot applications.

Last Updated October 20, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Brooks (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on September 17, 2025, would require the Department of State of the Commonwealth to report to the Legislature about election-related complaints. Specifically, within 45 days of the election, the Department would report for each non-anonymous complaint it received within five days of the election, whether through its Bureau of Commissions, Elections and Legislation, its formal election complaint website, or its Election Day hotline:

  • The complaint
  • The polling precinct and county in which the complaint originated
  • The actions the Department took to investigate


Last Updated September 18, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Boscola (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on December 22, 2025, would:

  • Eliminate the election of judges of election and inspectors of election by voters and instead require that members of district election boards be appointed by the county board of elections. Existing law requires judges and inspectors of election to be elected by voters at municipal elections for four-year terms.
  • Repeal statutory provisions governing tie votes for judge of election or inspector of election, which currently allow ties to be resolved by the majority inspector or by lot.
  • Require that the judge of election and majority inspector be members of the political party that received the highest number of votes in the district at the last general election, and that the minority inspector be a member of the political party receiving the second-highest number of votes. Existing law contains similar party-balance requirements but applies them in the context of elected officers rather than appointed ones.

This bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to vacancies.


Last Updated January 5, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Rothman (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 30, 2026, would expand the list of government agencies required to provide voter registration services by adding offices in the Commonwealth that provide applications for hunting and fishing licenses. Existing law only requires public assistance offices, marriage license bureaus, offices providing state-funded disability services, and armed forces recruitment centers to provide voter registration services.

Last Updated February 2, 2026

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT

data.author: Saval (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 4, 2026, would:

  • Expand the prohibition on law enforcement presence near voting locations by prohibiting federal law enforcement officials, in addition to police officers, from being within 100 feet of a polling place or ballot return site during a primary or election, unless voting, serving warrants, called upon to preserve the peace, or covered by the existing exception for police assigned to a police station or headquarters located in the same building or on the same premises. Existing law prohibits police officers from being within 100 feet of a polling place, subject to exceptions, but does not expressly include federal law enforcement officials or ballot return sites.
  • Increase penalties for unauthorized law enforcement presence by increasing the maximum fine from $500 to $3,000. Under the bill, offense remains a misdemeanor.

Last Updated June 9, 2026

data.bill_location: House State Government and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT AND ELECTIONS: Committee recommends measure to be held for further study

data.author: Brien (I)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 22, 2025, would require the state board of elections to take steps to verify the citizenship status of individuals on the voter registration list and establish a documentary proof of citizenship requirement. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the state board of elections to verify the citizenship status of all individuals on the voter registration list by comparing voter registrations with citizenship data from the DMV, the Help America Vote Verification information system, and any other available databases containing citizenship status. Existing law does not include a similar citizenship verification provision.
  • Establish a proof of citizenship requirement for both new applicants for registration and existing registrants. Officials would be required to attempt to confirm citizenship using available databases, and individuals whose citizenship could not be confirmed would be required to provide proof of citizenship in order to become or remain registered. Under this process:
    • Any resident whose citizenship cannot be confirmed from a comparison of one or more state, multi-state, or federal databases would removed from the list of eligible voters.
    • Individuals would receive notice in writing of an inability to confirm their status and they would be provided an opportunity to present evidence or documentary proof of citizenship to be restored to the eligible voter list.
  • Expressly state that it is unlawful for a noncitizen to vote in any public election. The bill would further specify that only citizens can register to vote. 

Last Updated June 2, 2025

data.author: Brien (I)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 29, 2025, would shorten the early voting period so that it lasts a total of 10 nonconsecutive days before the election instead of 20 days before. The bill would require early voting to be offered on the weekend days immediately before the election and eight weekdays. Existing law is not similarly explicit, but has been interpreted similarly during the longer voting period it provides. Finally, the bill would provide that "All the voter and election protections and procedures" for Election Day voting would apply to the new 10 day early voting period.

Last Updated March 5, 2025

data.author: Alzate (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 13, 2025, proposes an amendment to the state constitution that would eliminate the requirement that a person reside in Rhode Island for at least 30 days before they may register to vote in Rhode Island. If the bill passes, the amendment would then be sent to voters for ratification.

Last Updated February 14, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Baginski (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 13, 2025, would: 

  • Require local election boards to advertise early voting dates, hours, and locations in a newspaper before and during the early voting period and post a prominent, accessible notice on the municipality's website for the duration of early voting. Existing law does not specify a similar publication requirement for early voting information.
  • Allow local election boards to post voter registration deadline announcements on their municipality's website for at least ten days instead of advertising in a newspaper, provided the notice meets web content accessibility guidelines. Existing law requires newspaper advertisements regarding registration but does not include an online posting option. 

The bill also includes a provision not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to publication of local questions submitted to voters.  

Last Updated March 13, 2025

data.bill_location: House State Government and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT AND ELECTIONS: Committee recommends measure to be held for further study

data.author: Fascia (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 26, 2025, would: 

  • Add the Department of Environmental Management (Department) as an automatic voter registration agency. The Department would offer voter registration services to individuals applying for or renewing hunting and fishing licenses. Automatic voter registration is currently available at the Division of Motor Vehicles only.
  • Require the Department to provide voter registration applications alongside license applications, assist applicants in completing forms, and send completed applications electronically to the central voter registration system by the next business day, while allowing applicants to decline registration with confidentiality protections.  
  • Specify that individuals mistakenly registered through this process would not be at fault, but those who knowingly provide false information would be subject to prosecution under existing law.
  • Require the process to take effect once the Department certifies its computer system can support the requirements. 
  • Specify that any additional state agencies designated for automatic voter registration would follow similar procedures.

Last Updated March 11, 2025

This bill, as introduced on February 28, 2025, would require applicants for mail ballots and emergency mail ballots to include proof of their date of birth and a state driver’s license or identification number as part of their applications. Existing law only requires applications to be signed.

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT AND ELECTIONS: Committee recommends measure to be held for further study

data.author: Cruz (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 28, 2025, would facilitate voting opportunities for certain incarcerated people. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow any "specially qualified voter" to request a mail ("absentee") ballot, even if the person has not previously voted. A specially qualified voter is one who is:
    • Incarcerated in a Rhode Island jail or prison for any reason other than conviction of a felony
    • Domiciled in Rhode Island
    • Otherwise qualified to vote
  • Require the department of corrections to designate a "jail voting coordinator" to facilitate voting by qualified incarcerated people. Among other responsibilities, the coordinator would be required to submit a plan to the secretary of state detailing how the requirements of this bill would be implemented.
  • Require the department of corrections to post voting information in all correctional facilities in the place normally used for posting notices.
  • Create a new right to submit a complaint to the secretary of state for any incarcerated person eligible to vote under this bill whose access to voting has been denied.

Last Updated March 5, 2025

data.bill_location: House State Government and Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on STATE GOVERNMENT AND ELECTIONS

data.author: Messier (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 17, 2025, would impose documentary proof of citizenship requirements for individuals who are automatically registered to vote at the DMV and change the system from front-end to back-end automatic voter registration. Under existing law, individuals are automatically registered to vote during DMV transactions unless they opt out during the transaction, if they provide proof of identity and residence and certify their citizenship through documentation or declaration. Specifically, this bill would:

  • Limit automatic voter registration at the DMV to only those individuals who present documentary proof of U.S. citizenship during an application or renewal of a REAL ID or standard driver's license or personal ID document. DMV officials would be authorized to rely on citizenship documentation submitted in a previous REAL ID application. Submission of such documentation would be considered certification of citizenship for voter registration purposes. Under existing law, the automatic registration requires self-certification of citizenship but does not require documentation. Additionally, existing law does not specify requirements for REAL ID transactions. 
  • Specify that if an applicant does not opt out within 14 days of receiving the notice of their automatic registration, the applicant would be registered to vote. Existing law requires applicants choose whether to opt out during the transaction.
  • End automatic registration for individuals who do not present documentary proof of citizenship and instead provide a two-step, opt-in registration process for these individuals. These applicants would receive a notice and may register by affirmatively certifying their citizenship and returning a signed registration form. Under existing law, applicants may register at the DMV by self-certifying citizenship through declaration without the two-step process.
  • Specify that if the DMV has access to information confirming that an applicant is already registered, the agency would treat the transaction as an address update and transmit that information to election officials, even if the individual did not present citizenship documentation during the current transaction.
  • Exempt from automatic registration individuals using a business address instead of a residential address during their DMV transaction. These individuals would not be offered any registration opportunity under the bill. Under existing law, residency documentation is required, but there is no categorical exclusion for business addresses.
  • Prohibit automatic or streamlined voter registration for individuals who fail to certify, by documentation or declaration, that they are U.S. citizens. The bill would expressly prohibit offering any registration opportunity to individuals who have documented their non-citizenship. Existing law prohibits non-citizens from registering to vote, but does not expressly prohibit offering the registration opportunity in these cases.
  • Expand which additional agencies beyond the DMV may offer automatic voter registration, by permitting agencies to do so if they either collect documentary proof of citizenship or can verify citizenship by database matching or other means deemed reliable by the secretary of state. Existing law limits automatic voter registration to only those non-DMV agencies that verify citizenship through documentation. The bill would also require non-DMV agencies that process transactions for multiple individuals eligible for registration to register each individual according to the procedures applicable to the DMV process.
  • Permit agencies that are not authorized to offer full automatic voter registration to make automatic updates to voter registration address information, if the secretary verifies that the agency collects sufficient information to do so.

Last Updated June 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: Public Law No. 2026-003

data.author: O'Brien (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 1, 2026, reinstates an expired program under which eligible disabled and UOCAVA voters were permitted to receive electronically transmitted ballots. Specifically, the bill:

  • Permits the secretary of state to send ballots electronically to eligible voters, and receive the completed ballots, through an electronic service that has met security guidelines.
  • Defines eligible voters as:
    • UOCAVA voters
    • Disabled voters who are incapacitated to such an extent that it would be an undue hardship to vote at the polls because of illness, mental or physical disability, blindness, or a serious impairment of mobility

Last Updated April 2, 2026

data.author: Dimitri (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 14, 2025, would: 

  • Require local canvassing boards to publish early voting dates, hours, and locations in a newspaper of general circulation at least once prior to early voting and at least once during early voting. The bill would also require notice on the municipality’s website for the duration of the early voting period. Existing law does not include a similar notice requirement for early voting.
  • Allow municipalities to substitute newspaper advertisements of voter registration site and hour designations with a conspicuous website posting for at least 10 consecutive days before the voter registration deadline. Under existing law, local boards must advertise voter registration information in a newspaper of general circulation at least twice before the last day for voter registration deadline. 

The bill also includes provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to notice for local questions submitted to voters. 


Last Updated April 8, 2025

data.author: DiMario (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 14, 2025, proposes an amendment to the state constitution that would eliminate the requirement that an individual be a resident of the state, and the municipality in which they reside, for 30 days prior to an election in order to vote. Under existing law, voters who have been in the state for under 30 days may only cast votes for president and vice president.

If passed by both chambers of the legislature, this amendment would go before the voters at the next general election.

The bill also includes a provision not tracked by Voting Rights Lab related to candidates.

Last Updated March 26, 2025

This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would require the department of corrections to provide all incarcerated individuals prior to their release with information about voting rights restoration and registering to vote. Specifically, the department would be required to provide:



    • A written document detailing the necessary process to have voting rights restored

data.author: Burns (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2026, would enable voters to affiliate with a political party and would close political parties' primaries to voters without affiliation or to voters affiliated with another party. Parties would have the ability to choose to open their primaries if they preferred.

Voters could change their affiliation up to 30 days before a primary and participate in their new party's primary. The bill would also require the voter registration form to enable voters to pick a party, and provide that if a voter did not, they would be registered as independent. Finally, the bill would require the State Election Commission to assist county boards of election with maintaining the partisanship data.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Long S (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would change the state's existing open primary system to a semi-closed primary system. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow only registered members of a political party or registered independents to vote in a partisan primary or partisan advisory referendum beginning on May 31, 2026. Under existing law, voters do not need to affiliate with a political party to vote in any election.
  • Require independent voters who participate in a partisan primary to be registered as affiliated with that party. Those voters would be prohibited from changing affiliation for two years after the election.
  • Require voters wishing to switch parties in order to vote in a partisan primary to change their affiliation no later than 60 days before the primary.
  • Require voters to sign an affidavit re-affirming their affiliation at the polling place during a primary election. The bill would also authorize conforming changes to the voter registration and absentee voting application processes.

Last Updated February 3, 2025
