data.author: King (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would repeal a statute that disenfranchises individuals for conviction of any felony or an "offense against the election laws." Specifically, the bill would require any individual who has been incarcerated because of a criminal conviction to automatically be eligible to register to vote upon release from prison.


The bill would make conforming changes to voter registration forms to reflect the repeal of this provision.

Last Updated January 14, 2025

data.bill_name: Intend to Conduct Voter Registration Drives

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Newton (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would make changes to voter registration requirements, voter list maintenance, and third party registration drives. The bill would:

  • Require voter registration applicants to provide a South Carolina driver's license or state ID number on their application. Existing law requires an applicant to provide a Social Security Number. The bill would require individuals applying in person or by mail to provide a Social Security Number only if they do not have a South Carolina driver's license or state ID. Individuals applying online would need to provide the last 4 digits of their Social Security Number.
  • Establish that applying for voter registration in another state constitutes a change in domicile for voter registration purposes. The bill would also repeal existing language that allows a voter to consider addresses where they have a club membership or where they keep personal property when determining domicile.
  • Change the severity of the crime of false registration from a misdemeanor to a felony. The bill would also create a new felony offense for filling out a voter registration application for someone without their knowledge.
  • Require county and local clerks of court to send the State Election Commission a quarterly report of all persons who indicate they are not U.S. citizens when completing jury summonses.
  • Require a third party voter registration organization to register with the State Election Commission prior to conducting a voter registration drive. Failure to comply with this requirement would result in a fine up to $500.
  • Require third party voter registration organizations to turn in all completed voter registration forms from any voter registration drive to the appropriate county board within 10 days of completion. Failure to comply with this requirement would result in a fine up to $1000.

Last Updated February 3, 2025

data.bill_location: House Education and Public Works Committee

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on EDUCATION AND PUBLIC WORKS

data.author: Beach (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would clarify that a change of address form submitted by mail, in person, or electronically to the DMV for the purpose of a driver's license update shall also serve as a voter registration update unless the person opts out. The bill would also require the DMV to provide a copy of the voter's signature, along with the change of address information, to the State Election Commission or county board of registration and elections as appropriate.

Last Updated February 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Johnson J (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, and entitled the "Election Official Protection Act," would prohibit several actions against election officials and the voting process. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit interfering with an election official performing an official duty or coercing an official to refuse to comply with their lawful duty
  • Prohibit threatening, coercing, or intimidating an election official
  • Prohibit physically obstructing an election official's access to or egress from a polling place or other locations where election-related activities occur
  • Prohibit publishing an election official's personal information without their consent
  • Prohibit tampering with or accessing certain election equipment, including ballot boxes, without authorization
  • Prohibit the altering voter lists or accessing the statewide voter registration system without authorization by law
  • Create a misdemeanor offense for a violation of any of these provisions. An individual convicted under this law must be imprisoned not more than three years or fined not more than five thousand dollars or both.
  • Allow the attorney general, a solicitor, or an election official to bring a civil suit to prevent or restrain a violation of this bill.

Last Updated February 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: Act No. 183

data.author: Newton B (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 18, 2026, exempts compensation paid to managers and clerks of election from state income tax.

Last Updated May 19, 2026

data.bill_name: Secure Accurate and Verifiable Elections Act

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Harris (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would make changes to list maintenance procedures, voter registration procedures, polling place procedures, and absentee ballot voting and counting procedures. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all voter registrations to last for a term of ten years, after which time, a voter must provide valid ID to confirm his residency and citizenship to the county board of voter registration and elections prior to the completion of their voter registration process. The board must issue a new voter ID card each time a person successfully registers to vote. Under existing law, any person who is registered to vote remains permanently registered unless their name is "removed from the registration list for cause."
  • Require the executive director of the state election commission to establish and continuously update lists of voter registrations for active voter registrations, inactive voter registrations, and archived voter registrations and to provide any of these lists to a voter who requests them for free.
  • Would require that for all elections, the county board of elections appoint four managers of election for each polling place for the first 250 voters registered to vote in each precinct in the county, and may appoint four additional managers for each 250 voters after. Under existing law, the board must appoint three managers for each 500 voters.
  • Require two poll managers to initial a paper ballot prior to handing it to a voter to use. Any ballot without the two sets of initials would be null and void.
  • Require each county board of voter registration and elections to post its election results on the board's website or office before notification of its unofficial election results is sent to the state election commission.
  • Re-affirm existing law that specifies that only U.S. citizens who are registered to vote can vote.
  • Eliminate the requirement that in order to vote an absentee ballot, a voter be unable to vote during early voting.
  • Repeal the statute provision allowing voters 65 years of age or older to vote absentee.
  • Allow women expecting to give birth within three weeks of Election Day and homebound persons and nursing home occupants to vote absentee.
  • Require individuals requesting an absentee ballot to have an individual witness their signature on the application and to have the witness sign the application. Under existing law, neither signature is required.
  • Require the county board of voter registration and election, municipal election commission, or municipal party's executive committee that receives absentee ballots prior to an election to ensure that those ballots are sorted by precinct and securely transported to the appropriate precinct on Election Day. All absentee ballots would be required to remain in their envelopes until the close of polls on Election Day when they can be counted at their respective precincts. Existing law only provides that those entities are "responsible for the tabulation and reporting of ballots."
  • Require that if a voter submitted a ballot in-person and an absentee ballot, that the absentee ballot would be automatically null and void and not be counted.
  • Make the general election day a legal holiday.


Last Updated January 29, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Newton B (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would change the state's existing open primary system to a semi-closed primary system. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow only registered members of a political party or independent voters to vote in a partisan primary or partisan advisory referendum beginning on March 31, 2026. Under existing law, voters do not need to affiliate with a political party to vote in any election.
  • Require independent voters who participate in a partisan primary to be registered as affiliated with that party.
  • Require voters wishing to switch parties in order to vote in a partisan primary to change their affiliation no later than 45 days before the primary. The bill would also authorize conforming changes to the voter registration form and process.


Last Updated February 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Mitchell (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 16, 2025, would:

  • Authorize the State Election Commission to provide administrative assistance to county officials conducting post-election processes, including counting and canvassing, audits, certification, and recounts. 
  • Grant the Commission the authority to step in and take direct oversight of these processes from a county board of voter registration and elections if the Commission determines that county officials are not complying with lawful duties and the noncompliance could negatively impact the accuracy of results. Under existing law, in this circumstance, the Commission is permitted to supervise the county board, identify failures, establish a plan to correct the failures, and implement the plan.


Last Updated February 5, 2025

data.bill_name: Early Voting Days and Hours of Operation

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Newton B (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on March 5, 2025, would extend the hours for early voting at statewide primary elections so that voting hours end at 6 p.m. rather than 5 p.m. 

Last Updated March 21, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Harris (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 3, 2025, would be known as the "Gold Standard Elections Act" and would create a requirement to provide documentary proof of citizenship when registering, require voters to re-register every 10 years, and eliminate early voting, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require all voters to reapply to register to vote every 10 years. Under existing law, individuals who register to vote remain permanently registered unless their name is removed from the list for cause.
  • Require all individuals registering to vote to provide valid ID to confirm residency and citizenship each time they register to vote. Under existing law, voters are not required to provide documentary proof of citizenship and are only required to present ID if registering to vote for the first time in the county, but may provide other documentation to prove their identity.
  • Specify that only U.S. citizens with a current and valid voter registration are allowed to vote.
  • Eliminate early voting. Under existing law, early voting is available for statewide general elections beginning 15 days prior to Election Day through the third day prior to Election Day. For all other elections and primaries, early voting is available beginning 15 days before Election Day through the fourth day before Election Day.
  • Make changes to the list of voters who can vote by mail. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Repeal the statute allowing voters 65 years of age and older to vote by mail. Under existing law, only certain categories of voters who are unable to vote in-person can vote by mail.
    • Allow women expecting to give birth within three weeks of Election Day to vote by mail.
    • Allow homebound individuals and nursing home occupants to vote by mail.
  • Require all mail ballots to include the signature of the voter requesting the application and a witness to that signature. Under existing law, the voter does not need to have a witness.
  • Create new voter list maintenance procedures. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the executive director of the State Election commission to utilize the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services' SAVE services to identify individuals who are not U.S. citizens who are registered to vote in the state and to remove those individuals from the rolls and place them onto a separate list, which would be publicly available.
    • Require the executive director of the State Election Commission to compare the voter registration list with the U.S. Postal Service's National Change of address program and the U.S. social security's death index and master file on a monthly basis.
  • Prohibit electronic poll books at voting locations and only allow paper poll books. Under existing law, electronic poll books are used for all elections.
  • Make changes to the size of voting precincts. Specifically the bill would:
    • Require that each precinct have 1,500 to 2,000 qualified voters, plus an allowance of ten percent overage. Once the maximum number of qualified voters is met within a precinct, a plan for a new precinct must be established and met within one year. 
    • Create a new felony offense for any director of a county board of voter registration and elections that violates this provision.
    • Require the County Board of Voter Registration and Elections to appoint four managers of each polling place for every 250 voters rather than three managers for every 500 voters. 
  • Require that a ballot given to a voter at a polling place contain the initials of two poll managers before handing the ballot to the voter. Any marked ballot without the initials of two poll managers would be null and void. Under existing law, there is no similar requirement.
  • Require that all statewide primary, general, and special elections use paper ballots and be counted by hand. There is no similar requirement under existing law.
  • Require that all precincts count all ballots in a manner that can be observed by the public, both in-person and online via streaming, provided that the identity of each voter remain secret. Any member of the public watching the ballot count process in-person must conduct themselves in a manner that will not interfere with the counting process. There is no similar requirement under existing law.
  • Require each County Board of Voter Registration and Elections to post its election results on the board's website or office before notification of its unofficial election results are sent to the State Election Commission.
  • Create a legal holiday for Election Day.
  • ﻿Require any elector chosen to represent the state in any federal election process to refrain from voting for any candidate and would require them to object to the federal election process if the process permits electors from other states that: 1) employ voting machines that are connected to the internet; and 2) uses closed source code for its election process.

Last Updated April 8, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Bauer (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 9, 2025, would create early voting for presidential preference primaries by applying the same early voting rules for other elections to presidential preference primaries. Under existing law, early voting is available for all elections except for presidential preference primaries. As it pertains to presidential primary elections, this bill would:

  • Require each county board of voter registration and elections to establish at least one early voting center and up to seven early voting centers.
  • Require that the early voting period be from Monday through Saturday for the two-week period immediately preceding an election.
  • Require that the early voting centers be open from 8:30 a.m. until 6 p.m. each day of early voting
  • Require each county board of voter registration and elections to determine its locations for early voting centers by March 10 before that primary election.
  • Require the county board of voter registration and elections to publish the location and hours of each early voting center at least 14 days before the early voting period begins.

Last Updated April 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Pope (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 6, 2025 would:

  • Require each county board of voter registration and elections to maintain the following records and provide them to a voter upon request at no charge:
    • Proof of citizenship, any actions taken to verify citizenship, and any actions taken to remove noncitizens from the voter registration list
    • Cast vote records
    • Poll lists or precinct lists
    • Logic and accuracy testing reports
    • Ballot review and reconciliation reports
    • Chain of custody documents. Under existing law, official records of registration are open to public inspection. Those records contain voters': name; race; date of birth; residence; mailing address; and phone number.
  • Specify that "every citizen" of South Carolina may observe the election process "in its entirety," including:
    • The testing of any equipment or processes prior to the election
    • All operations during mail voting, early voting, and on election day
    • Chain of custody of election materials
    • All processes related to the canvassing, preparation, counting, and tabulation of ballots
    • The uploading of election results
    • Any postelection audits of processes of results. Under existing law, the election process is open to the public.
  • Require observers, to the extent that they do not overcrowd a polling location or other facility used in the election process, to:
    • Be allowed to view any component or election process while remaining at least five feet from voters
    • Refrain from communicating with a voter while the voter is inside the polling place
    • Be allowed to view the ballots for logic and accuracy testing, mail ballot verification, and postelection audits.
  • Require that any and all parts and components of the voting system be accounted for throughout the time and place of their use
  • Require a postelection audit of precinct and voting center election results. Any discrepancy between the audit and tabulated vote counts of .002 percent or greater must be investigated for election system tampering by a forensic audit team appointed by the Inspector General.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated August 21, 2025

data.bill_name: Voter Registration to Include Party Affiliation

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: White J (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 6, 2025, would change South Carolina's open primary system to a closed primary system. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require voter registration forms to include a party affiliation section. Under existing law, there is no option for a voter to affiliate with a party when registering. 
  • Allow a voter to change their party affiliation once annually.
  • Require a voter changing their affiliation to submit it so that it is received at least 60 days prior to the primary election.
  • Only allow voters registered with a party to vote in that party's primary election.
  • Require the State Election Commission to develop and implement procedures necessary to carry out the provisions of this act.

Last Updated May 7, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Caskey (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on May 8, 2025 would:

  • Allow the State Election Commission to extend the closing time of a polling place if that polling place is delayed in opening for 15 minutes or more or if voting is otherwise interrupted for 15 minutes or more. The Commission would be permitted to extend the closing time by an equal number of minutes not to exceed 120 minutes. Under existing law, there is no such requirement.
  • Permit an individual voting after closing time by virtue of an order from the commission to vote a provisional ballot.
  • Lay out the administrative requirements that the director of the county board must meet in order to extend polling place hours under these circumstances. 

Last Updated May 9, 2025

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Burns (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 12, 2026, would enable voters to affiliate with a political party and would close political parties' primaries to voters without affiliation or to voters affiliated with another party. Parties would have the ability to choose to open their primaries if they preferred.

Voters could change their affiliation up to 30 days before a primary and participate in their new party's primary. The bill would also require the voter registration form to enable voters to pick a party, and provide that if a voter did not, they would be registered as independent. Finally, the bill would require the State Election Commission to assist county boards of election with maintaining the partisanship data.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on EDUCATION AND PUBLIC WORKS

data.author: Pope (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill as introduced on February 25, 2026, would:

  • Prohibit the use of driver's licenses or ID cards issued to noncitizens as valid voter identification and require IDs issued to noncitizens be conspicuously labeled with the text "Limited Term."
  • Require license holders or applicants who previously provided the department of motor vehicles with information indicating they were not a U.S. citizen to notify the department within ten days of becoming a citizen. The department of motor vehicles is allowed to require supporting documentation before applying change to the license.

data.bill_name: Persons Disqualified from Registering or Voting

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Jackson (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would repeal a statute that disenfranchises individuals for conviction of any felony or an "offense against the election laws." Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require any individual who has been incarcerated because of a criminal conviction to automatically be eligible to register to vote upon release from prison. The bill would make conforming changes to voter registration forms to reflect the repeal of this provision.
  • Require the Department of Corrections to notify an individual that their voting rights are restored upon release along with instructions on how to register to vote. Under existing law, there is no such requirement.

Last Updated January 14, 2025

data.bill_name: Voting by Handicapped and Elderly Electors

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Grooms (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would change requirements for voters needing accommodations while voting and would expand the ways in which an individual under 18 can pre-register to vote. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Allow a qualified individual who is voting from their car due to physical handicap or age to choose a person to remain in the car while the voter is casting their ballot and to inform the manager of that individual. Under existing law, no one is allowed to stay in the car unless the voter needs assistance due to blindness, disability, or inability to read or write. 
  • Allow an individual who because of their handicap or age cannot enter the polling place or stand in line to receive assistance voting. Under existing law, only individuals who are unable to read or write or who are physically unable or incapacitated from preparing a ballot or voting are entitled to assistance.
  • Allow a qualified individual under the age of 18 to pre-register to vote by appearing before the county board of voter registration and elections, submitting an application online, or when submitting an application or change of address form for a driver's license. Under existing law, a qualified individual under the age of 18 can only pre-register to vote by appearing before the board and applying. 


Last Updated January 28, 2025

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Campsen (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025 would change the ways in which voting precincts and polling places are established. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the county board of voter registration and elections to establish at least one polling place within each precinct unless the board determines that there is no suitable location within the precinct. The board can designate a polling place located outside of a precinct if it is within five miles of the precinct boundary, unless a waiver is received from the executive director of the state election commission, in which case, the polling place does not need to be within five miles. Under existing law, the general assembly is responsible for establishing voting locations but there are no specific requirements for how locations must be established.
  • Require voters to vote at the designated polling place for the precinct of their residence, rather than the designated polling place within their precinct. This bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect the new requirements.
  • Allow the board to create a polling place that serves two precincts if there are no suitable locations for a polling place within a precinct. 
  • Repeal the statute allowing the board to establish multiple polling places within a precinct if the voters are assigned to the polling places alphabetically or geographically. 
  • Require the state election commission to report the names of all polling precincts by county that have more than 3,000 registered voters as of January 1 to the general assembly no later than the fourth Tuesday of each odd-numbered year. Under existing law, the commission must report precincts with more than 1,500 registered voters.
  • Repeal the statute requiring the state election commission to notify county boards of voter registration if the general assembly has failed to alter precincts so that no precinct will have more than 1,500 voters.
  • Require that any voter whose registration is transferred to another precinct be notified by mail by the board. Under existing law, only voters whose registration is transferred due to statutory requirements must be notified.
  • Repeal the statute requiring an alternative polling place for an emergency situation to be approved by the majority of the legislative delegation if the designation occurs more than seven days prior to an election and would instead require that the authority charged by law with conducting an election notify the members of the appropriate county legislative delegation with the location for the alternative polling place.
  • Repeal the statute requiring an alternative polling place to be outside the precinct only if no other location within the precinct is available for use as a polling place, and in that case, must be located in an adjoining precinct. The bill would instead require that the alternative polling place be selected with consideration of the distance the voters would be required to travel in order to vote.
  • Repeal the statute requiring the authority charged with conducting the election to certify in writing if an alternative polling place outside of the precinct is selected and to state that no other location within the precinct was available.
  • Require voters to be notified of an alternative polling place before the election and on Election Day through the media, on the website of the state election commission, on the website of the appropriate county board of voter registration and elections, and posted notice at the polling place. Under existing law, voters only need to be notified through the media and by posted notice.

Last Updated January 28, 2025

data.bill_name: Voting in a Partisan Primary Election

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Rice (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would change South Carolina's open primary system to a closed primary system effective May 31, 2026. Specifically, the bill would require:

  • Voters wishing to vote in a partisan primary after May 31, 2026 to register their affiliation with their chosen political party no later than 60 days before the primary.
  • Voters to sign an affidavit re-affirming their affiliation at the polling place during a primary election. The bill would make non-substantive and conforming changes to the voter registration and absentee voting application processes because of these changes.

Last Updated February 5, 2025

data.bill_name: Requirement that Persons Must Register in Order to Vote

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Cash (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would establish a closed primary system effective May 31, 2028. After that date, voters wishing to vote in a partisan primary would need to register their affiliation with their chosen political party no later than 30 days before the primary. Voters would also have to sign an affidavit re-affirming their affiliation at the polling place during the primary. Political parties could only open their primary to unaffiliated voters by advising the State Election Commission no earlier than 180 days and no later than 60 days before the primary. The bill would also authorize conforming changes to the voter registration and absentee voting application processes.

Violations of this prohibition would be punishable as a misdemeanor.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Johnson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would propose an amendment to the state constitution to require voters to provide documentary proof of citizenship with their voter registration applications. The bill would:

  • Require every voter registration applicant to provide a one of the following documents as proof of U.S. citizenship:
    • a current, valid South Carolina driver's license or state ID issued on or after October 1, 1996, or a current, valid driver's license or state ID issued by another state if the person has recently moved to South Carolina
    • a copy of a birth certificate stating the person was born in the U.S.
    • a current, valid U.S. passport or a copy of the pages in the person's U.S. passport showing the passport number
    • U.S naturalization documents or the number of a certificate of naturalization. The county board would have to verify a certificate number with the federal government
    • a Bureau of Indian Affairs card or card number, a tribal treaty card, or a tribal enrollment number. The county board would have to verify a BIA card number or tribal enrollment number with the federal government
    • any other document or method established by the federal Immigration Reform and Control Act of 1986. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to attest to the fact that they are a U.S. citizen.
  • Require each county board to transmit a list of all registered voters who have not provided proof of citizenship as required by the bill to the Attorney General by October 31, 2025. The Attorney General would attempt to verify the citizenship of the listed voters using all available resources, including databases from the DMV, the Social Security Administration, the U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services, the National Association for Public Health Statistics and Informations Systems, and other federal, state, county, or municipal database relating to voter registration to which the Attorney General may have access. The Attorney General would be required to issue a report concerning the findings regarding this list of voters to the Governor and the leaders of each chamber of the legislature by March 1, 2026.

To go into effect, each chamber of the legislature must pass the legislation by a two-thirds vote, voters must vote to approve the amendment by a simple majority, and then the legislature must approve the bill for a second time by a simple majority.

Last Updated February 3, 2025

data.author: Tedder (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2025, would create a back-end automatic voter registration process for eligible voters who complete an application for a driver's license or state ID card at the DMV. Under existing law, automatic voter registration is not available in South Carolina. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require DMV applications to advise applicants that their information, including their signature, will be shared with election officials for voter registration purposes. Applicants under the age of 18 would have their information remain private and would not be registered to vote until they turn 18. 
  • Require that failing to sign the DMV application would be treated as a declination of voter registration. The county board of voter registration and elections would also notify each voter for whom it receives information of the process to decline registration. If the voter does not decline registration within 15 days of the notice from the county board, then the transmission of information from the DMV would serve as a completed voter registration application.

data.author: Climer (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would: 

  • Change South Carolina's primary system from an open system to closed. Specifically, the bill would require voters to register as a member of a certified political party or as unaffiliated with a certified political party 45 days prior to a primary election in order to vote in the primary. The bill would make conforming and nonsubstantive changes because of this requirement. Under existing law, there is an open primary system and voters do not have the option to affiliate with a political party when registering to vote.
  • Allow an unaffiliated voter to become registered with a political party by voting for that party's candidate in an election. 
  • Require voter registration applications to provide a place for a voter to affiliate with a political party. If a voter failed to affiliate with a party, their status would remain unaffiliated unless the voter submitted a written request or confirmation or by voting in a primary for a party's candidate. The voter could submit the requested change on a voter registration application. 
  • Require the State Election Commission to ensure each voter's political party affiliation in the statewide voter registration file. 

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

data.author: Senate State Affairs Committee
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 5, 2026, modifies the process for verifying a voter's registration. Under prior law, when a voter registration application was received, the county auditor had to review it for eligibility and completeness and, if the applicant was ineligible or the application could not be completed, sent a notice explaining why the registration was not filed. Specifically, the bill:

Note: The Act takes effect December 1, 2026.

This bill, as enacted on March 26, 2026:

Further, on Election Day, the official in charge must provide an updated list.

data.bill_name: Voter Individual Provide Proof of Citizenship

status_actions.summary: Signed by GOVERNOR

data.author: Carley (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 26, 2026:

  • Requires individuals registering to vote to provide documentary proof of United States citizenship in order to register to vote in state elections. Under the bifurcated system, registration for federal elections continues to require a driver's license or Social Security number and an attestation of citizenship. Prior law did not impose additional documentation burdens in order to register to vote in state elections.
  • Defines acceptable documentation to demonstrate citizenship, including:
    • Driver's licenses or nondriver IDs indicating U.S. citizenship. South Dakota began issuing these licenses in 2025.
    • Legible photocopies of specified documents such as a birth certificate, U.S. passport, consular report of birth abroad issued by the United States Department of State, or certificate of naturalization
    • Tribal IDs
    • Any other type of acceptable documentary evidence of citizenship permitted under federal law.
  • Additionally requires auditors to designate individuals as federal-only voters if they provide only a post office box or commercial mail receiving address without a description of their physical habitation. Prior law allowed certain voters to be designated as federal voters based on residency documentation.
  • Makes conforming changes throughout the Code.

Last Updated March 27, 2026

data.author: Peterson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 26, 2026:

  • Requires the secretary of state to post a weekly update of the complete statewide voter registration file on the secretary’s website, recorded each Monday before 11:59 p.m. central time, and to include any updates transmitted from county master registration files. Prior law required weekly updates but did not specify that the "complete" registration file be posted.
  • Requires the secretary of state to publish a rolling five-year historical archive of the complete statewide voter registration file on the secretary’s website before 11:59 p.m. central time each Monday. Prior law did not require a publicly accessible rolling historical archive of voter registration files. Prior law, which the bill leaves in place, also restricted access to certain voter information such as Social Security number, driver's license and ID number, and month and day of birth.

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE & LOCAL GOVERNMENT:  Subcommittee on ELECTIONS & CAMPAIGN FINANCE

data.author: Keisling (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 16, 2025 would require individuals registering to vote to provide proof of citizenship, make changes to voter list maintenance procedures, and would make changes to the appeals process for voters flagged as non-citizens. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require an applicant to provide an original or a true and correct copy of a birth certificate, a U.S. passport, a U.S. naturalization document, or any document or method of citizenship established by federal law when registering to vote. Under existing law, a voter only needs to check a box stating that they are a citizen.
  • Require an individual registering to vote to attest under penalty of perjury that they meet the requirements for voter eligibility.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to compare the statewide voter registration database with the department of safety database to ensure non-citizens are not registered to vote on a monthly basis. Existing law does not articulate how often this comparison must be done.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to ensure that the citizenship of each registered voter is verified before early voting begins for each election. If the citizenship of an individual who has registered to vote prior to the registration deadline has not been verified, the individual would be allowed to vote a provisional ballot which would not be counted until their citizenship was verified. 
  • Require the coordinator of elections to notify both the state election commission and the county election commission on a monthly basis if sufficient evidence exists that a particular registered voter is not a citizen. Under existing law, the coordinator of elections is only required to notify the county election commission and no timeframe is specified. 
  • Require the county election commission to send notice to any voter flagged as not being a citizen within five business days of receiving notice from the coordinator of elections. The county election commission would be required to send the notice to the address at which the voter is registered by certified mail with return receipt requested. If the county election commission does not receive the return receipt within five business days after the postmark date of the notice, the voter is deemed to have received the notice at such time. Under existing law, the county commission is required to send the notice but no timeframe is specified.
  • Require that if a notice sent by the county election commission is returned as undeliverable, the administrator of elections of the county where the individual is registered would be required to purge the voter from the voter registration database but would be required to maintain the individual's registration and voting history. Under existing law, a voter can be purged from the list for failing to respond to the notice within 30 days of receipt of the notice.
  • Make changes to the appeals process for a voter who wishes to appeal the decision to purge their registration. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Allow a voter who has received notice that they did not provide proper proof of citizenship to appeal to the state election commission no later than 14 days after the county election commission has made their decision. The commission would be prohibited from purging the registration during the appeals process.
    • Require the voter to submit originals or true and correct copies of documentation of citizenship to the state election commission with the voter's notice of appeal.
    • Require the state election commission to conduct a hearing at which the voter must appear in person or by video. The state election commission would be required to enter an order determining whether the individual is a U.S. citizen or not and qualified to vote. If an individual is determined not to be a citizen, that finding would be reported to the district attorney.The determination would be final and subject only to judicial review. Under existing law, an individual who is unable to provide documentation to show proof of citizenship is entitled to appeal to the state election commission and to submit proof of citizenship in person or in writing. The commission is required to conduct a hearing and to forward a copy of its decision to the administrator where the voter lives. 
    • Require that a finding that a person who registered to vote is not a U.S. citizen would be public record. Under existing law, all documentation provided would be confidential and not available for inspection to the public.

This bill would go into effect on July 1, 2025.

Last Updated February 4, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY:  Subcommittee on CRIMINAL JUSTICE

data.author: Glynn (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025, would:

  • Establish automatic voter registration for individuals convicted of an "infamous crime." Under the bill, an individual convicted of such a crime would only be ineligible to vote while incarcerated, on probation, or on parole. Under existing law, for convictions after May 18, 1981, voting rights may be restored only by grant of a petition to a state court. 
  • Specify that eligibility to vote due to an inability or failure to pay any monetary obligations, including, but not limited to, civil or criminal penalties, restitution, court costs, or child support would not limit eligibility to vote.
  • Maintain permanent disenfranchisement for certain offenses committed after July 1, 1986, including: first-degree murder, aggravated rape, treason, or voter fraud.
  • Require the Department of Correction to transfer at least twice a month to the secretary of state a list of newly eligible individuals.

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE.  Substituted on HOUSE floor by S 367

data.author: Rudd (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025, would make several changes to registration, voter list maintenance, and mail voting. The bill would:

  • Require any voter who registers online or at an agency other than the County Election Commission or the Department of Safety to vote in-person in the first election after they register. Current law requires this of voters who register by mail only.
  • Create a new challenge process for voter registrations. Any registered voter in a county would be able to challenge the registration of any other voter in the county. The bill would not limit the number of challenges a single voter could make. Challenges would need to be in writing and state the grounds for the challenge. Challenged voters would receive notice by first-class mail and the County Election Commission would conduct a hearing. The challenger would bear the burden of proof at the hearing. Either party would be able to appeal the commission's ruling to the State Election Commission. Under existing law, there is no challenge process for voter registrations.
  • Change the existing qualification for all voters aged 60+ to be eligible to join the permanent mail voting list to apply only to voters age 65 or over.
  • Change the required cadence of address verification for voter list maintenance from biennially (with an option for counties to do so annually) to quarterly. The bill would also require certain data sources to be updated within 30 days of the end of each quarter.
  • Require counties to conduct "routine list maintenance" monthly
  • Require counties to consult various federal and state data sources monthly to confirm that non-citizens are not registered to vote. Current law requires the use of the data sources, but does not mandate a monthly process.
  • Require driver's licenses and state ID cards issued to non-citizens to include a prominently printed notation "NOT ELIGIBLE TO VOTE"
  • Require all ballots to include a watermark. Current law requires only mail ballots to include a watermark.
  • Require tabulation equipment to read marks created by the voter rather than a QR or barcode.
  • Require tabulation equipment to save a digital image of every ballot scanned. The secretary of state would be required to make all digital images publicly available online no later than the second Friday after Election Day.

Last Updated March 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE & LOCAL GOVERNMENT:  Subcommittee on ELECTIONS & CAMPAIGN FINANCE

data.author: Keisling (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 29, 2025 would:

  • Require that signatures on voter registration applications, applications for mail ballots, completed mail ballots, and related materials contain the full, legal handwritten signature of a registered voter in "legible English alphabetic characters." Under existing law, a signature includes a mark, the name being written near the mark and witnessed, or any other symbol or methodology executed or adopted by a party with intention to authenticate a writing or record, regardless of being witnessed.
  • Require a voter to include one or more initials with the voter's full legal last name in their signature if the voter's registration record or voter registration card uses only one or more initials and the voter's full last name.
  • Require a voter who is physically unable to write their name, to write their individual mark, such as an "X," with the voter's full legal name written in legible English alphabetic characters by a witness who is a registered voter in the same county and who witnessed the voter make their mark. The witness would be required to write their own name next to the voter's name and would be required to verify the identity of the voter using a legal document acceptable to register to vote. Under existing law, there is no similar requirement.
  • Create a new requirement that voters, including those who are homeless, must be responsible for notifying their county election commission and updating their voter registration whenever they move, whether within the county or to another county, state, or territory. Under existing law, a voter can transfer or cancel their registration and the county election commission may transfer or cancel a voter's registration, but there is no requirement for the voter to notify the county election commission when they move.

The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of these new requirements.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated March 6, 2025

data.author: Reneau (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would make changes to the process for voting by mail under the federal Uniformed and Overseas Citizens Absentee Voting Act ("UOCAVA"). Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require that individuals overseas or in the military who are registering to vote or requesting a mail ballot under UOCAVA provide documentary proof of U.S. citizenship, and if the applicant is a civilian overseas voter or a federal-only voter, documentary proof of overseas residence. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to attest to citizenship.
  • Allow the following documents to prove as documentary proof of citizenship:
      • A form of ID that complies with the REAL ID Act of 2005 that indicates the individual is a citizen
      • A valid, unexpired U.S. passport
      • A U.S. military ID card with a U.S. military record of service showing that the applicant's place of birth was in the U.S.
      • A valid government-issued photo ID card issued by a federal, state, or tribal government showing that the individual was born in the U.S. or presented with certain other government documents showing birth place.
  • Require the secretary of state to notify the federal voting assistance program administrator that the proof of citizenship requirement for voter registration must be added to state specific instructions of the federal postcard application for the state
  • Require the county election commission to notify an applicant if their application is missing any required information. The commission would be required to record a report indicating the missing information and how they attempted to reach the individual. 
  • Require the commission to deny an application by writing the reason for rejection and noting an applicant, if the applicant is ineligible to register to vote or did not provide documentary proof of citizenship. A copy of the notification must be retained on file in the county election commission for at least two years.
  • Specify that a UOCAVA mail ballot application not be rejected solely due to a mismatch between the identifying information of the voter on the application and the identifying information on file with the commission. Instead, the voter must be sent a provisional ballot. The voter could cure any discrepancy with the application by submitting an affidavit to the election official along with a copy of documentary proof of citizenship.
  • Require the secretary of state to establish procedures to allow a military or overseas voter or federal-only voter to apply for a mail ballot electronically.
  • Set forth the requirements for the processing of federal postcard applications.
  • Require the secretary of state, in coordination with the county election commissions, to establish a free system where overseas, military, and federal-only voters can determine whether their request or ballot has been processed, whether their ballot has any errors, and whether it was counted. 
  • Set forth requirements for the format of mail ballots.
  • Define "civilian overseas voter" to mean a U.S. citizen who:
    • Was last domiciled in the state
    • Temporarily lives outside of the U.S. or is outside the U.S. when the polls are open
    • Is not registered to vote in another state
    • Maintains a residence in this state
    • Otherwise satisfies the voter eligibility requirements in this state
  • Define "federal-only voter" to mean a U.S. citizen who:
    • Was last domiciled in the state
    • Indefinitely or permanently resides outside of the U.S. and
    • Before establishing a principal residence outside of the U.S., established a principal place of residence in this state.
    • Did not register to vote in another state or establish a principal place of residence in another state 
    • Satisfies the requirements for voter eligibility
  • Define "documentary proof of overseas residence" as one of the following documents:
    • A copy of a current, valid lease or title to a residence not in the U.S.
    • A copy of financial records bearing the name and address of the applicant
    • A document or official record of a foreign government bearing the name and overseas address of the applicant

Last Updated March 19, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on STATE & LOCAL GOVERNMENT:  Subcommittee on ELECTIONS & CAMPAIGN FINANCE

data.author: Fritts (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 4, 2025, would:

  • Require voters to present ID that includes the voter's name, photograph, and residential address when voting. Under existing law, the ID does not need to include the voter's residential address. The bill would leave in place the existing law allowing a voter to show more than one type of documentation to fulfill all of the requirements. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Limit the acceptable forms of ID to those forms of identification that must:
    • Have been obtained with documented proof of citizenship in the U.S.
    • Include the voter's photograph, current residential address, and expiration date
    • Indicate that the individual meets eligibility requirements for voting, including citizenship of the U.S.
    • Accommodate for the "permanently disabled" and include a process for permanent identification 
    • Not be an identification card issued to a student by an institution of higher learning. Under existing law, a voter can provide: 1) a Tennessee driver's license; 2) an ID card issued by the state of Tennessee that contains a photo; 3) a valid U.S. passport; 4) a valid employee photo ID card issued by the state of Tennessee or the U.S.; 5) a valid U.S. military ID that contains a photo; 6) an employee ID card for retired state employees.
  • Require the registrar to compare a voter's address from the poll book with the address on the voter's permanent registration record. If the voter's address has changed or their registration is inactive, the registrar would be required to send a confirmation notice to the voter as required under existing law.
  • Require the registrar to initial the application, note the date of the application, the number on the voter's ballot application, and the primary or general election in which the voter applied to vote upon verification of evidence of identification.
  • Require the registrar to print the voter's name on the application if their signature is illegible. 
  • Require the state coordinator of elections, in concurrence with the secretary of state, to provide public access to information regarding the type of ID needed to vote and where to obtain ID.
  • Require that provisional ballots be paper and be marked either by hand or a ballot-marking device. Under existing law, provisional ballots can be cast utilizing punchcard or optiscan technology.

Last Updated March 25, 2025

status_actions.summary: In HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY:  Subcommittee on CRIMINAL JUSTICE

data.author: Glynn (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 6, 2025, would:

  • Change the process for restoration of voting rights from an application-based process to an automatic process. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the department of corrections or the appropriate supervising authority to provide an individual convicted of an infamous crime with a certificate of final discharge upon the completion of their sentence. The individual would be required to provide that certificate to the administrator of elections to be eligible to vote. Under existing law, an individual convicted of an infamous crime must petition a court to have their rights of citizenship restored upon release from incarceration and the court has discretion on whether to grant it. After having their rights restored, the individual can request a certificate of restoration, which must be provided to them if they meet the requirements. That certificate must be provided to an elections official in order to register to vote. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement. 
  • Repeal the statute requiring a voter's registration to be purged upon conviction of an infamous crime and would instead require that the voter's registration be placed in suspended status. The bill would create logistical changes because of this requirement and specifically would:
    • Require the administrator of elections to place a voter in suspended status upon receiving information that a voter has been convicted of an infamous crime. The administrator would be required to maintain a list of suspended voter registrations and a list of restored voter registrations. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
    • Require the state coordinator of elections to formulate a uniform procedure for automatically verifying the registration eligibility of any individual convicted of an infamous crime and updating the appropriate administrator of elections of any changes to the suspended voter registration list. Upon receiving verification of an individual's eligibility to register, the administrator must allow the individual to become a registered voter or reactivate their registration.
  • Repeal the statute providing that if an individual petitioned for restoration after receiving a pardon, that the court would not have the authority or jurisdiction to alter, delete, or render void special conditions pertaining to the right of suffrage that may be contained in the pardon.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to send a certified letter and voter registration application on or before January 1, 2026, to the last known address of each individual who had a felony conviction after January 14, 1973 but prior to May 18, 198, informing them that they are eligible to vote, never lost the right to vote in the state, and do not need to have their rights restored. Under existing law, although individuals convicted of those offenses and during those time frames are not ineligible to vote, there is no requirement that they be contacted by the coordinator of elections.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to develop and implement a program to educate attorneys, judges, election officials, appropriate staff under the department of correction, and members of the public about the restoration of voting rights.
  • Require the department of correction to provide an individual being released from incarceration with the following:
    • A certificate of final discharge
    • Written information on how to register to vote
    • The amount of restitution owed
    • The amount of court costs owed
    • The amount of child support owed. Under existing law, there is no similar requirement.


Last Updated April 9, 2025

data.author: McKenzie (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would:

  • Allow individuals who are not current in child support obligations to apply for a voter registration card and have their right to vote provisionally restored if they enter into a payment plan to become current in all unpaid child support obligations. Under existing law, an individual who is not current in support obligations is not eligible to apply for a voter registration card and to have the right to vote restored.
  • Require the court that provisionally restored voting rights to an individual to revoke that individual's right to vote if that individual willfully fails to adhere to the terms of a child support payment plan. Prior to revoking that individual's right to vote, the court would be required to provide them with counsel, offer the individual an opportunity to be heard, and allow them to submit proof of their inability to pay.
  • Authorize the secretary of state, in consultation with the Department of Human Services and the administrative office of the courts, to create rules to effectuate this bill.

data.author: Akbari (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 26, 2026, would make requirements related to legal financial obligations less strict for restoration of voting rights after disenfranchisement for an individual convicted of an "infamous crime" after May 18, 1981. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Repeal the requirement that an individual pay all court costs before being eligible for restoration.
  • Modify requirements related to child support by mandating that individuals be compliant with all current child support orders for the previous 12 months, including orders related to child support arrearages, to qualify for restoration. Existing law requires that individuals be "current" in all child support obligations.

A previous version of the bill contained a provision that was amended out of this version. That provision would have:

  • Eliminated permanent disenfranchisement for certain offenses and instead permitted individuals convicted of those offenses to be eligible to register and vote three years after their sentence completion date and made conforming changes. Current law makes these individuals permanently ineligible to vote. This change would have applied to all permanently disqualifying offenses, which includes:
    • any individual convicted after July 1, 1986, of voter fraud, treason, first degree murder, or aggravated rape
    • any individual convicted after July 1, 1996, of any degree of murder or rape; and
    • any individual convicted after July 1, 2006, of certain offenses against administration of government and certain sexual offenses.

Last Updated March 31, 2026

data.author: Hensley (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025, creates certain requirements for signatures on voter registration and mail voting forms. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require voter signatures to be the full, legal handwritten signature of the signer in legible English alphabetic characters and allow a signer to use initials in lieu of a full name if doing so would match the registration record. Existing law has no analogous requirements. The bill does not specify what would happen to forms submitted with signatures that did not conform with these requirements.
  • Require voters who are unable to sign their names due to a disability have their making a mark on the relevant documentation witnessed. Under existing law, voters who are unable to sign their names can produce a mark without the involvement of a witness.

Last Updated February 24, 2025

data.author: Hensley (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025 would require individuals registering to vote to provide proof of citizenship, make changes to voter list maintenance procedures, and would make changes to the appeals process for voters flagged as non-citizens. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require an applicant to provide an original or a true and correct copy of a birth certificate, a U.S. passport, a U.S. naturalization document, or any document or method of citizenship established by federal law when registering to vote. Under existing law, a voter only needs to check a box stating that they are a citizen.
  • Require an individual registering to vote to attest under penalty of perjury that they meet the requirements for voter eligibility.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to compare the statewide voter registration database with the department of safety database to ensure non-citizens are not registered to vote on a monthly basis. Existing law does not articulate how often this comparison must be done.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to ensure that the citizenship of each registered voter is verified before early voting begins for each election. If the citizenship of an individual who has registered to vote prior to the registration deadline has not been verified, the individual would be allowed to vote a provisional ballot which would not be counted until their citizenship was verified. 
  • Require the coordinator of elections to notify both the state election commission and the county election commission on a monthly basis if sufficient evidence exists that a particular registered voter is not a citizen. Under existing law, the coordinator of elections is only required to notify the county election commission and no timeframe is specified. 
  • Require the county election commission to send notice to any voter flagged as not being a citizen within five business days of receiving notice from the coordinator of elections. The county election commission would be required to send the notice to the address at which the voter is registered by certified mail with return receipt requested. If the county election commission does not receive the return receipt within five business days after the postmark date of the notice, the voter is deemed to have received the notice at such time. Under existing law, the county commission is required to send the notice but no timeframe is specified.
  • Require that if a notice sent by the county election commission is returned as undeliverable, the administrator of elections of the county where the individual is registered would be required to purge the voter from the voter registration database but would be required to maintain the individual's registration and voting history. Under existing law, a voter can be purged from the list for failing to respond to the notice within 30 days of receipt of the notice.
  • Make changes to the appeals process for a voter who wishes to appeal the decision to purge their registration. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Allow a voter who has received notice that they did not provide proper proof of citizenship to appeal to the state election commission no later than 14 days after the county election commission has made their decision. The commission would be prohibited from purging the registration during the appeals process.
    • Require the voter to submit originals or true and correct copies of documentation of citizenship to the state election commission with the voter's notice of appeal.
    • Require the state election commission to conduct a hearing at which the voter must appear in person or by video. The state election commission would be required to enter an order determining whether the individual is a U.S. citizen or not and qualified to vote. If an individual is determined not to be a citizen, that finding would be reported to the district attorney.The determination would be final and subject only to judicial review. Under existing law, an individual who is unable to provide documentation to show proof of citizenship is entitled to appeal to the state election commission and to submit proof of citizenship in person or in writing. The commission is required to conduct a hearing and to forward a copy of its decision to the administrator where the voter lives. 
    • Require that a finding that a person who registered to vote is not a U.S. citizen would be public record. Under existing law, all documentation provided would be confidential and not available for inspection to the public.

This bill would go into effect on July 1, 2025.

Last Updated March 5, 2025

status_actions.summary: Public Chaptered.  Chapter No. 534

data.author: Briggs (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on February 23, 2026, no longer includes provisions tracked by Voting Rights Lab.


A previous version of the bill included provisions that were amended out of the current texts. Specifically, the bill would have:

  • Required any voter who registers online or at an agency other than the County Election Commission or the Department of Safety to vote in person in the first election after they register. Current law requires this of voters who register by mail only.
  • Created a new challenge process for voter registrations. Any registered voter in a county would be able to challenge the registration of any other voter in the county. The bill would not limit the number of challenges a single voter can make. Challenges would have needed to be in writing and state the grounds for the challenge. Challenged voters would have received notice by first-class mail and the County Election Commission would conduct a hearing. The challenger would have beared the burden of proof at the hearing. Either party would have been able to appeal the commission's ruling to the State Election Commission.
  • Changed the existing qualification for all voters age 60+ to be eligible to vote by absentee ballot to apply only to voters age 65 or over.
  • Changed the required cadence of address verification for voter list maintenance from biennially (with an option for counties to do so annually) to quarterly. The bill would have also required certain data sources to be updated within 30 days of the end of each quarter.
  • Required counties to conduct "routine list maintenance" monthly.
  • Required counties to consult various federal and state data sources monthly to confirm that non-citizens are not registered to vote. Current law requires the use of the data sources, but does not mandate a monthly process.
  • Required driver's licenses and state ID cards issued to non-citizens to include a prominently printed notation "NOT ELIGIBLE TO VOTE"
  • Required all ballots to include a watermark. Current law requires only absentee ballots to include a watermark.
  • Required tabulation equipment to read marks created by the voter rather than a QR or barcode.
  • Required tabulation equipment to save a digital image of every ballot scanned. The secretary of state would have been required to make all digital images publicly available online no later than the second Friday after Election Day.

Last Updated February 25, 2026

data.author: Bowling (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 5, 2025, would modify UOCAVA voting procedures, including creating new requirements for documentary proof of citizenship for first-time registrant voters, creating a UOCAVA ballot witness requirement, and expressly providing for notice of application deficiencies and ballot tracking, among other changes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Create a new documentary proof of citizenship requirement for UOCAVA voters who are not already registered when making a mail ballot request ("temporary registration" applicants). Existing law requires all UOCAVA ballot applicants to attest to citizenship and other voting qualifications, but has no documentation requirements. Under the bill, the following documents would be accepted as proof of citizenship:
    • REAL ID document that indicates U.S. citizenship
    • Valid, unexpired U.S. passport
    • U.S. military ID with military record of service showing U.S. as place of birth
    • Valid ID issued by federal, state, or tribal government showing U.S. as place of birth
    • Other form of government-issued ID with additional specified documentation showing U.S. as place of birth
  • Create a new documentary proof of overseas residence requirement for civilian overseas voters and federal-only ballot voters. Existing law has no comparable documentation requirement. The bill specifies that accepted documentation would include:
    • Copy of a current, valid lease or title to a residence not located in the United States, bearing the name and overseas address of the applicant
    • Copy of financial records, such as a bank account, tax return, or other record from a financial agency or institution bearing the name and overseas address of the applicant
    • Document or official record of a foreign government bearing the name and overseas address of the applicant
  • Mandate changes to the state-specific instructions for the federal postcard application, reflecting new documentary proof of citizenship rules.
  • Create a new affidavit and witness requirement for UOCAVA ballots. Existing law has no comparable requirements. The bill would require voters to sign an affidavit attesting to eligibility for UOCAVA voting, witnessed by two individuals aged 18 or older.  
  • Create new procedures related to notice and cure of UOCAVA application deficiencies. Existing law has no express requirements related to cure for UOCAVA applications with missing information. The bill would:
    • Require counties to notify UOCAVA applicants if their ballot application is missing a Tennessee residency address, overseas/current mailing address, voter ID, or documentary proof of citizenship. Officials would be required to contact the individual by email or phone if possible, and record attempts at contact, and the bill would expressly prohibit acceptance of an application lacking necessary documentation of citizenship and/or overseas residence.
    • For applications from individuals who are ineligible or have not provided documentary proof of citizenship, require counties to reject the application and notify the applicant of the grounds, and preserve records of notice for two years. 
    • Specify that a UOCAVA mail ballot application may not be rejected solely due to a mismatch between the identifying information of the voter on the application and the identifying information on file. Instead, the bill would require officials to provide the voter a provisional ballot that would be counted if (1) the ballot is received by the close of polls on Election Day, and (2) the voter provides an affidavit and necessary documentation before the close of the provisional ballot verification period. If the provisional ballot was rejected, the bill would require officials to notify the voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to establish procedures to allow a military or overseas voter or federal-only voter to apply for a mail ballot electronically.
  • Specify requirements for the processing of federal postcard applications, including delivery of a ballot to voters by email.
  • Require the secretary of state, in coordination with the county election commissions, to establish a free ballot tracking system for UOCAVA voters to determine the status of their ballot request and ballot return, any ballot deficiencies, and whether their ballot was counted. The state's existing ballot tracking system includes information on when a UOCAVA ballot is received, but statutory law does not expressly require additional information. 
  • Expressly prohibit electronic UOCAVA ballot return. 
  • Require recording of ballot counts by category of UOCAVA voter, and public reporting and access to data. 
  • Establish specific definitions for UOCAVA voters in the state. Existing statutory law has no express definitions for these categories. The bill would:
    • Define "civilian overseas voter" as U.S. citizen who:
      • Was last domiciled in the state
      • Temporarily lives outside of the U.S. or is outside the U.S. when the polls are open
      • Is not registered to vote in another state
      • Maintains a residence in the state
      • Otherwise satisfies the voter eligibility requirements in the state
    • Define "federal-only voter" as a U.S. citizen who:
      • Was last domiciled in the state
      • Indefinitely or permanently resides outside of the U.S.
      • Before establishing a principal residence outside of the U.S., established a principal place of residence in the state
      • Did not register to vote in another state or establish a principal place of residence in another state 
      • Satisfies the requirements for voter eligibility
    • Define a "military voter" as a U.S. citizen who is:
      • A member of the (1) Active or reserve components of the U.S. military; (b) National guard or the Tennessee National Guard; or (c) Merchant marine of the United States, and their spouse
      • Maintains a residence in the state
      • Otherwise satisfies the voter eligibility requirements in the state

Last Updated August 5, 2025
