data.author: Oliver (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 6, 2025, would:

  • Change the process for restoration of voting rights from an application-based process to an automatic process. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the department of corrections or the appropriate supervising authority to provide an individual convicted of an infamous crime with a certificate of final discharge upon the completion of their sentence. The individual would be required to provide that certificate to the administrator of elections to be eligible to vote. Under existing law, an individual convicted of an infamous crime must petition a court to have their rights of citizenship restored upon release from incarceration and the court has discretion on whether to grant it. After having their rights restored, the individual can request a certificate of restoration, which must be provided to them if they meet the requirements. That certificate must be provided to an elections official in order to register to vote. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement. 
  • Repeal the statute requiring a voter's registration to be purged upon conviction of an infamous crime and would instead require that the voter's registration be placed in suspended status. The bill would create logistical changes because of this requirement and specifically would:
    • Require the administrator of elections to place a voter in suspended status upon receiving information that a voter has been convicted of an infamous crime. The administrator would be required to maintain a list of suspended voter registrations and a list of restored voter registrations. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
    • Require the state coordinator of elections to formulate a uniform procedure for automatically verifying the registration eligibility of any individual convicted of an infamous crime and updating the appropriate administrator of elections of any changes to the suspended voter registration list. Upon receiving verification of an individual's eligibility to register, the administrator must allow the individual to become a registered voter or reactivate their registration.
  • Repeal the statute providing that if an individual petitioned for restoration after receiving a pardon, that the court would not have the authority or jurisdiction to alter, delete, or render void special conditions pertaining to the right of suffrage that may be contained in the pardon.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to send a certified letter and voter registration application on or before January 1, 2026, to the last known address of each individual who had a felony conviction after January 14, 1973 but prior to May 18, 198, informing them that they are eligible to vote, never lost the right to vote in the state, and do not need to have their rights restored. Under existing law, although individuals convicted of those offenses and during those time frames are not ineligible to vote, there is no requirement that they be contacted by the coordinator of elections.
  • Require the coordinator of elections to develop and implement a program to educate attorneys, judges, election officials, appropriate staff under the department of correction, and members of the public about the restoration of voting rights.
  • Require the department of correction to provide an individual being released from incarceration with the following:
    • A certificate of final discharge
    • Written information on how to register to vote
    • The amount of restitution owed
    • The amount of court costs owed
    • The amount of child support owed. Under existing law, there is no similar requirement.

Last Updated April 9, 2025

This bill, as introduced on February 2, 2026, would:

  • Repeal provisions allowing counties, with approval from the state, to establish countywide "convenient voting centers" in lieu of precinct-based polling places.
  • Require early voting satellite locations established by a county election commission to be limited to serving no more than four precincts and to be evenly distributed throughout the county to ensure geographic accessibility. Existing law authorizes early voting satellite locations but does not limit the number of precincts served by a location or require even geographic distribution.
  • Clarify that voting at satellite locations must end at the close of the early voting period and reaffirm that, on Election Day, voters may vote only in the precinct in which they reside. Existing law already requires Election Day voting to occur in a voter’s assigned precinct.

status_actions.summary: Chaptered.  Chapter No. 408

data.author: Maloy (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 25, 2026, creates a documentary proof of citizenship requirement for Utah voters. Specifically, the bill:

  • Requires voters to present documentary proof of citizenship, either at the time of registration to vote or before voting, to be eligible to vote for any office or proposition other than federal offices. Specifically, the bill:
    • Bifurcates elections by creating a "standard ballot," on which all offices and propositions would appear, and a "federal ballot" on which only federal offices would appear.
    • Requires voters who have not presented documentary proof of citizenship to vote a federal ballot. Specifically, the bill:
      • Requires both new registrants and existing registrants who have not been proven to be citizens to vote only a federal ballot until their citizenship is proven.
      • Requires clerks to inform registered voters who have not provided such proof that they will only be eligible to vote a federal ballot until they do so.
      • Permits a voter to view their status on the electronic election information system maintained by the state.
    • Defines documentary proof of citizenship as one of the following:
      • a Utah driver license number or state ID card number that verifies United States citizenship
      • a birth certificate that verifies U.S. citizenship
      • a U.S. passport
      • an alien registration number that verifies U.S. citizenship
      • naturalization documents
      • a Bureau of Indian Affairs card number, tribal treaty card number, or tribal enrollment number
      • a certificate of degree of Indian blood or a Bureau of Indian Affairs affidavit of birth
      • verification of citizenship through the federal SAVE database
      • any other document or method of proving citizenship that is established in accordance with federal law
    • Modifies voter registration forms to require applicants to specify whether they are intending to register to vote only on federal races. If so, the applicant is not required to provide documentary proof of citizenship.
    • Treats any proof of citizenship submitted by a voter as private.
  • Requires election officers, including the lieutenant governor, to review voter registration records independently to determine whether any noncitizens are registered to vote. Specifically, the bill:
    • Permits the officer to use any lawful tools at their disposal to make the determination.
    • Prohibits an officer from requiring an individual who registered to vote only on federal races to provide proof of citizenship.
    • Provides for a registered voter identified as a noncitizen under this process to be notified and given an opportunity to dispute the determination.
    • Requires a person identified as a noncitizen under this process to be removed from the rolls unless the person refutes the determination within 30 days.
    • Requires a person identified as a noncitizen to vote provisionally until the question of their citizenship is resolved.
  • Makes conforming changes throughout statute.

Last Updated March 30, 2026

data.author: Wilcox (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 24, 2026:

  • Requires faster removal of deceased voters. Under previous law, if the lieutenant governor provides data on deceased voters from the U.S. Social Security Administration, the county clerk was required to remove the voter from the rolls within ten business days. Under the bill, this task must now be completed within five business days.
  • Changes the notice requirement for early voting polling locations. Under previous law, such notice must be given on the election authority's website, at the physical address of the election authority's office, and at a specified mailing address and telephone number. Under the bill, such notice must be given in compliance with existing Utah law for "Class A" notice, which requires public notice on the Utah Public Notice website, the election authority's website, and at a public location reasonably likely to be seen by residents of the affected area.

The bill also contains numerous provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.


A previous version of this bill would have required the lieutenant governor to create a plan transferring some of their responsibilities in the event of a possible conflict of interest. Those provisions were amended out of the bill on February 26, 2026.

Last Updated March 26, 2026

status_actions.summary: Enacting clause struck

data.author: Burton (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 27, 2026, would:

  • Change the acceptable methods for returning a ballot that is mailed to the voter. Under existing law, a voter who receives a mail ballot may return the ballot by mail, by returning it to an Election Day voting center, or placing it in a drop box. Under the bill, a voter would only be permitted to return their ballot by mail if the ballot is sealed in a "mail-in return envelope." All voters would receive such envelopes with their mail ballots until 2029, but beginning in that year would have to specifically request one. UOCAVA voters would be an exception to the rule that a mail ballot must be returned in a mail-in return envelope.
  • Require a voter who is returning a ballot to a drop box to do so only when the drop box is attended by two or more poll workers. The voter would be required to present their voter ID, and their ballot could only be placed in the drop box by the poll worker if the poll worker is satisfied with the voter's identification.
  • Increase the number of drop boxes required for returning ballots. Existing law requires only one drop box per municipality, with additional drop boxes at an election official's discretion. The bill would:
    • Require a minimum of one drop box for every 30,000 voters in a jurisdiction, disbursed through different locations, though officials could open additional drop boxes as needed to avoid long lines.
    • Specify certain minimum hours of operation for the drop boxes, beginning with 8 hours of operation on the 8th day before the election and continuing through the day of the election, when drop boxes would be available from 7 a.m to 8 p.m. Drop boxes could be open for more than the minimum number of hours, but must be staffed at all times.
  • Require a voter who returns their ballot to a polling place to vote provisionally if they do not present voter ID. The voter's ballot would be counted only if they present identification to an election officer no later than the day before the canvass.
  • Remove a utility bill, bank statement, or other financial account statement as alternatives to providing photo ID for voter identification. The existing law permitting their use is set to expire in 2029.
  • Beginning in 2029 when the state ends its universal mail balloting system, require a voter who requests a mail ballot to specify whether they intend to return the ballot by mail or to a polling place or drop box.
  • Create a process under which a Division of Wildlife Resources office would provide voter registration forms and information to persons seeking a hunting or fishing license.

Last Updated March 3, 2026

data.author: Johnson (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 18, 2026:

  • Requires voters who wish to have their registration records withheld to submit an at-risk designation request form, along with proof that they meet one of the qualifications for such designation under existing law. Submission of such a form is not required under previous law. All voters currently designated as at-risk will have their registrations reclassified as public unless they complete the form.
  • Modifies the voter registration form to permit a voter to consent to the disclosure of their email or phone number to the political party they are affiliating with.
  • Permits the public disclosure of the voter identification number and date of signing of an at-risk voter with a private voter registration record who signs certain petitions. The bill modifies numerous petition forms accordingly to advise voters with private voter registration records that signing the petition might result in the public disclosure of their voter identification number and the date they signed the petition.
  • Requires faster removal of deceased voters from the rolls. Under previous law, if the lieutenant governor provided data on deceased voters from the U.S. Social Security Administration to a county clerk, the clerk was required to remove the voter from the rolls within ten business days. Under the bill, this task now must be completed within five business days.

Last Updated March 23, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chaptered.  Chapter No. 271

data.author: McKell (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 23, 2026:

  • Removes a utility bill, bank statement, or other financial account statement as alternatives to providing photo ID for voter identification. The previous law permitting their use was already set to expire in 2029.
  • Requires verification of the voter's signature on their mail ballot return envelope. Previous state law required verification of the signature only if the voter did not provide the last four digits of their Utah driver license number, Utah state identification card number, or social security number on the envelope.
  • Requires the lieutenant governor, the state's chief election official, to create a conflict of interest plan. The plan must identify any decisions or actions the lieutenant governor may be required to perform in their official capacity that could create an actual or possible conflict of interest due to their proximity to their candidacy for an office, and specify the actions that would be taken to mitigate the conflict.
  • Specifies that an election officer may not take any action in their official capacity that shows partiality in favor of or against a candidate or ballot measure, nor may they solicit or accept a donation from an employee or volunteer over whom they have authority.


The bill also contains numerous provisions not tracked by Voting Rights Lab, mostly concerning campaign finance or the filling of vacancies in office.


A previous version of this bill contained provisions that would have imposed a documentary proof of citizenship requirement on voter registration applicants, among other changes. Those provisions were amended out of the bill on February 17, 2026.

Last Updated March 25, 2026

data.bill_name: Removal of Electoral Board Members and Registrars

status_actions.summary: Acts of Assembly.  Chapter No. 1032

data.author: Simon (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 22, 2026, grants direct authority to the State Board of Elections and local electoral boards to remove board members and general registrars and establishes removal standards and procedures. Specifically, the bill:

  • Grants new authority to the State Board of Elections to remove any member of a local electoral board or a general registrar by a two-thirds majority vote following a public hearing, subject to judicial review only as required under the Administrative Process Act. Prior law allowed the State Board to initiate removal proceedings in court, but did not grant the Board itself the authority to remove officials.
  • Specifies the scope of grounds for removal. The bill replaces the broad prior standard of failing to "discharge the duties of his office in accordance with law" with a requirement of either: (1) neglect of a clear, ministerial duty, misuse of office, or incompetence that has a material adverse effect on the conduct of the office, or (2) an unambiguous indication of a future refusal or failure to carry out duties that would likely have a material adverse effect.
  • Requires a public hearing before the State Board of Elections can initiate court proceedings for the removal of a member of a local electoral board or a general registrar under their existing statutory authority.
  • Grants local election boards authority to directly remove general registrars by unanimous vote based on the grounds established for removal by the State Board following a public hearing. Prior law only allowed local boards to initiate removal proceedings in court.
  • Establishes a new appeal process for general registrars removed by their local electoral board. The bill grants the registrar the right to file an appeal with the State Board within three business days following a vote by their local board. The State Board must hold a public hearing and may reverse the local board's removal decision with a two-thirds majority vote. The removed registrar remains in office until the State Board's final decision, unless they fail to file an appeal by the deadline.

Last Updated May 15, 2026

This bill would eliminate certain methods an individual's voter registration could be challenged and would require any petition of challenge in a circuit court to be filed no later than 60 days before the election. status_actions.summary: Acts of Assembly.  Chapter No. 1081

data.author: Price (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 22, would:

  • Establish automatic restoration of voting rights for certain disenfranchised individuals upon release from incarceration. Under existing law, any individual convicted of a felony is disenfranchised and can only have voting rights restored by seeking a pardon from the Governor.
  • Define incarceration and explicitly excludes any period of parole, probation, post-release supervision, or confinement in a community correctional facility.
  • Clarify routine data sharing information and scheduling of data sharing between the Department of Elections and the Department of State Police.
  • Establish a process for those with properly restored voting rights but the Virginia voter registration system erroneously lists them as unqualified due to a felony conviction.
  • Require all local jails and regional jails to provide voter registration information to those released from incarceration.
  • Clarify mental incapacity as it relates to voting and adjudication of individuals disqualified to vote by an appropriate court, changing the standard to individuals who lack the capacity to understand voting.
  • Make conforming changes in the voter registration application.


This bill shall only become effective if the Virginia Constitution is amended to permit automatic rights restoration.

Last Updated April 24, 2026

data.author: Price (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on May 14, 2026, will make multiple changes to Virginia's list maintenance processes.


This bill:

  • Changes how the Department of Elections uses the Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) program. The bill will require the Department to check citizenship on a case by case basis and will prohibit an individual's registration being cancelled or rejected solely based on SAVE information. Under prior practice, the Department of Elections would conduct batch checks using the SAVE program.
  • Requires the Department of Elections to annually review data sources for list maintenance for accuracy and reliability.
  • Changes the identifying information state agencies, such as Vital Records, and Department of State Police, will be required to send to the Department of Elections.
  • Prohibits the Department from using data in voter registration lists from other states that do not use unique identifiers for voters.
  • Clarifies the process conducted by general registrars upon cancelling the registration of certain voters determined to be disqualified.

This bill will be effective July 1, 2026.

Last Updated May 26, 2026

status_actions.summary: Acts of Assembly.  Chapter No. 442

data.author: Tran (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 8, would establish stricter and more specific criteria related to disqualification for voting due to mental incapacity. Existing constitutional law provides that, "no person adjudicated to be mentally incompetent shall be qualified to vote until his competency has been reestablished," but state law does not otherwise specify the legal standard for incompetence. The bill would:

  • Prohibit individuals from being disqualified for voting due to mental incompetence unless a court makes a specific finding by clear and convincing evidence that the person is unable to understand the act of voting. In making this determination, the court would be prohibited from accepting the determination of a guardian ad litem unless a showing was made of reasonable accommodation provided to the individual. The bill would also prohibit courts from relying on results from, or refusal to comply with, evaluations of ability to read or write, educational achievement or knowledge of any subject, or moral character. 
  • Create a right of petition for any individual deemed disqualified before July 1, 2026, for a new determination of eligibility under the new criteria. For any individual who does not petition for review, the bill would require reconsideration at the next periodic review hearing for their status.
  • Make various conforming changes to reflect the new requirements.

Last Updated April 13, 2026

  • Modify language regarding mental capacity to vote by requiring a specific judicial finding that an individual lacks the capacity to understand the act of voting before being disenfranchised. Under existing law, all individuals "adjudicated to be mentally incompetent" are disenfranchised.

This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would require all in-person and absentee voters, to provide a copy of photo ID issued based on documented proof of U.S. citizenship. The bill does not provide an explicit list of the documents deemed acceptable as proof of citizenship.


The bill would require the presented ID to include the voter's photo, full legal name, current residential address. If the voter does not present satisfactory ID, the voter would receive a provisional ballot.


The bill would require absentee voters to provide either a copy of satisfactory voter ID or their full Social Security number on the ballot return envelope.


The bill would also require the Commissioner of Motor Vehicles to to provide a free nondriver ID card to all eligible voters upon presentation of documented proof of U.S. citizenship and proof of residency. The bill would require these free IDs to indicate the holder's U.S. citizenship.

data.author: Tagliavia (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2026, would require all individuals registering to vote to provide documentary proof of citizenship as part of the application process. The bill would also require election officials to verify the citizenship status of currently registered voters using federal data.


The bill would:

  • Require all voter registration applications submitted to a town clerk to include one of the following as proof of U.S. citizenship:
    • A U.S. birth certificate or equivalent;
    • a valid U.S. passport;
    • a U.S. certificate of naturalization or citizenship; or
    • any other document issued by a government agency that verifies U.S. citizenship.
  • Require individuals registering to vote at the same time they conduct a transaction with the Department of Motor Vehicles to provide a document from the list above as proof of U.S. citizenship. The Department will record the proof on the application form and, where feasible, verify the applicant's citizenship status using the Systematic Alien Verification for Entitlements (SAVE) Program;
  • Require town clerks to use the SAVE Program to verify the citizenship status of registered voters and remove any individuals confirmed to be noncitizens.
  • Require the Secretary of State, no later than January 1, 2027, to enter into a memorandum of agreement with the U.S. Department of Homeland Security to access and use the SAVE Program for verifying citizenship status during voter registration and as part of the state's voter list maintenance program.

Last Updated January 14, 2026

data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on May 14, 2026, would enact the "Vermont Voter Protections Act of 2026." The bill would:

  • Prohibit any voting qualification, standard, practice, or procedure that results in denial or abridgment of a citizen's right to vote based on race, color, or membership in a language minority group. The bill would authorize the Attorney General to bring a civil action to enforce its prohibitions;
  • Create criminal offenses related to intimidation of election officers and voters. The criminal offenses would be punishable by up to two years of imprisonment;
  • Authorize political candidates to use campaign funds to pay for security or care of a dependent family member, protective detail, or cybersecurity when such expenses are incurred as a direct result of campaign activity.
  • Require individuals obtaining publicly-available municipal voter lists to affirm that they will not use the list for commercial purposes or provide the information to a foreign government. Existing law only requires this affirmation from individuals obtaining a statewide voter list.

An earlier version of the bill would have created a misdemeanor for communicating false information to a voter, but that provision was amended out of the bill on April 29, 2026.

data.bill_name: Voting in Jails and State Hospitals

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Simmons (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 7, 2025, would require programs to be implemented in every county to assist individuals incarcerated in jails or state hospitals with registering to vote, voting, and voter education. Under existing law, there is no program to assist incarcerated individuals with registering or voting while incarcerated. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require each county auditor's office to designate one employee as the jail voting coordinator for the county and the state hospital voting coordinator for the county, if applicable.
  • Require each jail to designate one employee as the jail voting coordinator for the jail and would require each state hospital to designate one employee as the state hospital voting coordinator.
  • Require each county auditor, along with the jail voting coordinator, state hospital voting coordinator, and office of the secretary of state, to create a voting plan for each jail and state hospital in the county for adoption by the county auditor and jail or state hospital by January 1, 2026. The plans must be reviewed and readopted at least every two years.
  • Require that the voting plan detail how it would require the jail or state hospital to help incarcerated individuals to:
    • Register to vote or update their voter registration information
    • Access proof of their identification or residency as may be necessary to register to vote
    • Access information and materials necessary for voting rights restoration
    • Access nonpartisan information about candidates
    • Access writing materials to fill out ballots
    • Complete their ballots in private
  • Require that the voting plan detail how it would require the jail or state hospital and the county auditor to:
    • Provide visible information regarding registering to vote and accessing a ballot for people who are incarcerated
    • Ensure that all incarcerated individuals are made aware of key dates for registering to vote and returning a ballot
    • Establish a process by which voter registration and ballot return data is collected
    • Establish a process by which voting related requests and complaints may be documented and considered
    • Accommodate individuals with disabilities to register to vote and fill out ballots
    • Provide training and resources to help employees of jails and state hospitals understand how to support voters with disabilities
  • Require jails and state hospitals to permit election officials from the county auditor's office to enter the jail or state hospital at least 30 days before every election in order to provide voter registration outreach and education. 
  • Require jails and state hospitals to provide registration information to all individuals upon their release from custody.
  • Require jails and state hospitals to document all voting-related requests and complaints made by people who are incarcerated that are addressed to the county auditor as provided in the jointly created voting plan
  • Allow the attorney general to investigate and bring an action against a county, jail, or state hospital for violations of this bill beginning July 31, 2030.
  • Require the secretary of state, subject to the availability of funds, to enter into an agreement with the University of Washington Evans school of public policy and governance for the purpose of examining the processes associated with providing voter registration, voting materials, and voting assistance to incarcerated individuals. The school would be required to submit a final report to the governor, secretary of state, and the appropriate committees of the legislature by June 30, 2026.

A previous version of this bill contained a provision that was amended out. That provision would have:

  • Required the jail, state hospital, or county auditor to provide equal access to candidates who request to campaign in jails or hospitals.

Last Updated April 3, 2025

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution

data.author: Simmons (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2025, would narrow the list of offenses for which an individual can be disenfranchised and would enfranchise voters with felony convictions while they are incarcerated and voters who have been convicted of felony crimes punishable by death. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow individuals incarcerated for a felony conviction to vote while incarcerated. The bill would repeal the statute provision disenfranchising individuals for a felony conviction and automatically restore the right to vote for those individuals. Under existing law, an individual convicted of a felony loses the right to vote while incarcerated. Once an individual is released from custody, their right to vote is automatically restored and they are eligible to register to vote. The bill would make other conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect this new requirement.
  • Enfranchise eligible voters who previously lost the right to vote due to a felony conviction punishable by death. The bill would revoke the statute provision disenfranchising voters convicted of a felony conviction punishable by death and would automatically restore the right to vote for those individuals so long as they are not currently incarcerated. Those individuals would need to re-register prior to voting. Under existing law, an individual loses the right to vote when convicted of a state or federal crime punishable by death. The bill would make other conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect this new requirement.


Last Updated January 22, 2025

data.bill_location: House State Government and Tribal Relations Committee

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Doglio (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 13, 2025, would make it more difficult for a voter to be removed from voter lists and would create additional requirements for individuals challenging a voter's registration. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require the county auditor or secretary of state to confirm that a voter has died before their registration is cancelled. Under existing law, any voter may sign a statement, subject to penalties of perjury, to the effect that to their personal knowledge, another registered voter is deceased. Upon receipt of that signed statement, the county auditor or secretary of state must cancel the registration.
  • Require that a voter's registration only be canceled if the county recorder receives information indicating that the inactive voter has moved out of state and has remained inactive for two federal general elections. The bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect this new requirement. Under existing law, the county auditor must cancel an inactive voter registration when upon receiving information indicating that the voter has moved out of state or died.
  • Require a challenger to provide the challenged voter's actual residence on the challenge form and submit evidence that they exercised due diligence to verify that the challenged voter does not reside at their registered address. Under existing law, the challenger must provide one or the other. 
  • Eliminate the requirement that the challenger: 1) search local telephone directories to find another address belonging to the challenged voter, 2) search county auditor property records to determine whether the challenged voter owns any property in the county, and 3) search the statewide voter registration database to determine if the voter is registered at any other address in the state.
  • Require the challenger to send a certified letter with return service requested to the challenged voter's mailing address, using a form provided by the office of the secretary of state outlining the reason for their challenge. Under existing law, the challenger is required to send a non-certified letter with return service requested to the challenged voter's address.
  • Allow challengers to only challenge voters who live in the same city or town, or if the voter lives in an unincorporated area, the same county. Under existing law, a challenger can challenge any voter.
  • Require the challenger to file a separate voter registration challenge form or official electronic challenge form for each voter whose registration is challenged. Each form would be required to include: 1) the challenger's name, voting precinct, address, and phone number, 2) the challenged voter's name, voting precinct, address, and phone number, 3) a statement of the factual basis for the challenge based on personal firsthand knowledge, 4) the challenger's signature in ink certifying the information is true and correct, and 5) a statement providing that such certification is subject to penalty of perjury. Under existing law, a challenger must file a signed affidavit stating that to their personal knowledge and belief, the challenged voter is either not qualified to vote or does not live at their voter registration address.
  • Require voters who do not receive ballots because their registration is inactive to be immune from voter registration challenges. Under existing law, there is no such prohibition.
  • Require the county auditor to dismiss a challenge if it is not in proper form or the factual basis for the challenge does not meet the legal grounds for a challenge. Under existing law, the county auditor has the discretion to dismiss the challenge.
  • Permit the county auditor to conduct an initial review of the merits of a challenge prior to notifying a challenged voter of any challenge. If the county recorder determined that it is more likely than not that the challenge lacks merit, the county recorder would be allowed to dismiss the challenge. The challenger would then be allowed to refile the challenge with additional evidence. Under existing law, the county auditor may dismiss a challenge if it is not in proper form or the factual basis for the challenge does not meet the legal grounds for a challenge. In that case, the county auditor must notify the challenger of the reasons for the dismissal.
  • Require that the notice sent to challenged voters be in a form provided by the secretary of state and sent by forwardable certified mail to the address provided in the voter registration record and any other address at which the challenged voter is alleged to reside, or at an address where the county auditor reasonably expects the voter to receive notice. The county recorder would be required to send the notice by email and phone if possible. The notice would be required to include: 1) a copy of the challenge form and affidavit, 2) methods by which the voter can confirm eligibility, including by mail, online, phone, in-person, or at a scheduled hearing, 3) notice of any consequences for failing to respond to the challenge, and 4) for challenges based on a change of address, a clear statement that the voter remains registered and eligible to vote in that election. Under existing law, the voter must be provided with a copy of the affidavit. The bill would make conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect this new requirement.
  • Require the county auditor to reconfirm registration before scheduling a hearing.
  • Require the challenger to appear in person at the hearing and if they do not appear, the challenge would be dismissed. Under existing law, the challenger may either appear in person or submit testimony by affidavit. 
  • Allow the challenged voter to submit testimony by affidavit instead of appearing in person at the hearing.
  • Require the county auditor to only schedule a hearing if they are unable to confirm the voter's eligibility and based on the evidence provided by the challenger, there is probable cause that the challenger is not qualified to vote. The bill would require challenges that do not meet the probable cause standard to be dismissed without a hearing and if at any point the county auditor is able to independently verify the eligibility of the challenged voter, the challenge would be dismissed without a hearing. 
  • Require the hearing notification to be sent by certified forwardable mail, rather than just certified mail. The county auditor would also be required to notify the challenged voter by phone, including leaving a voicemail if possible, and email, if possible. If the county auditor receives no response from the challenged voter within 10 calendar days, the county auditor would be required to provide the same notice a second time. 
  • Require that any challenger that knowingly provides false information as part of a voter registration challenge would be guilty of perjury. The bill would also require that any challenger that knowingly challenges a person's voter registration without reasonable cause would be guilty of a misdemeanor. Each instance where a challenger made a challenge without reasonable cause would constitute a separate offense. Under existing law, there is no penalty for bringing a failed voter challenge.
  • Require a county auditor receiving official information that a voter has registered to vote in another state to send a notice to the voter and to change the voter's status to inactive. The bill would require official information to come directly from a government or state-led interstate source, such as ERIC. The auditor would not be allowed to remove the voter from the official state voter registration list unless the voter failed to respond to the notice, failed to vote, or failed to confirm their registration address during the time period that would include the next two general elections.

Last Updated January 28, 2025

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Shavers (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 7, 2025, would require county auditors to provide election instructions and materials in languages other than English when at least two and one-half percent of the voting-age or voting-qualified population in the county or on an "Indian reservation" has limited English proficiency and the rate of the limited English proficiency citizens with an education not exceeding fifth grade exceeds the national rate. Under federal law, jurisdictions must provide language accommodations if the members of a given language group in the jurisdiction exceeds five percent of the population or exceed 10,000.

Last Updated February 14, 2025

data.bill_name: Vote by Mail for Nonabsentee Voters
data.bill_location: House State Government and Tribal Relations Committee

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Marshall (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 23, 2025 would require all voters to present photo ID when voting in person, eliminate universal mail voting, shorten the timeframe for early voting, make changes to voter list maintenance procedures, make several changes related to accessible voting for individuals with disabilities, and make other changes to election administration. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require all individuals voting in-person to present photo ID. Under existing law, the county auditor is required to ask every voter to either sign a ballot declaration or provide ID. An individual who cannot provide ID would be issued a provisional ballot, which would be accepted if the signature on the declaration matches the signature on the voter's registration record. Under existing law, the identification must be a valid photo ID such as a driver's license, state ID card, student ID card, tribal ID card, or employer ID card.
  • Eliminate universal mail voting for all voters, including overseas voters, and would limit who can vote by mail. Specifically, the bill would require voters to submit requests for mail ballots rather than receive them automatically. The bill would require that a voter must meet one of the following requirements to vote by mail:
    • The voter is or will be 65 years old on the date of the election
    • The voter has a disability or illness that prevents them from voting in person
    • The voter is military personnel or a dependent of military personnel
    • The voter is a student who temporarily resides outside the county
    • The voter temporarily resides outside the state but maintains eligibility to vote in the state
    • The voter is incarcerated but has not had their voting rights revoked or has had their rights automatically restored
    • The voter is employed outside of the county during all voting hours
    • The voter participates in the address confidentiality program. The bill would make other nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this change.
  • Shorten the timeframe for in-person early voting from 18 calendar days through Election Day to five days before an election and through Election Day.
  • Require the county auditor to send every voter a notice at least once every two years and at least 90 days before a primary or general election for federal office, requesting change of address information. Under existing law, the county auditor is only required to request change of address information from the postal service for all mail ballots.
  • Allow a poll-site judge or inspector to challenge an individual's right to vote on Election Day if that individual is voting in-person. Under existing law, only another registered voter or the county prosecuting attorney can challenge another voter.
  • Change the name of "mail ballots" to "absentee ballots."
  • Require a voter submitting a request for a mail ballot to send it to the county auditor no earlier than 90 days nor later than the day before the election at which the individual seeks to vote. The request could be made orally in person, by telephone, electronically, or writing. Under existing law, voters are automatically sent mail ballots.
  • Create restrictions on the delivery of a mail ballot. Specifically, the bill would require:
    • That a mail ballot be picked up at the office of the issuing officer by the voter or a member of the voter's immediate family
    • That if the voter is a resident of a health care facility, they may apply by messenger for a mail ballot and the messenger may pick up the mail ballot. 
  • Allow a voter requesting a mail ballot for a primary election to also request a mail ballot for the following general election. An overseas or service voter requesting a mail ballot for a primary election would automatically receive a mail ballot for the following general election.
  • Require that a voter who has requested a mail ballot but wishes to vote in person be allowed to vote a provisional ballot. Existing law is silent on this requirement.
  • Require the county auditor to certify to the secretary of state within 24 hours of mailing out mail ballots that the ballots were sent. If the mail ballots were not sent on time, the auditor would be required to notify the secretary of state within 24 hours of the reason for the delay and the date the ballots would be mailed. If the county auditor did not provide this information to the secretary by the Monday 15 days before the election, the secretary would be required to contact the county auditor to inquire about the reason for the delay and the date that the ballots would be sent. Under existing law, the county auditor is required to certify to the office of the secretary of state the dates the ballots were mailed or the reason and date the ballots will be mailed if they were not mailed timely.
  • Require that a returned mail ballot missing a postmark that is received after Election Day be counted only if the voter signs a declaration attesting, under penalty of perjury, that the ballot was mailed on or before Election Day. The county auditor would be required to notify every voter whose ballot was missing a postmark by first-class mail. If the voter does not respond at least three days before the final meeting of the canvassing board, the auditor would be required to attempt to notify the voter by telephone, using the voter registration record information. Under existing law, if a mail ballot is missing a postmark, the date on the ballot declaration is considered the time of voting. 
  • Require that for service and overseas voters voting a mail ballot, the voting period would begin 30 days before each special election and 45 days before each primary and general election and would end at 8 p.m. on the day of the primary. Under existing law, UOCAVA voters may be eligible to receive a ballot up to 90 days before an election and the returned ballot must be received within nine days after a special election, 13 days after a primary, and within 20 days after a general election.
  • Require that each voting center provide information about polling places in the county, information about requesting a mail ballot and returning one, in addition to the other information provided under law.
  • Eliminate the requirement that a county auditor establish one ballot drop box for every 15,000 voters in the county and a minimum of one ballot drop box in each city, town, and census designated in the county with a post office.
  • Eliminate the requirement that the county auditor establish at least one ballot drop box on an Indiana reservation at the request of a federally recognized tribe. 
  • Repeal the statute allowing federally recognized tribes to designate at least one building as a ballot pickup and collection location. 
  • Eliminate the requirement that each student engagement hub allow students to download their exact ballot from an online portal. Under existing law, every state university, regional university, and the evergreen state college must open a student engagement hub on campus that allows students to register and provides voter registration materials. 
  • Specify that ballots returned by voters who were in line at the polling place by 8 p.m. on Election Day must be counted.
  • Require county auditors to designate polling places that are accessible to elderly voters and voters with disabilities and to do the following:
    • Make modifications such as installation of temporary ramps or relocation of polling places within buildings, where appropriate
    • Designate new, accessible polling places to replace those that are inaccessible
    • Continue to use polling places and voter registration locations which are accessible to elderly voters and voters with disabilities. The bill would require county auditors to seek alternative polling places or low-cost alternatives before incurring costs for these changes.
  • Require the secretary of state to establish procedures to assure that any elderly voter or voter with a disability assigned to an inaccessible polling place will, upon advance request of that voter, be permitted to vote at an alternative location. 
  • Allow polling places for the various precincts to be located outside the boundaries of the respective precincts if election officers deem it feasible.
  • Require the legislative authority of each county, municipality, and special district to make its facilities available for use as polling places when requested by the county auditor.
  • Require each state agency and entity of local government to permit the use of any of its buildings as polling places.
  • Require the county auditor to submit to the secretary of state a list showing the number of polling places in the county and specifying any that have been found inaccessible by April 1st of each even-numbered year.
  • Require that each polling place be accessible unless:
    • The county auditor determined that this is inaccessible and that no alternative polling places are available and no modifications can be made.
    • The secretary of state determines that a state of emergency exists that would otherwise interfere with the efficient administration of the election.
  • Require county auditors, as feasible, to solicit and use the assistance of voters with disabilities in reviewing sites and recommending inexpensive remedies to improve accessibility.
  • Require each county auditor to include a notice of accessibility of polling places in the notice of election
  • Allow the qualifications of a mail voter to be challenged before the voted ballot is received. The board would have the authority to determine the legality of any mail ballot challenged. 
  • Prohibit the interference with a voter in any way within a polling place or drop box site. An individual convicted of violating this provision would be charged with a misdemeanor.
  • Allow any political party or committee to designate an individual other than a precinct election officer for each polling place to check a list of registered voters of the precinct to determine who has and who has not voted.
  • Allow a voter to take any printed or written materials to assist in casting their vote into the voting booth.
  • Specify that if a voter incorrectly marks a ballot, they may return it and be issued a new ballot.
  • Require the county auditor to provide in each polling place a sufficient number of voting booths or voting devices along with any necessary supplies. 
  • Require the county auditor to provide voting and registration instructions printed in large type to be conspicuously displayed at each polling place and registration facility. The county auditor would also be required to make information available for deaf voters through telecommunications. 
  • Require each polling place to provide at least one voting unit that provides access to blind or deaf voters, enabling them to vote with privacy and independence.
  • Require that election judges and clerks be paid no less than the minimum hourly wage as provided under state law and that the respective boards of county commissioners for each county set the exact amount. Inspectors would be paid the same rate as judges and clerks plus an additional two hours of compensation. Precinct election officers picking up and returning supplies to the county auditor would be entitled to additional compensation as set forth by the county commissioners. Existing law is silent on this requirement.

Last Updated March 10, 2025

data.bill_name: Verification of Citizenship for Voter Registration
data.bill_location: House State Government and Tribal Relations Committee

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Marshall (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 23, 2025 would require individuals registering to vote to provide proof of citizenship and would require all existing registered voters to provide proof of citizenship or have their registration canceled. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require all voter registration applicants to provide proof of citizenship using one of the following documents:
    • A valid enhanced driver's license or ID card for which proof of U.S. citizenship is required
    • A valid U.S. passport
    • A U.S. certificate of naturalization
    • A U.S. department of state consular report of birth abroad
    • An original, certified birth certificate from a U.S. birth place, issued by a city, county, state, or territory that contains all of the following: 1) full name and date of birth, 2) place of birth which must be a U.S. location, 3) a registrar's raised, embossed, impressed or multicolored seal, 4) a control or file number that the issuing authority can confirm, 5) a registrar's signature, and 6) the date the certificate was filed with the registrar's office. Birth certificates from Puerto Rico issued prior to July 1, 2010 would not be accepted and neither would Washington wallet-sized birth registrations. Under existing law, a voter only needs to check a box to verify their citizenship or to have presented a document confirming citizenship as part of another government transaction.
  • Require a voter to submit proof of citizenship in order to register to vote electronically on the secretary of state's website. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to provide their ID number, tribal ID number, or the last four digits of their Social Security Number. The bill would require the applicant to submit one of the following types of proof of citizenship in order to register to vote electronically:
    • A Washington state enhanced driver's license. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to have a Washington state driver's license.
    • A Washington state ID card for which proof of U.S. citizenship is required. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to have a Washington state ID card.
    • A current and valid Washington tribal ID. Under existing law, an applicant only needs to have a current Washington tribal ID. An applicant can also provide a Washington state learner's permit or Social Security Number.
  • Require the county auditor, upon receipt of a voter registration application, to contact the department of licensing to determine whether the applicant has demonstrated proof of citizenship by applying for and receiving an enhanced driver's license or ID card. If the applicant has proven citizenship, the applicant would be registered. If the applicant has not proven citizenship, the county auditor would send a notice to the voter stating that their registration is incomplete and that they must appear in person at the auditor's office and present proof of citizenship. After presenting proof of citizenship, the applicant would be registered. They would be required to present one of the following forms of proof of citizenship:
    • A valid U.S. passport
    • A U.S. certificate of naturalization
    • A U.S. department of state consular report of birth abroad or
    • An original, certified birth certificate from a U.S. birth place containing all required information
  • Require each county auditor to consult with the department of licensing by July 1, 2025, to determine whether each registered voter in the county has demonstrated proof of citizenship by applying for and receiving an enhanced driver's license or ID card. If a voter has demonstrated proof of citizenship, they would remain registered. If they have not, the county auditor would be required to:
    • Send a notice to the voter informing them that they must appear in person at the auditor's office to demonstrate proof of citizenship. The voter would be required to show one of the following forms of proof of citizenship:
      • A valid U.S. passport
      • A U.S. certificate of naturalization
      • A U.S. department of state consular report of birth abroad
      • An original, certified birth certificate from a U.S. birth place containing all required information 
    • Send a second notice to the voter if they have not demonstrated proof of citizenship 30 days prior to the 2025 general election
    • Send a third notice to the voter if they have not demonstrated proof of citizenship 14 days prior to the 2025 general election informing them that their voter registration has been cancelled and informing them how to register. The county auditor would be required to cancel the voter's registration accordingly.
  • Require the county auditor to immediately reinstate a voter's registration upon being presented with proof of citizenship.

Last Updated February 4, 2025

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 211

data.author: Mena (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 25, 2026, makes changes to the state's Voting Rights Act. Specifically, the bill:

  • Defines "covered jurisdiction" as a political subdivision which, within the past 25 years, has become subject to a court order, government enforcement action, court-approved consent decree, or a settlement in which the political subdivision conceded liability based upon:
    • A violation of the state's voting Rights Act, the federal voting rights act, voting-related violations of the U.S. constitution, or a finding or concession that the political subdivision engaged in a pattern, policy, or practice of discrimination against members of a protected class in violation of state or federal law.
    • Any political subdivision that during the previous 25 years was found to have enacted or implemented a covered policy without obtaining preclearance
  • Defines "covered practice" as:
    • Any change to the method of election of members of a governing body
    • Any change within a 12 month period to the boundaries of the covered jurisdiction that reduces by more than five percentage points the proportion of the jurisdiction's citizen voting age population made up of members of a protected class
    • Any change to the boundaries of election districts or wards in the covered jurisdiction
    • Any change that restricts the ability of any individual to provide interpreter services to voters in any language other than English
    • Any change to the covered jurisdiction's plan of government
    • The method of election or district plans if, following each census, a covered jurisdiction maintains an at-large method of election or implements a district-based election system makes no revisions to its districting plans
    • A change that may have the effect of denying, abridging, or diluting the right to vote on account of race, color, or membership in a language minority group
  • Requires that prior to adopting or administering a covered practice, the governing body of a covered practice must submit such covered practice to the attorney general for issuance of a certificate of no objection.
  • Allows an aggrieved party to file an action in certain circumstances
  • Requires that no later than July 1st of each even-numbered year, the attorney general must designate the political subdivisions that qualify as covered jurisdictions in consultation with the office of financial management, and other relevant agencies. The bill would set forth the notice requirements for the attorney general to notify those jurisdictions.
  • Requires that once a covered practice is approved by the attorney general or a court, an action against the political subdivision based on the same covered practice may not be brought within four years of the approval.

Provisions from a previous version of the bill were amended out of the bill text. They would have:

  • Included an additional definition of a "covered jurisdiction" as:
    • A political subdivision where members of a protected class consist of at least 6,000 citizens of voting age or make up at least 15 percent of the voting age population where at any point during the previous 10 years:
      • The percentage of the citizen voting age population of the protected class that is registered to vote is at least 10 percentage points lower than the percentage of all citizens of voting age that are registered to vote or
      • The percentage of the citizen voting age population of the protected class that participated in any general election for any office of the subdivision is at least 10 percentage points lower than the percentage of all citizens of voting age that participated in the election or
      • The poverty rate of that protected class exceeded the poverty rate of the whole population of the political subdivision by at least 10 percentage points
  • Included additional definitions of a "covered practice" as:
    • A change in the number, location, or hours of any election day or early voting site or ballot drop boxes
    • The annexation or de-annexation of a political subdivision or the consolidation or division of political subdivisions

Last Updated April 6, 2026

data.bill_name: Automatic Voter Registration for Tribal Members
data.bill_location: House State Government and Tribal Relations Committee

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Stearns (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2025 would:

  • Require the secretary of state to establish an automatic voter registration process for eligible members of federally recognized "Indian tribes" no later than six months after July 1, 2025. The bill would set forth the requirements for how this process must be created. Individuals who are automatically registered through this process would receive a notice in the mail afterward allowing them to decline registration within 15 days from the date of mailing. If the voter declined registration after the 15 day timeline, their registration would be canceled. Under existing law, automatic voter registration is available to eligible individuals when applying for any government service that requires documentary proof of citizenship at the Department of Licensing (Washington's DMV-equivalent agency).
  • Require the secretary of state to process voter registration information for all members of a tribe by automated process if the following requirements are met:
    • The applicant meets requirements for voter registration
    • The applicant has provided their name, traditional or nontraditional residence address, mailing address and date of birth
    • The individual has provided documentation confirming that they are a U.S. citizen
    • The individual has provided a signature image
  • Require that an applicant be registered to vote or have their registration updated as of five business days after the receipt of the application. If the application is received after the deadline to register, the county auditor would be required to send a voter registration acknowledgment notice package within five business days after the election.
  • Allow a county auditor to communicate with potential and registered voters through email, phone, or text messaging in addition to mail. 
  • Require the auditor to mail a verification notice to an applicant if the application is not complete. The notice would be required to inform the applicant of the missing information. If the applicant provided the required information within 45 days, they would be registered to vote.

Last Updated March 10, 2025

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 215

data.author: Hill (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 25, 2026, makes minor changes to the "Washington Voting Rights Act." Specifically, the bill:

  • Provides a list of circumstances that would demonstrate when a government entity has not violated the Act.
  • Allows an individual to file an action alleging a violation of the Act without fulfilling the notice and exhaustion requirement if: 1) the individual is seeking preliminary relief with respect to an upcoming election; 2) the individual is seeking to intervene in or join an existing action; or 3) following the individual's submission of notice, the political group enacted a remedy that would not remedy the violation identified in the notice.
  • Requires a court to grant a temporary restraining order or a preliminary injunction with respect to an upcoming election if the court determined that the plaintiff was more likely than not to succeed on the merits and it was possible to implement an appropriate remedy that would resolve the alleged violation in the upcoming election.
  • Makes the bill null and void if specific funding for it is not included in the omnibus appropriations act by June 30, 2026.

Last Updated April 6, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 212

data.author: Doglio (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 25, 2026, makes it more difficult for a voter to be removed from voter lists and creates additional requirements for individuals challenging a voter's registration. Specifically, the bill:

  • Requires the county auditor or secretary of state to confirm that a voter has died before their registration is cancelled. Under current law, any voter may sign a statement, subject to penalties of perjury, to the effect that to their personal knowledge, another registered voter is deceased. Upon receipt of that signed statement, the county auditor or secretary of state must cancel the registration.
  • Requires the county auditor to cancel a voter's inactive registration when the county auditor receives a signed, written notice from the voter that the voter has moved out of state and wishes to have their registration canceled. The county auditor is required to verify that the signature on the notification matches the signature in the voter's file. Under current law, the county auditor must cancel an inactive voter registration when "receiving information indicating" that an inactive voter moved out of state.
  • Requires a challenger to provide the challenged voter's actual residence on the challenge form and submit evidence that they exercised due diligence to verify that the challenged voter does not reside at their registered address. Under current law, the challenger must provide one or the other. 
  • Eliminates the requirement that the challenger: 
    • Search local telephone directories to find another address belonging to the challenged voter
    • Search county auditor property records to determine whether the challenged voter owns any property in the county
    • Search the statewide voter registration database to determine if the voter is registered at any other address in the state 
    • Search the voter registration database of another state to determine if the voter is registered to vote in another state.
  • Requires the challenger to send a certified letter with return service requested to the challenged voter's mailing address, using a form provided by the office of the secretary of state outlining the reason for their challenge. Under current law, the challenger is required to send a non-certified letter with return service requested to the challenged voter's address.
  • Allows challengers to only challenge voters who live in the same county. Under current law, a challenger can challenge any voter.
  • Requires the challenger to file a separate voter registration challenge form or official electronic challenge form, as provided by the secretary of state, for each voter whose registration is challenged. Each form is required to include: 
    • The challenger's name, address, and contact information
    • The challenged voter's name and address
    • A statement of the factual basis for the challenge based on personal firsthand knowledge and any supporting documentation or evidence
    • The challenger's ink signature certifying that the information is true and correct
    • A clear statement providing that such certification is subject to penalty of perjury. Under current law, a challenger must file a signed affidavit stating that to their personal knowledge and belief, the challenged voter is either not qualified to vote or does not live at their voter registration address.
  • Requires voters who do not receive ballots because their registration is inactive to be immune from voter registration challenges. Under current law, there is no such prohibition.
  • Requires the county auditor or their designee to preside over challenge hearings. Under current law, only the county auditor can preside over the hearings. The bill makes non-substantive and conforming changes due to this requirement.
  • Requires the county auditor to publish the entire content of any voter challenge on the auditor's website within 10 days of receipt. The information can be removed 45 days following the completion or dismissal of the challenge. Under current law, the information must be published within 72 hours and may be removed 45 days following certification of an election.
  • Requires the county auditor to dismiss a challenge if it is not in proper form or the factual basis for the challenge does not meet the legal grounds for a challenge. Under current law, the county auditor has the discretion to dismiss the challenge and only requires that a challenge "substantially comply" with the form issued by the secretary of state.
  • Permits the county auditor to conduct an initial review of the merits of a challenge prior to notifying a challenged voter of any challenge. If the county recorder determines that it is more likely than not that the challenge lacks merit, the county recorder is allowed to dismiss the challenge. The challenger is then be allowed to refile the challenge with additional evidence. Under current law, the county auditor may dismiss a challenge if it is not in proper form or the factual basis for the challenge does not meet the legal grounds for a challenge. In that case, the county auditor must notify the challenger of the reasons for the dismissal.
  • Requires that the notice sent to challenged voters be in a form provided by the secretary of state and sent by forwardable certified mail to the address provided in the voter registration record and any other address at which the challenged voter is alleged to reside, or at an address where the county auditor reasonably expects the voter to receive notice. The county recorder is required to send the notice by email and phone if possible. The notice is required to include: 
    • A copy of the challenge form and affidavit, 
    • Methods by which the voter can confirm eligibility, including by mail, online, phone, in-person, or at a scheduled hearing
    • Notice of any consequences for failing to respond to the challenge, and 
    • For challenges based on a change of address, a clear statement that the voter remains registered and eligible to vote in that election. Under current law, the voter must be provided with a copy of the affidavit. The bill makes conforming and non-substantive changes to various provisions to reflect this new requirement.
  • Requires the challenger to appear in person at the hearing and if they do not appear, the challenge is dismissed. Under current law, the challenger may either appear in person or submit testimony by affidavit. 
  • Allows the challenged voter to submit testimony by affidavit instead of appearing in person at the hearing as required under current law. If the challenged voter fails to do either, the challenge is resolved on the available facts and the challenger still has the burden to prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the challenged voter's registration is improper. Under current law, the challenged voter must appear in person and if they do not appear in person, the challenge must be resolved based on the available facts.
  • Requires the county auditor to only schedule a hearing if they are unable to confirm the voter's eligibility and based on the evidence provided by the challenger, there is probable cause that the challenger is not qualified to vote. The bill requires challenges that do not meet the probable cause standard to be dismissed without a hearing and if at any point the county auditor is able to independently verify the eligibility of the challenged voter, the challenge is dismissed without a hearing. 
  • Requires that the hearing notification be sent by certified forwardable mail, rather than just certified mail as required under current law. The county auditor is also required to notify the challenged voter by phone, including leaving a voicemail if possible, and email, if possible. If the county auditor receives no response from the challenged voter within 10 calendar days, the county auditor is required to provide the same notice a second time. The hearing notice must include:
    • A copy of the challenge form and affidavit
    • Methods by which the voter can confirm eligibility, including by mail, online, phone, in-person, or at a scheduled hearing
    • Notice of any consequences for failing to respond to the challenge
    • Information about how responding to the challenge may help the voter remain registered to vote. Under current law, the hearing notice only must contain the time and place of the hearing.
  • Requires the secretary of state to create a standard notification form to be sent to challenged voters via mail or email.
  • Requires that the challenger has the burden to prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the challenged voter's registration is improper. Under current law, the challenger has the burden to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the challenged voter's registration is improper.
  • Permits a voter to correct their residence address on their voter registration if the challenge is based on the voter's address and the auditor or canvassing board sustains the challenge. If the voter does not provide their correct address, the voter is placed on inactive status and any challenged ballot is not be counted unless the voter provided their corrected residence address by the day before the certification of the election. If the voter provided an address that is outside of the state, their registration is canceled and their challenged ballot is not counted. Current law only provides that a voter is permitted to correct their address. 
  • Requires that any challenger that knowingly provides false information as part of a voter registration challenge is guilty of false swearing with a fine in an amount fixed by the court of not more than $10,000. The bill also requires that any challenger that knowingly challenges a person's voter registration without reasonable cause is guilty of a misdemeanor. Each instance where a challenger made a challenge without reasonable cause constitutes a separate offense. Under current law, there is no penalty for bringing a failed voter challenge.
  • Requires a county auditor to immediately cancel a voter's registration if they receive official information from an elections office in another state that a Washington voter has recently registered to vote in that state, and that voter's information matches the information in a Washington voter registration record. Under current law, a county auditor only needs to receive "official information" that a voter has registered to vote in another state in order to cancel their registration.
