data.author: Nobles (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on January 17, 2025, would adopt the national standards for uniformed and overseas civilian voting set out in federal law and make other changes related to voting for uniformed and overseas voters. Specifically, the bill would: 

  • Require election officials to send a UOCAVA ballot as soon as practicable after receiving a UOCAVA application. If an application is received within 20 days before an election and the voter has provided an email address, a notification must be immediately provided to them on how to obtain a ballot by email or online in the event that the voter does not receive it by Election Day. The bill would require the county auditor to request an email address from each applicant. Under existing federal law, election officials must send UOCAVA ballots out at least 45 days before an election.
  • Require that a UOCAVA ballot returned by mail be submitted no later than 8 p.m. on Election Day and received by the deadline for certification of the election. In Washington, the certification deadline is 10 days after a special election, 10 days after a presidential primary, 14 days after a primary, and 21 days after a general election. If a voter signs the ballot declaration that the ballot was timely submitted, the ballot would not be rejected on the basis that it has a late postmark, an unreadable postmark, or no postmark. Under existing federal law, ballots must be received by nine days after a special election, 13 days after a primary, and within 20 days after a general election.
  • Allow voters to request electronic delivery of a ballot for all elections held in which the voter is eligible to vote as a UOCAVA voter.
  • Require the secretary of state to develop an electronic free-access system for uniformed and overseas voters to determine by telephone, email, or internet whether their application has been received and accepted and whether their ballot has been received and its current status. 
  • Require the secretary of state to explore options for an electronic ballot portal to be available for uniformed and overseas voters who are not able to access standard mail return.
  • Require the county auditor to prepare a list of known candidates and issues to be used in conjunction with a federal write-in absentee ballot at least 90 days before a regularly scheduled election. Uniformed and overseas voters would be able to request this information. 
  • Require that when a ballot contains a mistake or omission that does not prevent determination of whether that individual is eligible to vote, the document would be accepted. If a voter used a write-in ballot containing a mistake or omission, if the intention of the voter is discernible, the ballot would be accepted. Under existing law, there is no similar requirement.
  • Require that no voter lose residence by reason of their absence (1) while employed in the military, (2) while engaged in the navigation of waters of Washington or the United States, or (3) while a student at any institution of learning. Under existing law, no voter could gain residence or lose residence from those specified activities.
  • Require that no voter gain residence while serving a sentence in "total confinement" in any public prison. Under existing law, a voter could not gain or lose residence as a result of serving a sentence in prison.
  • Would allow an individual whose voter application information could not be verified to provide proof of eligibility under UOCAVA as an alternate form of ID in addition to the other forms of alternate ID allowed under existing law. Under existing law, an individual can provide the following alternate forms of ID: (1) valid photo ID, (2) an enrollment card of a federally recognized tribe in Washington state, (3) a copy of a current utility bill, (4) a current bank statement, (5) a copy of a current government check, (6) a copy of a current paycheck, or (7) a government document showing the individual's name and address.
  • Require that the statute regarding alternate forms of ID when registering to vote apply to overseas or service voters and to voters updating information on their registration record. Under existing law, the statute does not apply to overseas or service voters who register to vote by signing the return envelope of an absentee ballot or to a registered voter transferring their registration record or to voters updating their information.

Last Updated February 28, 2025

data.bill_location: Senate State Government, Tribal Affairs & Elections Committee

status_actions.summary: 2025 REGULAR SESSION: By Order of Resolution
meta: Reintroduced and retained in present status.

data.author: Fortunato (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on April 21, 2025, would be called the state elections confidence using rigorous examination (SECURE) act. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require voters to present documentary proof of citizenship as defined in Executive Order No. 14248 when registering to vote. Executive Order No. 14248 defines documentary proof of citizenship as:
    • A United States passport
    • An ID compliant with the requirements of the REAL ID Act of 2005 "that indicates the applicant is a citizen of the United States"
    • An official military ID card that indicates that the applicant is a citizen of the United States
    • A valid federal or state government-issued photo ID indicating that the individual is a U.S. citizen or is accompanied by proof of U.S. citizenship. Under existing law, voters are not required to show documentary proof of citizenship when registering to vote. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Require that voters present ID when voting by mail in addition to when voting in person. Under existing law, in Washington, elections are conducted by mail and voters are not required to present ID when voting by mail but must present ID when voting in person.
  • Limit the types of ID that an individual can present when voting. The bill would limit the types of acceptable IDs to: driver's licenses; state ID cards; tribal ID cards; and federal government-issued photo ID cards. Under existing law, voters are also allowed to present student ID cards and employer ID cards. 
  • Create new rules for when mail ballots must be rejected. Specifically, the bill would require that if a voter fails to enclose a photo ID or the photo ID does not have the same name as on the registration file, the election official must notify the voter by first-class mail, telephone, email, and text message and inform them of the deficiency and the correct procedures for completing the ballot. If the ballot is received within five business days of the final meeting of the canvassing board or the voter has been notified and has not responded, then the election official must attempt to notify the voter by telephone and leave a voicemail. 
  • Require that when the department of licensing provides information to the secretary of state regarding an individual who has registered to vote, that they also provide the name and title of the department agent who processed the registration. 
  • Allow election officials to provide electronic means for submitting a ballot declaration signature if needed to cure an incomplete ballot.
  • Require that if a mail voter's ballot is rejected in two consecutive primary or general elections due to a lack of valid photo ID, the election official must contact the voter by telephone, text message, or email and request that the voter provide valid photo ID the next time they vote.
  • Prohibit a voter from curing a missing or mismatched photo ID for purposes of counting the ballot in a recount.
  • Require that a record be kept of all ballots with missing and mismatched photo ID. The record must contain the date on which the voter was contacted or notice was mailed and when the voter provided updated information. The record must be updated daily.
  • Require that the state provide free photo ID cards (identicards) to individuals receiving public assistance grants and participants in the Washington women, infants, and children program.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.


Last Updated April 22, 2025

This bill, as enacted on March 25, 2026:

Current law requires county election offices to refer these requests to the Secretary of State for fulfillment but does not explicitly prohibit officials from providing records to requestors. data.bill_name: Voting By Absentee Ballot in Person

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Krug (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on November 17, 2025, would require early voting to be available at the office of the municipal clerk or an alternate site for specified hours based on the number of registered voters. 

  • Specifically, the bill would require that during the 14 day period of early voting and ending on the Sunday before the election:
    • A municipality with less than 600 registered voters provide at least 10 hours of in-person early voting
    • A municipality with between 600 and 799 registered voters provide at least 15 hours of in-person early voting
    • A municipality with 800 or more registered voters provide at least 20 hours of in-person early voting. This requirement would only apply to the spring and November general elections and elections held concurrently with the spring and November general elections. Under existing law, there is no set time that those offices must be open and the municipality has the authority to set the hours. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Require the Department of Administration to award grants to counties and municipalities to pay for requirements under this bill.
  • Permit a municipality to disregard the mandatory early voting hours requirements if reimbursement funds are not available.
  • Require the Secretary of Administration to notify municipalities at least 30 days in advance of the fourth Tuesday before each spring and November election as to whether funds will be available to reimburse the municipality for mandatory early voting.
  • Allow a municipality that is unable to comply with the early voting hours requirements to do one of the following:
    • Jointly designate with an adjacent municipality an alternate site to serve voters from both municipalities. Those two municipalities would be required to enter into a memorandum of understanding. The municipality where the voting site is located would be required to obtain the approval of the county board and county clerk.
    • Designate the office of the county clerk as an alternate mail ballot site if the office of the county clerk is located no more than 25 miles from the municipal clerks' office.
  • Provide that if two municipalities join together to provide early voting, that the size of the largest of those municipalities controls for purposes of determining the number of required early voting hours that they must provide.
  • Require a municipality that enters into a memorandum of understanding with another municipality or with the county itself to be open for in-person early voting for at least the same number of hours that the office of the municipal clerk was open for during the 2024 spring election or November general election.
  • Require a municipality that enters into a memorandum of understanding with another municipality or with the county and who designated an alternate early voting site for the 2024 spring or November general election to to designate at least one alternate site in the municipality, located as near as practicable to the office of the municipal clerk and which must be open the same number of house as it was open in 2024.


A previous version of the bill contained a provision that was amended out of this version. That provision would have:

  • Required that in-person early voting be available for at least 20 hours during the early voting period at the office of the municipal clerk or an alternate site. 

Last Updated November 24, 2025

data.bill_name: Enforcement of the Federal Help America Vote Act

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Wichgers (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on July 8, 2025, would require the Wisconsin Elections Commission ("WEC") to examine and investigate all complaints, including complaints against the WEC itself, regarding possible HAVA violations in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner. Specifically, the WEC would be required to do the following in compliance with federal law:

  • Transmit to the complainant and all known interested parties and acknowledgement of receipt of the complaint within five business days from the date of its receipt
  • Make a final determination with respect to the merits of the complaint and issue a decision no later than 89 days following the date on which the complaint was filed
  • Conduct a hearing, if requested, no later than 30 days after receiving a complaint and to publish the results of all dismissed complaints on the WEC's website and to provide those complaints to the legislature.
  • Take corrective action, if the WEC finds that a complaint has merit, to remedy the violation. The complainant would be permitted to appeal to a court of competent jurisdiction.

The Wisconsin Supreme Court ruled in 2022 that the WEC could not hear complaints of possible HAVA violations made against it.


Last Updated July 9, 2025

data.bill_name: Compliance With the Federal Electoral Count Reform Act

status_actions.summary: Act No. 214

data.author: Krug (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on April 8, 2026, alters certification and canvassing deadlines in order to comply with the federal Electoral Count Reform Act. Specifically, the bill:

  • Regarding canvassing:
    • Moves up the start date for canvassing a presidential election to no later than noon on the Friday after the election. Under existing law, canvassing for all elections must begin no later than 9 a.m. on the Monday after the election.
    • Requires the board of canvassers to reconvene no later than noon on the Friday after a presidential election to count provisional ballots. Under existing law, the board is only required to reconvene no later than 9 a.m. on the Monday after the election. 
    • Permits the board to not reconvene if no provisional ballots were received from the time that the board completed their initial canvass and noon on the Friday after the election. Under existing law, the board would not be required to reconvene if no provisional ballots were received by 4 p.m. on the Friday after the election.
    • Moves up the deadline for counties to deliver statements of canvass to the elections commission from 14 days after the presidential election to 8 days after the presidential election.
    • Extends the deadline for the elections commission to conclude the state canvass and make their certifications and determinations from 16 days after the presidential election to 21 days after the presidential election.
    • Allows a county board of canvassers to adjourn only to the extent permitted by the commission when canvassing a presidential election. Under existing law, a board of canvassers may adjourn no longer than one day at a time and no more than two days in all.
    • Requires the election commission chairperson or their designee to canvass unofficial returns for the county clerk, if by the 21st day after a presidential election, a county board of canvassers has failed to certify a statement or return.
  • Regarding processing ballots:
    • Moves up the start date for processing ballots in a presidential election to no later than noon on the Saturday after the election. Under existing law, processing begins no later than 9 a.m. on the Tuesday after each election.
  • Regarding certification:
    • Specifies that if returns have not been received from any election district or ward in the county by the deadline, the district attorney would be required to immediately initiate an enforcement action to obtain the returns in court.
    • Requires the governor to transmit the certified statement of electors as soon as possible, but no later than the sixth day before the date on which the presidential electors are scheduled to convene. Under existing law, the commission is required to transmit the certificate to the U.S. administrator of general services but no deadline is provided. The governor is required to submit a duplicate of the certificate to one of the presidential electors on or before the first Monday after the second Wednesday in December.
  • Regarding curing provisional ballots:
    • Shortens the deadline for a voter to correct any issues with their provisional ballot from 4 p.m. on the Friday following a presidential election to noon on the Friday following a presidential election. 

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated April 15, 2026

This bill, as introduced on July 31, 2025, would add to the list of acceptable forms of identification when voting a driver's license or ID card issued by any state that satisfies the federal REAL ID requirements. Under existing law, an individual can present:

  • Unexpired driver's license or ID card receipt

data.bill_name: Voter's Change of Address and Registration Status

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Behnke (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on October 24, 2025, would make several changes to voter list maintenance procedures. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Remove a voter from the registration list if they become ineligible to vote for any reason. Under existing law, a voter who becomes ineligible to vote has their status changed from eligible to ineligible. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Require election officials, upon receiving change of address information from a voter via the US postal service's change of address form to verify the information and determine whether the voter has signed the form. 
  • Require election officials to notify the WEC any time a voter is removed from the registration list along with the date of removal and reason for removal.
  • Require each municipality to enter into an arrangement with the US postal service to automatically receive change of address information regarding individuals residing in the municipality no less than once every 60 days. Under existing law, the US postal service must provide this information no less than once every two years preceding the close of registration for a partisan primary and municipalities are permitted, but not required to enter into an agreement.
  • Require the WEC to create an online form for voters to use to submit a change of address or to verify a change of address. The form must be available to submit via mail or electronically. Under existing law, a voter can only change or update their address by re-registering to vote.
  • Require election officials to examine change of address forms submitted by voters no later than 60 days before each election. Clerks would also be required to review change of address information received from the US postal service to verify that the address listed is the voter's correct address and that the information is signed by the voter.
  • Prohibit an election official from sending a mail ballot to a voter without first verifying their address with the US postal service.

Last Updated October 29, 2025

data.bill_name: Compliance With the Federal Help America Vote Act

status_actions.summary: In ASSEMBLY. Placed on Calendar

data.author: Krug (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill was vetoed by Governor Tony Evers on April 8, 2026. As passed by the legislature, the bill would have made changes to voter list maintenance processes, the complaint process under the Help America Vote Act, and set a fee for obtaining a voter registration list. Specifically, the bill would have:

Regarding voter list maintenance:

  • Required the Wisconsin Elections Commission (WEC) to:
    • Enter into an agreement with the Department of Transportation (DOT) to provide the WEC with access to personally identifiable information in order to verify that individuals on the registration list are U.S. citizens. In the event that an individual is identified, the DOT would have been required to provide the WEC with the individual's name, date of birth, and ID number. 
    • Immediately verify the information on the list every time an individual registers to vote or modifies their registration and to conduct matching of information on a daily basis. Under existing law, the WEC and DOT are required to enter into an agreement to share personally identifiable information maintained by DOT but not for the purpose of verifying citizenship.
    • Enter into an agreement with the Office of Vital Records (OVR) to match personally identifiable information from the registration list with the death records maintained by OVR. The WEC and the OVR would have been required to conduct matching of personally identifiable information on a daily basis and to notify the WEC of any registered voter with a death record. Under existing law, the municipal clerk is responsible for checking vital statistics reports to identify voters who have died.
    • Enter into an agreement with the Department of Corrections (DOC) requiring the DOC to transmit a list of individuals who have been convicted of a felony and whose civil rights have not been restored to the WEC on a daily basis. Under existing law, the DOC is only required to do this "on a continuous basis." The bill would require the DOC and WEC to update their agreement at least once every two years.
    • Remove a voter from the registration list if they become ineligible to vote for any reason. Under existing law, a voter who becomes ineligible to vote has their status changed from eligible to ineligible. The WEC would be required to keep a record of all removals, including the date and reason for removal. The bill would have made nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Required the Legislative Audit Bureau (LAB) to:
    • Conduct an audit of the official registration list to identify individuals who are not U.S. citizens in each odd-numbered year. 
    • Enter into agreements to compare the registration list with information received from the DOT, the state registrar, the OVR, and the DOC to identify any non-citizens. If the LAB identifies a non-citizen, they would have been required to verify the individual's citizenship with the systematic alien verification for entitlements database maintained by the federal Department of Homeland Security. 
    • Notify the WEC of any voters identified as non-citizens and to send notice by first class mail or email to each voter informing them that if they do not provide proof of citizenship in less than 30 days after receiving the notice, they will be removed from the registration list.
  • Required voters who receive notice that they have been identified as a noncitizen to provide proof of citizenship, which includes one of the following:
    • A birth certificate
    • A naturalization certificate
    • A U.S. passport

Regarding complaints under the Help America Vote Act:

  • Required an individual making a complaint raising a substantial question of material fact under the Help America Vote Act to make a written request for a hearing, if desired. Under existing law, voters making any complaint may request a hearing. 
  • Allow the WEC to hold a hearing on a complaint even if the complainant has not requested one. The bill would have specified that the WEC must hold all hearings in open session and would require that all proceedings be recorded as is required under current law for administrative hearings.
  • Required the WEC to hear complaints against itself if those complaints allege that the WEC has violated the HAVA and raise a substantial question of material fact. Under existing law, WEC is required to hear complaints of violations of HAVA, including complaints against itself, however, both WEC and the Wisconsin Supreme Court issued decisions stating that WEC could not hear complaints against itself. In June 2025, the WEC received a letter from the Department of Justice stating that this position violates the HAVA. Under existing law, the WEC is not limited to hearing complaints against itself that raise a substantial question of material fact.
  • Allowed any individual who is aggrieved by an order to appeal the decision to the circuit court. The WEC would have been required to publish the results of all complaints dismissed on their website and all decisions would be required to be in writing, as is required under existing law.

Regarding fees for obtaining a registration list: 

  • Created an established fee for an individual seeking to obtain a copy of the official registration list of $1,000 or less. Under existing law, the WEC was required to establish a fee.

Last Updated April 9, 2026

data.bill_name: Voters to Automatically Receive Absentee Ballots

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Duchow (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on October 29, 2025, would allow all voters to join the permanent mail voting list. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow any voter to join the permanent mail voting list and eliminate the existing permanent mail voting list beginning on January 1, 2029. Under existing law, only voters who are indefinitely confined because of age, physical illness, infirmity, or disability are eligible to join the list. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this provision.
  • Allow voters to stay on the permanent mail voting list even if they do not return a mail ballot. Under existing law, voters who fail to return a mail ballot are sent a notice informing them that they must renew their application within 30 days or be removed from the list.
  • Require voters to present unexpired proof of identification with their application in order to join the permanent mail voting list. Under existing law, voters who are eligible to join the permanent mail voting list must submit a signed statement from a doctor but are not required to present proof of identification.
  • Require a voter on the permanent mail voting list who no longer wishes to automatically receive a mail ballot to notify the municipal clerk in writing. Under existing law, the notification does not need to be in writing.
  • Allow voters with disabilities to have an authorized disability advocate that is an employee of a disability rights organization assist them with transmitting a copy of their proof of ID for purposes of filling out a request to receive a mail ballot. Under existing law, a voter who is unable to write their name due to physical disability can authorize an application to be made by any voter on their behalf. 
  • Create reporting requirements for advocates assisting voters. The bill would require the advocates to keep a record of all voters that they helped and to report that information to the municipal clerk no later than the Friday following Election Day. Under existing law, there is no reporting requirements for individuals assisting voters with disabilities to join the permanent mail voting list.
  • Create a felony for any advocate who knowingly transmits a fraudulent proof of ID to a municipal clerk. 
  • Require municipal clerks to notify all existing voters on the permanent mail voting list that they must submit valid proof of identification upon the passage of this bill in order to stay on the list. 


Last Updated October 30, 2025

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Krug (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on November 19, 2025, would:

  • Move up the deadline by which a voter must request a mail ballot from five days before an election to seven days before an election and by no later than 5 p.m.
  • Require municipal clerks to return mail ballots with improperly completed certificates that are received no later than seven days before an election to the voter and to notify the voter via telephone or other means. Under existing law, this requirement is permissive, not mandatory. 
  • Expand the ways in which voters can track their ballot and application. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require election officials to alert a voter registration applicant via text message that their application has been received or if there is an issue with their ballot if the registrant included a phone number with their application. Under existing law and practice, voters do not receive a notification that their ballot is received. The bill would also require election officials to publish information regarding text message notifications to voters in advance of elections. 
    • Require election officials to notify a voter via text message that their ballot has been received or if there is an issue with their ballot. Under existing law, voters can track the status of their ballot online.
  • Make changes to laws regarding central count locations to conform with existing practice. Specifically, the bill would prohibit municipalities from using central count locations for canvassing Election Day ballots. Under existing law, after an election, municipalities that use electronic voting systems can adjourn the canvass to a central counting location. Under the bill, municipalities could continue to use central counting locations to canvass mail ballots.
  • Make changes to the process after a voter is found incompetent and ineligible to vote or found to be eligible again. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the circuit court to notify the elections commission by email and the municipal clerk of the court's determination regarding incompetency and ineligibility to register to vote. Under existing law, the court must notify election officials "in writing." 
    • Require the election commission, upon receiving a determination of incompetency and ineligibility or a determination of restoration of an individual's right to vote, to change the status of that voter within three business days and to notify the voter and the voter's municipal clerk. Under existing law, any subsequent determination of the court must be "communicated" by the clerk.
    • Make changes to the voter registration form by including a box that registrants can check if they have been disqualified to vote and and the ground on which they have been disqualified. Under existing law, voters who have been disqualified from voting are not allowed to register to vote.
  • Eliminate the requirement that voters who have been flagged on the poll list as needing to provide proof of residence provide proof of residence when voting. 
  • Require that all alternate mail ballot sites be located in a building or facility constituting a "fixed location." Under existing law, alternate mail ballots sites must be located as "near as practicable" to the office of the municipal clerk or board of election commissioners. Voters can apply for, vote at, and return mail ballots to these locations.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated November 24, 2025

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Snyder (R)
data.public_commentary:
This resolution, as introduced on January 6, 2024, would propose an amendment to the state constitution to require all voters to present photo ID to cast a ballot. While existing constitutional law is silent as to voter ID, statutory law requires voters to present one of the following when voting in person or requesting a mail ballot: an unexpired (or expired after the date of the most recent general election) Wisconsin driver's license, Wisconsin ID card, military ID, or passport; naturalization certificate issued within two years of Election Day; unexpired driver's license or ID card receipt; tribal ID; unexpired veteran's ID card; or qualifying student ID from a Wisconsin university/college/technical college (or specified alternative). 


The resolution would propose a constitutional amendment to: 

  • Require voters to present ID issued by:
    • the state government
    • the federal government
    • a federally recognized Native American tribe in the state of Wisconsin
    • a college or university in the state of Wisconsin that verifies the individual's identity
  • Expressly require voters who do not provide valid photo ID on Election Day to vote a provisional ballot that may only be counted if they present an accepted form of ID under requirements set by the legislature. Existing statutory law requires such voters to provide required ID at the polling place before the closing of polls (8 p.m.) or at the office of the municipal clerk or board of election commissioners no later than 4 p.m. on the Friday after the election.  

The provisions of this resolution were first passed by the 2023 legislature. Final adoption of the amendment requires passage of the resolution by the current legislature, and approval by a majority of voters at the April 2025 election. 



Last Updated January 13, 2025

data.bill_name: Enforcement of the Federal Help America Vote Act

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Jacque (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 19, 2025, would require the Wisconsin Elections Commission ("WEC") to examine and investigate all complaints, including complaints against the WEC itself, regarding possible HAVA violations in a uniform and nondiscriminatory manner. Specifically, the WEC would be required to do the following in compliance with federal law:

  • Transmit to the complainant and all known interested parties an acknowledgement of receipt of the complaint within five business days from the date of its receipt
  • Make a final determination with respect to the merits of the complaint and issue a decision no later than 89 days following the date on which the complaint was filed
  • Conduct a hearing, if requested, no later than 30 days after receiving a complaint and to publish the results of all dismissed complaints on the WEC's website and to provide those complaints to the legislature.
  • Take corrective action, if the WEC finds that a complaint has merit, to remedy the violation. The complainant would be permitted to appeal to a court of competent jurisdiction.

The Wisconsin Supreme Court ruled in 2022 that the WEC could not hear complaints of possible HAVA violations made against it.

Last Updated June 25, 2025

data.author: Carpenter (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on June 27, 2025, would add to the list of acceptable forms of identification when voting a driver's license or ID card issued by any state that satisfies the federal REAL ID requirements. Under existing law, an individual can present:

  • One of the following documents that is unexpired or if expired, has expired after the date of the most recent general election:
    • a Wisconsin driver's license, 
    • U.S. passport, 
    • an ID card issued by a U.S. uniformed service, 
    • an operator's license or ID card issued by the Department of Transportation.
  • Naturalization certificate issued within two years of Election Day
  • Unexpired driver's license or ID card receipt
  • Tribal ID
  • Unexpired veteran's ID card
  • Qualifying student ID from a Wisconsin university, college, or technical college


Last Updated July 9, 2025

data.bill_name: Compliance With the Federal Electoral Count Reform Act

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Feyen (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on July 17, 2025, would alter certification and canvassing deadlines in order to comply with the federal Electoral Count Reform Act. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Move up the start date for canvassing a presidential election to no later than 5 p.m. on the Friday after the election. Under existing law, canvassing for all elections must begin no later than 9 a.m. on the Monday after the election.
  • Move up the start date for processing ballots in a presidential election to no later than noon on the Saturday after the election. Under existing law, processing begins no later than 9 a.m. on the Tuesday after each election.
  • Specify that if returns have not been received from any election district or ward in the county by the deadline, the district attorney would be required to immediately initiate an enforcement action to obtain the returns in court.
  • Allow a county board of canvassers to adjourn only to the extent permitted by the commission when canvassing a presidential election. Under existing law, a board of canvassers may adjourn no longer than one day at a time and no more than two days in all.
  • Move up the deadline by which the county clerk must deliver the certified statement of canvass results to no later than six days after a presidential election. Under existing law, the clerk must deliver it no later than 14 days after the general election.
  • Require the election commission chairperson or their designee to canvass unofficial returns for the county clerk if by the 16th day after a presidential election, a county board of canvassers has failed to certify a statement or return.
  • Require the governor to transmit the certified statement of electors as soon as possible, but no later than the sixth day before the date on which the presidential electors are scheduled to convene. Under existing law, the commission is required to transmit the certificate to the U.S. administrator of general services but no deadline is provided. The governor is required to submit a duplicate of the certificate to one of the presidential electors on or before the first Monday after the second Wednesday in December.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated July 23, 2025

data.author: Quinn (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on September 29, 2025, would make changes to laws pertaining to election observers. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Specify that election officials are required to provide election observers "uniform and nondiscriminatory access to all stages of the election process" including, but not limited to the certification of machinery, early voting, canvassing, voter appeals, vote tabulation, and recounts. Under existing law, election observers are permitted to observe "all public aspects of the voting process."
  • Specify that a recount be performed publicly in accordance with this bill. Under existing law, all steps of the recount must be performed publicly.
  • Create a misdemeanor offense for any election official who violates the provisions of this bill.

Last Updated October 2, 2025

  • Require each municipality to enter into an arrangement with the US postal service to automatically receive change of address information regarding individuals residing in the municipality no less than once every 60 days. Under existing law, the US postal service must provide this information no less than once every two years preceding the close of registration for a partisan primary and municipalities are permitted, but not required to enter into an agreement.
  • Require election officials to send mailing address verification cards to certain voters following each general election. Under existing law, election officials are not required to do this if they enter into an arrangement with the US postal service.
  • Require the Wisconsin Elections Commission to create an online form for voters to use to submit a change of address or to verify a change of address. The form must be available to submit via mail or electronically. Under existing law, a voter can only change or update their address by re-registering to vote.
  • Require election officials to examine change of address forms submitted by voters no later than 60 days before each election. Clerks would also be required to review change of address information received from the US postal service to verify that the address listed is the voter's correct address and that the information is signed by the voter.

  • Remove a voter from the registration list if they become ineligible to vote for any reason. Under existing law, a voter who becomes ineligible to vote has their status changed from eligible to ineligible. The WEC would be required to keep a record of all removals, including the date and reason for removal. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this requirement.
  • Require the Legislative Audit Bureau (LAB) to:
    • Conduct an audit of the official registration list to identify individuals who are not U.S. citizens in each odd-numbered year. 
    • Enter into agreements to compare the registration list with information received from the DOT, the state registrar, the OVR, and the DOC to identify any non-citizens. If the LAB identifies a non-citizen, they would be required to verify the individual's citizenship with the systematic alien verification for entitlements database maintained by the federal Department of Homeland Security. 
    • Notify the WEC of any voters identified as non-citizens and to send notice by first class mail or email to each voter informing them that if they do not provide proof of citizenship in less than 30 days after receiving the notice, they will be removed from the registration list.
  • Require voters who receive notice that they have been identified as a noncitizen to provide proof of citizenship, which includes one of the following:
    • A birth certificate
    • A naturalization certificate
    • A U.S. passport

Regarding complaints under the Help America Vote Act:

  • Requires an individual making a complaint raising a substantial question of material fact under the Help America Vote Act to make a written request for a hearing, if desired. Under existing law, voters making any complaint may request a hearing. 
  • Allow the WEC to hold a hearing on a complaint even if the complainant has not requested one. The bill would specify that the WEC must hold all hearings in open session and would require that all proceedings be recorded as is required under current law for administrative hearings.
  • Require the WEC to hear complaints against itself if those complaints allege that the WEC has violated the HAVA and raise a substantial question of material fact. Under existing law, WEC is required to hear complaints of violations of HAVA, including complaints against itself, however, both WEC and the Wisconsin Supreme Court issued decisions stating that WEC could not hear complaints against itself. In June 2025, the WEC received a letter from the Department of Justice stating that this position violates the HAVA. Under existing law, the WEC is not limited to hearing complaints against itself that raise a substantial question of material fact.
  • Allow any individual who is aggrieved by an order to appeal the decision to the circuit court. The WEC would be required to publish the results of all complaints dismissed on their website and all decisions would be required to be in writing, as is required under existing law.

Regarding fees for obtaining a registration list: 

  • Create an established fee for an individual seeking to obtain a copy of the official registration list of $1,000 or less. Under existing law, the WEC was required to establish a fee.


Last Updated October 31, 2025

data.bill_name: Voters to Automatically Receive Absentee Ballots

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Tomczyk (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on October 29, 2025, would allow all voters to join the permanent mail voting list. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Allow any voter to join the permanent mail voting list and eliminate the existing permanent mail voting list beginning on January 1, 2029. Under existing law, only voters who are indefinitely confined because of age, physical illness, infirmity, or disability are eligible to join the list. The bill would make nonsubstantive and conforming changes because of this provision.
  • Allow voters to stay on the permanent mail voting list even if they do not return a mail ballot. Under existing law, voters who fail to return a mail ballot are sent a notice informing them that they must renew their application within 30 days or be removed from the list.
  • Require voters to present unexpired proof of identification with their application in order to join the permanent mail voting list. Under existing law, voters who are eligible to join the permanent mail voting list must submit a signed statement from a doctor but are not required to present proof of identification.
  • Require a voter on the permanent mail voting list who no longer wishes to automatically receive a mail ballot to notify the municipal clerk in writing. Under existing law, the notification does not need to be in writing.
  • Allow voters with disabilities to have an authorized disability advocate that is an employee of a disability rights organization assist them with transmitting a copy of their proof of ID for purposes of filling out a request to receive a mail ballot. Under existing law, a voter who is unable to write their name due to physical disability can authorize an application to be made by any voter on their behalf. 
  • Create reporting requirements for advocates assisting voters. The bill would require the advocates to keep a record of all voters that they helped and to report that information to the municipal clerk no later than the Friday following Election Day. Under existing law, there is no reporting requirements for individuals assisting voters with disabilities to join the permanent mail voting list.
  • Create a felony for any advocate who knowingly transmits a fraudulent proof of ID to a municipal clerk. 
  • Require municipal clerks to notify all existing voters on the permanent mail voting list that they must submit valid proof of identification upon the passage of this bill in order to stay on the list. 


Last Updated October 31, 2025

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass pursuant to Senate Joint Resolution 1

data.author: Cabral-Guevara (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on November 14, 2025 would:

  • Expand the ways in which voters can track their ballot and application. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require election officials to alert a voter registration applicant via text message that their application has been received if the registrant included a phone number with their application. Under existing law and practice, voters do not receive a notification that their ballot is received. The bill would also require election officials to publish information regarding text message notifications to voters in advance of elections. 
    • Require election officials to notify a voter via text message that their ballot has been received. Under existing law, voters can track the status of their ballot online.
  • Create new requirements for where an early voting ("alternate ballot site") must be located. Under existing law, they must be located as near as practicable to the office of the municipal clerk or board of election commissioners. Under the bill, the alternate ballot site would have to be in a fixed structure and not a mobile site.
  • Move up the deadline to apply for a mail ballot so that the municipal clerk must receive the ballot by the seventh day before the election rather than 5 p.m. on the fifth day before the election.
  • Require each municipal clerk to report the number of ballots canvassed beginning at 10 p.m. on election night and each hour after until the canvass is complete. The clerk would be required to post each statement online.
  • Make changes to the process of finding a voter incompetent and ineligible to vote. Specifically, the bill would:
    • Require the circuit court to notify the elections commission by email of the court's determination regarding incompetency and ineligibility to register to vote. Under existing law, the court must notify election officials "in writing." 
    • Require the election commission, upon receiving a determination of incompetency and ineligibility or a determination of restoration of an individual's right to vote, to change the status of that voter within three business days and to notify the voter and the voter's municipal clerk. Under existing law, any subsequent determination of the court must be "communicated" by the clerk.
    • Make changes to the voter registration form by including a box that registrants can check if they have been disqualified to vote and and the ground on which they have been disqualified. Under existing law, voters who have been disqualified from voting are not allowed to register to vote.
  • Make changes to laws regarding central count locations to conform with existing practice. Specifically, the bill would prohibit municipalities from using central count locations for canvassing Election Day ballots. Under existing law, after an election, municipalities that use electronic voting systems can adjourn the canvass to a central counting location. Under the bill, municipalities could continue to use central counting locations to canvass mail ballots.

The bill also contains additional provisions related to topics that Voting Rights Lab does not track.

Last Updated January 14, 2026

data.author: Hillenbrand (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 10, 2026, would:

  • Require mail ballots to be received by the close of polls at 8 p.m. on Election Day in order to be counted. Under existing law, ballots postmarked on or before Election Day are considered timely as long as they are received by the start of the canvass (five days after the election, not counting Sundays).
  • Move the deadline to apply for a mail ballot earlier from the sixth day before Election Day to the 13th day.

Last Updated February 13, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Anders (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2026, would: 

  • Restrict mail ("absentee") voting to active duty members of the military and "handicapped" and disabled voters only. The bill would eliminate all other existing excuses. Under existing law, the following excuses qualify a voter for mail voting: 
    • Confinement due to disability, illness, injury, another medical reason, physical disability or immobility because of extreme advanced age, or incarceration or home detention (for crimes other than election bribery)
    • Absence from the county during election hours due to personal or business travel, attendance at an educational institution, or employment of which hours or distance from the county make voting impossible 
    • Absence from the county due to employment as a state or federal officer, or a temporary work assignment outside the county of less than four years 
    • Inability to access their designated voting location due to physical disability  
    • Participation in the Address Confidentiality Program
    • Service as a first responder deployed to an emergency outside the county
  • Require all elections to be conducted using paper ballots. Existing law does not require paper ballots.

status_actions.summary: To SENATE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Holstein (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 21, 2026, would:

  • Grant the legislature the authority to bring legal action against state officials who make or attempt to make unauthorized changes in state election laws or rules, or waive such laws or rules, including through settlement agreements or consent decrees.
  • Grant the legislature legal standing to intervene as a matter of right as a party in any lawsuit seeking declaratory or injunctive relief by or against state agencies, officials or departments that might change election law or regulations.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Butler (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 28, 2026, would prohibit the use of tabulators, require certain voting system information to be made available to the public, and require certain investigations of election crimes. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Prohibit the secretary of state from approving the use of any voting system (including tabulating equipment) that is produced entirely or partially in a foreign country. The bill would also prohibit systems that incorporate hardware or software designed, produced, owned or licensed by an entity that is owned, operated or majority-controlled by a foreign company or a domestic company registered in another county or owned by a person who is not a U.S. citizen. If enacted, the bill would effectively ban ballot tabulators.
  • Require the secretary of state to make copies of program codes, operator manuals and voting system software available to the public on the secretary of state's website at least three months before an election in which it will be used. Existing law does not require such publication.
  • Require local sheriffs to thoroughly and impartially investigate any allegations of illegal voting, voter fraud or election fraud received during an election from any qualified voter. The bill would authorize the county sheriff to request support from other state agencies if the allegations require forensic information technology assets beyond the county's capabilities. The bill would also require the county sheriff to issue a report of the findings from any investigation to the county prosecutor, county clerk, state attorney general and local U.S. attorney stating conclusions as to whether a violation has occurred.
  • Require random, annual physical and internal operating code inspections in each county of specified voting equipment.
  • Specify that Public Election Related Information and Data is subject to disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act. The bill would define Public Election Related Information and Data as data contained within or transferred to or by the Election Management System database, except for the proprietary database schema.

data.author: Moore (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 25, 2026, would: 

  • Require the following information to be included on a form and inserted into the envelope with a mail ("absentee") ballot to be returned by voters: 
    • Name
    • Legal residence physical address
    • Date of birth
    • Statement of the voter's eligibility as prescribed by the Secretary of State and notice of the penalty for false swearing
    • Last four digits of the voter's social security number, driver's license number, or state-issued identification number, with an option for voters without these to sign an attestation under oath
    • Voter's signature, or voter's mark with the signature of the assisting witness
    • Written instructions for voting an absentee ballot
  • Require election officials to confirm that the information on the form provided by voters with their mail ballots matches the voter registration form, and enter any challenge, if any, to the ballot. Currently law does not specify the verification process for mail ballots, however, ballots may be challenged by election workers or poll clerks on numerous grounds, including that the signature on the ballot envelope does not match the one on the voter's ballot application or voter registration record.

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Moore (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 05, 2026, would create new inspection and audit requirements to verify that ballot tabulating equipment cannot connect to the internet. Specifically, the bill would:

  • Require county commissions to inspect all precinct tabulators no later than one week before the start of the in-person voting period.
  • Require county commissions to verify that no modems or SIM cards are installed, ensure the equipment is sealed, and submit a report of their findings to the secretary of state.
  • Require the secretary of state to conduct a pre-election audit of at least 10 percent of precinct tabulating equipment across a minimum of five randomly selected counties, no later than 14 days before Election Day.
  • Require the secretary of state to prepare a public report of their audit findings.
  • Prohibit the use of any tabulating equipment found to be noncompliant until the secretary of state confirms it meets legal standards.

Existing state law prohibits tabulating equipment from being connected to the internet, but has no comparable provision requiring specific pre-election inspections and audits by county commissions or the secretary of state.

Last Updated March 6, 2026

data.bill_name: Vote an Absentee Ballot by Mail

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Funkhouser (R)
data.public_commentary: Summary pending as of 4/7/2026

status_actions.summary: Signed by GOVERNOR

data.author: Funkhouser (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 3, 2026:

  • Limits the voting rights of overseas citizens. Specifically, the bill:
    • Eliminates voting rights for citizens who have established permanent residency in another country. Under prior law, voting rights for citizens whose last U.S. residence was in West Virginia could not be taken away as long as they remained a citizen.
    • Limits the voting rights of citizens born abroad to a U.S. citizen parent whose last U.S. residence was in West Virginia to voting in federal elections. Under prior law, such citizens could vote full ballots based on their West Virginian parent's last residence.
    • Requires overseas voters to hold a valid U.S. passport or other proof of U.S. citizenship. The bill does not specify a procedure for how voters must prove they hold these documents. Prior law did not include any such documentation-holding requirement for overseas voters.
  • Restricts domestic mail voting eligibility. Specifically, the bill:
    • Eliminates the right to vote by mail for voters with disabilities that do not confine them to one place yet make their polling place inaccessible.
    • Repeals explicit grants of mail voting eligibility to individuals serving in an elected or appointed state or federal position and those residing elsewhere for periods of four or fewer years due to a work assignment. Such individuals may continue to qualify to vote by mail under other qualifying excuses.

The bill additionally creates a distinction between voters who are "temporarily residing abroad" and those who are "overseas state and federal voters" who do not have an intent to return to the U.S. or whose intent is unknown. This distinction does not impact their voting experience or rights in any way.

Last Updated April 21, 2026

status_actions.summary: To HOUSE Committee on JUDICIARY

data.author: Funkhouser (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 10, 2026, would narrow the acceptable forms of voter ID to exclude drivers licenses issued by states besides West Virginia and student IDs. Remaining forms of voter ID would be a valid West Virginia drivers licenses, a valid U.S. passport or passport card, a valid employee ID card issued by the federal government or West Virginia, a valid military ID, a valid voter registration card, or another ID issued by West Virginia, a subdivision of West Virginia, or the U.S. government that contains a photo of the voter.

The bill also contains provisions related to drivers licensing that are not tracked by Voting Rights Lab.

Last Updated February 11, 2026

data.author: Woelfel (D)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on January 14, 2026, would modify an existing section of the election code by specifying five distinct misdemeanor offenses related to preventing or obstructing election activities. Existing law makes it a misdemeanor to obstruct an election or prevent any “officer” by force, menace, fraud or intimidation from performing their election duties, this includes by violence, threatening gestures, and speeches. The bill would specifically prohibit conduct that by force, physical acts, or intimidating language:

  • prevents, or attempts to prevent, any election officer, election worker, or other person whose job it is to assist in holding an election (including counting ballots and certifying election results) from discharging their duties.
  • prevents, or attempts to prevent, an election being held;
  • obstructs, or attempts to obstruct, the holding of an election;
  • prevents, or attempts to prevent, a voter from attending an election or freely exercising their right to vote; or
  • prevents, or attempts to prevent, an election officer or election worker from entering or exiting a polling place.

status_actions.summary: Failed to pass SENATE

data.author: House Select Committee on Management Audit
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as amended on February 11, 2026, would modify procedures for a post-election ballot audit. Under existing law, each county clerk is required to conduct an audit of election results not later than the first Thursday following a primary, general, or special election. Under the bill:

  • The clerk would be responsible for ensuring minutes of the audit are taken.
  • The clerk would be required to notify party leaders and independent candidates of the time and place of the audit. The audit would be observed by at least two voters in the county who are members of different parties, and other party representatives and candidates would be permitted to be present. If insufficient observers are available, the clerk would appoint persons to serve as observers.

Last Updated February 17, 2026

data.bill_name: Removal of County Officers Election Code Violations

status_actions.summary: Died in Committee

data.author: House Select Committee on Management Audit
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as introduced on February 9, 2026, changes who is permitted to submit a verified complaint to the governor against a county officer alleging misconduct or malfeasance in office. Under existing law, this can be done by a qualified elector in the county or the county's board of commissioners. Under the bill, the secretary of state would also be permitted to do so if the complaint is against a county clerk and the alleged misconduct or malfeasance in office is for a violation of the state's election code.

Last Updated March 12, 2026

status_actions.summary: Chapter No. 9

data.author: Joint Interim Committee on Corporations, Elections & Political Subdivisions
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on February 27, 2026, makes a change to preregistration for persons less than 18 years old. Under previous Wyoming law, a person who is not yet 18 years of age could preregister to vote if the person would be 18 years old by the date of the next general election. Under the bill, the word "general" is struck from the statute, meaning that a person may preregister only if they will be 18 by the date of the next election (whether that election is a general election or not).

Last Updated March 12, 2026

data.author: Crago (R)
data.public_commentary:
This bill, as enacted on March 7, 2026, exempts judges serving on audit boards from the general June 30 appointment deadline, and also exempts judges conducting a hand count audit from the general requirement that a judge of election not be a member of a counting board.

Last Updated March 12, 2026
